records.title: The Access portal
records.text: The Access portal. The AWS-IAM-style UI for assigning access — pick a principal, a role or permission, a scope, and allow or deny.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: The Access portal
records.text:
**Settings → Access** is where you assign access, the way you'd attach policies in the AWS IAM portal. It shows
every grant in the organization and lets you create new ones.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: Granting access
records.text: Click **Grant access** and fill in the four parts of a [grant](/access-control/grants-and-scopes):

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: Who
records.text: Pick a **member** or a **team** . A team grant reaches everyone on the team.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: Allow or Deny
records.text: **Allow** confers access; **Deny** is an explicit exception that overrides any inherited allow.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: What
records.text:
Choose a **Role** (built-in or [custom](/access-control/custom-roles)) **or** a **single permission**
(e.g. `project:deploy`).

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: Scope
records.text:
**Entire organization**, or a specific **project / runner / cloud identity**. A grant on a container flows
down to its children.
 
 

The grants table lists each entry with its **principal**, **effect** (Allow/Deny), **role or permission**, and
**scope**, and a button to revoke it.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: The deny-exception workflow
records.text:
The portal is where you build the canonical exception — broad access with a precise carve-out:

 
 Grant **Allow · viewer · organization** to Dana. 
 Grant **Deny · `project:view` · project `S`** to Dana. 
 Dana sees the other projects, but not `S`. 
 

See [grants & scopes](/access-control/grants-and-scopes) for the precedence rules behind this.

records.title: The Access portal
records.heading: What you can pick from
records.text:
- **Principals** — the org's members and teams.
- **Roles** — the four built-ins plus your [custom roles](/access-control/custom-roles).
- **Permissions** — the full `resource:action` catalog for single-permission grants.
- **Resources** — your projects, runners, and cloud identities, for scoped grants.

 
 In the open-source Community edition there's a single owner who already holds every
 permission, so there's nothing to assign — the Access portal is an Enterprise surface.

records.title: Activity
records.text: Activity. A record of everything that happens in your organization — every action and every denied attempt — browsable in Community, exportable in Enterprise.

records.title: Activity
records.heading: Activity
records.text:
Every Alethia deployment keeps a full **Activity log**, browsable in **Settings → Activity**. This is
 open-source — it works in the free edition.

records.title: Activity
records.heading: What it records
records.text:
The Activity log is a **complete record of what happens in the org**. The Policy Decision Point writes a row for:

- **Every action** — create, edit, deploy, destroy, the `manage_*` operations (identities, members, integrations,
 billing), exports, and more.
- **Every denied authorization** — someone tried something they weren't allowed to do.
- **Governance changes** — custom roles created/edited/deleted, access grants assigned/revoked, and member
 lifecycle (joins, removals, role changes).

Successful **reads** (viewing a page or listing resources) are not recorded — they fire constantly and would
drown out the signal; their *denials* still are.

Each entry reads as a natural-language line: the **actor**, what they did, the **resource**, and **when**. Denied
attempts are flagged. Filter by time range, user, project, and event type.

 
 The log is engine-agnostic: whether the deployment uses the Postgres engine or the
 [OpenFGA engine](/access-control/openfga), decisions are recorded the same way, so Activity looks
 identical.

records.title: Activity
records.heading: Export
records.text:
Exporting the log to **CSV** (for a SIEM, a spreadsheet, or a compliance archive) is an Enterprise feature,
gated by the `activityExport` entitlement. The **Export CSV** button appears in the header when the entitlement is
held; the export is also enforced server-side.

records.title: Activity
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- How decisions are made (and recorded): [**architecture**](/access-control/architecture).
- The edition split: [**feature matrix**](/editions/feature-matrix).

records.title: Architecture
records.text: Architecture. How the authorization layer is built — the PDP contract, the PostgresRbacPDP engine, registry-as-code, the open-core seams and enterprise loader, the request lifecycle, and the path to swapping the engine for OpenFGA.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Architecture
records.text: This page is for contributors and operators who want to know *how* authorization is implemented, not just how to use it. It maps the concepts from the rest of this section onto the code.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The PDP contract
records.text:
All authorization goes through one interface — the **Policy Decision Point**:

interface Pdp {
 can(actor, action, resource): Promise ; // boolean + reason
 enforce(actor, action, resource): Promise ; // throws ForbiddenError on deny
 bulkCheck(actor, checks[]): Promise ; // many at once; never loop can()
 listAccessible(actor, action, resourceType): Promise ; // ids you may act on
}

Call sites depend on this interface only — never on a concrete engine. That's what makes the engine swappable.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The community engine: PostgresRbacPDP
records.text:
The default implementation answers a check with a single SQL gather followed by a pure decision:

 
 
**Gather grants.** One query selects the actor's grants that match the org, the principal, and the required permission key (` : `), returning each grant's `resource_id` (where `NULL` = org-wide).
 
 
**Walk the hierarchy.** A recursive CTE computes the target resource's ancestor ids (project → org).
 
 
**Decide (pure).** `coversResource(grantResourceIds, resourceId, ancestorIds)`: a grant with `NULL` resource_id (org-wide) covers everything; otherwise a grant covers the resource if its id is the resource itself or one of its ancestors. No matching grant ⇒ **deny**.
 
 
**Enforce & record.** `enforce` throws `ForbiddenError` (→ 403) on denial and writes the decision to the Activity log; every denial and every successful non-read action is recorded (successful reads are skipped).
 
 

 
The decision logic (`coversResource`, permission-key helpers) is a **pure function** in `evaluate.ts` with no database or IO, so it's exhaustively unit-tested (org-wide grants, exact match, hierarchy inheritance, default-deny, wildcards) without needing a live database. The SQL gathering is integrati

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Registry-as-code

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The open-core seams
records.text:
The core exposes extension points and consults them through small accessor functions, each with a community default:

 Seam Accessor Community default Enterprise fills with 
 --- --- --- --- 
 Policy engine `getPdp()` `PostgresRbacPDP` `OpenFgaPdp` (optional) 
 Tenancy scope `getActiveScope(userId)` personal org (`orgId == userId`) the member's active org 
 Auth plugins `getAuthPlugins()` `[]` Better Auth `organization()` (+ SSO later) 
 Entitlements `getEntitlements(actor)` all off license-derived flags 

All four read from `lib/enterprise.ts` — if no enterprise module is registered, they return the community default.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The enterprise loader

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Request lifecycle
records.text:
server action sa["authorize(action, resource)"]
 req --> CLI / stream route cli["authorizeCli(req, action, resource)"]
 sa --> scope["getActiveScope(requireOwner) → actor"]
 cli --> vtok[verify token] --> scope
 scope --> enforce["getPdp().enforce(...)"]
 enforce --> deny forbid[403 Forbidden]
 enforce --> allow query["query under withScope() / withOwnerScope()"]
 query --> rls["PostgreSQL RLS restricts rows to the active org"]
`} />

Two independent gates on every path: the **PDP** (permission) and **RLS** (tenancy). On the CLI/stream paths — which use the service connection that bypasses RLS — the route additionally scopes its query explicitly by the active org, so the tenancy guarantee holds there too.

 
There is a deliberate guard against regressions: a lint (`check:authz-scope`) fails the build if any server action, CLI route, or stream route does ad-hoc `user_id` ownership filtering instead of going through `authorize`/`enforce` + org scoping.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Swapping the engine
records.text: Because every call site depends only on the `Pdp` interface and obtains the engine via `getPdp()`, switching from the Postgres engine to **OpenFGA** (for relationship-based authorization at large scale) is a **binding flip plus a tuple backfill** — the enterprise module registers an `OpenFgaPdp`, and not a single call site changes. The community engine remains the default and the fallback.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: See also
records.text:
- [Roles & Permissions](/access-control/roles-and-permissions) — the registry contents in user terms.
- [Open-Core Model](/access-control/open-core) — the boundary and the CI guards.
- [Security → Row-Level Security](/concepts/security) — the database-level blast wall.
- [Authentication](/concepts/authentication) — how the actor's identity is established before authorization runs.

records.title: Custom roles
records.text: Custom roles. Author your own roles with a tailored permission set — the permission-matrix editor, grouped per resource, like an AWS IAM policy.

records.title: Custom roles
records.heading: Custom roles
records.text:
The four [built-in roles](/access-control/roles-and-permissions) cover the common cases. When you need
something exact — "can plan and deploy projects, but never destroy, and nothing else" — author a **custom role**.

Custom roles are **organization-scoped**: they live in your org, alongside the built-ins, and can be granted
just like them through the [Access portal](/access-portal).

records.title: Custom roles
records.heading: The permission matrix
records.text:
Open **Settings → Roles → New role**. The editor groups every permission **per resource** ("per service",
like AWS IAM), so you toggle exactly what the role can do:

 Group Example permissions 
 --- --- 
 Projects `project:view` · `project:create` · `project:edit` · `project:plan` · `project:deploy` · `project:destroy` 
 Runners `runner:view` · `runner:create` · `runner:edit` · `runner:deploy` · `runner:destroy` 
 Cloud identities `cloud_identity:view` · `cloud_identity:manage_identities` · `cloud_identity:test` 
 Jobs · Connectors · Org · Members · Alerts · Fleet · Support · Activity · Billing … 

Because the provisioning operations are themselves distinct permissions, you get **job-type granularity**: a
role can grant `project:plan` without `project:deploy`, or `runner:deploy` without `runner:destroy`. Every job type a
member can trigger is independently grantable.

 
 The permission catalog is **registry-as-code** — one typed list of resources × actions. New capabilities
 appear in the editor automatically when the registry grows; see
 [architecture](/access-control/architecture).

records.title: Custom roles
records.heading: Editing re-derives access instantly
records.text:
Change a role's permissions and **every grant that uses it updates immediately** — no re-assigning. Under the
hood the change re-expands the role into the underlying permission set (and, when OpenFGA is enabled,
re-syncs the relationship tuples).

records.title: Custom roles
records.heading: Roles vs single permissions
records.text:
A custom role is a reusable *bundle*. For a one-off — grant or deny a single `resource:action` to one person —
you don't need a role at all; grant the [single permission](/access-control/grants-and-scopes) directly in the
Access portal.

records.title: Custom roles
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- Assign roles in the [**Access portal**](/access-portal).
- The full permission catalog: [**roles & permissions**](/access-control/roles-and-permissions).

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.text: Grants & scopes. How access is actually assigned — grants of a role or a single permission to a user or team, scoped to the org or a resource, as an allow or an explicit deny.

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Grants & scopes
records.text:
A **grant** is the unit of access. Every grant answers four questions:

 
 --- --- 
 **Who** a principal — a **user**, or a **team** (everyone on the team) 
 **What** a **role** (a bundle of permissions) **or** a **single permission** (e.g. `project:deploy`) 
 **Where** a **scope** — the whole organization, or one specific resource (a project, runner, …) 
 **Effect** **allow** (the default) or an explicit **deny** 

This is the same shape as an AWS IAM statement, and you create them through the
[Access portal](/access-portal) (or, in Community, the single owner already holds everything).

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Scope and inheritance
records.text:
Resources form a hierarchy: **Org → Project** (runners, cloud identities, and connectors hang directly off
the org). A grant flows *down* that hierarchy:

- A grant on the **organization** applies to every resource in it.
- A grant on a single **project** applies to just that project.

So "let Dana view this one project" grants exactly that — you grant once, at the right
level, instead of enumerating children.

 
 This inheritance is the same in both editions and in both engines. Community resolves it by walking the
 `resource_hierarchy` table; the OpenFGA engine resolves it through relationship tuples. The decision is
 identical.

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Explicit deny — the exception you actually need
records.text:
Inheritance is convenient until you need an *exception*: "Dana can view the org's projects — **except this one
project**." That's an **explicit deny**.

Deny has AWS-IAM semantics:

- A **deny always wins** over an allow, no matter how the allow was granted (directly or inherited). There's no
 "most specific wins" tie-breaking — if any deny covers the resource, access is refused.
- A deny **inherits down** the hierarchy just like an allow. A deny on the *organization* excludes all of its
 projects; a deny on a single *project* excludes just that one.

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Worked example
records.text:
Give Dana org-wide read access, then carve out one project:

 
 **Allow** the `viewer` role on **the organization**. Dana can now view every project. 
 **Deny** the `project:view` permission on **project `S`** (one of the org's projects). 
 Result: Dana sees the *other* projects, but **not `S`**. The deny on `S` overrides the inherited allow. 
 

The decision rule, in one line:

> **allowed ⟺ an allow covers the resource (directly or via an ancestor) AND no deny covers it.**

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Role grants vs single-permission grants
records.text:
Two ways to say "what":

- **Grant a role** — the managed-policy model. Roles bundle permissions; assign the whole bundle. Use built-in
 roles, or author a [custom role](/access-control/custom-roles) for an exact set.
- **Grant a single permission** — the inline model. Attach one `resource:action` (e.g. `project:deploy`) without a
 role. Handy for one-off exceptions, and for denies (deny exactly `project:view` on `S`, nothing more).

Editing a role's permissions instantly re-derives every grant that uses it — there's no need to re-assign.

records.title: Grants & scopes
records.heading: Where this lives
records.text:
- Create and revoke grants in the [**Access portal**](/access-portal). 
- The permissions you can pick come from the [**roles & permissions**](/access-control/roles-and-permissions)
 registry.
- How the two engines evaluate all of the above: [**architecture**](/access-control/architecture).

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.text: Access Control Overview. How Alethia decides who can do what — the Policy Decision Point, roles, permissions, and the tenancy model, with a single mental model that scales from one user to many organizations.

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: Access Control Overview
records.text:
Every action in Alethia — viewing a project, deploying infrastructure, destroying a runner, inviting a teammate — passes through one **authorization layer** before it runs. This page gives you the mental model; the rest of the section is the detail.

 
**One sentence:** an **actor** (you, acting in an **organization**) is granted **roles**, each role carries a set of **permissions**, and a permission is the right to take an **action** on a **resource**. A request is allowed only if the actor holds a matching permission — everything else is denied.

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: The mental model
records.text:
(you + org)"] --> holds grant
 grant --> of a role["role (owner/admin/…)"]
 role --> bundles perms["permissions (project:deploy, …)"]
 perms --> gate act["actions on resources"]
`} />

- **Actor** — a verified user plus their **active organization** (the tenancy scope). In the free Community edition there is exactly one organization per user — your *personal organization* — so "actor" is effectively just "you".
- **Resource** — a thing you act on: an organization, a **project** (an infrastructure application), a **runner**, a **cloud identity** (a connected cloud account), a job, a connector (git / integration provider), a member, alerts, the managed fleet, support cases, Activity logs, or billing.
- **Action** — what you're trying to do: `view`, `create`, `edit`, `plan`, `deploy`, `destroy`, `test`, and the management actions (`manage_identities`, `manage_members`, `manage_connectors`, `view_alerts`, `manage_alerts`, `view_activity`, `export_activity`, `manage_billing`, `manage_support`).
- **Permission** — a single `resource:action` pair, e.g. `project:deploy`. The complete, exhaustive list is fixed in code (see [Roles & Permissions](/access-control/roles-and-permissions)).
- **R

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: Default-deny
records.text: The system is **default-deny**: if nothing explicitly grants you a permission for what you're attempting, the answer is *no*. There is no implicit "owner of the row can do anything" shortcut scattered through the code — every decision is made in one place (the **Policy Decision Point**, or PDP) and recorded.

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: One decision point
records.text:
A single component answers every authorization question:

- **`enforce(action, resource)`** — the gate at the top of every server action and API route. It allows the request or throws a `403 Forbidden`.
- **`listAccessible(type)`** — the "which ones can I see?" query that powers list views, so a list only ever returns resources you're allowed to see.

Because all decisions flow through this one point, the rules are consistent everywhere, every denial can be audited, and the underlying engine can be swapped without touching a single call site (see [Architecture](/access-control/architecture)).

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: Two layers of protection
records.text:
Authorization is enforced in **two independent layers**, so a bug in one cannot expose your data:

 
 
 Application-level role + permission checks: *can this actor take this action on this resource?* Rich, hierarchical, role-aware.
 
 
 A coarse PostgreSQL guarantee that a request only ever sees rows belonging to its active organization — enforced by the database itself, beneath the application.
 
 

The PDP decides *what you may do*; Row-Level Security guarantees *whose data you can even touch*. Together they mean a cross-tenant leak would require both layers to fail at once.

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: Community vs Enterprise at a glance
records.text:
The whole model above ships in the **free, open-source (AGPL) Community edition**. Organizations with multiple members are the main thing the commercial **Enterprise edition** adds.

 Community (free) Enterprise 
 --- --- --- 
 Roles & permissions (owner/admin/operator/viewer) ✅ ✅ 
 Per-project grants + inheritance ✅ ✅ 
 Row-Level Security tenant isolation ✅ ✅ 
 Activity log of decisions ✅ ✅ 
 **Organizations, teams, members, invitations** — (single personal org) ✅ 
 **SSO (SAML / OIDC)** — ✅ 
 **Custom roles, the access portal, activity export** — ✅ 
 **OpenFGA fine-grained engine** — ✅ (optional) 

For the authoritative, capability-by-capability breakdown, see the [**Editions** feature matrix](/editions/feature-matrix).

In Community, every user is the sole `owner` of their own personal organization — so the org/roles machinery is fully present but you simply never see another member. The same code lights up into real multi-member organizations under an Enterprise license, with **no change to how permissions are decided**. See [Open-Core Model](/access-control/open-core).

records.title: Access Control Overview
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
The four built-in roles, the full permission matrix, grants, and how a grant on the organization flows down to its projects.
 
 
 Multi-member organizations, invitations, and how a member's role determines what they can do (Enterprise).
 
 
 What's free vs licensed, and the boundary that keeps the Community build complete on its own.
 
 
 For contributors: the PDP internals, the registry-as-code, the seams, and the enterprise loader.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.text: Members & invitations. How people join an organization — the invitation lifecycle from email to membership, and how membership becomes authorization.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Members & invitations
records.text:
In the open-source Community edition your organization has exactly one member: you, the
owner. The Enterprise edition adds **multiple members** and the flow to bring them in.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Invite
records.text:
In **Settings → Members → Invite member**, enter an email and pick a role. An invitation is created and an
email is sent with an accept link.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Accept
records.text:
The invitee clicks the link and lands on the accept page. If they're not signed in, they're sent to sign-in
first and returned afterward — and because email sign-in doubles as sign-up, this works even if they don't have
an account yet.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Join
records.text: Accepting creates their **membership** in the organization with the chosen role.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Authorize
records.text:
Membership is turned into real authorization: the member's role is synced into a **grant**, so the
[Policy Decision Point](/access-control/architecture) starts authorizing them immediately.
 
 

 
 The last step matters. An organization's membership role and the authorization **grant** are kept in step
 automatically — adding a member, changing their role, or removing them updates their access without any
 manual grant management.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Roles for members
records.text:
When you invite or edit a member you pick one of the [roles](/access-control/roles-and-permissions) — the four
built-ins or a [custom role](/access-control/custom-roles). For finer control (scoped or deny grants), use the
[Access portal](/access-portal) after they've joined.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Switching organizations
records.text:
A member can belong to more than one organization and switch between them with the **organization switcher** in
the dashboard header. The active organization scopes everything — what you see, and what the PDP and Row-Level
Security enforce.

records.title: Members & invitations
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- Group members for bulk grants with [**teams**](/access-control/teams).
- Manage the org itself in [**organizations**](/access-control/organizations).

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.text: Open-Core Model. What's free in the AGPL Community edition vs the commercial Enterprise edition, how entitlements work, and the architectural boundary that keeps the Community build complete and self-contained.

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.heading: Open-Core Model
records.text:
Alethia is **open-core**. The entire product — including the authorization engine, roles, grants, hierarchy, and tenant isolation — is free and open source under the **AGPL-3.0**. A separate **Enterprise edition** adds organization-scale features under a commercial license. This page explains the **engineering** that keeps the split honest.

 
 Looking for the *feature* comparison or how licensing works? That's the [**Editions**](/editions) section —
 the [feature matrix](/editions/feature-matrix) and [licensing](/editions/licensing). This page is the
 contributor-facing view: the dependency boundary and the CI guards that enforce it.

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.heading: What's free vs licensed
records.text:
The summary below is a quick reference; the [Editions feature matrix](/editions/feature-matrix) is authoritative.

 Capability Community (AGPL) Enterprise (commercial) 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Policy Decision Point + `enforce` / `listAccessible` ✅ ✅ 
 Built-in roles (owner / admin / operator / viewer) ✅ ✅ 
 Per-resource grants + Org → Project inheritance ✅ ✅ 
 Explicit deny + single-permission grants ✅ ✅ 
 Row-Level Security tenant isolation ✅ ✅ 
 Activity log of authorization decisions ✅ ✅ 
 Single-user personal organization ✅ ✅ 
 **Multi-member organizations, teams, invitations** — ✅ 
 **SSO (SAML / OIDC)** — ✅ 
 **Custom roles + the access portal** — ✅ 
 **Activity export** — ✅ 
 **OpenFGA-backed policy engine at scale** — ✅ (optional) 

 
The dividing line is **"single tenant vs many tenants."** Everything needed to run Alethia for yourself or your team-as-one-account is free. The Enterprise edition is about *organizations with multiple members and identities* — the things larger teams and companies need.

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.heading: Entitlements
records.text: The Enterprise edition gates its features behind **entitlements** derived from a license. The core has **no `if (licensed)` checks scattered through it** — it simply asks the enterprise layer "is this feature on?" and the enterprise layer answers from the license. When no license is present (the Community build), every entitlement is off and the corresponding features are simply absent.

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.heading: The boundary that makes it work
records.text:
Open-core only stays maintainable if the free edition is genuinely complete on its own. Alethia enforces that with a strict, mechanically-checked rule:

 
**The AGPL core never imports the Enterprise package.** The dependency only goes one way: Enterprise may build on core; core must run without Enterprise even present.
 

How it's enforced:

- **Seams, not branches.** The core exposes a handful of extension points (the policy engine, the tenancy resolver, the auth plugins, the entitlement gate). In the Community build they return community defaults; the Enterprise package fills them in.
- **One integration file.** Exactly one core file is allowed to load the Enterprise package, and it does so *tolerantly* — if the package isn't installed, it quietly falls back to community behavior.
- **CI guards.** Three lint checks run on every build:
 - **boundary guard** — fails if any file outside the allowed integration point imports the Enterprise package;
 - **authz-scope guard** — fails if any code does ad-hoc ownership checks instead of going through the decision point;
 - **no-Supabase guard** — a legacy guard from the database migration, kept green.
- **Builds without it.** The Communi

records.title: Open-Core Model
records.heading: For self-hosters
records.text:
If you self-host the Community edition you get the full single-tenant product: roles, grants, hierarchy, the Activity log, and database-level tenant isolation, all under the AGPL. You can run it indefinitely with no license. Adding the Enterprise edition is what unlocks shared organizations and the features in the table above.

See [Self-Hosting](/self-hosting) for deployment, and [Architecture](/access-control/architecture) for exactly how the seams and the loader work.

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.text: OpenFGA engine. The optional relationship-based authorization engine — what it is, how the model is generated, and how to stand it up.

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.heading: OpenFGA engine
records.text:
Alethia's authorization runs behind one interface (the [Policy Decision Point](/access-control/architecture)),
so the **engine** underneath can be swapped without touching a single call site. The default engine,
`PostgresRbacPDP`, is open-source and handles everything described in this section. The **OpenFGA engine** is an
optional Enterprise alternative — a dedicated [OpenFGA](https://openfga.dev) relationship-graph server (ReBAC)
for fine-grained authorization at scale.

 
 OpenFGA is **optional even with a license.** Enterprise works perfectly on the Postgres engine; turn OpenFGA
 on when you want a purpose-built relationship engine. Leaving `OPENFGA_*` unset keeps the Postgres engine.

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.heading: How it stays in sync with everything you've read
records.text:
The key design choice: **Postgres stays the source of truth.** You author roles, grants, and exceptions exactly
as documented elsewhere — they're stored in Postgres — and every change is *also* mirrored into OpenFGA as
relationship **tuples**. The OpenFGA engine reads those tuples to decide.

Two consequences:

- **Custom roles need zero model changes.** A role is a Postgres bundle of permissions that *expands to tuples*
 when granted. Add or edit a role and the tuples update — the OpenFGA model itself never changes.
- **The model is generated from the registry.** The authorization model (types, relations, the deny
 exclusions, the hierarchy inheritance) is produced directly from the same `resource × action` registry that
 drives everything else — so it covers every permission by construction.

The Postgres and OpenFGA engines are kept in **parity**: for any (actor, action, resource), they return the
same decision — including the allow/deny precedence and hierarchy inheritance from
[grants & scopes](/access-control/grants-and-scopes).

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.heading: Standing it up
records.text:
The high-level operator steps (the full runbook is in
[Enterprise setup](/editions/enterprise-setup)):

 
 Bring up the `enterprise` compose profile — the OpenFGA server and its own datastore. 
 Create a store and point the app at it (`OPENFGA_API_URL`, `OPENFGA_STORE_ID`). 
 On boot the app writes the generated model and backfills tuples from your existing grants + hierarchy. 
 From then on, every grant/role/team change dual-writes to OpenFGA; the engine answers all checks.

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.heading: When to use it
records.text:
The Postgres engine is the right default for most deployments. Reach for OpenFGA when you want a dedicated
authorization service — very large tenancies, cross-service authorization, or a relationship graph you'll grow
beyond Alethia. Because the swap is a binding flip, you can start on Postgres and move later with a tuple
backfill, no application changes.

records.title: OpenFGA engine
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- The engine interface and the binding flip: [**architecture**](/access-control/architecture).
- Turning it on: [**Enterprise setup**](/editions/enterprise-setup).

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.text: Organizations & Teams. How tenancy works — the personal organization in Community, and multi-member organizations with invitations and per-member roles in Enterprise.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Organizations & Teams
records.text: An **organization** is the tenancy boundary: every project, runner, cloud identity, and job belongs to exactly one organization, and members of that organization see only its resources. Your **active organization** is half of your [actor](/access-control) identity — it's the scope every permission check runs against.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Community: your personal organization
records.text:
In the free Community edition there is **one organization per user** — your *personal organization* — and you are its sole `owner`. You never see another member. Everything you create is scoped to it automatically.
 

Under the hood, your personal organization's id is simply your user id, and you're given a single org-wide `owner` grant when your account is created. That's the entire setup — no organization to create, no members to manage. The full roles-and-grants engine is present and enforcing; it just has one member: you.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Enterprise: multi-member organizations
records.text:
The commercial Enterprise edition turns the organization into a real, shared, multi-member tenancy boundary:

 
 
 Named, shared tenancy boundaries. The creator becomes the owner . All existing resources stay scoped to their organization.
 
 
 Users who belong to an organization, each holding a role ( owner / admin / operator / viewer ) that determines what they can do.
 
 
 Invite a teammate by email; they accept and join with the role you choose.
 
 
 Group members so grants can target a whole team at once (rather than each user).

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Lifecycle
records.text:
**Create an organization.** The creator is automatically its `owner` — they get an org-wide owner grant, so the authorization engine recognizes them as fully privileged within the new org immediately.
 
 
**Invite members by email.** Each invitation carries the role the new member will hold. The invitee accepts and becomes a member.
 
 
**Members act within the org.** Every request a member makes is checked against *their* role in *that* organization — an `operator` can deploy infrastructure but not manage billing; a `viewer` can only look.
 
 
**Scope flows everywhere.** Because the member's organization is their active scope, list views, actions, and the database isolation layer all key off it — a member of org A can never see org B's resources.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: How a member's role becomes access
records.text:
When a member makes a request, the system resolves their **active organization** and checks their grant in it:

member: carol, role = operator, in org "Acme"
 request: deploy project "Acme/payments"
 ⇒ operator includes project:deploy → ALLOWED (and scoped to Acme only)

 request: invite a new member
 ⇒ operator does NOT include member:manage_members → FORBIDDEN

Roles compose with the [Org → Project hierarchy](/access-control/roles-and-permissions#inheritance-the-org--project-hierarchy): you can give someone `operator` org-wide, or narrow them to just one project.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Tenancy isolation
records.text:
Organization isolation isn't only an application-level check. The database enforces it too: every query runs with the active organization in scope, and PostgreSQL Row-Level Security only returns rows for that organization. So even a flaw in the application layer cannot surface another organization's data. See [Security → Row-Level Security](/concepts/security).

 
A member can belong to **several organizations** and switch between them with the **organization switcher** in
the dashboard header; the active organization scopes everything the PDP and Row-Level Security enforce.
[**SSO**](/access-control/sso) sign-in, [**teams**](/access-control/teams), the
[**invitation flow**](/access-control/members-and-invitations), and the [**access portal**](/access-portal)
are all part of the Enterprise edition.

records.title: Organizations & Teams
records.heading: Relationship to roles
records.text: Organizations are *where* you have access; [roles](/access-control/roles-and-permissions) are *what* that access is. A member always has both: an organization (the scope) and a role within it (the capability). Changing someone from `viewer` to `operator` changes what they can do; moving them between organizations changes what they can see.

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.text: Roles & Permissions. The four built-in roles, the complete resource × action permission matrix, how grants assign roles at a scope, and how the Org → Project hierarchy makes a single grant flow downward.

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: Roles & Permissions
records.text: This is the reference for *what each role can do* and *how access is assigned*. The model is **registry-as-code**: the resources, actions, and role templates below are defined in one place in the source and seeded into the database, so the docs, the UI, and the enforcement never drift.

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: Permissions
records.text: A **permission** is a single `resource:action` pair, e.g. `project:deploy`. Permissions are not freeform — only the pairs in the matrix below exist. Anything not listed is not a permission and can never be granted (default-deny by construction).

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: Resources
records.text:
The resource types are the `RESOURCES` union in `apps/console/lib/authz/registry.ts`.

 Resource What it is 
 --- --- 
 `org` An organization (the tenancy boundary). 
 `project` An infrastructure application (owns N [environments](/concepts/glossary), each with a cluster + databases + networking + …). 
 `runner` An execution agent that runs provisioning jobs. 
 `cloud_identity` A connected **cloud account** — the keyless-OIDC credential anchor for AWS / GCP / Azure / Hetzner. This is the RBAC resource for a cloud account. 
 `job` A provisioning job (`plan` / `deploy` / `destroy` / …). 
 `connector` A **git / pluggable integration provider** connection (e.g. GitHub, GitLab). The connector catalog names provider *types*; a connected cloud *account* is a `cloud_identity`, not a `connector`. 
 `member` A member of an organization. 
 `activity` The Activity log. 
 `billing` Subscription & billing. 
 `alert` Alert channels, rules, and deliveries. 
 `fleet` Managed warm-pool (in-app scaler) config. Provisions real cloud VMs, so it's an owner/admin-only capability. 
 `support_case` A help-desk support case. Any member may open/track/reply to their own; `manage_support` is the staff-triage

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: The matrix
records.text:
Each resource permits an exact set of actions — the `MATRIX` in `registry.ts`. A `resource:action` pair not listed here is not a permission and can never be granted (default-deny by construction).

 Resource Actions 
 --- --- 
 `org` `view` · `edit` · `manage_billing` 
 `project` `view` · `create` · `edit` · `plan` · `deploy` · `destroy` 
 `runner` `view` · `create` · `edit` · `deploy` · `destroy` 
 `cloud_identity` `view` · `manage_identities` · `test` 
 `job` `view` · `create` · `edit` 
 `connector` `view` · `manage_connectors` 
 `member` `view` · `manage_members` 
 `activity` `view_activity` · `export_activity` 
 `billing` `manage_billing` 
 `alert` `view_alerts` · `manage_alerts` 
 `fleet` `view` · `create` · `edit` · `destroy` 
 `support_case` `view` · `create` · `reply` · `manage_support` 

The `test` action re-runs a cloud identity's server-side connection verify; `plan` / `deploy` / `destroy` map to the corresponding `tofu` provisioning jobs.

 
Adding a capability is a **one-line change** to the `RESOURCES` / `ACTIONS` / `MATRIX` registry in `registry.ts`. Because the resource and action sets are exhaustive string-literal unions, a new action that isn't handled becomes a c

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: The four built-in roles
records.text:
Roles are bundles of permissions. There are four built-in templates:

 
 
 Everything. Full control of the organization including members and billing. The creator of an organization (and every Community user, in their personal org) is an owner.
 
 
 Everything **except billing**. Can manage members, identities, integrations, and all infrastructure — but not the subscription.
 
 
 Operate infrastructure: `view` / `create` / `edit` / `plan` / `deploy` / `destroy` on projects, runners, and jobs, plus `view` on the org and connectors and read access to alerts (`view_alerts`). **Cannot** manage cloud identities, members, billing, the managed fleet, or the Activity log.
 
 
 Read-only. Can `view` everything (including alert config) but change nothing — plus open/reply to their own support cases.
 
 

 Role Infra (projects/runners/jobs) Cloud identities Connectors Members Alerts Fleet Activity Billing 
 --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- --- 
 **owner** full manage manage manage manage manage view + export manage 
 **admin** full manage manage manage manage manage view + export — 
 **operator** full — view — view — — — 
 **viewer** view only view view view view view — — 

Every role also hol

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: Grants — assigning a role
records.text:
You never attach a permission to a user directly. Instead you create a **grant**:

> *principal* gets *role* at *scope*.

- **principal** — a user (or, in Enterprise, a team).
- **role** — one of the roles above.
- **scope** — what the grant applies to: the whole **organization**, or a single **project**.

A grant scoped to the whole organization is a **wildcard** grant — it applies to every resource in the org. (This is exactly how the single grant each Community user receives works: *owner, org-wide*.)

grant: alice → operator → project "production" # operator on that project
grant: bob → viewer → org # read-only across the whole org
grant: you → owner → org # the Community default — full control of your personal org

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: Inheritance: the Org → Project hierarchy
records.text:
Resources form a tree — the parent chain in `apps/console/lib/authz/fga-hierarchy.ts`. `project`, `runner`, `cloud_identity`, and `connector` all hang directly off the `org`:

 project
 org --> runner
 org --> cloud_identity
 org --> connector
`} />

A grant **flows downward**. If you grant someone `operator` on the **organization**, they automatically have those permissions on **every project inside it** — you don't grant each project separately. A project-scoped grant covers just that project.

grant: dana → operator → project "staging-api"
 ⇒ dana can plan/deploy/destroy project "staging-api"
 ⇒ dana CANNOT touch project "production-api" (no grant)

This is why a handful of grants covers an entire estate: assign at the highest scope that makes sense and let inheritance do the rest. The decision engine resolves this by walking up from the target resource to see whether any of your grants cover it or an ancestor — see [Architecture](/access-control/architecture).

records.title: Roles & Permissions
records.heading: How this shows up
records.text:
- **List views** only show resources you can `view` — the list itself is filtered by your grants, not just hidden in the UI.
- **Actions** (deploy, destroy, invite, …) are checked the instant you trigger them; if you lack the permission you get a clear *Forbidden*, and the attempt is recorded in the Activity log.
- **Community**: with a single org-wide `owner` grant, you can do everything in your own personal organization — the role machinery is invisible until you add teammates under [Enterprise organizations](/access-control/organizations).

records.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
records.text: Single Sign-On (SSO). Let your team sign in through your own identity provider over OIDC or SAML — Alethia as the service provider.

records.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
records.heading: Single Sign-On
records.text:
SSO lets members sign in through **your** identity provider (IdP) instead of (or alongside) email and social
login. Alethia is the **Service Provider** that *consumes* your IdP — it never runs an identity provider of its
own.

You bring the IdP. Supported via standard protocols (see [OIDC](/standards/oidc) and [SAML & SCIM](/standards/scim-saml) for the protocol details):

- **[OIDC](/standards/oidc)** — Okta · Microsoft Entra ID · Google Workspace · **AWS IAM Identity Center** · OneLogin · Ping · …
- **[SAML 2.0](/standards/scim-saml)** — the same IdPs over SAML.

Under the hood SSO is the Better Auth `@better-auth/sso` plugin (shipped in the enterprise package); a provider stores either an `oidcConfig` or a `samlConfig` and can be scoped per organization (`sso_provider.organizationId`) or left instance-wide.

 
 There's nothing to run on Alethia's side — no Hydra, no Alethia-as-IdP. Each organization registers its own
 provider, so different orgs can use different IdPs.

records.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
records.heading: Registering a provider
records.text:
In **Settings → Single Sign-On → Register provider**, choose the protocol and fill in the IdP's details:

 
 

 Field What it is 
 --- --- 
 Provider ID A short slug for the provider (e.g. `okta`) 
 Email domain The domain whose users route to this provider (e.g. `company.com`) 
 Issuer URL Your IdP's issuer / discovery URL 
 Client ID / Client secret The OIDC application credentials from your IdP 

 
 

 Field What it is 
 --- --- 
 Provider ID A short slug (e.g. `entra`) 
 Email domain The domain whose users route to this provider 
 IdP Entity ID Your IdP's SAML entity / issuer ID 
 SSO URL (entry point) The IdP's SAML sign-in endpoint 
 X.509 certificate The IdP's signing certificate

records.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
records.heading: Provisioning into the organization
records.text:
When a member signs in through SSO for the first time, Alethia **provisions** them into the organization with a
role (least-privileged by default, or mapped from your IdP's group/role claim). From that point they're a
normal [member](/access-control/members-and-invitations) — the [PDP](/access-control/architecture) authorizes
them exactly as it would anyone else.

records.title: Single Sign-On (SSO)
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- How members are authorized once they're in: [**grants & scopes**](/access-control/grants-and-scopes).
- Turning SSO on for a deployment: the [**Enterprise setup runbook**](/editions/enterprise-setup).

records.title: Teams
records.text: Teams. Group members so you can grant access to a whole team at once — group-based grants.

records.title: Teams
records.heading: Teams
records.text:
A **team** is a named group of organization members. Grant access to a team, and **everyone on it inherits the
grant** — add or remove people from the team and their access follows automatically.

records.title: Teams
records.heading: Why teams
records.text:
Without teams, sharing a project with five engineers means five grants — and five revokes when the work ends.
With a team, it's **one grant to the team**, and membership is the only thing you manage afterward.

Teams are the natural principal for durable, role-based access: "the platform team can deploy the
production projects" is one grant on one team.

records.title: Teams
records.heading: Managing teams
records.text:
In **Settings → Teams**:

 
 **New team** — give it a name (e.g. `platform`). 
 **Manage** — add or remove organization members. 
 Grant access to the team in the [Access portal](/access-portal) — pick the team as the principal.

records.title: Teams
records.heading: How team grants resolve
records.text:
A grant whose principal is a team applies to a user whenever they're a member of that team. This is resolved in
**both** authorization engines:

- The Postgres engine matches team grants by joining your team memberships at decision time.
- The OpenFGA engine models a team as a group (`team:… # member`), so a team grant reaches members through the
 relationship graph.

Either way, removing someone from a team **immediately** removes the access that came from it.

 
 Teams compose with everything else: grant a [custom role](/access-control/custom-roles) to a team, scope it
 to a [project](/access-control/grants-and-scopes), and add per-member [deny](/access-control/access-portal)
 exceptions on top.

records.title: Teams
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- Assign team grants in the [**Access portal**](/access-portal).
- Bring people into the org first via [**members & invitations**](/access-control/members-and-invitations).

records.title: Authentication
records.text: Authentication. Device Authorization Grant flow, token storage, and automatic refresh.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Authentication
records.text: alethia uses the **Device Authorization Grant** (RFC 8628) for authentication. You never type a password in the terminal. For the full technical details, see [Authentication Flows](/concepts/authentication).

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Login
records.text: alethia login

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: CLI generates device code
records.text: A UUID identifying this login attempt.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Browser opens
records.text: Navigates to `{console_url}/cli/login?device_code={code}`.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Approve in browser
records.text:
Sign in with any OAuth provider (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Google) or email OTP. Console binds the pending
`device_code` to your account and approves it — nothing is typed back into the terminal.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: CLI polls for the token
records.text:
While you approve in the browser, the CLI shows a spinner and polls `POST /api/auth/cli/exchange` with the
`device_code`. Console returns HTTP 404 while pending; once approved it returns a JWT pair (access + refresh).

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Tokens stored
records.text: Saved to `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json`.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Force Re-authentication
records.text:
alethia login --force

Re-authenticates even if a valid token already exists. Useful when switching accounts.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Logout
records.text:
alethia logout

Removes `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json`.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Token Lifecycle
records.text:
Token TTL Purpose 
 ------- ----- --------- 
 Access token 1 hour `Authorization: Bearer` header on API calls 
 Refresh token 90 days Obtain new access tokens transparently 

Before each API call, alethia decodes the access token's `exp` claim. If it has expired (or expires within a minute), the CLI refreshes automatically via `POST /api/auth/cli/refresh` and rewrites the stored access token. The user never notices.

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Identity & Active Org
records.text:
After logging in, confirm who you are signed in as and which organization the CLI is scoped to:

alethia whoami # user, active org, role, plan, default runner
alethia config # control-plane URL, active org, on-disk file paths

The active organization is sent as the `X-Alethia-Org` header on every request. Set or change it
with `alethia org switch`. See [Identity & Global Options](/cli/identity) and
[Organizations, Members & Teams](/cli/commands/organizations).

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Environment Variables
records.text:
Variable Default Purpose 
 ---------- --------- --------- 
 `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` Production URL (baked at build) Override the Alethia API URL 

alethia login # authenticates against local dev server

records.title: Authentication
records.heading: Token Storage
records.text:
~/.config/alethia/credentials.json

{
 "access_token": "eyJ...",
 "refresh_token": "eyJ...",
 "user_email": "you@example.com"
}

The access-token expiry is read from the JWT itself (the `exp` claim), so no separate timestamp is
stored. The active-org context is kept separately in `~/.config/alethia/config.json` (see
[Configuration](/cli/configuration)).

 
This file contains authentication tokens. It is not encrypted — protect it with filesystem permissions.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.text: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO). Manage RBAC roles and access grants, and inspect SSO providers, from the alethia CLI.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: Access Control
records.text:
Alethia's authorization model has three CLI-manageable parts:

- **Roles** are permission bundles. Four **built-in** templates ship with every org
 (`owner`, `admin`, `operator`, `viewer`) and are read-only; **custom** roles are org-scoped
 and editable.
- **Grants** bind a **principal** (a user or a team) to a role **or** a single permission, at a
 resource scope, as an **allow** or an explicit **deny** (deny wins).
- **SSO providers** are the configured OIDC/SAML identity providers (read-only from the CLI).

All commands operate on the **active organization** (set via `alethia org switch`); override
per-invocation with `--org `. Every command accepts the global
[`-o, --output `](/cli/identity#output-formats) flag.

 
Authoring **custom roles** and **access grants** requires an **Enterprise** license. Listing roles
(the built-in templates) and reading SSO providers work on any plan. SSO provider secrets and
config JSON are never returned by the API.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: alethia roles
records.text: Alias: `role`.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: roles list
records.text:
Lists the active organization's roles — the four built-in templates (flagged) followed by any
custom roles, with a permission count.

alethia roles list
alethia roles list -o json

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: roles create
records.text:
Creates a custom role. Repeat `--permission` for each `resource:action` key the role should carry.

alethia roles create deployers \
 --permission project:plan \
 --permission project:deploy \
 --permission job:view

 Flag Description 
 -------------- ---------------------------------------------------- 
 `--permission` Permission key to grant (repeatable, e.g. `project:deploy`)

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: roles delete <id>
records.text:
Deletes a custom role (prompts for confirmation). Built-in roles cannot be deleted; grants
referencing the role are removed too.

alethia roles delete 3f1c…

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: alethia grants
records.text:
Aliases: `grant`, `access`. A grant binds a principal to **exactly one** of a role (`--role`) or a
single permission (`--permission`), at a resource scope. Omit `--resource` for an **org-wide** grant.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: grants list
records.text:
Lists the active organization's access grants.

alethia grants list
alethia grants list -o json

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: grants add
records.text:
Assigns a grant.

bash

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: Bind a user to a custom role, org-wide
records.text: alethia grants add --principal-type user --principal --role

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: Allow a team a single permission on one project
records.text:
alethia grants add --principal-type team --principal \
 --permission project:deploy --resource-type project --resource

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: Explicitly deny destroy on a project (deny wins over any allow)
records.text:
alethia grants add --principal --effect deny \
 --permission project:destroy --resource-type project --resource 

 Flag Description 
 ------------------ ------------------------------------------------------------- 
 `--principal-type` `user` (default) or `team` 
 `--principal` Principal id (required) 
 `--effect` `allow` (default) or `deny` 
 `--role` Role id to bind (XOR `--permission`) 
 `--permission` Single permission key to bind (XOR `--role`) 
 `--resource-type` Resource type to scope to (`project`, `runner`, `cloud_identity`, `org`; default `org`) 
 `--resource` Resource id to scope to (omit for org-wide)

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: grants remove <id>
records.text:
Revokes a grant (prompts for confirmation). The principal loses this access.

alethia grants remove a1b2…

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: alethia sso
records.text:
Inspect the active organization's configured SSO identity providers (OIDC and SAML). Read-only —
registering a provider is done in the console. Secrets and config JSON are never shown; `Enabled`
reflects whether the provider's domain is verified.

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: sso list
records.text:
alethia sso list
alethia sso list -o json

records.title: Access (Roles, Grants & SSO)
records.heading: sso get <id>
records.text:
Shows a single provider as a detail card.

alethia sso get 3f1c…

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.text: Billing, Usage & Fleet. Inspect the active organization's billing and usage, and configure managed-fleet warm pools, from the alethia CLI.

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.text:
Three related views into an organization's plan and the platform's provisioning capacity:

- **`billing`** — the active organization's plan, subscription status, seats, and period boundaries.
- **`usage`** — current consumption: seats, runner-minutes, projects, and AI credits.
- **`fleet`** — the managed pool of warm provisioning runners (platform-operator config).

`billing` and `usage` operate on the **active organization** (set via `alethia org switch`); override
per-invocation with `--org `. Every command accepts the global
[`-o, --output `](/cli/identity#output-formats) flag.

 
`billing` and `usage` are **read-only** — manage the subscription, cards, and invoices in the
console. No Stripe customer ids or charge amounts are ever returned by the CLI.

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: alethia billing
records.text:
Shows the active organization's billing state as a detail card: its plan, subscription status, the
purchased seat count, the Stripe subscription id, and the trial / current-period boundaries. An
organization with no subscription reads as the free `community` plan.

alethia billing
alethia billing -o json

 Field Meaning 
 -------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------- 
 `Plan` `community`, `team`, or `enterprise` 
 `Status` Subscription status (`active`, `trialing`, `none`, …) 
 `Seats` Purchased seats (per-seat plans); a dash on flat/no subscription 
 `Subscription` Stripe subscription id, if any 
 `Trial ends` When a `trialing` subscription's trial ends 
 `Period ends` End of the current paid period

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: alethia usage
records.text:
Shows the active organization's current usage as a detail card: billable seats used vs the purchased
cap, managed-runner minutes consumed this period, the project count, and AI credits used in the
trailing week vs the plan's weekly grant.

alethia usage
alethia usage -o json

 Field Meaning 
 ---------------- ------------------------------------------------------------- 
 `Seats` Billable seats used **/** purchased cap 
 `Runner minutes` Managed-runner minutes consumed this billing period 
 `Projects` Projects in the organization 
 `AI credits` Included AI credits used this week **/** the weekly grant

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: alethia fleet
records.text:
The **managed fleet** is the platform's pool of warm provisioning runners — one pool per cloud — that
the Fleet Controller reconciles toward a declarative target (warm floor, ceiling, locations, version).

 
Fleet configuration is **platform-operator infrastructure** (the pools are global, not per-org). It
is only available on **self-managed deployments**, to organization **owners/admins**. On the hosted
service `fleet list` returns an empty list, and `fleet set` is refused.

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: fleet list
records.text:
Lists the configured warm pools. On a TTY this opens the interactive table; with `--output json csv`
(or `--no-input`) it prints a static table.

alethia fleet list
alethia fleet list -o json

Columns: `Provider`, `Enabled`, `Warm` (floor), `Max` (ceiling), `Slots` (concurrent jobs per
runner), `Locations`, and `Version` (a pinned version, or the tracked channel).

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: fleet set <provider>
records.text:
Updates the warm pool for a provider (`aws`, `gcp`, `azure`). Only the flags you pass are changed; the
rest keep their stored value. A pinned `--version` and a release `--channel` are **mutually exclusive**
— a version pin clears the channel.

 Flag Updates 
 ------------ --------------------------------------------------- 
 `--warm-min` Always-warm floor of runners 
 `--max` Hard ceiling on instances 
 `--slots` Concurrent jobs per runner 
 `--enabled` Enable (`true`) or pause (`false`) the pool 
 `--channel` Release channel to track (e.g. `stable`) 
 `--version` Pin an exact runner version (clears the channel) 

bash

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: Raise the AWS pool's warm floor and ceiling
records.text: alethia fleet set aws --warm-min 2 --max 20

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: Pin a runner version (clears any tracked channel)
records.text: alethia fleet set gcp --version v1.4.0

records.title: Billing, Usage & Fleet
records.heading: Pause a pool (drains its runners — prompts for confirmation)
records.text:
alethia fleet set azure --enabled=false

 
Disabling a pool drains its runners (a capacity reduction), so `fleet set --enabled=false` prompts
for confirmation on a TTY. Pass `--no-input` in scripts.

records.title: Classification Commands
records.text: Classification Commands. Read and edit the org's governed taxonomy — dimensions, values, and per-resource assignments — from the terminal.

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: Classification Commands
records.text:
The `classification` group (alias `class`) drives the same **governed taxonomy** as the console's
[Classification](/console/classification) page — from the terminal. Instead of free-form tags, an
admin defines a fixed set of **dimensions** (axes) and their **allowed values** once; every resource
is then labeled from that closed vocabulary. The CLI lets you list that taxonomy, read a resource's
labels, and set or clear them.

 
The CLI is a client of the same Console API — it authors nothing locally. Every subcommand calls
`/cli/classification/*` with your [CLI token](/cli/authentication) and operates on the **active
organization** (`alethia org switch`, or `--org ` per invocation).

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: The model
records.text:
Three concepts, in a strict hierarchy — the CLI vocabulary matches the console's exactly:

- **Dimension** — a named axis (one question you can ask about every resource: *Environment*,
 *Team*, *Data classification*). It has a `key` slug (immutable, wire-stable), a `label`, an
 optional description, a **single/multi** mode (`Multi bool`), and an `applies_to` list of resource
 kinds it targets (empty = all resource kinds).
- **Value** — an allowed answer on that axis (`dev` / `staging` / `prod`). Each has an immutable
 `value` slug and a `label`. `assign` takes the **value slug**, so slugs must be discoverable —
 which is why `dimensions` prints them, not just a count.
- **Assignment** — a value pinned to one concrete resource, addressed by its **kind** and **id**.

 
Classification is **organizational metadata** — it does not enforce anything today. No policy,
permission check, provisioning step, or the [verify](/concepts/glossary) gate reads these labels to
allow, deny, or route. See [Classification](/console/classification#what-it-is-not--yet).

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: Addressing a resource: <kind> <id>
records.text:
`show`, `assign`, and `unassign` all target a resource by its **kind** (a snake-case record type) and
its **id** (a UUID). For example a project's default environment is `project_environment `.
The classifiable kinds mirror the console — projects, environments, clusters, cloud identities
(connectors), runners, members, roles, alert policies, alert channels, support cases. Audit-trail
records (alert *deliveries*, runner *usage sessions*) are intentionally not classifiable.

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: alethia classification dimensions
records.text:
List the org's dimensions and, for each, its allowed value slugs.

alethia classification dimensions

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: alias:
records.text:
alethia class dimensions

Columns: **Key**, **Label**, **Mode** (`single` `multi`), **Applies to** (the resource kinds, or
`all resources`), **Values** (the value slugs, comma-separated). In an interactive TTY this renders
as a navigable [TUI table](/cli/tui); otherwise it prints a plain table. Add `-o json` for the raw
`ClassificationDimension` objects (including value `id`/`label` and per-dimension `description`).

KEY LABEL MODE APPLIES TO VALUES
environment Environment single project, project_environment dev, staging, prod
team Team multi all resources platform, payments, data
data-class Data classification single project_environment, cluster public, internal, restricted

Backing call: `GET /cli/classification/dimensions`.

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: alethia classification show
records.text:
Show the values currently assigned to one resource.

alethia classification show 

 Argument Required Description 
 ---------- ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Resource kind, e.g. `project_environment`, `cluster`, `cloud_identity`. 
 ` ` Yes The resource UUID. 

Columns: **Dimension**, **Value** (both rendered as their human labels). Prints `Not classified.`
when the resource carries no values. Backing call:
`GET /cli/classification/assignments?kind= &id= `.

alethia classification show project_environment 8f3c... -o json

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: alethia classification assign
records.text:
Pin a value to a resource.

alethia classification assign 

 Argument Required Description 
 ---------- ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Resource kind. 
 ` ` Yes Resource UUID. 
 ` ` Yes The dimension's `key` (from `dimensions`). 
 ` ` Yes The value's slug on that dimension (from `dimensions`). 

For a **single**-valued dimension, assigning a new value **replaces** the current one; for a
**multi**-valued dimension, values accumulate. On success:

✓ Assigned environment=prod to project_environment 8f3c...

Backing call: `POST /cli/classification/assignments` with
`{ kind, id, dimension_key, value_slug }`; it returns the resource's full updated assignment set.

bash

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: Label a project's default environment as prod, and tag two owning teams (multi dimension)
records.text:
alethia classification assign project_environment 8f3c... environment prod
alethia classification assign project_environment 8f3c... team platform
alethia classification assign project_environment 8f3c... team payments

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: alethia classification unassign
records.text:
Clear a value from a resource (addressed by its **slug**, not the dimension key).

alethia classification unassign 

 Argument Required Description 
 ---------- ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Resource kind. 
 ` ` Yes Resource UUID. 
 ` ` Yes The value slug to remove. 

✓ Cleared payments from project_environment 8f3c...

Backing call:
`DELETE /cli/classification/assignments?kind= &id= &value_slug= `.

records.title: Classification Commands
records.heading: See also
records.text:
- [Classification (Console)](/console/classification) — authoring dimensions/values, single vs multi,
 where the taxonomy applies, and usage insight.
- [Authentication](/cli/authentication) — how the CLI obtains the token these commands use.
- [Command Reference](/cli/commands) — the full command tree and global flags.

records.title: Cluster Commands
records.text: Cluster Commands. List provisioned Kubernetes clusters from the CLI.

records.title: Cluster Commands
records.heading: Cluster Commands
records.text:
The command is `alethia cluster` (singular) with a `clusters` alias, so both forms work
interchangeably.

records.title: Cluster Commands
records.heading: alethia cluster list
records.text:
List all provisioned Kubernetes clusters.

alethia cluster list

records.title: Cluster Commands
records.heading: or, equivalently:
records.text:
alethia clusters list

Output:

NAME PROVIDER REGION ENDPOINT ARGOCD
api-backend-production aws eu-west-1 https://ABC123.gr7.eu-west-1.eks.amazonaws.com https://argocd.example.com
data-pipeline-staging gcp us-east4 https://34.123.45.67 https://argocd-staging.example.com

Shows clusters from all active Projects with:
- Cluster name and cloud provider
- Region
- Kubernetes API endpoint
- ArgoCD dashboard URL (if installed)

records.title: Cluster Commands
records.heading: Accessing a Cluster
records.text:
After identifying a cluster, configure kubectl using the provider-specific command:

 
 
 aws eks update-kubeconfig --name api-backend-production --region eu-west-1
 kubectl get nodes
 
 
 gcloud container clusters get-credentials data-pipeline-staging --region us-east4
 kubectl get nodes
 
 
 az aks get-credentials --name my-cluster --resource-group my-rg
 kubectl get nodes

records.title: Connector Commands
records.text: Connector Commands. Connect AWS, GCP, and Azure cloud accounts to Alethia entirely from the terminal.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Connector Commands
records.text:
The `connector` command group connects your cloud accounts to Alethia so the platform can
provision infrastructure into them. It drives the **same connection flow as the web console's
[Connectors](/console/connectors) page**, but end-to-end from the CLI — there is no need to
open a browser for the default flows.

 
Each connection is a **[cloud identity](/concepts/glossary)** — the credential anchor a
[project](/concepts/glossary) links to (`cloud_identity_id`). "Connector" is the catalog of provider
*types*; the record `alethia connector` creates for your account is a cloud identity. A project
points at exactly one. Every cloud-identity query is filtered by provider, so accounts never leak
across providers.
 

Every connection uses **short-lived, keyless credentials** — AWS cross-account roles, GCP
Workload Identity Federation, and Azure federated identity. No access keys or client secrets are
ever created or stored. For the security model and how the [Runner](/runner) consumes these
credentials at provision time, see:

- [AWS Connector](/console/connectors/aws), [GCP Connector](/console/connectors/gcp), [Azure Connector](/console/connectors/azure) — per-provider concepts
- [Runne

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: How It Works
records.text:
Each `connector` subcommand runs the same three-stage pipeline, verified server-side:

 aws · gcp · azure · alibaba] --> B[Init pending cloud identity]
 B --> C[Run provider setup IAM OIDC role · WIF pool · federated cred · RAM OIDC]
 C --> D[Submit captured credentials]
 D --> E[Server-side verify auth + permission probe]
 E --> F{Verdict}
 F --> G[Connected]
 F --> H[Degraded]
 F --> I[Disconnected]
`} />

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Initialize
records.text: The CLI creates (or reuses) a pending cloud identity for the provider.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Run provider setup
records.text:
The CLI drives the provider's own tooling to create the resources Alethia needs (an IAM OIDC provider +
role, a workload identity pool, or an app registration) and captures the resulting credential
material.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Submit & verify
records.text:
The captured credentials are sent to Alethia, which verifies them **instantly, server-side** — an
auth + provisioning-permission probe (no runner). The identity is marked `Connected`, `Degraded`, or
`Disconnected` and is ready to provision into. Existing resources are discovered separately by the
server-side [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory).

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- You must be authenticated: run [`alethia login`](/cli/authentication) first.
- For the **default** flows, the matching provider CLI must be installed and authenticated
 locally:
 - GCP → `gcloud` (used to drive Google Cloud Shell)
 - AWS → `aws`
 - Azure → `az`
- The `--manual` flows require **none** of the above — you run a one-liner in the provider's
 browser shell and paste the result back.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Execution Models
records.text:
Provider Default Why 
 ---------- --------- ----- 
 GCP Runs the installer inside your **Google Cloud Shell** (via local `gcloud`) Cloud Shell is already authenticated to your GCP org and has every tool preinstalled 
 AWS Deploys CloudFormation with the **local `aws` CLI** AWS CloudShell is browser-only — there is no remote-exec CLI 
 Azure Runs the setup script with the **local `az` CLI** Azure Cloud Shell is browser-only — there is no remote-exec CLI 
 Alibaba Writes an IaC module you **apply yourself**, then paste the RAM role ARN Alibaba has no browser one-liner installer — the flow is apply-then-paste (no `--manual` variant) 

AWS, GCP, and Azure also support `--manual`, which prints a link + command to run in the browser
shell and prompts you to paste the result. Use it when you cannot or prefer not to install the local
CLI. Alibaba is always apply-then-paste (see [`alethia connector alibaba`](#alethia-connector-alibaba)).

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector gcp
records.text:
Connect a Google Cloud project using Workload Identity Federation.

alethia connector gcp --project my-gcp-project

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--project` No GCP project ID. Prompted interactively if omitted. 
 `--manual` No Run the installer in the browser Cloud Shell and paste the result instead of using local `gcloud`. 

By default, the CLI runs an installer **inside your Google Cloud Shell**. Cloud Shell is
authenticated as you, so the service account, workload identity pool, and AWS provider are created
directly in your project — and the CLI captures the generated WIF credential config automatically.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Enter the project
records.text: Provide `--project`, or type it at the prompt.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Cloud Shell setup
records.text:
The CLI invokes `gcloud cloud-shell ssh`, pushes the installer, and runs it. The first run can take
a minute while Cloud Shell provisions.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Connection test
records.text:
The captured WIF config is submitted, verified instantly server-side, and the identity is marked Connected (or Degraded/Disconnected).

 

Sample run:

◈ Project ── ◇ Cloud Shell setup ── ◇ Connection test

Running installer via gcloud Cloud Shell (the first run can take a minute)...
✔ Cloud Shell ready.
✔ Installer running in Cloud Shell.
...
--- START CONFIG (copy everything below until END CONFIG) ---
{ ...WIF credential config... }
--- END CONFIG ---

✓ Verifying connection (server-side)...
✓ Connected

✓ GCP project "my-gcp-project" connected

 
If `gcloud` is not logged in, Cloud Shell cannot authenticate non-interactively and you will see a
reauth error. Run `gcloud auth login`, then retry — or use `alethia connector gcp --manual`.

✗ gcloud is not authenticated
Run this, then retry:

 gcloud auth login

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Manual GCP setup
records.text:
With `--manual`, the CLI prints a Cloud Shell link and a command, then waits for you to paste the
WIF config:

alethia connector gcp --project my-gcp-project --manual

1. Open [Google Cloud Shell](https://shell.cloud.google.com/cloudshell/open?shellonly=true&show=terminal).
2. Run the printed command:
 curl -sO https://alethia-connector-assets.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/alethia-gcp-setup.sh \
 && bash alethia-gcp-setup.sh my-gcp-project
 3. Paste everything between `--- START CONFIG ---` and `--- END CONFIG ---` at the prompt.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector aws
records.text:
Connect an AWS account using a keyless, issuer-direct IAM role (an IAM OIDC provider trusting the Alethia
issuer — no access keys, no external id).

alethia connector aws

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--region` No AWS region for the CloudFormation stack. 
 `--role-name` No Name for the IAM role. Defaults to `AlethiaProvisionerRole`. 
 `--manual` No Deploy from the AWS console and paste the role ARN instead of using local `aws`. 

 
 

The CLI deploys the bundled CloudFormation template with your local `aws` credentials, then reads
the created role ARN from the stack outputs and submits it automatically.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Initialize
records.text: Alethia creates a pending cloud identity for the connection.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Create the IAM role
records.text:
The CLI runs `aws cloudformation deploy` (stack `AlethiaConnect`), creating an IAM OIDC provider for the
Alethia issuer + a role that trusts it via `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` (pinned to the fixed audience and
subject).

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Connection test
records.text:
The role ARN is submitted, the connection is verified instantly server-side, and the identity is marked Connected (or Degraded/Disconnected).

 

alethia connector aws --region eu-west-1

 
 

With `--manual`, the CLI prints a CloudFormation **quick-create** link pre-filled with the Alethia issuer
URL and prompts for the resulting role ARN:

alethia connector aws --manual

1. Open the printed quick-create link (it pre-fills the template and issuer URL).
2. Acknowledge the IAM capabilities checkbox and create the stack.
3. Copy the `RoleArn` from the stack **Outputs** tab and paste it at the prompt:
 ? Role ARN: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AlethiaProvisionerRole

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector azure
records.text:
Connect an Azure subscription using federated identity (no client secret).

alethia connector azure --subscription 

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--subscription` No Azure subscription ID. Prompted interactively if omitted.
