 `--manual` No Run setup in Azure Cloud Shell and paste the result instead of using local `az`. 

 
 

The CLI runs the setup script with your local `az` login. It creates an app registration, a
federated credential trusting the Alethia AWS runners, and a Contributor role assignment, then
captures the tenant, client, and subscription IDs automatically.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Enter the subscription
records.text: Provide `--subscription`, or type it at the prompt.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Create the app registration
records.text:
The CLI runs the setup script via `az`, creating the app registration, federated credential, and
role assignment.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Connection test
records.text:
The captured IDs are submitted, the connection is verified instantly server-side, and the identity is marked Connected (or Degraded/Disconnected).

 

 
 

With `--manual`, the CLI prints a command to run in Azure Cloud Shell and prompts for the three
IDs the script outputs:

alethia connector azure --subscription --manual

1. Open [Azure Cloud Shell](https://shell.azure.com).
2. Run the printed command:
 curl -sO https://alethia-connector-assets.s3.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/alethia-azure-setup.sh \
 && bash alethia-azure-setup.sh 
 3. Paste the **Tenant ID**, **Client ID**, and **Subscription ID** it prints at the prompts.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector alibaba
records.text:
Connect an Alibaba Cloud account using keyless `AssumeRoleWithOIDC`. Alibaba has no browser
one-liner installer, so the flow is **apply-then-paste**: the CLI writes a small IaC module locally,
you apply it in your Alibaba account, then paste back the resulting RAM role ARN.

alethia connector alibaba

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--region` No Alibaba region for the RAM provider. Defaults to `cn-hangzhou`. 
 `--dir` No Directory to write the connector module into. Defaults to `./alethia-alibaba-connector`.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Initialize
records.text: Alethia creates a pending [cloud identity](/concepts/glossary) for the connection.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Apply the connector module
records.text:
The CLI writes `main.tf` into the target directory. It creates a RAM OIDC provider trusting the
Alethia issuer (` /api/oidc`) plus a RAM role. Authenticate to Alibaba (the `aliyun`
CLI, or `ALICLOUD_ACCESS_KEY` / `ALICLOUD_SECRET_KEY`) and apply it:

cd alethia-alibaba-connector
tofu init
tofu apply -var "region=cn-hangzhou" -var "alethia_issuer_url=https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc"

 
The module is plain HCL — the CLI's printed hint says `terraform init` / `terraform apply`, but
`tofu` runs it identically. Copy the `role_arn` output when the apply finishes.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Connection test
records.text:
Paste the RAM role ARN (`acs:ram::…:role/…`) at the prompt. It is submitted, verified instantly
server-side, and the identity is marked Connected (or Degraded/Disconnected). Alethia stores nothing
but the role ARN — no AccessKey.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector list
records.text:
List all connected (verified) cloud accounts.

alethia connector list

Output:

PROVIDER ACCOUNT CONNECTED
AWS AWS (123456789012) 2 hours ago
GCP GCP (my-gcp-project) 5 days ago
AZURE Azure (3f9a1c2b-...) 5 days ago

The table is interactive — sort and navigate with the keyboard (see [TUI Components](/cli/tui)).

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: alethia connector remove
records.text:
Disconnect a cloud account.

alethia connector remove gcp

If you omit the provider, the CLI shows a picker of connected accounts.

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `-y`, `--yes` No Skip the confirmation prompt. 

 
Disconnecting resets the identity to a pending state and **orphans any projects** that referenced it
(their `cloud_identity_id` is cleared). It does not destroy already-provisioned cloud resources —
run [`alethia project destroy`](/cli/commands/plan-and-apply) first to tear those down. You can
reconnect the same provider later.

records.title: Connector Commands
records.heading: Troubleshooting
records.text:
Symptom Cause & fix 
 --------- ------------- 
 `gcloud is not authenticated` / reauth error Cloud Shell cannot prompt non-interactively. Run `gcloud auth login`, then retry — or use `--manual`. 
 `gcloud CLI not found on PATH` Install the [Google Cloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install), or use `--manual`. 
 `aws CLI not found on PATH` / `az CLI not found on PATH` Install the provider CLI, or use `--manual` to set the connection up from the browser. 
 `could not find WIF config in installer output` The Cloud Shell installer did not finish. Re-run, or use `--manual` and paste the config yourself. 
 `Captured WIF config is not valid JSON` The pasted block was truncated. Copy everything between `--- START CONFIG ---` and `--- END CONFIG ---`. 
 Connection test reaches `FAILED` Alethia could not assume the credentials. Confirm the role trust policy pins the Alethia issuer + sub/aud (AWS), the workload identity binding (GCP), or the federated credential + Contributor role (Azure). Inspect with `alethia jobs logs `. 
 `Invalid format. Expected: arn:aws:iam::…` The pasted role ARN is malformed — copy the exact `RoleArn` from the stack Outputs.

records.title: Command Reference
records.text: Command Reference. Complete reference for all alethia CLI commands organized by group.

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Command Reference
records.text:
alethia organizes its commands into groups (`connector`, `project`, `runner`, `jobs`, `cluster`,
`org`, `members`, `teams`, `channels`, `alerts`, `roles`, `grants`, `sso`, `fleet`,
`classification`, `ops`) plus top-level auth (`login`/`logout`/`init`), identity
(`whoami`/`config`/`open`), `billing`, `usage`, `activity`, and utility commands. All commands
communicate with the Alethia [Console](/concepts/glossary) API — they never run
[OpenTofu](/concepts/glossary) (the `tofu` binary) locally. Provisioning (`tofu plan`/`apply`/
`destroy`) happens on a [Runner](/runner); the CLI only queues and observes it.

Every command also accepts the [global flags](/cli/identity#output-formats) `-o, --output ` and `--no-input`.

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Command Tree
records.text:
alethia
├── init # first-run: pick control-plane URL, then log in
├── login [-f/--force] [--web-origin ]
├── logout
├── whoami # current user, active org, role, plan, default runner
├── config # show resolved config + file paths + web-origin source
│ ├── set # key: web-origin
│ ├── get [web-origin active-org]
│ └── clear-context # clear the active org
├── version
├── open [console docs] # aliases: docs, dashboard
├── completion [bash zsh fish powershell]
├── org # aliases: orgs
│ ├── list
│ └── switch [org]
├── members # alias: member
│ ├── list
│ ├── add [--role member]
│ └── remove 
├── teams # alias: team
│ ├── list
│ ├── create 
│ └── delete 
├── connector # manage cloud identities (credential anchors)
│ ├── gcp [--project ] [--manual]
│ ├── aws [--region ] [--role-name ] [--manual]
│ ├── azure [--subscription ] [--manual]
│ ├── alibaba [--region ] [--dir ]
│ ├── list
│ └── remove [provider] [-y/--yes]
├── project
│ ├── list
│ ├── get [--open]
│ ├── create --region [--cloud-identity-id ] [--stage ] [--iac-version ]
│ ├── env # manage a project's environments (--project )
│ │ ├── list
│ │ └── add [--stage ] [--region ]
│ ├── component # alias: components, comp (--project )
│ │ ├

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Interactive Selection
records.text:
When `--project-id` (or `--runner-id`) is omitted from `project plan`, `project apply`, or
`project destroy`, alethia launches an interactive TUI selector — provided stdin is a TTY and
`--no-input` is off. See [TUI Components](/cli/tui) for how this works.

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Connect a Cloud Account
records.text: bash

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Connect a GCP project (runs the installer in your Cloud Shell)
records.text: alethia connector gcp --project my-gcp-project

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: List connected accounts
records.text:
alethia connector list

See [Connector Commands](/cli/commands/connector) for AWS and Azure flows.

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Plan-Review-Apply
records.text: bash

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: 1. Plan
records.text: alethia project plan --project-id

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: 2. Stream logs to review
records.text: alethia jobs logs --follow

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: 3. Apply
records.text: alethia project apply --project-id --plan-job-id

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Scripting with --wait
records.text: bash

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Block until plan completes, then apply
records.text:
alethia project plan --project-id --wait
alethia project apply --project-id --plan-job-id --wait

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Log Tailing
records.text: bash

records.title: Command Reference
records.heading: Stream logs like tail -f
records.text: alethia jobs logs --follow

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.text: First-Run Setup (init). One command to point the CLI at a control plane and log in — the hosted default, or a self-hosted / dev URL.

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: First-Run Setup
records.text:
`alethia init` is the guided one-time setup: it picks the **control-plane URL**, persists it, then
runs the browser [device-code login](/cli/authentication). It's exactly the composition of
[`alethia config set web-origin`](/cli/commands) + [`alethia login`](/cli/authentication) in a single
prompt — nothing more.

alethia init

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Choose the control-plane URL
records.text:
A form asks for the **control-plane URL**, pre-filled with the current value — the hosted
`alethialabs.io` by default, or your self-hosted / dev origin. The entered value is validated
(must be `http`/`https` with a host) and saved with the trailing slash stripped.

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Log in
records.text:
The device-code flow runs immediately: the CLI opens ` /cli/login?device_code= ` in your
browser and polls ` /api/auth/cli/exchange` until you approve, then writes the tokens. See
[Authentication](/cli/authentication) for the full RFC 8628 flow.

 

 
**On the hosted platform you can skip `init` entirely** — the default origin already points at
`alethialabs.io`, so `alethia login` on its own is enough. Reach for `init` (or set `web-origin`
directly) when you run a **self-hosted** console or a **local dev** stack.

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: How the control-plane URL resolves
records.text:
The CLI resolves the origin in this precedence — highest first:

 B[persisted config web-origin]
 B --> C[hosted default alethialabs.io]
`} />

- **`ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN`** — an environment variable, wins for the current shell. Handy for CI or a
 throwaway override without touching the config file.
- **Persisted `web-origin`** — what `alethia init`, `alethia config set web-origin `, and
 `alethia login --web-origin ` write. Stored in the on-disk CLI config.
- **Hosted default** — the built-in `alethialabs.io` origin, used when neither of the above is set.

`alethia config` shows the resolved origin **and its source** (`env` / `config` / `default`), plus
the on-disk config and credentials paths:

alethia config
alethia config get web-origin # just the resolved origin

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Equivalent one-shots
records.text:
`init` is convenience glue. Any of these do the same job piecemeal:

bash

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Set the origin once, then log in
records.text:
alethia config set web-origin https://alethia.internal.example.com
alethia login

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Or set-and-log-in in a single command (persists the origin, then runs the device flow)
records.text: alethia login --web-origin https://alethia.internal.example.com

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Per-shell override without persisting (e.g. dev / CI)
records.text:
alethia login

 
`init`'s **URL prompt** honors `--no-input`: with prompting disabled it takes the already-resolved
origin without asking. The **login** step still needs a browser to approve the device code — for a
fully non-interactive machine, provision credentials another way (`ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` + a
pre-seeded credentials file). See [Configuration](/cli/configuration).

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: Where things are stored
records.text:
`init` writes to the standard per-user config directory (`os.UserConfigDir()/alethia/`): the
control-plane URL and active-org context to the CLI config file, and the login tokens to a separate
credentials file. `alethia config` prints both exact paths. See
[Configuration](/cli/configuration) for the file formats and
[Authentication](/cli/authentication) for token lifetimes and refresh.

records.title: First-Run Setup (init)
records.heading: See also
records.text:
- [Authentication](/cli/authentication) — the device-code flow, token refresh, `logout`.
- [Configuration](/cli/configuration) — config keys, env vars, file locations.
- [Command Reference](/cli/commands) — the full command tree.

records.title: Job Commands
records.text: Job Commands. List, stream logs, and cancel infrastructure jobs.

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: Job Commands
records.text:
Monitor and manage provision jobs from the terminal. For how jobs work internally, see [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

 
There is no `alethia jobs wait` command. To block until a job finishes, pass `-w/--wait` to the
command that queues it — `project plan apply destroy` and `runner deploy destroy`. See
[Plan & Apply](/cli/commands/plan-and-apply).

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: alethia jobs list
records.text:
List jobs for the active organization.

alethia jobs list [--status ] [-n/--limit ]

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--status` No Filter by status: `QUEUED`, `CLAIMED`, `PROCESSING`, `SUCCESS`, `FAILED`, `CANCELLED`. 
 `-n`, `--limit` No Jobs per page (default `20`). 

Output:

ID TYPE STATUS PROJECT CREATED
a1b2... PLAN SUCCESS api-backend 2 min ago
c3d4... DEPLOY PROCESSING api-backend 30 sec ago
e5f6... DESTROY QUEUED data-platform just now

The **TYPE** column is one of the full job-type set the Runner executes — not just the three above:

 Type What the Runner does 
 ------ ---------------------- 
 `PLAN` `tofu plan` + the [verify](/concepts/glossary) gate over the plan JSON — no mutation. 
 `DEPLOY` `tofu plan` → verify gate → `tofu apply` → ArgoCD scaffold. 
 `DESTROY` `tofu destroy` — tears the project's infrastructure down. 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` / `UPDATE_RUNNER` / `DESTROY_RUNNER` Provision, upgrade, or tear down a self-deployed Runner. 
 `ANALYZE_REPO` Inspect a connected git repository (repo-onboarding analysis). 
 `DETECT_DRIFT` `tofu plan -refresh-only` → a per-environment drift posture. 
 `AUDIT` Re-run the verify controls against current

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: alethia jobs get
records.text:
Get detailed information about a specific job. The job ID is a **positional argument**.

alethia jobs get 

Shows: type, status, assigned Runner, all timestamps, error message (if failed), and execution metadata (OpenTofu outputs, cost data, and the `verify_result` report on PLAN/DEPLOY jobs).

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: alethia jobs logs
records.text:
Stream job logs to the terminal. The job ID is a **positional argument**.

alethia jobs logs [-f/--follow]

 Argument / Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Job ID (positional) 
 `-f`, `--follow` No Continuously stream new log entries (like `tail -f`) 

Without `--follow`, prints all existing logs and exits. With `--follow`, the command blocks and prints new log lines as the Runner writes them. Logs include `tofu` (OpenTofu) plan/apply output, resource creation progress, and error messages.

Press `Ctrl+C` to stop following.

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: alethia jobs cancel
records.text:
Cancel a queued or in-progress job. The job ID is a **positional argument**.

alethia jobs cancel 

Sets the job status to CANCELLED. If the job is currently being executed by a Runner, the Runner detects the cancellation on its next status check and terminates the `tofu` process gracefully.

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: Waiting for jobs
records.text:
To block on a job from a script, queue it with `-w/--wait` rather than polling:

bash

records.title: Job Commands
records.heading: Plan and block until it finishes, then apply and block again
records.text:
alethia project plan --project-id $ID --wait
alethia project apply --project-id $ID --plan-job-id $PLAN_JOB --wait

See [Plan & Apply](/cli/commands/plan-and-apply) for the full workflow.

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.text: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity). Manage notification channels and alert rules, and read the delivery/activity log, from the alethia CLI.

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: Notifications
records.text:
Alethia's notification system has three parts, all manageable from the CLI:

- **Notification channels** are delivery destinations — webhook, email, Slack, PagerDuty, and more —
 that alert rules fan out to.
- **Alert rules** bind product events (event-key patterns) to one or more channels.
- The **activity log** is a durable record of every action, denial, and alert delivery.

All commands operate on the **active organization** (set via `alethia org switch`); override
per-invocation with `--org `. Every command accepts the global
[`-o, --output `](/cli/identity#output-formats) flag.

 
Alerts require a **Pro plan or higher**. Channel secrets (webhook/Slack URLs, PagerDuty routing
keys) are encrypted at rest and never returned by the API — the CLI only shows whether a secret is
configured.

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alethia channels
records.text: Alias: `channel`.

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: channels list
records.text:
Lists the active organization's notification channels. On a TTY this opens the interactive table;
with `--output json csv` (or `--no-input`) it prints a static table.

alethia channels list
alethia channels list -o json

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: channels create
records.text:
Creates a channel. The destination depends on `--type`; the endpoint is **verified before the
channel is saved** (a channel never exists unverified).

 Type Required destination flag(s) 
 ---------------------------- ----------------------------------- 
 `email` one or more `--recipient ` 
 `slack` / `webhook` / others `--url ` (+ `--signing-secret`) 
 `pagerduty` `--routing-key ` 

bash

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: Slack/webhook
records.text: alethia channels create "Ops Slack" --type slack --url https://hooks.slack.com/services/XXX

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: Email (repeat --recipient for multiple)
records.text: alethia channels create "On-call" --type email --recipient sre@example.com --recipient lead@example.com

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: PagerDuty
records.text: alethia channels create "PD" --type pagerduty --routing-key R0UT1NGKEY

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: channels verify <id>
records.text:
Sends a synthetic test event through the channel and marks it verified.

alethia channels verify 3f1c…

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: channels delete <id>

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alethia alerts
records.text:
Alias: `alert`. An alert rule binds one or more **event-key patterns** (e.g. `system.job.failed`,
`authz.*.denied`) to notification channels.

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alerts list
records.text:
Lists the active organization's alert rules with their bound channel count.

alethia alerts list
alethia alerts list -o json

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alerts create
records.text:
Creates a rule. Repeat `--event` and `--channel` for multiple values.

alethia alerts create "Job failures" \
 --event system.job.failed \
 --channel 3f1c… \
 --severity critical

 Flag Description 
 ------------ ---------------------------------------------------- 
 `--event` Event-key pattern (repeatable, required) 
 `--channel` Channel id to route to (repeatable, required) 
 `--severity` `info`, `warning` (default), or `critical`

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alerts delete <id>
records.text:
Deletes an alert rule (prompts for confirmation). Matching events will no longer be routed.

alethia alerts delete a1b2…

records.title: Notifications (Alerts, Channels & Activity)
records.heading: alethia activity
records.text:
Reads the active organization's delivery/activity log — every recorded action and denial, newest
first, including alert deliveries.

alethia activity # latest 50
alethia activity -n 200 # latest 200
alethia activity -o json

 Flag Description 
 --------------- -------------------------------------------- 
 `-n, --limit` Maximum number of entries to show (default 50)

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.text: Organizations, Members & Teams. Switch the active organization and manage its members and teams from the CLI.

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.text:
alethia scopes everything you do — projects, runners, jobs, connectors — to an **active
organization**. The active org is persisted locally (in `~/.config/alethia/config.json`) and sent as
the `X-Alethia-Org` header on every request.

The `members` and `teams` commands operate on the active org by default; pass `--org ` to target
a different one for a single invocation.

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: alethia org
records.text: Manage the organizations you belong to. Aliases: `orgs`, `organization`, `organizations`.

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: alethia org list
records.text:
List the organizations you belong to. The active org is marked in the `Active` column.

alethia org list

Output:

NAME SLUG ROLE PLAN ACTIVE
Acme acme owner pro ◆
Personal you owner free

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: alethia org switch
records.text:
Set the active organization. Pass an org **id, slug, or name**; with no argument, pick one
interactively.

bash

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: Explicit
records.text: alethia org switch acme

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: Interactive picker (TTY only)
records.text:
alethia org switch

The selection is persisted locally and sent as `X-Alethia-Org` on subsequent commands. Clear it with
[`alethia config clear-context`](/cli/configuration#alethia-config).

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: alethia members
records.text:
Manage members of the active organization. Alias: `member`.

 Command Description 
 --------- ------------- 
 `alethia members list` List members of the active org. 
 `alethia members add [--role member]` Invite a member by email. `--role` defaults to `member`. 
 `alethia members remove ` Remove a member by id. 

alethia members list
alethia members add teammate@example.com --role member
alethia members remove mbr_123

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: Target a specific org instead of the active one
records.text:
alethia members list --org org_456

 Flag Description 
 ------ ------------- 
 `--org ` Operate on this org instead of the active one. 
 `--role ` (`members add` only) Role for the invited member. Default `member`.

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: alethia teams
records.text:
Manage teams within the active organization. Alias: `team`.

 Command Description 
 --------- ------------- 
 `alethia teams list` List teams (name + member count). 
 `alethia teams create ` Create a team. 
 `alethia teams delete ` Delete a team by id. 

alethia teams list
alethia teams create platform
alethia teams delete team_123

records.title: Organizations, Members & Teams
records.heading: Target a specific org
records.text:
alethia teams list --org org_456

 Flag Description 
 ------ ------------- 
 `--org ` Operate on this org instead of the active one. 

 
All of these commands honor the global `-o, --output ` flag — e.g.
`alethia members list -o json jq`. See [Identity & Global Options](/cli/identity).

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.text: Plan & Apply. Queue plan, deploy, and destroy jobs from the CLI.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Plan & Apply
records.text:
These `project` subcommands queue infrastructure operations that [Runners](/runner) execute. They
implement the Plan-Review-Apply workflow from the terminal.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: alethia project plan
records.text:
Queue an [OpenTofu](/concepts/glossary) plan job — the Runner runs `tofu plan`.

alethia project plan [--project-id ] [--runner-id ] [--wait]

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--project-id` No Project UUID (interactive selector if omitted) 
 `--runner-id` No Specific Runner to execute (any available if omitted) 
 `--wait` No Block until the job completes 

On success, prints the job ID. Use `alethia jobs logs --follow` to stream the plan output.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: alethia project apply
records.text:
Queue a deployment (apply) job.

alethia project apply [--project-id ] [--runner-id ] [--plan-job-id ] [--wait]

 Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `--project-id` No Project UUID (interactive selector if omitted) 
 `--runner-id` No Specific Runner to execute 
 `--plan-job-id` No Reference a specific plan to apply 
 `--wait` No Block until the job completes 

The `--plan-job-id` flag ensures the apply uses the exact plan you reviewed. If the Project configuration changed since the plan was generated, the job fails with a [config hash mismatch](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply).

 
A DEPLOY job is `tofu plan` → **[verify](/concepts/glossary) gate** → `tofu apply` → ArgoCD scaffold.
The verify gate is **fail-closed**: a hard control failure blocks *before* `tofu apply` unless an
authorized override waives it. The report is attached to the job's `execution_metadata` and rendered
in the console Plan tab — inspect it with `alethia jobs logs --follow`.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: alethia project destroy
records.text:
Destroy a Project's provisioned infrastructure.

alethia project destroy [--project-id ] [--runner-id ] [--wait]

Queues a DESTROY job. The Runner runs `tofu destroy` to tear down all resources. OpenTofu state is preserved (it lives behind the Console state proxy, not on the Runner), so accidental destroys can be recovered by re-applying.

 
To tear down a deployed **Runner** (not a Project), use [`alethia runner destroy`](/cli/commands/runners).

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Interactive Selection
records.text:
When `--project-id` is omitted, alethia launches a TUI selector:

1. Select a Project from the list
2. The command proceeds with the selected Project

See [TUI Components](/cli/tui) for details on the interactive selectors.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Scripting Examples
records.text: bash

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Plan and wait, then apply
records.text:
JOB_ID=$(alethia project plan --project-id $PROJECT_ID --wait 2>&1 grep "Job ID" awk '{print $3}')
alethia project apply --project-id $PROJECT_ID --plan-job-id $JOB_ID --wait

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Destroy with wait
records.text: alethia project destroy --project-id $PROJECT_ID --wait

records.title: Project Commands
records.text: Project Commands. List and inspect infrastructure configurations from the CLI.

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: Project Commands
records.text:
Projects are infrastructure configurations (an *app* that owns one or more environments). The CLI
can both **read** projects (`list`, `get`) and **author** them — create a project, manage its
environments, and CRUD its component resources — so the whole project lifecycle is scriptable.

All authoring commands operate on the **active organization** (set via `alethia org switch`, or
`--org`). Every command accepts the global `-o, --output ` flag.

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: alethia project list
records.text:
List all Projects.

alethia project list

Output:

ID PROJECT PROVIDER REGION ENV STATUS
a1b2... api-backend aws eu-west-1 production ACTIVE
c3d4... data-pipeline gcp us-east4 staging DRAFT

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: alethia project get
records.text:
Get detailed information about a specific Project. The project is identified by a **positional
project name** (not a flag).

alethia project get api-backend

 Argument / Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Project name (positional) 
 `--open` No Open the project in the web console. (Long-only — `-o` is the global `--output` flag.) 

Shows the full Project configuration including:
- Project name, provider, region, environment
- Status and estimated monthly cost
- Component summary (network, cluster, databases, caches, etc.)
- Cloud identity reference

When run interactively without `--open`, the CLI offers to open the project in the browser after
printing it.

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: alethia project create
records.text:
Create a new project in the active organization. A default environment is created with it; add
component resources afterwards with `alethia project component add`.

alethia project create api-backend --region eu-west-1 --cloud-identity-id 

 Argument / Flag Required Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 ` ` Yes Project name (positional) 
 `--region` Yes* Cloud region to provision into (*prompted on a TTY when omitted) 
 `--cloud-identity-id` No Cloud account to link (interactive picker on a TTY when omitted) 
 `--stage` No Initial environment stage: `development` (default), `staging`, `production` 
 `--iac-version` No OpenTofu version to pin (defaults server-side) 

The created project prints as a card (or the typed object with `--output json`).

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: alethia project env
records.text:
A project owns N independently-provisionable environments. The project is named with `--project`
(its name **or** id).

bash

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: List a project's environments
records.text: alethia project env list --project api-backend

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: Add an environment
records.text:
alethia project env add staging --project api-backend --stage staging --region us-east-1

 Command Flags Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `env list` `--project ` List the project's environments (default first) 
 `env add ` `--project ` `--stage ` `--region ` Add an environment (name is slugified; region inherits the project's when omitted)

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: alethia project component
records.text:
One uniform group authors **all** of a project's component resources. The project is named with
`--project`.

Supported **kinds** — singletons (one per project, name-less): `network`, `cluster`, `dns`,
`observability`, `repositories`; and multi (named, many per project): `databases`, `caches`,
`queues`, `topics`, `nosql_tables`, `container_registries`, `secrets`, `storage_buckets`.

bash

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: List the supported kinds
records.text: alethia project component kinds

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: List a project's components (optionally filter by --kind)
records.text: alethia project component list --project api-backend --kind databases

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: Add a database (multi kind → --name required)
records.text:
alethia project component add --project api-backend --kind databases --name main \
 --set engine=postgres --set port=5432 --set iam_auth=true

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: Configure the cluster singleton (--name ignored; upserts)
records.text:
alethia project component add --project api-backend --kind cluster \
 --set cluster_version=1.31 --set node_min_size=2 --set 'instance_types=["t3.medium"]'

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: Remove components (destructive → confirms)
records.text:
alethia project component remove --project api-backend --kind databases --name main
alethia project component remove --project api-backend --kind network

 Command Flags Description 
 ------ ---------- ------------- 
 `component kinds` — List the supported component kinds + their cardinality 
 `component list` `--project ` `--kind ` `--env ` List components (all kinds, or one) 
 `component add` `--project ` `--kind ` `--name ` `--set key=value`… Create a component 
 `component remove` `--project ` `--kind ` `--name ` Delete a component (confirms)

records.title: Project Commands
records.heading: The --set field setter
records.text:
`--set key=value` is repeatable and generic. Each value is parsed as JSON when it can be, else taken
literally — so numbers, booleans, and arrays are typed correctly:

--set port=5432 # number
--set iam_auth=true # boolean
--set instance_types='["t3.medium"]' # array
--set engine=postgres # string

Fields are **validated server-side** against the component's schema: an unknown or mistyped field
fails with a clear `400`, naming the offending field and the allowed set. Singleton kinds ignore
`--name` (they upsert the project's single row); multi kinds require it.

records.title: Runner Commands
records.text: Runner Commands. Deploy, list, and tear down runner agents from the CLI.

records.title: Runner Commands
records.heading: Runner Commands
records.text: Runners execute infrastructure jobs. Manage them with `alethia runner`.

records.title: Runner Commands
records.heading: alethia runner deploy
records.text:
Deploy a new runner to a cloud account. Creates a runner record and queues a
`DEPLOY_RUNNER` job using the latest stable release.

alethia runner deploy --cloud-identity-id --name --region 

 Flag Description 
 ------ ------------- 
 `--cloud-identity-id` Cloud identity to deploy into 
 `--name` Runner name 
 `--region` Cloud region 
 `--assigned-runner-id` Which runner executes the deployment 
 `--wait`, `-w` Wait for job completion

records.title: Runner Commands
records.heading: alethia runner list
records.text:
List all runners (status, operator, version, last heartbeat). The **Operator** column shows `managed` or, for self runners, `self · deployed` / `self · registered`.

alethia runner list

records.title: Runner Commands
records.heading: alethia runner destroy
records.text:
Tear down a runner's cloud infrastructure. Queues a `DESTROY_RUNNER` job; another
runner executes the teardown.

alethia runner destroy --runner-id 

 Flag Description 
 ------ ------------- 
 `--runner-id` ID of the runner to destroy 
 `--assigned-runner-id` Which runner executes the teardown 
 `--wait`, `-w` Wait for job completion

records.title: Runner Commands
records.heading: alethia runner remove
records.text:
Remove a runner's database record only (no cloud teardown). Run
`alethia runner destroy` first to tear down cloud resources.

alethia runner remove

records.title: Configuration
records.text: Configuration. CLI configuration files, environment variables, and credential storage.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Configuration
records.text:
alethia stores configuration and credentials in the user's config directory (`~/.config/alethia`
on Linux/macOS — exact location follows the OS user-config dir). Inspect the resolved values and
on-disk paths at any time with [`alethia config`](#alethia-config).

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: File Locations
records.text:
File Purpose 
 ------ --------- 
 `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json` CLI authentication tokens (access + refresh JWT) 
 `~/.config/alethia/config.json` Active organization context (id, name, slug)

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: credentials.json
records.text:
Created by `alethia login`. Contains JWT tokens for API authentication:

{
 "access_token": "eyJ...",
 "refresh_token": "eyJ...",
 "user_email": "you@example.com"
}

The access token is refreshed automatically before each API call if expired. See [Authentication](/cli/authentication).

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: config.json
records.text:
Created/updated by `alethia org switch`. Records the active organization, which is sent as the
`X-Alethia-Org` header on every request:

{
 "active_org_id": "org_123",
 "active_org_name": "Acme",
 "active_org_slug": "acme"
}

Clear it with `alethia config clear-context`.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: alethia config
records.text:
Show the resolved CLI configuration: the control-plane URL (`ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN`), the active
organization context, and the on-disk paths of the credentials and config files.

alethia config

Field Value
web origin https://app.alethialabs.io
active org Acme
active org id org_123
credentials /Users/you/.config/alethia/credentials.json
config /Users/you/.config/alethia/config.json

`alethia config clear-context` clears the active organization context. `alethia config -o json`
emits the same values as a JSON object.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Runner Credentials
records.text:
The `alethia` CLI does not store Runner credentials. A self-operated Runner is a
separate binary configured entirely through environment variables — register the
Runner in the console (or via the API) to obtain a `runner_id` / `runner_token`,
then set `ALETHIA_RUNNER_ID` and `ALETHIA_RUNNER_TOKEN` on the host. See
[Self-Operated Runners](/runner/self-hosted) for the full env contract.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Environment Variables
records.text:
Variable Default Purpose 
 ---------- --------- --------- 
 `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` Production URL (baked at build time) Override the Alethia API base URL 

This is the only environment variable alethia uses. All other configuration comes from the JSON files above or command-line flags.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Global Flags
records.text:
Two persistent flags are available on every command:

 Flag Default Description 
 ------ --------- ------------- 
 `-o, --output ` `table` Output format. 
 `--no-input` off Disable interactive prompts (fail instead of prompting). Auto-enabled when stdin is not a TTY. 

See [Identity & Global Options](/cli/identity) for output-format examples and scripting tips.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Build-Time Configuration
records.text:
The GoReleaser build injects `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` as an `ldflags` variable. The production build points to the production Alethia URL. For development, override with the environment variable:

alethia login # authenticates against local server

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.text: Homebrew Core (bare install). How and when to get the literal `brew install alethia` with zero tap — via Homebrew core.

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: Homebrew Core
records.text:
Today the CLI installs from our own tap:

brew install alethialabs-io/tap/alethia

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: or: brew tap alethialabs-io/tap && brew install alethia
records.text:
The **literal** bare `brew install alethia` (no tap at all) is only possible once the formula
lives in [**homebrew-core**](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core), Homebrew's default tap.
This page is the runbook for getting there.

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: The gate: notability, not engineering
records.text:
homebrew-core will **reject** a formula whose upstream isn't "notable." The rule of thumb the
maintainers apply is roughly **>75 stars, or >30 forks, or >30 watchers**, plus: a stable
tagged release, active maintenance, an [OSI license](https://opensource.org/licenses) (AGPL-3.0
qualifies), and no duplication of an existing formula. `alethia` is free in core, but at a
handful of stars a submission will be closed on notability grounds.

**So this is an adoption milestone, not a code task.** Keep the tap as the primary channel; submit
to core once the repo clears the notability bar.

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: Recommended: submit from a dedicated CLI repo
records.text:
homebrew-core maintainers dislike formulae that download a **large monorepo tarball** and build a
subdirectory. The clean vehicle is the dedicated public repo
[`alethialabs-io/alethia`](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethia) — it already exists as a
read-only mirror of the CLI with `vX.Y.Z` tags (see the "read-only mirror" phase in the repo
strategy). Today it mirrors the `apps/cli/` subdirectory verbatim; the one remaining prep before a
core submission is to **flatten it to a root-level Go module** (so the formula does a plain
root-level `go build` on a small tarball). A focused star count on that repo also helps clear the
notability bar.

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: The formula (build-from-source)
records.text:
homebrew-core builds Go formulae from source. A submit-ready `alethia.rb` (monorepo variant —
drop the `cd` for a dedicated repo):

class Alethia < Formula
 desc "CLI for the Alethia multi-cloud Kubernetes control plane"
 homepage "https://alethialabs.io"
 url "https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/archive/refs/tags/cli-v0.3.0.tar.gz"
 version "0.3.0"
 sha256 " "
 license "AGPL-3.0-only"
 head "https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs.git", branch: "main"

 depends_on "go" => :build

 def install
 ldflags = "-s -w -X github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/apps/cli/internal/version.Version=#{version}"
 cd "apps/cli" do
 system "go", "build", *std_go_args(ldflags:)
 end
 end

 test do
 assert_match version.to_s, shell_output("#{bin}/alethia --version")
 end
end

Notes:
- The `version` string is set explicitly because the `cli-v*` tag prefix isn't semver — Homebrew
 can't infer it from the URL.
- `std_go_args` installs the binary as `alethia` into `bin`; the `ldflags` bake the version, matching
 `apps/cli/.goreleaser.yml`.
- The from-source build resolves `packages/core` through the `replace` directive in
 `apps/cli/go.mod` (verified with `cd apps/cli && GOWORK=off go buil

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: Submission steps
records.text:
1. Confirm the notability bar is met and there's a stable `cli-v*` (or dedicated-repo `v*`) release.
2. `brew create --go --set-name alethia ` and paste in the formula above.
3. Validate locally:
 brew install --build-from-source alethia
 brew test alethia
 brew audit --new --strict --online alethia
 4. Open a PR to [Homebrew/homebrew-core](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core) adding
 `Formula/a/alethia.rb`. BrewTestBot builds it on macOS + Linux; maintainers review.
5. After merge, `brew install alethia` works with zero tap.

records.title: Homebrew Core (bare install)
records.heading: Ongoing version bumps
records.text:
Once in core, bump with a one-liner (or Homebrew's autobump, if the repo is enabled for it):

brew bump-formula-pr --version=X.Y.Z alethia

GoReleaser continues to own the **tap** formula (`alethialabs-io/homebrew-tap`); the core formula
is maintained via these PRs. Keeping both is fine — core for discoverability, the tap for the latest.

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.text: Identity & Global Options. whoami, config, output formats, the --no-input flag, and utility commands (version, open, completion).

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: Identity & Global Options
records.text:
This page covers the top-level commands that tell you **who you are** and **how the CLI is
configured**, plus the global flags and utility commands available everywhere.

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: alethia whoami
records.text:
Show who you are signed in as: the authenticated user, the active organization, your role in it, the
org's plan, and its default runner.

alethia whoami

Field Value
User you@example.com
Name Ada Lovelace
Organization Acme
Slug acme
Role owner
Plan pro
Default runner acme-eu-west-1

Use `--output json` for scripting:

alethia whoami -o json jq '.active_org.slug'

If no organization is active, the `Organization` row shows a dash — set one with
[`alethia org switch`](/cli/commands/organizations#alethia-org-switch).

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: alethia config
records.text:
Show the resolved CLI configuration: the control-plane URL (`ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN`), the active
organization context, and the on-disk paths of the credentials and config files.

alethia config

`alethia config clear-context` clears the active organization. See
[Configuration](/cli/configuration#alethia-config) for the full output and file layout.

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: Output Formats
records.text:
Every command accepts a global output flag:

-o, --output (default: table)

 Format Behavior 
 -------- ---------- 
 `table` Human-readable. On a TTY, list/get commands render an **interactive** table (sort, navigate, quit). 
 `json` Static, machine-readable JSON — ideal for piping into `jq`. 
 `csv` Static comma-separated rows — ideal for spreadsheets and `cut`/`awk`. 

bash

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: Pipe JSON into jq
records.text: alethia project list -o json jq '.[].name'

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: Export a CSV of jobs
records.text:
alethia jobs list -o csv > jobs.csv

When output is **not** `table` (or stdout is piped / `--no-input` is set), list/get commands print
static output instead of the interactive table — so they're safe to use in scripts and CI.

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: The --no-input Flag
records.text:
--no-input Disable interactive prompts (fail instead of prompting).

Interactive selectors and confirmation prompts are disabled with `--no-input`. It is **auto-enabled
when stdin is not a TTY** (a pipe, CI, etc.), so the CLI never hangs waiting for input that can't
come. With prompting disabled, supply required values explicitly (`--project-id`, `--runner-id`, an
org argument, …) or the command fails with a clear error. See [TUI Components](/cli/tui).

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: alethia version
records.text:
Print the CLI version. If a newer release is known (from the daily background check), it also prints
the latest available version.

alethia version

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: alethia open
records.text:
Open the Alethia web console (default) or the docs in your browser. Aliases: `docs`, `dashboard`.

alethia open # opens the console
alethia open docs # opens the documentation
alethia open console # explicit

`alethia docs` (the alias) defaults to the docs target.

records.title: Identity & Global Options
records.heading: alethia completion
records.text:
Generate a shell completion script (standard Cobra completion).

alethia completion bash
alethia completion zsh
alethia completion fish
alethia completion powershell

Follow the script's own header comments to install it for your shell — for example, source it from
your shell profile or drop it into your completions directory.

records.title: alethia CLI
records.text: alethia CLI. Go command-line tool for managing multi-cloud infrastructure via the Alethia platform.

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: alethia CLI
records.text: alethia is the command-line interface for the platform. It provides terminal-based access to all infrastructure operations — planning and deploying Projects, monitoring jobs, and managing Runners.

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Overview
records.text:
Property Value 
 ---------- ------- 
 Language Go 
 Framework [Cobra](https://github.com/spf13/cobra) (commands) + [Charmbracelet](https://charm.sh/) (TUI) 
 Version Managed by release-please (`alethia-v*` tags) 
 Distribution Homebrew · Scoop · install script (get.alethialabs.io) · .deb/.rpm/.apk · Docker · GitHub Releases (macOS/Linux/Windows, amd64/arm64)

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: How It Works
records.text:
alethia is a **thin client** that communicates with the Alethia API over HTTPS. It does not run OpenTofu directly — instead, it queues jobs that [Runners](/runner) execute. The CLI handles:

1. **Authentication** — [Device code flow](/concepts/authentication) with tokens stored locally
2. **Configuration** — Creating and inspecting Projects
3. **Job submission** — Queuing Plan / Deploy / Destroy jobs
4. **Monitoring** — Streaming logs, checking job status, waiting for completion
5. **Management** — Listing clusters, managing Runners

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Command Groups
records.text:
Connect AWS, GCP, and Azure cloud accounts.
 
 
 List and inspect infrastructure configurations.
 
 
 Queue plan, deploy, and destroy jobs.
 
 
 List, stream logs, and cancel jobs.
 
 
 Register, list, and remove Runners.
 
 
 List provisioned Kubernetes clusters.
 
 
 Switch the active org and manage members and teams.
 
 
 Alerts and channels (console-managed today).
 
 
 `whoami`, `config`, output formats, and utility commands.

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Global Flags
records.text:
Every command accepts two persistent flags:

 Flag Default Description 
 ------ --------- ------------- 
 `-o, --output ` `table` Output format. `json`/`csv` print static, machine-readable output. 
 `--no-input` off Disable interactive prompts; fail instead of prompting. Auto-enabled when stdin is not a TTY. 

List/get commands render an **interactive table** only on a TTY with the default `table` format.
With `json`, `csv`, a pipe, or `--no-input`, they print static output instead — ideal for scripting:

alethia project list -o json jq '.[].name'

See [Identity & Global Options](/cli/identity) for details.

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Quick Start
records.text: bash

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Install
records.text: brew install alethialabs-io/tap/alethia

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Point at your control plane and authenticate
records.text: alethia login

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Confirm who you are and pick an active org
records.text:
alethia whoami
alethia org switch

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Connect a cloud account (AWS, GCP, or Azure)
records.text: alethia connector gcp --project my-gcp-project

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Plan infrastructure (Projects are created in the console)
records.text: alethia project plan --project-id

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Stream logs
records.text: alethia jobs logs --follow

records.title: alethia CLI
records.heading: Deploy
records.text: alethia project apply --project-id --plan-job-id

records.title: Installation
records.text: Installation. Install the Alethia CLI via Homebrew, Scoop, an install script, a Linux package, Docker, a binary download, or from source.

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Installation
records.text:
The `alethia` CLI ships for **macOS, Linux, and Windows** (amd64 + arm64). Pick whichever
method fits your platform — every one below installs the same binary and self-updates against
GitHub Releases.

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Homebrew (recommended)
records.text:
One line, no prior setup:

brew install alethialabs-io/tap/alethia

Or tap once, then install (and reinstall) by the short name:

brew tap alethialabs-io/tap
brew install alethia

Upgrade with `brew upgrade alethia`. (The literal tap-free `brew install alethia` requires
[Homebrew core](/cli/homebrew-core) — a future adoption milestone.)

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Install script
records.text:
No Homebrew? Pipe the installer — it detects your OS/arch, verifies the SHA-256 checksum, and
drops the binary in `~/.local/bin`:

curl -fsSL https://get.alethialabs.io sh

Pin a version or change the install dir with env vars:

curl -fsSL https://get.alethialabs.io ALETHIA_VERSION=0.3.0 sh
curl -fsSL https://get.alethialabs.io ALETHIA_INSTALL_DIR=/usr/local/bin sh

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Linux packages (.deb / .rpm / .apk)
records.text:
Every release attaches Debian, RPM, and Alpine packages. Download the one for your arch from the
[latest release](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/releases) and install it:

bash

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Debian / Ubuntu
records.text: sudo dpkg -i alethia_*_linux_amd64.deb

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Fedora / RHEL
records.text: sudo rpm -i alethia_*_linux_amd64.rpm

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Alpine
records.text: sudo apk add --allow-untrusted alethia_*_linux_amd64.apk

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Scoop (recommended)
records.text:
scoop bucket add alethia https://github.com/alethialabs-io/scoop-bucket
scoop install alethia

Upgrade with `scoop update alethia`.

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Install script
records.text:
irm https://get.alethialabs.io/install.ps1 iex

Honors `$env:ALETHIA_VERSION` and `$env:ALETHIA_INSTALL_DIR`; installs to
`%LOCALAPPDATA%\Alethia\bin` and adds it to your user `PATH`.

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Docker
records.text:
docker run --rm ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/alethia --version

Images are published to GHCR for every release (`:latest` and `:X.Y.Z`, amd64 + arm64).

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Binary download
records.text:
Pre-built archives are on [GitHub Releases](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/releases)
for every tagged version:

 Platform Architecture Archive 
 ---------- ------------- --------- 
 macOS Intel (amd64) `alethia_Darwin_x86_64.tar.gz` 
 macOS Apple Silicon (arm64) `alethia_Darwin_arm64.tar.gz` 
 Linux amd64 `alethia_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz` 
 Linux arm64 `alethia_Linux_arm64.tar.gz` 
 Windows amd64 `alethia_Windows_x86_64.zip` 
 Windows arm64 `alethia_Windows_arm64.zip` 

tar xzf alethia_Darwin_arm64.tar.gz
mv alethia /usr/local/bin/
alethia --version

Each release also ships `checksums.txt` — verify with `sha256sum -c` (Linux) or
`shasum -a 256 -c` (macOS) before installing.

records.title: Installation
records.heading: From source
records.text:
Requires Go 1.25+:

cd apps/cli
go build -o alethia .
./alethia --version

records.title: Installation
records.heading: Build system
records.text:
Alethia releases are built by [GoReleaser](https://goreleaser.com/) via GitHub Actions on each
`cli-v*` tag. See [Release System](/concepts/release-system) for the full pipeline.

The GoReleaser config (`apps/cli/.goreleaser.yml`) produces:
- binaries for macOS / Linux / Windows × amd64 / arm64 (CGO disabled), archived with checksums
- Linux packages (`.deb` / `.rpm` / `.apk`) and Docker images (GHCR)
- the Homebrew formula (`alethialabs-io/tap`) and Scoop manifest (`alethialabs-io/scoop-bucket`), updated automatically

The `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` build variable is baked into the binary, pointing to the production Alethia URL.

records.title: TUI Components
records.text: TUI Components. Terminal UI components — interactive selectors, progress steppers, and Bubble Tea spinners.

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: TUI Components
records.text:
alethia uses the [Charmbracelet](https://charm.sh/) suite for rich terminal UI. Three libraries are used:

 Library Purpose 
 --------- --------- 
 [**huh**](https://github.com/charmbracelet/huh) Interactive forms and selection prompts 
 [**bubbletea**](https://github.com/charmbracelet/bubbletea) Full TUI framework (used for the login spinner) 
 [**lipgloss**](https://github.com/charmbracelet/lipgloss) Terminal styling and layout

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: Interactive Selectors
records.text:
The interactive selectors are **not a standalone command** — they appear automatically whenever a
command needs a resource you didn't specify on the command line and prompting is allowed (a TTY with
`--no-input` off). For example, omitting `--project-id` from `project plan`, `project apply`, or
`project destroy` opens a Project picker; omitting `--runner-id` opens a Runner picker; and
`org switch` / `connector remove` with no argument open their own pickers.

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: Project Selector
records.text:
? Select a Project:
 > production / eu-west-1 (ACTIVE)
 staging / eu-west-1 (DRAFT)
 development / us-east-1 (PROVISIONING)

Each Project shows its environment stage, region, and current status.

 
With `--no-input` (or when stdin is not a TTY — e.g. in CI or a pipe), the selectors are disabled.
Pass the required IDs explicitly (`--project-id`, `--runner-id`, an org argument, …) or the command
fails with a clear "interactive input required" error instead of hanging.

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: Progress Stepper
records.text:
The visual progress stepper shows deployment phase progress using diamond icons:

◆ Authentication ← completed
◆ Configuration ← completed
◈ OpenTofu Plan ← current (running)
◇ Cost Analysis ← pending
◇ Reporting ← pending

- `◆` (filled diamond) — completed step
- `◈` (diamond with dot) — currently executing
- `◇` (empty diamond) — pending

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: Login Spinner
records.text:
The `alethia login` command uses a Bubble Tea model for the authentication flow:

🔐 Waiting for browser authentication...

 Visit: https://alethia.example.com/cli/login?device_code=abc123
 Enter the verification code shown in your browser:

 > _

 ⠋ Waiting for approval...

The spinner animates while polling the exchange endpoint every 2 seconds.

records.title: TUI Components
records.heading: Config Printer
records.text:
Job details and Project configurations are displayed with formatted JSON output using lipgloss styling:

- Monospace font for values
- Color-coded keys
- Indented structure for nested objects
- Truncated long values with ellipsis

records.title: How Alethia compares
records.text: How Alethia compares. Where Alethia sits between IaC orchestrators and BYOC app-PaaSes — and the honest tradeoffs versus Porter, Qovery, and Spacelift.

records.title: How Alethia compares
records.heading: How Alethia compares
records.text:
Most infrastructure tools fall into two camps, and Alethia deliberately sits in the gap
between them:

- **IaC orchestrators** (Terraform Cloud, Spacelift) run the IaC *you* write. Powerful, but
 they hand you plumbing — you still author and operate everything.
- **BYOC app-PaaSes** (Porter, Qovery, Northflank) give you a product, but the control plane
 is *their* hosted SaaS, the deploy pipeline is proprietary, and pricing meters your compute.

**Alethia is a self-hostable control plane that designs and provisions real infrastructure
with zero stored credentials, and hands you standard EKS + ArgoCD + OpenTofu you own.**

 Alethia IaC orchestrators BYOC app-PaaS 
 --- --- --- --- 
 Self-host the control plane **Yes** (~4 containers, AGPL) Top tier only No 
 Stores your cloud credentials **No** (role assumed at run time) N/A (you run IaC) Usually yes / privileged 
 Runs your bring-your-own code **Per-job [sealed sandbox](/isolated-execution)** (no shared secrets, no cloud metadata, allowlisted egress) Shared workers / your own runners Their hosted pipeline 
 App delivery **Real ArgoCD, GitOps, portable** CI apply on push Proprietary pipeline 
 What you own when you leave Cluster + O

records.title: Alethia vs Porter
records.text: Alethia vs Porter. Porter is a BYOC app-PaaS with a hosted control plane and a proprietary deploy pipeline. Alethia is self-hostable, GitOps-native, and open source — same ownership of your cloud, opposite ownership of the platform.

records.title: Alethia vs Porter
records.heading: Alethia vs Porter
records.text:
**Porter** ("Heroku in your own cloud") points at a GitHub repo and builds, deploys, and
autoscales your app on a managed cluster in your cloud account. It's a mature, YC-backed
BYOC PaaS. The key difference is **who owns the platform layer**.

 Dimension Alethia Porter 
 --- --- --- 
 Own / self-host the **control plane** **Yes** — ~4 containers (Postgres + S3 + app + runner) No — control plane is Porter's hosted SaaS 
 Zero stored cloud credentials **Yes** — runner assumes roles at run time Yes for AWS/GCP (AssumeRole / WIF); Azure SP is the exception 
 App delivery **Real ArgoCD wired to your repo** (GitOps, portable) Proprietary build+deploy pipeline (`porter.yaml`) 
 Lock-in if you leave Cluster + OpenTofu + ArgoCD keep working Cluster keeps working; the deploy pipeline + console don't 
 Open source **AGPL core** + small commercial `ee/` Closed product (launched MIT, monorepo since pulled) 
 Pricing Free self-host; commercial for orgs/SSO Usage-based per vCPU/GB-RAM on top of your cloud bill; no free tier 
 Multi-cloud EKS today; GKE/AKS in progress AWS/GCP/Azure all GA 
 Day-2 ops (logs/metrics/rollbacks) Thinner today Mature, SRE-monitored

records.title: Alethia vs Porter
records.heading: Where Alethia wins
records.text:
- **You can own the entire control plane** — self-host with no SaaS dependency. Porter's
 control plane is irreducibly their hosted service (self-host was promised in 2021, never shipped).
- **Standard, portable GitOps** — real ArgoCD wired to your Git; if Alethia disappears, apps
 still deploy from a `git push`. Porter's pipeline is proprietary.
- **Open source + no compute meter** — no per-vCPU/GB markup on top of your cloud bill.

records.title: Alethia vs Porter
records.heading: Where Porter wins (honest)
records.text:
- **Maturity of day-2 ops** — logs, metrics, autoscaling, preview environments, rollbacks,
 alerting, all SRE-monitored.
- **Multi-cloud is GA today** across AWS/GCP/Azure; Alethia leads with AWS.
- **Polished app-deploy UX** for the "push a repo, get a running app" path.

 
Bottom line: pick Porter if you want a managed, batteries-included app-deploy experience and
don't mind a hosted control plane + usage pricing. Pick Alethia if you must **own the control
plane**, keep **standard portable GitOps**, and avoid a compute meter.

records.title: Alethia vs Qovery
records.text: Alethia vs Qovery. Qovery is the closest structural analog — a BYOC Kubernetes control plane. The difference is a hosted, privileged orchestrator vs a self-hostable, credential-free one.

records.title: Alethia vs Qovery
records.heading: Alethia vs Qovery
records.text:
**Qovery** is a BYOC Kubernetes control plane / app-PaaS — connect a repo + your cloud, and it
provisions managed K8s in your account and deploys into it (recently repositioned around
"Kubernetes for humans **and AI agents**"). It's the closest structural analog to Alethia; the
divergence is **ownership and the credential model**.

 Dimension Alethia Qovery 
 --- --- --- 
 Control plane **Self-hostable** (~4 containers, AGPL) Qovery-hosted SaaS by default; self-host only on Enterprise 
 Credential model **Runner assumes roles at run time; control plane holds nothing** Hosted control plane is a privileged orchestrator into your account (even with STS) 
 App delivery **Real ArgoCD wired to your repo** (portable) Qovery's engine *is* the delivery layer (remove it → rebuild your pipeline) 
 Provisioned stack Cluster **+ Aurora/ElastiCache/DynamoDB/SQS-SNS/ECR/S3/Secrets/Route53/WAF** in one Project App-platform-centric; most managed data services left to you 
 Pricing Free self-host; commercial for orgs/SSO Per-org + seats + deploy-minutes; self-host gated to Enterprise 
 Open source **AGPL core** Proprietary

records.title: Alethia vs Qovery
records.heading: Where Alethia wins
records.text:
- **Truly credential-free** — the control plane never holds privileged access; Qovery's hosted
 brain is always a privileged orchestrator into your account.
- **Self-host on day one, for free** — AGPL, ~4 containers. Qovery gates self-host behind Enterprise.
- **Portable app-delivery** — real ArgoCD; delete Alethia and your cluster + GitOps keep running.
- **Broader stack in one Project** + no per-seat / per-deploy-minute meter.

records.title: Alethia vs Qovery
records.heading: Where Qovery wins (honest)
records.text:
- **Multi-cloud breadth GA** (EKS/GKE/AKS/Scaleway + bring-your-own clusters) today.
- **Mature platform** — well-funded, established, with a polished console and the new
 AI-agent guardrails/policy story.
- **Managed app-platform UX** is further along than Alethia's V1 dashboard.

 
Bottom line: Qovery is the more mature managed control plane. Choose Alethia when "the control
plane must be ours and hold no credentials" is a hard requirement.

records.title: Alethia vs Spacelift
records.text: Alethia vs Spacelift. Spacelift orchestrates the IaC you write. Alethia delivers the outcome — a complete cluster + ArgoCD + data services — and still hands you OpenTofu you own.

records.title: Alethia vs Spacelift
records.heading: Alethia vs Spacelift
records.text:
**Spacelift** is an IaC orchestration platform (a "TACOS" — Terraform Automation and
Collaboration Software): point it at a repo of Terraform / OpenTofu / Pulumi / etc. and it runs
plan/apply pipelines with policy-as-code, drift detection, and state management. It does **not**
ship you a cluster — it orchestrates whatever IaC *you* author.

 Dimension Alethia Spacelift 
 --- --- --- 
 What you get **A complete production cluster** (EKS + Aurora/ElastiCache/DynamoDB/SQS-SNS/ECR/S3/Secrets/Route53/WAF) + ArgoCD + operators A runner for **IaC you write & maintain** 
 App delivery **Real ArgoCD/GitOps wired to your repo** CI apply-on-push; not a live GitOps loop, not an app platform 
 Effort to first cluster Design a Project → provision Author all the IaC yourself first 
 Self-host the control plane **Yes — AGPL, a handful of containers** Self-hosted edition only on the top Enterprise+ tier 
 Open source **AGPL core** Proprietary SaaS (only the worker is OSS) 
 Credentials Runner assumes roles at run time You wire cloud creds into the runner

records.title: Alethia vs Spacelift
records.heading: Where Alethia wins
records.text:
- **Delivers an outcome, not plumbing** — one Project → a batteries-included cluster, handed to you.
 Spacelift only runs IaC you must author and maintain.
- **Real GitOps app-delivery** wired to your repo — Spacelift is a CI runner, not ArgoCD.
- **Self-hostable for everyone** (AGPL), and you still **own the OpenTofu** it generates.

records.title: Alethia vs Spacelift
records.heading: Where Spacelift wins (honest)
records.text:
- **Best-in-class for orchestrating existing, complex IaC** at scale — multi-tool (Terraform,
 Pulumi, CloudFormation, Ansible), policy-as-code (OPA/Rego), drift detection, mature collaboration.
- **Established, well-funded**, OpenTofu founding member; deep governance for large IaC estates.
- If you already have a big hand-written IaC codebase, Spacelift manages it; Alethia generates a new one.

 
Bottom line: Spacelift orchestrates IaC you own and operate. Alethia gives you the cluster +
GitOps as a product *and* hands you portable OpenTofu — choose it when you want the outcome,
not just the pipeline.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.text: Authentication Flows. OAuth for web, Device Code Grant for CLI, and token-based auth for Runners.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Authentication Flows
records.text: The platform has three distinct authentication mechanisms — one for each component.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Web Authentication (Console)
records.text:
Console uses **Better Auth** (in-process, with the Drizzle adapter) for web sessions. Two sign-in paths are supported — social OAuth and email OTP:

 Provider Method 
 ---------- -------- 
 GitHub OAuth 2.0 (native) 
 Google OAuth 2.0 (native) 
 GitLab OAuth 2.0 (genericOAuth) 
 Bitbucket OAuth 2.0 (genericOAuth) 
 Email 6-digit OTP (email OTP plugin)

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: User clicks provider button
records.text: The sign-in page shows buttons for each OAuth provider.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Redirect to provider
records.text: Better Auth redirects to the provider's authorization endpoint (e.g., `github.com/login/oauth/authorize`).

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: User authorizes
records.text: The user grants the application access to their account.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Callback with code
records.text: The provider redirects back to `/api/auth/[...all]` with an authorization code. Better Auth exchanges it for access and refresh tokens.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Session created
records.text:
Better Auth creates a **server-side session** (a row in the `session` table) and sets a signed,
HTTP-only session cookie. The web session is a cookie-backed Better Auth session — **not** a JWT (the
`jwt()` plugin is not enabled); JWTs are used only for the CLI and workload identity (see
[**Standards → JOSE**](/standards/jose)).

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Email OTP Flow
records.text: For password-less email sign-in, the user enters their email, receives a **6-digit one-time code**, and submits it back. Better Auth verifies the code and issues the same cookie-backed session as the OAuth path.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Session Management
records.text:
- Sessions are managed by Better Auth (creation, refresh, revocation)
- The session lives in a signed HTTP-only cookie backed by the `session` table; the Next.js middleware validates it on every request
- Data access runs through the app's Policy Decision Point (PDP), which sets the active org via `set_config('app.current_org', …)` before queries
- Coarse Postgres RLS keyed on `org_id` enforces per-organization data isolation (not `auth.uid()`)

Authentication is *who you are*; **authorization** is *what you can do*. Once you're signed in, the PDP decides
every action — see [**Access Control**](/access-control) for roles, permissions, grants (allow/deny), and the
org/team model.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: CLI Authentication (alethia)
records.text:
alethia uses a **Device Authorization Grant** flow ([RFC 8628](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8628))
— the same browser-hands-off-to-terminal pattern as GitHub CLI, Azure CLI, and AWS SSO. Alethia's flow
is a **simplified variant**: the CLI generates the `device_code` itself and binds it directly in the
browser URL, so there is no separately-typed `user_code`. See [**Standards → Device Code**](/standards/device-code)
for the full protocol mapping.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Why Device Code?
records.text:
- Users never type passwords in the terminal
- Works with any OAuth provider (the browser handles the actual login)
- Refresh tokens are long-lived (90 days), so users rarely re-authenticate

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: CLI generates a device code
records.text: `alethia login` generates a UUID `device_code` and opens the browser to `{console_url}/cli/login?device_code={code}`.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: User approves in the browser
records.text:
The user is already signed into Console (or signs in via OAuth / email OTP). The Console page binds the
pending `device_code` to the authenticated user and approves it — no code is typed back into the terminal.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: CLI polls for the token
records.text:
While the user approves, the CLI polls `POST /api/auth/cli/exchange` with the `device_code`. Console
returns **HTTP 404** while the request is still pending; once approved it returns a CLI-specific JWT pair.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Token stored locally
records.text:
The tokens are saved to `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json` (`os.UserConfigDir()/alethia/credentials.json`).
The access token (1-hour TTL) is used for API calls; when it expires the CLI transparently refreshes via
`POST /api/auth/cli/refresh` using the 90-day refresh token.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: JWT Structure
records.text:
CLI tokens are signed with HS256 using `CLI_JWT_SECRET`:

{
 "sub": "user-uuid",
 "email": "user@example.com",
 "type": "access",
 "iss": "urn:example:issuer",
 "aud": "urn:example:audience",
 "exp": 1719936000
}

- **Access token**: 1-hour TTL, used in `Authorization: Bearer` header
- **Refresh token**: 90-day TTL, used only to obtain new access tokens

 
The `iss`/`aud` values above are the current placeholder claims (`urn:example:issuer` / `urn:example:audience`)
and are slated to be set to real Alethia identifiers. Treat them as implementation detail, not a stable contract.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Runner Authentication
records.text: Runners authenticate with Console using **token-based auth** via HTTP headers.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Registration
records.text:
When a Runner is registered (via UI or CLI), the server:

1. Generates a random token
2. Hashes it with SHA-256
3. Stores the **hash** in `runners.token_hash`
4. Returns the **plaintext** token to the user (shown once, never stored)

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Request Authentication
records.text:
Every Runner API call includes two headers:

X-Runner-ID: {runner-uuid}
X-Runner-Token: {plaintext-token}

The server:
1. Looks up the runner by `X-Runner-ID`
2. Hashes the provided `X-Runner-Token` with SHA-256
3. Compares against `runners.token_hash`
4. Rejects if mismatch (401)

 
The plaintext token is shown only once at registration. If lost, the Runner must be re-registered. This is intentional — it prevents token reuse and ensures tokens are not stored in a recoverable location.

records.title: Authentication Flows
records.heading: Authentication Summary
records.text:
Component Method Token Lifetime Storage 
 ----------- -------- --------------- --------- 
 **Console** (web) Better Auth (OAuth + email OTP) Session-based (auto-refresh) Signed HTTP-only cookie (session table) 
 **alethia** (CLI) Device Code Grant (RFC 8628 variant) Access: 1h, Refresh: 90d `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json` 
 **Runner** (agent) SHA-256 token hash Permanent (until re-registration) Environment variable

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.text: Bring Your Own IaC. Attach a git repo holding your own OpenTofu root module to a project environment — Alethia provisions from your module instead of its built-in template, behind a fail-closed static safety gate, a frozen variable contract, and the same verify gate as template provisioning.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Bring Your Own IaC
records.text:
Alethia normally provisions from its own per-cloud project templates (EKS / GKE / AKS / ACK / Talos +
supporting services). **Bring-your-own IaC (BYO IaC, "E3")** lets you point a project **environment** at
a git repository holding your **own OpenTofu root module** — Alethia then provisions that environment
from *your* module instead of the built-in template.

Because a customer's OpenTofu is arbitrary code, running it is a trust decision. This page describes the
contract Alethia gives your module, the static safety gate that vets it, who is allowed to run it, and
the lifecycle that keeps the whole thing TOCTOU-safe.

 
 **v1 is REPLACE mode.** A BYO source attached to an environment provisions *instead of* the built-in
 template — not alongside it. There is at most **one** source per environment. Attaching is rejected if
 the environment already has deployed (or in-flight) template state; destroy it first. Composing your
 module *with* an Alethia template is not a v1 capability.
 

 
 **Flagged off by default.** BYO IaC is gated by `ALETHIA_BYO_IAC_ENABLED` (unset / not `true` = the
 whole feature is unavailable — attach, scan, and provisioning all refuse). On the **managed fleet*

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Why a gate at all
records.text:
Running `tofu plan` on arbitrary HCL is **remote code execution**. Providers are arbitrary binaries
fetched at `init` time; an `external` data source executes a program during `plan`; `local-exec` /
`remote-exec` provisioners run commands at `apply`. So BYO IaC is protected by two independent controls:

1. A **fail-closed static safety gate** (`iacsafety`) that *parses* the module — never evaluates
 expressions, never runs `tofu`, never fetches remote modules — and blocks anything dangerous before a
 single `tofu` command runs.
2. The **execution sandbox** (on the managed fleet) that contains a hostile provider even if it slips the
 gate. See [Isolated Execution](/isolated-execution).

The gate is the first line; the sandbox is the backstop. Neither is trusted alone.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: The Alethia variable contract
records.text:
When Alethia runs your module it injects **platform context** as OpenTofu variables via `TF_VAR_*`
environment variables. This set is a **frozen public contract** — author your module's `variable` blocks
against these exact names:

 Variable Meaning 
 --- --- 
 `alethia_project` The project name 
 `alethia_environment` The environment stage (e.g. `production`, `staging`) 
 `alethia_region` The primary region 
 `alethia_project_id` The Alethia project/config id 
 `alethia_environment_id` The Alethia environment id 

Declare the ones you want in your root module:

variable "alethia_project" { type = string }
variable "alethia_environment" { type = string }
variable "alethia_region" { type = string }

 
 **Frozen means additive-only.** These names never rename or disappear — new keys may only be *added*.
 A module that declares **none** of them is unaffected (OpenTofu silently ignores undeclared
 `TF_VAR_*`); a module that declares one receives its value.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: The alethia_ namespace is reserved
records.text:
Your own variable values (`var_values`) are passed to `tofu` as a var-file, which is **higher precedence**
than `TF_VAR_*` env vars. To stop a customer value from *shadowing* the platform context (e.g. spoofing
`alethia_project_id`), any `var_values` key starting with **`alethia_`** is **dropped** with a warning.
Name your own variables anything outside that prefix.
