Customer variable values are also **coerced to scalars** at the write step: only `string` / number /
`bool` pass through. Nested objects, arrays, and other shapes are rejected (skipped with a warning) so
nothing structured or injectable reaches the tfvars file; a `null` is skipped so your module's own default
applies.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: The static safety gate
records.text:
Before any `tofu` runs, `iacsafety.Scan` walks the root module and every **local** child module it
references (staying inside the clone), parsing configuration only. It produces `error`- and
`warning`-severity findings; **any error-severity finding fails the deploy closed** — the provisioner
never reaches `tofu`. The gate runs at **attach/scan time** *and* is **re-run inline on every
plan/deploy/destroy** (defense in depth — the recorded scan result is never trusted on its own).

What the gate enforces:

 Rule Severity Why 
 --- --- --- 
 **Provider not on the allowlist** error `tofu init` downloads and executes provider binaries; only vetted providers are allowed. Covers both explicit `required_providers` sources and providers *implied* by a resource/data type. 
 **`provisioner` blocks** error `local-exec` / `remote-exec` run arbitrary commands at apply. 
 **`data "external"`** error Executes a program during `plan`. 
 **Remote / absolute module sources** error A non-local (`./` or `../`) module source — registry, git, http, s3/gcs, absolute or `~` path — cannot be statically vetted (`tofu init` would fetch and run code the gate never saw, including provider binaries the unfetched

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Provider allowlist
records.text:
The default allowed provider set:

hashicorp/aws hashicorp/google hashicorp/google-beta
hashicorp/azurerm hashicorp/azuread hashicorp/alicloud
hashicorp/kubernetes hashicorp/helm hashicorp/tls
hashicorp/random hashicorp/time hashicorp/cloudinit
hashicorp/dns hashicorp/local hashicorp/null
hashicorp/template aliyun/alicloud hetznercloud/hcloud

Registry-host prefixes are normalized away, so `registry.opentofu.org/hashicorp/aws` and
`registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws` both match `hashicorp/aws`; a provider on **any other** registry
host keeps its host and therefore never matches — **unknown registries fail closed**. An operator can
override the set with `ALETHIA_BYO_IAC_PROVIDER_ALLOWLIST` (comma-separated provider source addresses);
an unset or empty value falls back to the default.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: State is Alethia's, not yours
records.text:
Your module may declare a `backend` / `cloud` block, but Alethia **overrides it**: an empty `http`
backend override is written into the cloned module (under both `.tf` and `.tofu` extensions, to close the
OpenTofu 1.8+ precedence gap), and its `-backend-config` binds to Alethia's per-job **console HTTP state
proxy**. So:

- Your backend block is **ignored** (and flagged as a warning; a customer-committed *override file* is
 rejected outright as a footgun).
- State lives behind Alethia's **per-job state proxy**, keyed per environment. The runner holds no
 authoritative state and no object-storage credential — one job's token can only touch its own state.

See [OpenTofu State Management](/concepts/terraform-state) for the state model, and
[Isolated Execution](/isolated-execution) for how the state-proxy source-drop is part of the sandbox.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Cluster wiring is opportunistic
records.text:
Alethia's post-apply steps — the reachability gate, kubeconfig capture, ArgoCD install, infra-services —
key off the module's OpenTofu **outputs**:

- A module that outputs **`cluster_name`** (and `cluster_endpoint`) opportunistically gets the
 reachability gate and ArgoCD/GitOps wiring, exactly like a template-provisioned cluster.
- A module that outputs **neither degrades gracefully**: Alethia provisions it, captures its outputs, and
 stops there — no kubeconfig probe, no ArgoCD. Perfectly valid for a module that provisions something
 other than a cluster.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Verification: the same gate as templates
records.text:
A BYO plan flows through the **same fail-closed [elench](/elench) verify gate** as template
provisioning — the deterministic controls (keyless / least-privilege / OIDC-sub, with honest
`not_evaluable` for what the plan can't show) run between `tofu plan` and `tofu apply`, and a hard
control failure blocks the apply unless an authorized override waives it. Every plan/apply also produces
an **ed25519-signed evidence receipt** sealing the report to the exact plan (`plan_sha256`).

 
 The receipt seals the *plan* (`plan_sha256`) + control catalog + verdict. The **git provenance** of a
 BYO deploy — which commit actually ran — is pinned separately on the environment's source record
 (the scanned `commit_sha`, and the `deployed_commit_sha` of the last successful deploy), not embedded
 in the receipt itself.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Lifecycle
records.text:
1. **Attach** (console, per environment) — you attach a repo + ref + module path (+ optional variable
 values and a git credential). Rejected if the environment already has a source, or has deployed /
 in-flight **template** state (replace mode would orphan it — destroy first).
2. **`IAC_SCAN`** — a scan job clones the repo, **pins the exact commit SHA it checked out**, inventories
 the module (providers + module sources), runs the static safety gate + `tofu validate`, and writes the
 report back. Provisioning stays **locked** until a clean scan pins a `commit_sha`; a failed / not-ok
 scan clears the pin.
3. **Plan / Deploy** — run the **pinned commit** (never the moving ref). The gate is re-run inline
 (fail-closed), the backend is overridden to the state proxy, the frozen `TF_VAR_alethia_*` context is
 published, and the plan flows through the verify gate. A successful deploy records its
 `deployed_commit_sha`.
4. **Detect drift** — the pinned commit is refreshed against real state, producing a drift posture like
 any environment.
5. **Destroy** — runs the **last-deployed** commit (`deployed_commit_sha`), then purges the state. A
 source that never deployed is freely detachable;

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Operator model — who can run BYO IaC
records.text:
This is the honest availability boundary. BYO IaC runs **untrusted customer code**, so *where* it runs
determines whether it is available:

- **Self-hosted runners (`operator=self`)** run BYO IaC at the **customer's own risk boundary** — the
 runner is in the customer's own cloud with native permissions. This is the **generally-available** path
 under the `ALETHIA_BYO_IAC_ENABLED` flag.
- **Managed runners** are **fail-closed blocked** from executing untrusted BYO IaC **unless** the
 per-job container sandbox with egress enforcement is active — i.e. `ALETHIA_SANDBOX_BACKEND=container`
 **and** `ALETHIA_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ENFORCED` **and** `ALETHIA_SANDBOX_ENFORCE_MANAGED`. That sandbox
 itself stays disabled until the **real-VM isolation canary** is proven on the fleet (nested podman,
 `/proc` isolation, IMDS unreachable, domain-allowlist egress).

 
 **Managed BYO IaC is trusted-only / gated until the isolation canary passes.** Do not assume managed
 availability. The container sandbox and its turn-on sequence are documented in
 [Isolated Execution → Architecture](/isolated-execution/architecture) and the
 [Managed provisioning runbook](/self-hosting/managed-provisioning#untrusted-byo

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: 1. A minimal BYO module
records.text:
The smallest valid BYO module needs no cluster and no cloud — a `random`/`null` module exercises the
whole path (attach → scan → plan → deploy → destroy) with zero spend. Put this in a repo directory (e.g.
`envs/dev/`):

hcl

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: main.tf — a minimal BYO IaC module

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Platform context from the frozen Alethia contract (declare only what you use).
records.text:
variable "alethia_project" { type = string }
variable "alethia_environment" { type = string }

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Your own variables (any name NOT starting with alethia_).

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: 2. Enable the feature
records.text:
Set on the console env (self-hosted):

ALETHIA_BYO_IAC_ENABLED=true

For the **managed fleet**, additionally satisfy the sandbox gate — see the
[operator model](#operator-model--who-can-run-byo-iac) above.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: 3. Attach it to an environment
records.text:
In the console, open the project's **Architecture** surface for the target environment and attach the
source: **repo URL** (https or ssh — `file://` and other local schemes are rejected on the untrusted
path), **ref** (branch/tag), **module path** (e.g. `envs/dev`, `""` = repo root), an optional **git
credential** (for a private repo), and optional **variable values** (e.g. `greeting = "hi"`). Attaching
auto-queues the `IAC_SCAN`; once it lands `done`, the commit is pinned and Plan/Deploy unlock.

records.title: Bring Your Own IaC
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
The per-job container sandbox that contains untrusted BYO code on the managed fleet — the runtime
 backstop behind the static gate.
 
 
 The fail-closed verify gate and signed evidence receipt every plan/apply flows through.
 
 
 How state is stored — and why Alethia overrides your backend with its per-job state proxy.
 
 
 Config snapshot → ProjectConfig → tfvars → infrastructure, shared by template and BYO paths.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.text: Cloud Provider Abstraction. What Alethia can provision, and how it abstracts AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba Cloud, and Hetzner behind one interface.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.text: Alethia provisions across **five providers** — AWS, GCP, Azure, and Alibaba Cloud (full managed clouds) plus Hetzner (a lean, self-managed Talos Kubernetes cluster on cheap VMs). A single form, a single CLI, and a single Runner agent work across all of them, through a multi-layered abstraction.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: What Alethia can provision
records.text:
Alethia models a cloud-indifferent set of **capability categories** (the `ProjectConfig` service model in `packages/core/types/project_config.go`). Each maps to a concrete resource per provider and is gated by a `create_*`/`provision_*` toggle the Runner emits from your Project config:

Kubernetes cluster · VPC/network · managed SQL database · managed Redis/cache · message queue · pub/sub topic · object storage · NoSQL table · container registry · DNS zone · secrets manager · WAF · CDN / managed TLS certificate — plus the cluster autoscaler and the [pluggable-connector seam](/concepts/connectors) (swap the cloud-native DNS/secrets/registry for a tool you already run).

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Provider capability matrix
records.text:
The registry (`lib/cloud-providers/registry.ts`) maps each capability to the provider's cloud-native service. The **four managed clouds are at feature parity** across every category; **Hetzner is intentionally lean** — it provisions a cluster + private network only, and managed-service categories are covered **in-cluster** (bring-your-own via the [add-on marketplace](/console/add-ons), e.g. a Postgres/Redis/MinIO Helm chart) rather than as managed cloud services.

 Capability AWS GCP Azure Alibaba Hetzner 
 ----------- ----- ----- ------- --------- --------- 
 Kubernetes EKS GKE AKS ACK Talos (self-managed) 
 Network VPC VPC Network VNet VPC Private Network 
 Database Aurora Cloud SQL Azure Database ApsaraDB RDS in-cluster (BYO) 
 Cache ElastiCache Memorystore Azure Cache for Redis ApsaraDB for Redis in-cluster (BYO) 
 NoSQL DynamoDB Firestore Cosmos DB Tablestore in-cluster (BYO) 
 Queue SQS Pub/Sub Service Bus MNS in-cluster (BYO) 
 Topic SNS Pub/Sub Service Bus MNS in-cluster (BYO) 
 Object storage S3 Cloud Storage Blob Storage OSS in-cluster (BYO) 
 Container registry ECR Artifact Registry ACR ACR external 
 DNS Route 53 Cloud DNS Azure DNS AliDNS external 
 Secrets Secrets Man

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Infra services (post-apply)
records.text:
After `tofu apply`, the Runner wires a set of **in-cluster infra services** over ArgoCD (external-dns, the external-secrets store, ingress, the default storage class, the ArgoCD URL). Not every service maps cleanly to every cloud — some are cloud-inherent gaps, not bugs. Rather than silently omit a service (or ship it broken), the pipeline records a **machine-readable per-service decision** with an honest reason — the same `not_evaluable`/"honest N/A" discipline the [verify gate](/concepts/security) applies to the plan.

The decisions come from `argocd.InfraServiceDecisions` (`packages/core/argocd/decisions.go`), computed from the **same gates** the templates render against (`InfraFacts.DNSProvider()`, the per-cloud ClusterSecretStore conditions), so a recorded decision can never drift from what actually shipped. The Runner forwards them on `execution_metadata["infra_services"]` (status + reason only — non-sensitive), and the console renders them alongside the add-on status.

 Infra service AWS GCP Azure Alibaba Hetzner 
 --------------- ----- ----- ------- --------- --------- 
 external-dns installed (Route 53, IRSA) installed when Workload Identity SA present installed when manag

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Region Mapping
records.text:
Regions are mapped across providers for cross-cloud Project duplication:

AWS us-east-1 ←→ GCP us-east4 ←→ Azure eastus
AWS eu-west-1 ←→ GCP europe-west1 ←→ Azure westeurope
AWS ap-south-1 ←→ GCP asia-south1 ←→ Azure centralindia

Each provider has a default region: `us-east-1` (AWS), `us-east4` (GCP), `eastus` (Azure).

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Instance Type Mapping
records.text:
Compute instance types are mapped for Project conversion:

 AWS GCP Azure Profile 
 ----- ----- ------- --------- 
 t3.medium e2-medium Standard_B2s General (2 vCPU, 4 GiB) 
 t3.large e2-standard-2 Standard_D2s_v5 General (2 vCPU, 8 GiB) 
 m5a.large n2-standard-2 Standard_D2as_v5 Compute (2 vCPU, 8 GiB) 
 c5.large c2-standard-4 Standard_F2s_v2 Compute-optimized 
 r5.large n2-highmem-2 Standard_E2s_v5 Memory-optimized

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Database Engine Mapping
records.text:
AWS GCP Azure 
 ----- ----- ------- 
 aurora-postgresql cloudsql-postgresql azure-postgresql 
 aurora-mysql cloudsql-mysql azure-mysql 

Capacity units differ: AWS uses **ACUs** (Aurora Capacity Units, 0.5–128), GCP uses **vCPUs** (1–96), Azure uses **vCores** (1–64). When converting across providers, the platform maps equivalent capacity ranges and warns when units change.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: The provider_config JSONB Column
records.text:
Provider-specific configuration that doesn't fit the shared schema is stored in a `provider_config` JSONB column on component tables. For example:

- **Cluster**: `{ "enable_karpenter": true }` (AWS) vs `{ "enable_autopilot": true }` (GCP) vs `{ "enable_cluster_autoscaler": true }` (Azure)
- **DNS**: `{ "waf_cloudfront": true, "waf_alb": false }` (AWS) vs `{ "cloud_armor": true }` (GCP) vs `{ "azure_waf": true }` (Azure)
- **NoSQL**: `{ "global_table": true }` (AWS DynamoDB) vs `{ "multi_region": true }` (Azure Cosmos DB)

This keeps the shared schema clean while allowing provider-specific features.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: How the Form Adapts
records.text:
When the user switches providers via the Provider Ribbon in the Design a Project form:

1. **Instance types** update to the target provider's catalog
2. **K8s versions** update (e.g., 1.32 for AWS EKS, 1.31 for GCP GKE)
3. **Database engines** switch (Aurora → Cloud SQL → Azure Database)
4. **Cache node types** switch (cache.r6g.large → M2 → C2)
5. **Network options** adapt (VPC → VPC Network → VNet)
6. **DNS/WAF options** change (CloudFront WAF → Cloud Armor → Azure WAF)
7. **Autoscaler** toggles change (Karpenter → GKE Autopilot → Cluster Autoscaler)
8. **Region dropdown** repopulates with the provider's regions

All form fields reset to provider-appropriate defaults. See [Multi-Cloud Conversion](/concepts/multi-cloud-conversion) for how existing Projects are converted across providers.

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: OpenTofu Templates
records.text:
Each provider has its own set of OpenTofu templates:

- `infra/templates/project/aws/` — EKS, Aurora, ElastiCache, DynamoDB, S3, ECR, etc.
- `infra/templates/project/gcp/` — GKE, Cloud SQL, Memorystore, Firestore, GCS, Artifact Registry, etc.
- `infra/templates/project/azure/` — AKS, Azure Database, Azure Cache, Cosmos DB, Blob Storage, ACR, etc.
- `infra/templates/project/alibaba/` — ACK, ApsaraDB RDS, KVStore, Tablestore, OSS, CR, etc.
- `infra/templates/project/hetzner/` — Talos Kubernetes + private network (Cilium CNI, hcloud CCM/CSI).

The runner selects the correct template directory based on the project's cloud provider and generates `terraform.tfvars.json` (the tfvars file `tofu` reads) from the project configuration (`packages/core/cloud/ _provider.go` `ProviderTfvars`).

records.title: Cloud Provider Abstraction
records.heading: Pluggable connectors
records.text: The same registry pattern extends one level down to **per-category connectors** — letting a Project swap the cloud-native default for a tool you already run (Cloudflare DNS, Vault secrets, Docker Hub registry, Datadog/Grafana/Prometheus observability). See [Connectors Architecture](/concepts/connectors).

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.text: Connectors Architecture. How pluggable per-category connectors are declared, stored, and composed into a provision.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Connectors Architecture
records.text: Connectors decouple **what** a Project needs (a DNS zone, secrets, a registry, observability) from **who** provides it. Cloud (AWS/GCP/Azure) and Git (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket) connectors are first-class; the **pluggable per-category** connectors — Cloudflare, Vault, Docker Hub, Datadog, Grafana Cloud, Prometheus — are added by registration, not by editing call sites.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Single source of truth
records.text:
Every pluggable provider is declared once in a manifest, `packages/core/categories/catalog.json` (slug, category, `auth_method`, icon, credential fields, provider-config fields, module path). That manifest is consumed three ways so the provider set never drifts:

- **Go** loads it with `go:embed` (`packages/core/categories`) to resolve which module to compose and what credential a provider needs.
- **Codegen** (`pnpm -F console gen:connectors`) emits the typed TS registry (`lib/connectors/registry.generated.ts`) that drives the credential form and the Project provider selectors, plus the SQL seed of `connectors` catalog rows.
- **Behavior** that can't be data — `Tfvars()` and `Validate()` — lives in small per-slug Go impls.

This mirrors the [cloud-provider registry](/concepts/cloud-abstraction) one level down, per category.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: The CategoryProvider contract
records.text: Each provider impl exposes a small interface: `Category()`, `Slug()`, `ModulePath()`, `Tfvars(ctx)` (maps the component's config + decrypted credential into module variables), and `Validate(ctx)` (rejects nonsensical combinations before a plan is produced). A registry resolves a provider by `(category, slug)`.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Credential storage
records.text: API-key credentials live in the `connector_credentials` table, owner/org-scoped under the same RLS as everything else. Secret fields are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM (`ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY`); non-secret fields (a Vault address, a Docker Hub username) are stored in clear. A lightweight verification ping marks a credential verified.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Runtime credential flow
records.text:
The zero-credential model is preserved end to end:

1. A Project's `config_snapshot` carries only **identifiers** — including each component's chosen provider slug — never secrets.
2. When a runner **claims** a job, the claim endpoint resolves the connectors the Project references, decrypts their credentials, and attaches them to the claim response (alongside the cloud identity).
3. The runner injects them into OpenTofu variables **in memory** at provision time.

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Module composition
records.text: For each component with a pluggable provider, the runner copies the provider's module from `infra/templates/categories/ / /` into the working directory, writes a generated `_categories.tf.json` wiring the module with its variables, and sets a ` _provider` guard variable. The cluster-cloud templates gate their native resources on that variable (`count = var.dns_provider == "native" ? 1 : 0`), so the native resource is **skipped** when a pluggable provider is chosen. Observability is purely additive (no native default).

records.title: Connectors Architecture
records.heading: Open-core
records.text: Connectors are core value and stay AGPL/community — the registry-of-record (`connectors` table) and the category contract are community. Only premium connectors or org-scoped connector governance would live in `ee/` (see [Open Core](/access-control/open-core)).

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.text: Cost Estimation. How the platform estimates infrastructure costs in real-time and via Infracost analysis.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Cost Estimation
records.text: The platform provides two layers of cost estimation: a **real-time cost sidebar** during Project configuration, and a **post-plan Infracost analysis** for precise per-resource costs.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Cost Sidebar (Real-Time)
records.text: The cost sidebar appears on the right side of the Design a Project form. As the user configures infrastructure, the estimated monthly cost updates immediately.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: How Pricing Works
records.text:
1. When a region is selected, the `fetchPrices(region)` action fetches pricing from the AWS Pricing API
2. Prices are cached in the Zustand store with a 24-hour TTL
3. Each component contributes a cost line item

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Pricing Sources
records.text:
The `getRegionPrices(region)` server action fetches:

 Resource Pricing Key Source 
 ---------- ------------ -------- 
 EKS control plane Fixed $0.10/hour Hardcoded 
 EC2 instances Per instance type AWS Pricing API (on-demand, Linux, shared tenancy) 
 NAT Gateway $0.048/hour AWS Pricing API 
 Aurora ACU $0.14/ACU-hour AWS Pricing API 
 ElastiCache nodes Per node type AWS Pricing API 
 WAF web ACL ~$5/month Hardcoded 
 Secrets $0.40/secret/month Hardcoded

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Cost Calculation
records.text:
Monthly cost = Σ (hourly_rate × 730 hours/month)

Per component:
- **Cluster**: EKS control plane ($73/mo) + node_desired_size × average_instance_rate × 730
- **NAT Gateway**: $0.048 × count × 730 (single = 1, HA = AZ count)
- **Database**: min_capacity × ACU_rate × 730 per instance
- **Cache**: node_rate × num_cache_nodes × 730 per cache
- **WAF**: $5 per enabled WAF option
- **Secrets**: $0.40 per secret
- **NoSQL/Queues/Topics**: $0 (usage-based, not estimable from config)

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Fallback Pricing

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Infracost Analysis (Post-Plan)
records.text: After a runner runs `tofu plan`, it optionally runs [Infracost](https://www.infracost.io/) for a precise cost breakdown based on the actual planned resources.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: tofu plan completes
records.text: The runner generates a plan JSON via `tofu show -json plan.tfplan`.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Infracost analyzes the plan
records.text:
If an `INFRACOST_API_KEY` is configured, the runner runs:
infracost breakdown --path plan.json --format json

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Cost data stored
records.text: The Infracost output is parsed and stored in the job's `execution_metadata.cost_breakdown` field.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: UI displays breakdown
records.text: The Plan tab in the Project detail page shows per-resource costs and a total monthly estimate.

records.title: Cost Estimation
records.heading: Why Both?
records.text:
Cost Sidebar Infracost 
 --- --- --- 
 **When** During form editing After plan generation 
 **Speed** Instant Requires plan job 
 **Accuracy** Approximate (simple formulas) Precise (analyzes actual planned resources) 
 **Scope** Major cost drivers only Every resource in the plan 
 **Use case** Quick feedback while configuring Final review before apply 

The sidebar gives instant feedback to guide decisions. Infracost gives the precise number for sign-off before deploying.

records.title: Database Schema
records.text: Database Schema. Complete PostgreSQL schema reference — tables, enums, functions, and Realtime subscriptions.

records.title: Database Schema
records.heading: Database Schema
records.text: The platform uses **PostgreSQL via Drizzle ORM** as its sole database. Tenant isolation is enforced by coarse, org-scoped Row Level Security (RLS) keyed on `org_id` via `set_config('app.current_org', …)` through a Policy Decision Point (PDP). Real-time features are powered by Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY`, fanned out to browsers over SSE.

records.title: Database Schema
records.heading: projects
records.text:
Infrastructure configuration — the central entity.

 -------- ------ ------------- 
 `id` uuid (PK)

 `org_id` uuid Coarse tenancy scope for the RLS blast wall (community: `org_id = user_id`) 
 `cloud_identity_id` uuid (FK → cloud_identities) Cloud account to provision in 
 `project_name` text Infrastructure project name 
 `environment_stage` environment_stage enum development / staging / production 
 `region` text Cloud region (e.g., eu-west-1) 
 `iac_version` text no default (required) 
 `status` project_status enum DRAFT → ACTIVE → DESTROYED 
 `estimated_monthly_cost` numeric Calculated cost estimate 
 `created_at` timestamptz 
 `updated_at` timestamptz

and the expiry columns) live on the **`account`** table — there is no separate `provider_tokens` table.

records.title: Database Schema
records.heading: Key Enums

records.title: Database Schema
records.heading: claim_next_job()

records.title: Database Schema
records.heading: Realtime Subscriptions
records.text:
The following tables are broadcast via Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` (delivered to browsers over SSE) for live UI updates:

- `job_logs` — log streaming (INSERT events)
- `jobs` — job status changes (UPDATE events)
- `runners` — Runner status changes (UPDATE events)

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.text: Domain Model & Terminology. The entity graph — organization, project, environment, component — and the lifecycle states each moves through.

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Domain Model & Terminology
records.text:
Every object in Alethia maps to one row in one Postgres table under `apps/console/lib/db/schema/`.
This page describes that graph and the state machines its rows move through. For the canonical name
of each concept — and the words the product deliberately avoids — start with the
[Glossary](/concepts/glossary); this page assumes those terms.

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: The core shape
records.text:
The tenancy and configuration graph is four levels deep:

 (RLS boundary, org_id)"] --> 1:N Proj["project (projects)"]
 Proj --> N:1 CI["cloud identity (cloud_identities)"]
 Proj --> 1:N Env["environment (project_environments)"]
 Env --> Comp["components (project_* tables)"]
 Env --> Jobs["jobs (provisioning work)"]
 Jobs --> Logs["job_logs"]
`} />

- An **organization** (`org_id`) is the tenancy boundary. Coarse Postgres RLS keyed on `org_id`
 filters every org-scoped table (see [Security](/concepts/security#row-level-security-rls)).
- A **project** (`projects`) is a declarative infrastructure *app*. It links to **exactly one**
 [cloud identity](/concepts/glossary#connectivity) (`projects.cloud_identity_id`) and carries the
 `region` and `iac_version`. It owns **N** environments.
- An **environment** (`project_environments`) is an independently-provisionable deployment target.
 This is the level most other tools collapse to 1:1 — here it is **1:N**: `staging` and `production`
 are two rows of the same project, each with its own state file, its own components, and its own
 provisioning `status`.
- **Components** are the cloud resources an environment declares (`project_cluster`,
 `

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Environments
records.text:
`project_environments` is the pivot of the whole model. Each row has:

 Column Type Meaning 
 --- --- --- 
 `name` `text` Slug feeding the OpenTofu state key (`{project}-{name}-{region}/tofu.tfstate`) and the URL. Unique per project. 
 `stage` `environment_stage` `development` \ `staging` \ `production`. Drives provider template defaults (instance sizing, HA knobs). Distinct from `name`. 
 `status` `project_status` The per-environment provisioning lifecycle (see below). 
 `is_default` `boolean` Exactly one row per project is the representative for single-value surfaces — the CLI wire, the project list's "Env"/status column, the project-detail header. Enforced by a partial unique index. 
 `lifecycle` `environment_lifecycle` `persistent` (long-lived dev/staging/prod) or `ephemeral` (disposable, TTL'd). 
 `region` `text` `NULL` inherits `projects.region`.

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: The ephemeral seam (inert today)
records.text:
`lifecycle`, `expires_at`, `cost_cap`, and the reaper bookkeeping columns (`reap_attempts`,
`last_reap_at`, `reap_gave_up_at`) exist so a future reaper — a `drift`-style reconciler — and the UI
can build disposable "sandbox" environments on top. **Nothing reads them yet**: every environment is
effectively `persistent`. The column is a forward seam, not a live feature.

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Components attach to (project, environment)
records.text:
Two shapes, distinguished only by their unique constraint (`apps/console/lib/db/schema/project-components.ts`):

**Singletons** — `UNIQUE (project_id, environment_id)`, so zero-or-one per environment:

 
 VPC / VNet / VPC Network 
 EKS / GKE / AKS / ACK / Talos 
 Route 53 / Cloud DNS / Azure DNS 
 Log/metric delivery config 
 GitOps repo references 
 Bring-your-own IaC module (E3) 
 

**Multi-instance** — `UNIQUE (project_id, environment_id, name)`, so zero-or-many per environment,
each addressed by `name`:

 
 Aurora / Cloud SQL / Azure DB / RDS 
 ElastiCache / Memorystore / Azure Cache (redis \ valkey) 
 SQS / Pub/Sub / Service Bus 
 SNS / Pub/Sub / Service Bus Topics 
 DynamoDB / Firestore / Cosmos DB 
 ECR / Artifact Registry / ACR 
 Secrets Manager / Secret Manager / Key Vault 
 S3 / Cloud Storage / Storage Account 
 Marketplace add-ons via ArgoCD (keyed by `addon_id`)

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Environment lifecycle
records.text:
The per-environment `status` (`project_status` enum) is the state machine a provision walks. It is a
property of the **environment**, not the project — provisioning `production` never moves `staging`.

 DRAFT
 DRAFT --> QUEUED: submit plan/deploy
 QUEUED --> PROVISIONING: runner claims the job
 PROVISIONING --> ACTIVE: tofu apply succeeds
 PROVISIONING --> FAILED: apply fails
 FAILED --> QUEUED: retry
 ACTIVE --> QUEUED: re-deploy a change
 ACTIVE --> DESTROYING: destroy
 DESTROYING --> DESTROYED
 DESTROYING --> FAILED
 DESTROYED --> [*]
`} />

 Status Meaning 
 --- --- 
 `DRAFT` Configuration written but never submitted for provisioning 
 `QUEUED` A provisioning job is waiting for a runner to claim it 
 `PROVISIONING` A runner is actively executing `tofu` for this environment 
 `ACTIVE` Infrastructure is provisioned and live 
 `FAILED` A plan/apply/destroy failed (retryable — re-enters `QUEUED`) 
 `DESTROYING` A `DESTROY` job is tearing the environment down 
 `DESTROYED` Infrastructure has been torn down

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Component status
records.text:
Each component carries its own `component_status`, independent of its environment's status — a
cluster can be `ACTIVE` while a newly-added database is still `CREATING`.

 Status Meaning 
 --- --- 
 `PENDING` Declared, awaiting first provisioning 
 `CREATING` Being created by `tofu apply` 
 `ACTIVE` Successfully provisioned 
 `UPDATING` A configuration change is being applied 
 `FAILED` Creation or update failed 
 `DESTROYING` Being torn down 
 `DESTROYED` Successfully removed

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Jobs
records.text:
A **job** (`jobs`) is one unit of runner work. It references a `(project_id, environment_id,
cloud_identity_id)` and, once claimed, a `runner_id`. Its `provision_job_status` walks:

 QUEUED
 QUEUED --> CLAIMED: claim_next_job / SKIP LOCKED
 CLAIMED --> PROCESSING: runner starts execution
 PROCESSING --> SUCCESS: reported ok
 PROCESSING --> FAILED: error / stale-job sweep
 QUEUED --> CANCELLED: user cancels
 CLAIMED --> CANCELLED
 SUCCESS --> [*]
 FAILED --> [*]
 CANCELLED --> [*]
`} />

See [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue) for the atomic-claim mechanics and
[Runner Scaling](/concepts/runner-scaling) for how capacity appears.

records.title: Domain Model & Terminology
records.heading: Job types
records.text:
The `provision_job_type` enum (`apps/console/lib/db/schema/enums.ts`) is the full catalog of work a
runner can claim — not just plan/apply/destroy:

 Type What it does 
 --- --- 
 `PLAN` `tofu plan` (+ optional Infracost estimate); uploads a plan artifact 
 `DEPLOY` `tofu plan` → [verify gate](/concepts/security) → `tofu apply` → ArgoCD install + manifest scaffold 
 `DESTROY` `tofu destroy` with graceful cleanup 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` Provision a self-operated runner's infrastructure 
 `UPDATE_RUNNER` Roll a self-operated runner to a newer release 
 `DESTROY_RUNNER` Tear down a self-operated runner's infrastructure 
 `ANALYZE_REPO` Clone + statically scan a git repo into a `RepoDigest` (repo-first onboarding) 
 `DETECT_DRIFT` `tofu plan -refresh-only` → a drift `Posture` (`packages/core/drift`) 
 `AUDIT` Run the Elench `verify` engine over a bring-your-own plan/manifests — **provisions nothing** 
 `CHART_SCAN` Render + scan a bring-your-own Helm chart (`helm template` → `verify.EvaluateManifests`); zero secrets, deny-all egress 
 `IAC_SCAN` Scan a bring-your-own IaC module (clone → pin commit → inventory providers/modules → `tofu validate`); its result **gates** that environment's `PLAN

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.text: GitOps & ArgoCD. How ArgoCD is installed automatically and configured with the App of Apps pattern.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.text: After a successful `tofu apply`, the runner automatically installs **ArgoCD** on the provisioned Kubernetes cluster. ArgoCD manages all subsequent application deployments via GitOps — Git is the source of truth.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: What Gets Installed
records.text:
Every provisioned cluster comes with ArgoCD and a set of supporting components:

 Component Purpose 
 ----------- --------- 
 **ArgoCD** GitOps continuous delivery engine 
 **AWS Load Balancer Controller** Kubernetes ingress via ALB/NLB (AWS) 
 **External DNS** Automatic DNS record management from K8s resources 
 **External Secrets Operator** Sync secrets from cloud stores to K8s Secrets 
 **Karpenter** Just-in-time node provisioning (AWS) 
 **Metrics Server** Resource metrics for Horizontal Pod Autoscaler 
 **Storage Class** EBS / Persistent Disk / Managed Disk provisioner

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: tofu apply completes
records.text: The runner provisions all infrastructure (VPC, cluster, databases, etc.) via `tofu`. Outputs include cluster name, endpoint, and ARN.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Exec-plugin kubeconfig (CLI-free)
records.text:
The runner is **CLI-free** — it does not shell out to `aws eks update-kubeconfig`, `gcloud`, or `az`.
It writes an **exec-credential** kubeconfig (`packages/core/cloud.writeExecKubeconfig`) built from the
`tofu` cluster outputs (endpoint + CA), whose exec block invokes the runner itself as the credential
plugin: `runner kube-token --provider ` (`apps/runner/internal/agent/kube_token.go`).
So kubectl/helm mint a fresh short-lived token in-process on demand, exchanging the keyless cloud
credential for cluster access — no cluster CLIs, no long-lived kubeconfig secret.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: ArgoCD installed via Helm
records.text: The Runner runs `helm upgrade --install argocd` with the ArgoCD Helm chart. It waits for all ArgoCD pods to become Ready.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Admin password stays in the cluster
records.text:
The ArgoCD admin password lives in the `argocd-initial-admin-secret` Kubernetes Secret and is **never extracted or stored by Alethia** — retrieve it on demand:

kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \
 -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' base64 -d

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Infrastructure facts rendered
records.text: `tofu` outputs (cluster name, VPC ID, RDS endpoints, ElastiCache endpoints, etc.) are rendered into a **Helm values file** called "infra facts." This file provides concrete resource identifiers to ArgoCD applications.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: App of Apps configured
records.text: ArgoCD Application manifests are rendered from templates and applied to the cluster.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Metadata reported
records.text: The ArgoCD URL and cluster metadata are stored in the job's execution metadata and written to the Project's component tables. Credentials (the admin password, kubeconfigs, generated secret values) are scrubbed and never persisted.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: App of Apps Pattern
records.text:
ArgoCD uses the **App of Apps** pattern: a single "parent" Application watches a directory of YAML files, each defining a child Application.

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

The parent Application points to this directory. When a new YAML file is added (via Git push), ArgoCD automatically deploys the new application.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Infrastructure Facts
records.text:
`tofu` outputs are injected into ArgoCD applications as Helm values. This bridges the gap between infrastructure provisioning (OpenTofu) and application deployment (ArgoCD).

Example infra facts:

cluster:
 name: api-backend-production
 endpoint: https://ABC123.gr7.eu-west-1.eks.amazonaws.com
 oidcProviderArn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/...

database:
 endpoint: api-backend-production.cluster-abc123.eu-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com
 port: 5432

cache:
 endpoint: api-backend-production.abc123.euw1.cache.amazonaws.com
 port: 6379

dns:
 domain: api.example.com
 zoneId: Z1234567890

ArgoCD applications reference these values in their Helm charts. For example, an application's database connection string uses `{{ .Values.database.endpoint }}`.

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: ArgoCD Templates
records.text:
Templates are stored in `infra/templates/argocd/` and staged into the Runner Docker image during the release build. Each template is a Kubernetes manifest with Helm-style placeholders:

 Template What It Deploys 
 ---------- ---------------- 
 `aws-load-balancer-controller.yaml` ALB/NLB ingress controller 
 `external-dns.yaml` Route53/Cloud DNS/Azure DNS sync (renders only when DNS is enabled with a domain and the cloud has a supported backend + workload identity) 
 `external-secrets-operator.yaml` Secrets Manager → K8s Secrets 
 `karpenter.yaml` Node auto-provisioning 
 `metrics-server.yaml` CPU/memory metrics for HPA 
 `storage-class-gp3.yaml` GP3 EBS storage class 
 `project-infra.yaml` ArgoCD AppProject boundary

records.title: GitOps & ArgoCD
records.heading: Post-Installation
records.text:
After ArgoCD is installed:

1. **All infrastructure components are managed by ArgoCD** — changes to Git repos are automatically synced
2. **The ArgoCD URL is displayed** in the Console Clusters page with a clickable link
3. **Applications can be added** by pushing new YAML files to the `manifests/applications/` directory
4. **Drift detection** — ArgoCD continuously compares the live cluster state against Git and alerts on drift

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.text: Glossary & Naming. The canonical name for every core concept — one term per thing, and the words we deliberately avoid.

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Glossary & Naming
records.text:
Alethia uses **one name per concept**. This page is the source of truth: every other page in these
docs defers to the terms below. If you find a doc using a rejected term, it's a bug — the canonical
column is what the product, the schema, and the CLI actually call it.

 
 A quick disambiguation up front: **Alethia** is the whole platform, **Console** is the web app you
 sign into, and **`alethia`** (lowercase) is the CLI binary. **Alethia Labs** is the company.

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Core objects
records.text:
Concept Canonical term Definition Avoid 
 --- --- --- --- 
 Tenancy boundary **organization** ("org") The top-level owner of every resource. In Community a personal org is created per user; the Enterprise edition turns orgs into shared, multi-member tenants. Enforced by coarse Postgres RLS keyed on `org_id`. ~~workspace~~ 
 Infrastructure app record **project** A declarative infrastructure configuration under an org. A project is an *app* that **owns N environments**. Links to exactly one [cloud identity](#connectivity). — 
 Deployment target **environment** An independently-provisionable target inside a project (`project_environments`). A project owns **N**; each has a `stage` (development / staging / production) and a `lifecycle` (`persistent` or `ephemeral`). The per-environment provisioning **status** lives here. 1:1 phrasing; treating a project as having a single environment 
 Configured resource **component** A cloud resource a project's environment declares — network, cluster, DNS, database, cache, queue, registry, secret, etc. Every component belongs to a `(project, environment)` pair. — 

 
 **A project owns N environments — not one.** A common stale assumption is Project↔

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Connectivity
records.text:
Concept Canonical term Definition Avoid 
 --- --- --- --- 
 A connected cloud account **cloud identity** The credential anchor for one connected cloud account (AWS / GCP / Azure / Hetzner / …). Keyless via a single Alethia OIDC issuer for the managed clouds; the runner assumes into it at job time. Queries always filter by `provider`. ~~connector~~, ~~integration~~, ~~cloud connection~~, ~~connection~~ (for the account) 
 The catalog of provider types **connector** The registry of *connector types* per category (cloud, git, observability, registry, dns, secrets) plus git/pluggable providers. "Connector" names the **type in the catalog**, never a specific connected account. using "connector" to mean a cloud identity

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Provisioning & execution
records.text:
Concept Canonical term Definition Avoid 
 --- --- --- --- 
 IaC engine **OpenTofu** (`tofu`) The binary the runner executes: `tofu plan` / `tofu apply` / `tofu destroy`. ~~Terraform~~ — except when referring to the HCL *language/format* itself 
 Execution agent **runner** The Go daemon that claims jobs and executes them. Described by two axes below. — 
 Who runs & bills a runner **operator** = `managed` \ `self` `managed` = Alethia runs it in the platform account and bills provisioned hours; `self` = the customer runs it in their own cloud. ~~mode~~ (legacy `cloud-hosted`/`self-hosted` is a back-compat alias only) 
 How a self runner exists **provisioning** = `deployed` \ `registered` `deployed` = provisioned into the customer's cloud by an existing runner; `registered` = customer brought their own (own IaC or `alethia runner start`). Null for `managed`. "self-hosted" as a synonym for `registered` 
 Managed capacity **in-app scaler + Hetzner VM fleet** A 60s in-app loop (`apps/console/lib/fleet/`) sizes per-provider warm pools by queue depth and creates/destroys cheap Hetzner VMs that self-register. ~~ECS Fargate~~, ~~Lambda scaler~~, ~~EventBridge~~ (retired) 
 The verification la

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Surfaces & brand
records.text:
Concept Canonical term Avoid 
 --- --- --- 
 Whole product / platform **Alethia** ~~ADP~~, ~~itgix~~ 
 The web control plane **Console** (`apps/console`) calling the web app "Alethia" 
 The command-line tool **`alethia`** (CLI) — 
 The company **Alethia Labs** — 
 Apply a project's plan from the CLI **`alethia project apply`** ~~`alethia apply`~~ (no such top-level command)

records.title: Glossary & Naming
records.heading: Job types at a glance
records.text:
Jobs are the unit of work a runner claims. The full set (see [Job Queue](/concepts/job-queue) and
[Runner → Job execution](/runner/job-execution)):

 Type Purpose 
 --- --- 
 `PLAN` `tofu plan` (+ optional Infracost) 
 `DEPLOY` `tofu plan` → verify gate → `tofu apply` → ArgoCD install 
 `DESTROY` `tofu destroy` with graceful cleanup 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` / `UPDATE_RUNNER` / `DESTROY_RUNNER` Lifecycle of a self-operated runner 
 `ANALYZE_REPO` Clone + statically scan a repo into a `RepoDigest` 
 `DETECT_DRIFT` `tofu plan -refresh-only` → drift `Posture` 
 `AUDIT` Run the Elench verify engine over bring-your-own plan/manifests (provisions nothing) 
 `CHART_SCAN` Render + scan a bring-your-own Helm chart (zero secrets, deny-all egress) 
 `IAC_SCAN` Scan a bring-your-own IaC module; gates the environment's PLAN/DEPLOY/DESTROY 
 `STATE_SURGERY` Two-person-approved, audited repair of a corrupt tofu state (ships inert / fail-closed)

records.title: Platform Overview
records.text: Platform Overview. High-level architecture of the multi-cloud infrastructure orchestration platform.

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Platform Overview
records.text: This platform is a hybrid multi-cloud infrastructure orchestration system. It combines a visual web interface, a command-line tool, and autonomous execution agents to provision production-grade Kubernetes clusters with databases, caches, networking, DNS, secrets, and GitOps — across AWS, GCP, and Azure.

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Three-Component Model
records.text:
Component Type Language Role 
 ----------- ------ ---------- ------ 
 **Console** Web application TypeScript (Next.js) Visual configuration, job management, real-time monitoring 
 **alethia** CLI tool Go Terminal-based operations, scripting, CI/CD integration 
 **Runner** Execution agent Go Runs OpenTofu (`tofu`), installs ArgoCD, streams logs back to Console

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Data Flow
records.text:
Q["Job Queue (Postgres)"]
 CLI["alethia CLI"] --> Q
 Q --> claim Runner["Runner (agent)"]
 Runner --> keyless assume Cloud["Cloud Provider"]
 Runner --> logs Notify["LISTEN/NOTIFY"]
 Notify --> SSE Web
`} />

1. The user configures infrastructure through Console (web form) or the `alethia` CLI
2. Actions are queued as jobs in Postgres
3. A runner claims the job atomically and assumes temporary, keyless cloud credentials
4. The runner executes `tofu` and streams logs back in real time over LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE
5. On completion, ArgoCD is installed for GitOps-managed deployments

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Multi-Cloud Support
records.text:
Every infrastructure component has provider-specific implementations across three clouds:

 Capability AWS GCP Azure 
 ----------- ----- ----- ------- 
 Kubernetes EKS GKE AKS 
 Relational DB Aurora PostgreSQL/MySQL Cloud SQL Azure Database 
 Cache ElastiCache (Redis/Valkey) Memorystore Azure Cache for Redis 
 NoSQL DynamoDB Firestore Cosmos DB 
 Messaging SQS + SNS Pub/Sub Service Bus 
 DNS Route 53 Cloud DNS Azure DNS 
 Secrets Secrets Manager Secret Manager Key Vault 
 Container Registry ECR Artifact Registry ACR 
 Object Storage S3 Cloud Storage Storage Account 
 WAF AWS WAFv2 Cloud Armor Azure WAF

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Tech Stack
records.text:
**Web (Console):** Next.js 15, PostgreSQL + Drizzle ORM, Better Auth, LISTEN/NOTIFY + SSE realtime, S3-compatible storage (SeaweedFS), Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Zustand

**CLI (alethia):** Go, Cobra, Charmbracelet (lipgloss, huh, bubbletea), Homebrew tap

**Agent (Runner):** Go single binary — on a managed Hetzner fleet VM or the customer's own cloud; bundles `tofu` + kubectl + Helm, **CLI-free** (no aws-cli / gcloud / azure-cli; keyless OIDC + in-process `runner kube-token`)

**Shared (core):** Go package — provisioner, OpenTofu wrapper, cloud provider abstraction, ArgoCD rendering, Infracost, Git, Helm, and the Elench `verify` + `drift` engines

**Infrastructure:** OpenTofu, S3-compatible storage / SeaweedFS (state), the in-app fleet scaler (Hetzner VMs), release-please

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Concept Pages
records.text:
Deep dives into the platform's key technical patterns:

 
 
 Project/Runner entities, lifecycles, and relationships.
 
 
 Zero-credential model, RLS, cloud provider federation.
 
 
 Full PostgreSQL schema — tables, enums, functions, LISTEN/NOTIFY.
 
 
 Atomic claiming, FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, failure recovery.
 
 
 The in-app scaler sizing per-provider warm pools on Hetzner VMs by queue depth.
 
 
 Registry, region mapping, instance types, provider_config JSONB.
 
 
 Form values → config snapshot → ProjectConfig → terraform.tfvars.json → infrastructure.
 
 
 LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE, log streaming, store synchronization.
 
 
 AWS Pricing API, real-time sidebar, Infracost integration.
 
 
 OAuth (web), Device Code Grant (CLI), token auth (Runner).
 
 
 Cross-provider Project duplication with mapping and warnings.
 
 
 S3-compatible backend, per-environment state paths, plan artifacts.
 
 
 App of Apps pattern, infra facts, Helm values.
 
 
 Provision from your own OpenTofu module — safety gate, variable contract, operator model.
 
 
 release-please, GoReleaser, Docker builds, CI/CD.

records.title: Platform Overview
records.heading: Monorepo Structure
records.text:
alethialabs/
├── apps/
│ ├── console/ # Next.js web platform
│ ├── cli/ # Go CLI tool
│ ├── runner/ # Go execution agent
│ └── docs/ # Documentation site (Fumadocs)
├── packages/
│ ├── core/ # Shared Go library (provisioner, verify, drift, argocd)
│ ├── @repo/ui/ # Shared shadcn/ui design system
│ └── @repo/* # Shared web packages (brand, plan-catalog, email, config)
├── infra/
│ ├── templates/project/ # Infrastructure templates (AWS/GCP/Azure/Alibaba/Hetzner)
│ ├── templates/argocd/ # ArgoCD application templates
│ ├── templates/runner/ # Self-deployed runner template (ECS) — NOT the managed fleet
│ └── connector/ # Cloud account bootstrap (OIDC federation setup)
├── apps/console/lib/fleet/ # In-app managed-fleet scaler (Hetzner)
├── apps/console/lib/db/migrations/ # PostgreSQL schema migrations (Drizzle)
└── .github/workflows/ # CI/CD (release-please, release-cli, release-runner)

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.text: Job Queue Pattern. How infrastructure operations are queued, claimed atomically, and executed by Runners.

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Job Queue Pattern
records.text:
Every provisioning operation in the platform — planning, deploying, destroying, drift detection,
bring-your-own scans — is modeled as a **job** in a PostgreSQL queue. Runners poll for jobs, claim
them atomically, execute them, and report results. (Cloud-account connection is *not* a job; it runs
server-side in the Console — see [Connectors](/concepts/connectors).)

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Why a Job Queue?
records.text:
Infrastructure provisioning is slow (minutes to hours) and must be reliable. A job queue provides:

- **Decoupling** — the web UI and CLI queue work without waiting for completion
- **Exactly-once execution** — atomic claiming prevents two Runners from running the same job
- **Resumability** — if a runner dies, the job can be retried from persisted OpenTofu state
- **Auditability** — every operation is logged with timestamps, status, and metadata

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Job Types
records.text:
The common provisioning jobs are below; the full `provision_job_type` catalog (including
`ANALYZE_REPO`, `DETECT_DRIFT`, `AUDIT`, `CHART_SCAN`, `IAC_SCAN`, and `STATE_SURGERY`) is documented
in the [Domain Model](/concepts/domain-model#job-types) and the [Glossary](/concepts/glossary#job-types-at-a-glance).

 Type Purpose Triggered By 
 ------ --------- ------------- 
 `PLAN` `tofu plan` + optional Infracost analysis Planning an environment 
 `DEPLOY` `tofu plan` → verify gate → `tofu apply` → install ArgoCD Applying an environment 
 `DESTROY` `tofu destroy` with graceful cleanup Destroying an environment 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` Provision a self-operated runner's infrastructure Deploying a self-operated runner 
 `UPDATE_RUNNER` Roll a self-operated runner to the latest release Updating a runner 
 `DESTROY_RUNNER` Tear down a self-operated runner's infrastructure Removing a deployed runner

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Job Lifecycle
records.text:
QUEUED
 QUEUED --> CLAIMED: claim_next_job()
 CLAIMED --> PROCESSING: execution starts
 PROCESSING --> SUCCESS
 PROCESSING --> FAILED
 QUEUED --> CANCELLED: user cancels
 CLAIMED --> CANCELLED
 SUCCESS --> [*]
 FAILED --> [*]
 CANCELLED --> [*]
`} />

 Status Meaning 
 -------- --------- 
 `QUEUED` Waiting for a Runner to pick it up 
 `CLAIMED` A Runner has locked it (prevents double-execution) 
 `PROCESSING` Runner is actively running `tofu` 
 `SUCCESS` Completed successfully 
 `FAILED` Failed with error message 
 `CANCELLED` User cancelled before completion

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Atomic Job Claiming

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Config Snapshot
records.text:
When a job is created, the current Project configuration is serialized as a **config_snapshot** in the job record. This ensures the Runner executes exactly the configuration that was planned, even if the user modifies the Project after queuing.

For DEPLOY jobs, a **plan hash** is validated: if the Project config changed since the plan was generated, the deploy fails with a hash mismatch error, forcing the user to re-plan.

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Job Created
records.text:
User plans / applies / destroys an environment in the Console (or runs `alethia project plan` /
`alethia project apply` / `alethia project destroy` in the CLI). A `jobs` row is inserted with status `QUEUED`.

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Runner Claims Job
records.text: A runner's poll loop calls `POST /api/jobs/claim`. The server executes `claim_next_job()`. If a job is returned, the runner proceeds.

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Credential Assumption
records.text: The runner reads the `cloud_identity` from the job and assumes temporary, **keyless** credentials — `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` (AWS), Workload Identity Federation token exchange (GCP), or federated identity (Azure) — using a minted OIDC assertion, no static key. See [Security](/concepts/security).

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Execution
records.text: The runner runs the job-type-specific logic (`tofu` plan/apply/destroy, ArgoCD install, etc.) while streaming logs.

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Result Reporting
records.text: On completion, the runner calls `PUT /api/jobs/{id}/status` with SUCCESS or FAILED, plus execution metadata (`tofu` outputs, cost data, cluster info, and the verify report).

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Finalization
records.text: For DEPLOY jobs, the Console calls `finalizeDeployment()` to extract cluster metadata from `tofu` outputs and update the environment's component tables (cluster endpoint, ArgoCD URL, database endpoints, etc.).

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Failure Recovery
records.text:
When a Runner dies mid-job:

1. Heartbeat stops → Console marks Runner OFFLINE after 60 seconds
2. Job stays in PROCESSING with no new log updates
3. After 5 minutes of no log activity, Console marks the job FAILED
4. User can **retry** the job — creates a new job with the same config
5. OpenTofu state is preserved in S3-compatible storage, so the retry continues from the last successful state

records.title: Job Queue Pattern
records.heading: Real-time Status Updates
records.text: Job status changes are broadcast via [Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE](/concepts/realtime). The UI subscribes to `jobs` UPDATE events and updates the jobs store in real-time. The job detail page also subscribes to `job_logs` INSERT events for live log streaming.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.text: Multi-Cloud Conversion. How Project configurations are converted across AWS, GCP, and Azure when duplicating.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.text:
Users can duplicate an existing Project and target a different cloud provider. The platform converts the configuration automatically, mapping resources, instance types, and capacity units across providers.

 

 
 A converted project doesn't just *provision* on the new cloud — it **deploys apps** there too. The
 ArgoCD/GitOps layer is cloud-agnostic (per-cloud workload identity), so GCP and Azure get a working
 external-dns + app-sync just like AWS. See [Multi-cloud GitOps seam](/elench/multi-cloud).

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: When Conversion Happens
records.text:
1. User clicks **"Duplicate & Edit"** on an existing Project
2. Selects a different cloud identity (e.g., switching from AWS to GCP)
3. The `convertProjectConfig()` function transforms the configuration
4. Conversion **warnings** are displayed for items that need manual review
5. User adjusts the converted config and submits

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Region
records.text:
Regions are mapped via a cross-provider table:

AWS us-east-1 → GCP us-east4 → Azure eastus

If no mapping exists, the target provider's default region is used, and a warning is shown.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Cluster
records.text:
- **Instance types**: Mapped via `INSTANCE_TYPE_MAP` (e.g., `t3.medium` → `e2-medium` → `Standard_B2s`). Unmapped types generate a warning.
- **K8s version**: Reset to the target provider's default (versions differ across providers)
- **Autoscaler**: Key name converted in `provider_config` (`enable_karpenter` → `enable_autopilot` → `enable_cluster_autoscaler`)

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Databases
records.text:
- **Engine**: Mapped (e.g., `aurora-postgresql` → `cloudsql-postgresql` → `azure-postgresql`)
- **Capacity**: Clamped to target provider's min/max range. Units change (ACU → vCPU → vCores), and a warning explains the difference.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Caches
records.text: - **Node type**: Mapped (e.g., `cache.t3.medium` → `M2` → `C2`). Unmapped types fall back to the target provider's default.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Network
records.text:
- If the source Project uses an **existing network** (`provision_network = false`), the conversion switches to **create new** (`provision_network = true`) with a warning: "Existing network cannot be reused across providers."
- CIDR blocks are preserved (they're provider-agnostic).

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: DNS & WAF
records.text:
- **Zone ID and domain**: Cleared (DNS zones are provider-specific)
- **WAF config**: Cleared with a warning (WAF rules differ significantly across providers)
- User must re-select a DNS zone and re-configure WAF in the target provider.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: NoSQL
records.text:
- **DynamoDB → Firestore**: Warning about data model differences ("Firestore is document-based, not key-value")
- **Range key support**: Checked per provider. If the target doesn't support range keys natively, a warning is shown.

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Messaging
records.text: - **FIFO queues**: Warning when targeting GCP Pub/Sub ("No direct FIFO support; consider ordering keys")

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: Conversion Warnings

records.title: Multi-Cloud Conversion
records.heading: What Cannot Be Converted
records.text:
Some configurations are fundamentally provider-specific:

- **IAM roles / service accounts** — identity models differ
- **Cluster admin ARNs** — IAM identifiers are provider-specific
- **Existing network references** — VPC IDs don't translate across providers
- **DNS hosted zones** — zone IDs are provider-specific
- **WAF rules** — rule schemas differ significantly

These are cleared during conversion and must be re-configured manually.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.text: Provisioning Pipeline. How project form values flow through config snapshots and provider-specific mappings into OpenTofu variables.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Provisioning Pipeline
records.text:
When a user plans or applies an environment, the platform transforms the project's form configuration into OpenTofu variables through a five-stage pipeline. Each stage has a single responsibility: serialize, freeze, deserialize, map, execute.

 

 
 The Project config can be authored by hand **or inferred from repos** — the "A" flow scans one or more
 repos (monorepo-aware), infers the stack, and produces the same `ProjectFormData` this pipeline
 consumes. See the [elench Architecture](/elench/architecture) page for the full repos → verified cluster
 loop.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Stage 1: Form → Database
records.text:
The project form in Console (or `alethia project plan` in the CLI) calls `createProject()`, which inserts the configuration into normalized component tables:

 Table Data 
 ------- ------ 
 `projects` Project name, region, environment, cloud_identity_id, iac_version 
 `project_network` CIDR block, provision_network flag, existing network_id 
 `project_cluster` K8s version, instance types, node min/max/desired, cluster_admins, provider_config 
 `project_dns` Enabled flag, zone_id, domain_name, WAF settings (in provider_config) 
 `project_repositories` Apps destination (GitOps) repo URL 
 `project_source_repos` Scanned source repos (1:N) + detected services — used to generate app manifests 
 `project_databases` Engine, instance class, scaling config (per database) 
 `project_caches` Node type, cluster size (per cache) 
 `project_queues` Queue name, config (per queue) 
 `project_topics` Topic name, subscriptions (per topic) 
 `project_nosql_tables` Table/collection config with provider_config 
 `project_secrets` Secret name and metadata 

Each table stores the shared schema. Provider-specific options live in `provider_config` JSONB columns — see [Cloud Provider Abstraction](/concepts/

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Stage 2: Database → Config Snapshot
records.text:
When a job is queued, `buildConfigSnapshot()` in `app/server/actions/projects.ts` queries all component tables in parallel and assembles a single JSON object. This object is stored in `jobs.config_snapshot`.

The snapshot is a **point-in-time freeze**. Even if the user edits the Project after queuing, the job executes the exact configuration that was planned.

config_snapshot = {
 ...project fields,
 provider: "aws" "gcp" "azure",
 network: { provision_network, cidr_block, network_id, ... },
 cluster: { cluster_version, instance_types, node_min_size, ... },
 dns: { enabled, zone_id, domain_name, provider_config, ... },
 databases: [...],
 caches: [...],
 queues: [...],
 topics: [...],
 nosql_tables: [...],
 secrets: [...],
 git_access_token: "" // fetched at runtime, not stored
}

 
The git access token is intentionally left empty in the snapshot. The Runner fetches it at runtime via `POST /api/jobs/{id}/git-token` to avoid storing secrets in the job record.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Stage 3: Config Snapshot → ProjectConfig
records.text:
The Runner claims the job and deserializes the JSON snapshot into a strongly-typed Go struct:

func snapshotToProjectConfig(snapshot map[string]any) (*types.ProjectConfig, error) {
 data, _ := json.Marshal(snapshot)
 var sc types.ProjectConfig
 json.Unmarshal(data, &sc)
 return &sc, nil
}

The `ProjectConfig` struct in `packages/core/types/project_config.go` mirrors the snapshot shape with typed fields: `Network`, `Cluster`, `DNS`, `Databases[]`, `Caches[]`, and so on.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Stage 4: ProjectConfig → terraform.tfvars.json
records.text:
Each cloud provider implements a `ProviderTfvars()` method that maps `ProjectConfig` fields to provider-specific OpenTofu variable names:

 ProjectConfig Field AWS GCP Azure 
 ----------------- ----- ----- ------- 
 `ProjectName` `project_name` `project_name` `project_name` 
 `Region` `region` `region` `location` 
 `CloudAccountID` `aws_account_id` `project_id` `subscription_id` 
 `Network.ProvisionNetwork` `provision_vpc` `provision_network` `provision_vnet` 
 `Network.CIDRBlock` `vpc_cidr` `network_cidr` `vnet_cidr` 
 `Cluster.ClusterVersion` `eks_cluster_version` `gke_cluster_version` `aks_cluster_version` 
 `Cluster.InstanceTypes` `eks_instance_types` `gke_instance_types` `aks_instance_types` 
 `Databases[]` `rds_config` `cloud_sql_engine`, ... `azure_db_engine`, ... 
 `Caches[]` `redis_instance_type` `memorystore_instance_type` `azure_cache_sku` 
 `Queues[]` + `Topics[]` `sqs_queues`, `sns_topics` `pubsub_topics` `service_bus_queues`, `service_bus_topics` 
 `NosqlTables[]` `ddb_table_configuration` `firestore_databases` `cosmos_db_collections` 
 `StorageBuckets[]` `bucket_configuration` `cloud_storage_buckets` `storage_containers` 

The result is a `map[string]interface{}` wri

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Template Selection
records.text: The Runner resolves the template directory based on `ProjectConfig.Provider` — one of `aws/`, `gcp/`, or `azure/` under the templates path.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Backend Configuration
records.text: OpenTofu state is stored in S3-compatible storage. The backend key follows the pattern `{project}-{env}-{region}/tofu.tfstate`. See [OpenTofu State](/concepts/terraform-state).

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Plan
records.text: `tofu plan` runs with the generated tfvars. For PLAN jobs, an Infracost analysis is also generated. The plan artifact is uploaded to S3-compatible storage.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Apply
records.text: For DEPLOY jobs, `tofu apply` runs the plan. If a `plan_job_id` is linked, the cached plan artifact is downloaded and applied directly.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Post-Apply
records.text:
After apply, the runner extracts `tofu` outputs (cluster endpoint, database endpoints, ARNs), writes an exec-plugin kubeconfig in-process (CLI-free — see [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd)), installs ArgoCD, and renders application templates (per cloud — see [Multi-cloud](/elench/multi-cloud)). It then **generates app manifests** for the detected services and scaffolds them into the GitOps repo — only when that repo has no manifests yet, so a bring-your-own repo is never overwritten. See [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd).

Between `plan` and `apply` the [elench verification gate](/elench/engine-internals) runs (fail-closed) and seals a signed receipt.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Plan Hash Validation
records.text: When a DEPLOY job references a prior PLAN job, the platform validates that the configuration hasn't changed between plan and apply. If the `config_snapshot` hash differs, the deploy fails with a hash mismatch error and the user must re-plan. This prevents applying a stale plan against a modified Project.

records.title: Provisioning Pipeline
records.heading: Key Implementation Files
records.text:
Component Location 
 ----------- ---------- 
 Config snapshot builder `apps/console/app/server/actions/projects.ts` — `buildConfigSnapshot()` 
 ProjectConfig struct `packages/core/types/project_config.go` 
 AWS tfvars mapping `packages/core/cloud/aws_provider.go` — `ProviderTfvars()` 
 GCP tfvars mapping `packages/core/cloud/gcp_provider.go` — `ProviderTfvars()` 
 Azure tfvars mapping `packages/core/cloud/azure_provider.go` — `ProviderTfvars()` 
 Deploy orchestration `packages/core/provisioner/deploy.go` — `RunDeployV2()` 
 Snapshot deserialization `apps/runner/internal/agent/runner.go` — `snapshotToProjectConfig()` 
 OpenTofu CLI wrapper `packages/core/tofu/tofu.go`

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.text: Real-time Architecture. How Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY fanned out over SSE powers live log streaming, job status updates, and store synchronization.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Real-time Architecture
records.text: The platform uses **Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY fanned out to the browser over SSE** (Server-Sent Events) to push live updates. Three features depend on this: log streaming, job status updates, and Runner status tracking.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: How LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE Works
records.text:
When a row changes, a Postgres trigger emits a `NOTIFY` on a named channel with a small JSON payload. The console holds a single `LISTEN` connection per app instance and receives every notification. It fans those notifications out to connected browsers over an SSE stream, filtering each event to the subscriber that should see it.

 L["Console LISTEN (1 per instance)"]
 L --> S["SSE stream"]
 S --> B["Browser"]
`} />

The implementation lives in `apps/console/lib/realtime/index.ts`. A single LISTEN connection avoids one connection per browser tab. For multi-instance scale-out, an optional Redis transport relays notifications between console instances (enterprise).

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Log Streaming
records.text: The most latency-sensitive use case. When a runner executes `tofu`, stdout/stderr are streamed to the browser in near real-time.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Runner writes logs
records.text: The `JobLogger` buffers `tofu` output and flushes every 2 seconds (or when the buffer exceeds 10KB) via `POST /api/jobs/{id}/logs`.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Console inserts into database
records.text: The API route inserts the log chunk into `job_logs` with a stream type (STDOUT, STDERR, or SYSTEM).

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Postgres emits NOTIFY
records.text: An `AFTER INSERT` trigger on `job_logs` fires `NOTIFY` on the log channel. The console's LISTEN connection receives it immediately.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Browser receives chunk
records.text: The job detail page holds an SSE connection scoped to its `job_id`. The console relays the matching notification down the stream and the chunk appears in the log viewer within milliseconds.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Client Subscription

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: End-to-End Latency
records.text:
Step Latency 
 ------ --------- 
 `tofu` → JobLogger buffer ≤ 2 seconds 
 POST to Console API ~50ms 
 Database INSERT ~5ms 
 NOTIFY → SSE delivery ~50ms 
 **Total** **~2–3 seconds**

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Job Status Updates
records.text: The dashboard layout opens an SSE stream for job changes to update the jobs store in real-time.

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Subscription (Dashboard Layout)

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: What triggers updates:
records.text:
- Job created (INSERT) → new row appears in job list
- Runner claims job (UPDATE: QUEUED → CLAIMED)
- Execution starts (UPDATE: CLAIMED → PROCESSING)
- Job completes (UPDATE: PROCESSING → SUCCESS/FAILED)

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Runner Status Tracking

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Environment Status Updates

records.title: Real-time Architecture
records.heading: Store Synchronization Pattern
records.text:
All Zustand stores follow the same pattern for real-time updates:

1. **Initial fetch** on page mount (with 30-second stale threshold to avoid redundant API calls)
2. **SSE subscription** on dashboard layout mount
3. **Optimistic updates** via `addOrUpdate` methods that upsert by ID
4. **Session persistence** for filters and pagination state (via `sessionStorage`)

This means the UI stays current without polling. Job status changes, Runner heartbeats, and Project provisioning progress all appear instantly.

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.text: Build, Release & CI/CD. release-please versioning, GoReleaser for alethia, Docker builds for Runner, and GitHub Actions workflows.

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.text: alethia CLI and Runner (agent) are versioned and released independently using **release-please** with conventional commits. Each component has its own release workflow, tag format, and distribution channel.

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: release-please Configuration

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: alethia (CLI) Release Pipeline
records.text:
**Trigger:** `release-please` cuts a `cli-v*` tag; `release-please.yml` then calls the reusable workflow.

**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/release-cli.yml`

 Setup["Checkout + Go setup"]
 Setup --> GR["GoReleaser (-f apps/cli/.goreleaser.yml, --skip=validate)"]
 GR --> Build["Build: darwin + linux + windows × amd64/arm64"]
 GR --> Arch["Archive: tar.gz / zip + checksums"]
 GR --> Nfpm["nfpm: .deb / .rpm / .apk"]
 GR --> Rel["GitHub Release: archives + packages + checksums"]
 GR --> Docker["Docker: ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/alethia (amd64+arm64)"]
 GR --> Brew["Homebrew: Formula/alethia.rb → homebrew-tap"]
 GR --> Scoop["Scoop: alethia.json → scoop-bucket"]
`} />

**GoReleaser config** (`apps/cli/.goreleaser.yml`):
- Builds binaries across macOS/Linux/Windows × amd64/arm64 (`project_name: alethia`)
- Creates a GitHub Release with archives, `.deb`/`.rpm`/`.apk` packages, and checksums
- Publishes/updates the Homebrew formula and Scoop manifest automatically

**Distribution channels:**
- `brew install alethialabs-io/tap/alethia` (macOS/Linux via Homebrew) — or `brew tap alethialabs-io/tap && brew install alethia`
- `scoop bucket add alethia https://github.com/alethialabs-io/scoop-bucket

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: CLI mirror (alethialabs-io/alethia)
records.text:
`.github/workflows/mirror-cli.yml` publishes a **read-only mirror** of the CLI — `apps/cli` plus its
shared `packages/core`, copied verbatim (the relative layout keeps `apps/cli/go.mod`'s `replace`
valid, so it compiles standalone; the workflow runs `go build` as a gate before pushing) — to the
public `alethialabs-io/alethia` repo on every `main` push and `cli-v*` tag. It's a browsable front
door + focused issue target: **releases stay on the monorepo**, and PRs opened on the mirror are
directed back here (`.github/mirror/` seeds its README + CONTRIBUTING). It uses the same GitHub App.

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: Go-live checklist (maintainer)
records.text:
Everything is wired in the repo; three one-time steps make the install channels live.
The tap/bucket repos (`alethialabs-io/homebrew-tap`, `alethialabs-io/scoop-bucket`) already
exist, and the source repo is already **public** (so anonymous brew/scoop/curl/docker
downloads work the moment a release exists).

1. **Publish credentials (GitHub App).** The cross-repo publishes (formula, scoop manifest,
 CLI mirror) authenticate as a **GitHub App** — CI mints a short-lived token per run via
 `create-github-app-token`, scoped to just the target repo. The App's id + private key live
 in the `TAP_APP_ID` / `TAP_APP_PRIVATE_KEY` repo secrets.

 *This is already provisioned for the hosted alethialabs.io release.* Run the steps below only to
 set up a fresh pipeline (e.g. a fork), or to rotate the key:

 **Easy path** — run the helper once (you must be an `alethialabs-io` org owner, logged into `gh`):
 bash scripts/setup-release-github-app.sh
 It creates the App with the right permissions (**Contents: Read and write**, no webhook) via
 GitHub's manifest flow and sets both secrets automatically. Then click the printed **Install**
 link → select `homebrew-tap`, `scoop-bucket`, `alethia`.

 **Ma

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: Runner Release Pipeline
records.text:
**Trigger:** Push to tag matching `runner-v*`

**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/release-runner.yml`

 CO["Checkout"]
 CO --> Stage["Stage templates → build context (project/{aws,gcp,azure,…} + runner + argocd)"]
 Stage --> BX["Docker buildx (multi-arch)"]
 BX --> ECR["Push to AWS ECR"]
 BX --> GHCR["Push to GHCR (ghcr.io)"]
 ECR --> Meta["POST /api/releases/runner { version, release_notes }"]
 GHCR --> Meta
 Meta --> Roll["ECS roll: aws ecs update-service --force-new-deployment"]
`} />

 
 The **ECS roll** at the end of this workflow updates the **self-deployed / release** runner service
 — it is *not* how the managed fleet runs. Managed capacity comes from the in-app scaler booting
 Hetzner VMs from the same GHCR image (see [Runner Scaling](/concepts/runner-scaling)). Publishing a
 runner release makes the image available to both paths; only the self-deployed ECS service is rolled
 here.
 

**Key details:**
- **Template staging:** infrastructure templates are copied into the Docker build context so the final image carries all OpenTofu modules (AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, Hetzner) plus the runner + ArgoCD templates
- **Dual registry:** ECR for the ECS self-deployed path, GHCR for the

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: Runner Dev Deployment
records.text:
**Trigger:** Manual (`workflow_dispatch`)

**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/deploy-runner.yml`

For development/hotfix deployments without a version tag. Builds and deploys using the git SHA as the image tag instead of a semver version. Same Docker build and ECS deployment steps as the release workflow.
