records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: release-please Automation
records.text:
**Workflow:** `.github/workflows/release-please.yml`

**Trigger:** Push to default branch

Runs `googleapis/release-please-action` which:
1. Reads `release-please-config.json` and `.release-please-manifest.json`
2. Analyzes conventional commits since the last release per component
3. Creates/updates release PRs with changelogs
4. On PR merge, creates git tags that trigger release workflows

**Manifest** (`.release-please-manifest.json`) tracks current versions:

{
 "apps/cli": "0.1.7",
 "apps/runner": "0.0.0"
}

records.title: Build, Release & CI/CD
records.heading: Version Tracking in the Platform
records.text:
When a Runner release is published, the Console API receives the version and release notes via `POST /api/releases/runner`. This creates a `runner_releases` record. Registered Runners reference their `release_id` so the platform can:

- Display current version per Runner in the UI
- Show release notes and changelogs
- Detect outdated Runners

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.text: Runner Scaling. The in-app scaler that sizes per-provider warm pools by queue depth and converges them onto cheap Hetzner VMs.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Runner Scaling
records.text:
Managed runner capacity is driven by an **in-app scaler** — a control loop that lives inside the
Console process (`apps/console/lib/fleet/`), not a separate AWS service. A 60-second tick, plus an
on-demand wake whenever a job is enqueued, sizes a per-provider **warm pool** by queue depth and
converges it through a `FleetProvider`. The default provider is **Hetzner Cloud**
(`FLEET_PROVIDER=hcloud`): cheap VMs that boot a per-cloud runner image from GHCR and self-register.

 

 
 The legacy managed fleet — AWS ECS Fargate services driven by a Lambda scaler behind a Function
 URL, with an EventBridge fallback tick — is **retired** (`infra/fleet-aws` is gone). If you see
 those terms in old material, they no longer describe the system. The ECS runner template under
 `infra/templates/runner/aws/` still exists, but only for the **self-deployed** runner path — it is
 not the managed fleet.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: The control loop
records.text:
`apps/console/lib/fleet/` splits into a pure planner and an impure driver:

 File Role 
 --- --- 
 `scaler.ts` Hosts the 60s tick (`TICK_INTERVAL_MS = 60_000`) and the fire-on-enqueue `wakeFleetScaler`. Serializes passes per process so two overlapping ticks can't over-provision. 
 `plan.ts` **Pure** `plan(project, observed)` — diffs a pool target against observed reality and returns the minimal, safe create/drain/destroy actions. Reconciles four axes in priority order: **health → count → placement → version**. 
 `controller.ts` Applies the planned actions idempotently through the provider and records each to the `fleet_actions` ledger. 
 `hcloud.ts` The Hetzner `FleetProvider` — `list`/`create`/`destroy` over the Hetzner REST API (`fetch`, no SDK). 
 `config.ts` Parses `FLEET_POOLS` (a JSON array of pool targets). Unset or invalid → `[]` → the controller is a **no-op**. 

 wakeFleetScaler Tick
 Timer["60s tick"] --> Tick["scaler.ts (serialized pass)"]
 Tick --> Plan["plan.ts — pure diff health → count → placement → version"]
 Plan --> Ctl["controller.ts — apply actions"]
 Ctl --> create / drain / destroy Prov["Hetzner FleetProvider"]
 Ctl --> Ledger["fleet_actions ledger"]
 Prov --

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Sizing: warm pools, not scale-to-zero
records.text:
Each pool target (a `FLEET_POOLS` entry) is a small set of knobs (`config.ts`):

 Knob Default Meaning 
 --- --- --- 
 `warmMin` `1` The warm floor — healthy runners kept ready even at zero backlog. 
 `max` `10` Hard ceiling on healthy instances. 
 `buffer` `1` N+1 headroom added on top of demand. 
 `slotsPerRunner` `1` Concurrent jobs one runner claims. 
 `surge` `1` Max new VMs created per tick (bounded ramp). 
 `scaleDownGraceTicks` `5` Consecutive idle-surplus ticks before a runner is drained. 
 `locations` `["fsn1"]` Hetzner locations to spread across. 

The desired healthy count each tick is:

targetCount = clamp( max(warmMin, demand + buffer), 0, max )
demand = max(recentPeak, ceil(backlog / slotsPerRunner))

So a pool holds `warmMin` runners warm, lifts toward `demand + buffer` as the queue grows, and never
plans the claimable (online) count below the warm floor. A non-zero `warmMin` trades a little idle
cost for **zero cold-start latency** on the common case; set `warmMin: 0` to allow scale-to-zero for
a pool that tolerates boot latency.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: VM lifecycle: cloud-init self-registration
records.text:
When the planner calls for a new instance, the Hetzner provider creates a VM whose **cloud-init**
runs the per-cloud runner image from GHCR (`ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner- `). The image
self-registers rather than being pre-provisioned with credentials:

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Create
records.text:
`controller.ts` calls `hcloud.create()`; a VM boots with cloud-init carrying an
`ALETHIA_RUNNER_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` — a **short-TTL, instance-bound** token, not a shared fleet secret.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Self-register
records.text:
The runner binary exchanges the bootstrap token for its identity via `POST /api/runners/register`,
receiving its own `ALETHIA_RUNNER_ID` / `ALETHIA_RUNNER_TOKEN`.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Claim & work
records.text:
The runner enters its poll loop (`POST /api/jobs/claim`, ~10s) and starts claiming jobs for the
clouds it serves. See [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Drain & destroy
records.text:
When backlog falls and a runner has been surplus for `scaleDownGraceTicks` consecutive ticks, the
planner emits a `drain` (finish the current job, accept no new ones) then a `destroy`. The VM is
deleted; billing stops.

 

Every action the controller takes is written to the **`fleet_actions`** ledger
(`create` \ `drain` \ `destroy` \ `noop`) so there is a durable record of *why* the scaler
changed the pool.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Concurrency ceiling
records.text:
`scheduleTick()` serializes passes **within a process** — a wake arriving mid-pass coalesces into a
single follow-up so a wake-storm can't fan out into N parallel `read → plan → act` passes (each
would read the same stale instance list and over-provision). This is **not** cross-replica: the
serializer state is per-process, so more than one replica could still run concurrent passes against
the same pool. Production runs a single box today, so this is not live exposure; true multi-replica
safety would need a Postgres advisory lock around `reconcilePool`.

records.title: Runner Scaling
records.heading: Heartbeats & liveness
records.text:
While running, a runner heartbeats every **30 seconds** (`POST /api/runners/heartbeat`), carrying
its binary version and resetting its status to `ONLINE`. If heartbeats stop for 60 seconds the
Console marks the runner `OFFLINE`; jobs stuck in `PROCESSING` with no log activity are swept to
`FAILED` — see [Failure Recovery](/concepts/job-queue#failure-recovery).

The runner is **CLI-free**: its images bundle `tofu`, `kubectl`, and `helm` but **not** aws-cli /
gcloud / azure-cli. Cluster tokens are minted in-process via `runner kube-token`, and cloud auth is
keyless OIDC — so the images stay small and hold no static cloud credentials. See
[Security](/concepts/security#execution-isolation-managed-untrusted-byo).

records.title: Security Architecture
records.text: Security Architecture. Zero-credential model, cloud provider authentication, CLI auth, and data isolation.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Security Architecture
records.text: The platform's security model is built on one principle: **never store static cloud credentials**. Every cloud access is temporary, scoped, and revocable.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Zero-Credential Model
records.text:
Traditional infrastructure tools require users to provide long-lived access keys (AWS Access Key ID, GCP service account JSON, Azure client secret). These keys are stored in configuration files, environment variables, or vault systems — creating a persistent attack surface.

Console takes a different approach. The platform never stores cloud credentials. Instead, the control plane is **its own OIDC identity provider** (see [the Alethia OIDC issuer](#the-alethia-oidc-issuer-workload-identity) below): it mints short-lived, audience-scoped assertions that each cloud federates against. **All four managed clouds are keyless** — no access key, service-account JSON, or client secret anywhere:

 Provider Mechanism Session 
 ---------- ----------- --------- 
 AWS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` directly into the customer role (IAM OIDC provider — no platform account, no ExternalId) ~1 hour, auto-refreshed 
 GCP Workload Identity Federation (OIDC token exchange) ~1 hour, auto-refreshed 
 Azure Federated Identity via Entra ID (client assertion / `ARM_OIDC_TOKEN`) ~1 hour, auto-refreshed 
 Alibaba `AssumeRoleWithOIDC` (anonymous — no AccessKey, no Alethia Alibaba account) ~1 hour, auto-refreshed

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: The Alethia OIDC issuer (workload identity)
records.text:
Rather than assume into each cloud with a static platform key, the control plane runs a minimal,
single-purpose **workload-identity OIDC issuer** at `${APP_URL}/api/oidc` (`lib/oidc/issuer.ts`). It is
the trust root every managed cloud federates against:

- **Mint.** `mintWorkloadToken({ audience })` signs a short-lived (≤10 min) RS256 assertion with a fixed
 subject (`alethia-connector`) and the cloud's expected **audience** (e.g. `api://AzureADTokenExchange`,
 `sts.amazonaws.com`, `sts.aliyuncs.com`). The audience scopes the token so it can't be replayed at a
 different cloud.
- **Publish.** The public half is served as a JWKS at `${APP_URL}/api/oidc/jwks` (plus an OIDC discovery
 document). The customer's cloud fetches it to verify signatures — the private key never leaves the vault.
- **Verify.** Each cloud validates the assertion (issuer + subject + audience + signature) and exchanges it
 for its own ~1 hour credential. A successful exchange **is** the proof of access.

Because the exchanged credential is short-lived, the runner keeps a live assertion available for the whole
job — it re-mints into a token **file** (or self-refreshing recipe) that the provider re-reads, so a
`t

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
The user deploys a CloudFormation template (or OpenTofu module) in their AWS account. This creates:

1. **An IAM OIDC provider** for the Alethia issuer (`${APP_URL}/api/oidc`) — the account's trust root for Alethia
2. **`AlethiaProvisionerRole`** — an IAM role whose trust policy allows **only** `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` from that provider, pinned to the fixed audience (`sts.amazonaws.com`) and subject (`alethia-connector`)

There is **no ExternalId and no trust in an Alethia AWS account** — the cryptographic sub/aud pin replaces the
confused-deputy shared secret entirely, and no other identity (not even a different Alethia token) can assume
the role.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Runtime Flow
records.text:
The control plane (for server-side connection health / inventory) and managed runners (for provisioning)
federate **directly** into the customer role with a minted assertion — a single hop, no platform account:

 >STS: AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity(customer RoleArn, OIDC assertion) — no access key
 STS-->>R: Temporary customer credentials (~1h)
 Note over R: tofu runs; the assertion auto-refreshes from the re-read token file
`} />

A managed runner has no ambient AWS identity, so it writes the assertion to a web-identity token file and an
AWS profile (`AWS_CONFIG_FILE`/`AWS_PROFILE`); the SDK re-reads the file and re-assumes on expiry, so an apply
past the ~1 hour session survives. (Self-operated runners run in the customer's own cloud with their own
credentials and assume the customer role directly.)

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Permissions scope
records.text:
The OIDC trust above controls *who* may assume the role. This section is *what* the role is allowed to do —
a separate, independent axis.

**Exactly what Alethia provisions in AWS** (every service the project templates can touch — feature-gated,
so a given Project uses a subset): EC2/VPC (subnets, NAT, route tables, security groups, VPC endpoints),
EKS (cluster, managed node groups, add-ons, IRSA OIDC provider + roles), RDS (Aurora), ElastiCache
(Redis/Valkey), Route 53, ACM, Secrets Manager, DynamoDB, SQS/SNS, ECR, S3, KMS (envelope keys), CloudWatch
Logs + Kinesis Firehose (log delivery), EventBridge (Karpenter interruption), Application Auto Scaling, and
the IAM roles for the in-cluster controllers (external-dns, the AWS Load Balancer Controller, EBS CSI,
Karpenter, …).

 
**Least-privilege by default — no account admin.** The setup attaches **four scoped policies** instead of
`AdministratorAccess`: Compute / Data / Edge (each limited to exactly the services above) and an
action-enumerated IAM policy that lets the templates create the roles they need but **explicitly denies**
attaching admin-grade managed policies (`AdministratorAccess`/`PowerUserAccess`/`IAMFullAccess`), scopes

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
The user configures a Workload Identity Pool and Provider in their GCP project:

1. Creates a Workload Identity Pool
2. Adds an OIDC Provider with the Console issuer URL and audience
3. Binds a GCP service account to the pool with `roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser`

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Runtime Flow
records.text:
>STS: Present minted OIDC token (Console issuer)
 STS-->>R: Short-lived GCP access token (~1h)
 Note over R: tofu runs with the exchanged credentials
`} />

No service account JSON key files are created or stored. The OIDC token exchange happens at runtime for each job.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Permissions scope
records.text:
**Exactly what Alethia provisions in GCP:** Compute/VPC (networks, subnets, Cloud Router + NAT, firewall
rules, Cloud Armor), GKE (cluster + node pools), Cloud SQL, Memorystore (Redis), Cloud DNS, Artifact
Registry, Secret Manager, Cloud Storage, Firestore, Pub/Sub, and the workload-identity service accounts +
bindings for in-cluster controllers.

 
**Least-privilege by default — no `roles/editor`.** The service account is granted the **minimal set of
predefined `*.admin` roles** for exactly the services above (container / compute.network / compute.security /
servicenetworking / cloudsql / redis / dns / artifactregistry / secretmanager / storage / datastore /
pubsub, plus `iam.serviceAccountAdmin`+`User` for the add-on identities). The templates were **refactored to
resource-scoped IAM** — external-dns gets `dns.admin` on the project's managed zone (not project-wide), and a
legacy project-level workload-identity binding was removed — so the provisioner needs **no project
`setIamPolicy`**, closing the owner-equivalent escalation path. The one remaining residual,
`iam.serviceAccountAdmin`, is escalation-adjacent (setIamPolicy on SAs) and is a candidate for a
resource-conditioned cust

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Azure: Federated Identity
records.text:
Azure is keyless with **no per-customer app registration**. Alethia registers **one multi-tenant Entra
app** (in Alethia's own tenant) with a federated-identity credential trusting the Alethia OIDC issuer
(subject `alethia-connector`, audience `api://AzureADTokenExchange`) — and **no client secret**.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
The customer runs the connect script, which creates only a **service principal for Alethia's app** in
their tenant and grants it Contributor on the target subscription. They register no app of their own and
store nothing:

1. `az ad sp create --id ` — a service principal for the platform app in their tenant
2. `az role assignment create` — grants it the appropriate RBAC role on the subscription

(Operator setup of the multi-tenant app is documented in
[Managed cloud connectors](/self-hosting/managed-cloud-connectors).)

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Runtime Flow
records.text:
>AZ: Present minted OIDC assertion as a client assertion (ARM_OIDC_TOKEN)
 AZ-->>R: Access token for Azure Resource Manager (~1h)
 Note over R: tofu runs; the runner re-mints into the token file so long applies refresh
`} />

No client secret is ever generated or stored. Access revocation is instant by deleting the service
principal in the customer tenant.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Permissions scope
records.text:
**Exactly what Alethia provisions in Azure:** Resource Group, AKS (+ node pools), VNet (subnets, NSGs, NAT
gateway, public IPs), PostgreSQL Flexible Server (+ private DNS zone), Redis Cache, DNS zones, Container
Registry, Key Vault (+ secrets), Storage, Service Bus, Cosmos DB, WAF policy, and the managed identities +
role assignments for in-cluster workload identity.

 
**Least-privilege by default — a custom role, not `Contributor`.** The app's service principal is granted a
**custom role definition** ("Alethia Provisioner") enumerating only the actions the services above need —
including the AKS credential-list and per-resource-provider registration that `Contributor` omits (so this
also *fixes* a gap: pure `Contributor` cannot create the external-dns role assignment the templates require).
The one escalation-grade permission it needs — `roleAssignments/write` — is **constrained by an ABAC
condition** to a single built-in role (*DNS Zone Contributor*), so the connector can grant that to the
external-dns identity and **nothing else** (no path to Owner). See the
[Azure connector guide](/console/connectors/azure).

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Alibaba: RAM Role via OIDC (account-free)
records.text:
Alibaba is keyless **and account-free** — Alethia holds no Alibaba account and no AccessKey. The customer
applies an OpenTofu module that creates a **RAM OIDC provider** (fixed name `alethia`) trusting the Alethia
issuer plus a **RAM role**, then pastes the role ARN (Alethia derives the provider ARN from it).

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Runtime Flow
records.text:
>STS: AssumeRoleWithOIDC(RoleArn, OIDCProviderArn, minted token) — anonymous, no AccessKey
 STS-->>R: Temporary STS credentials (~1h)
 Note over R: tofu runs; the runner re-mints into the token file so long applies refresh
`} />

`AssumeRoleWithOIDC` is an anonymous STS action — the OIDC token itself authenticates the call, so there is
no AccessKey and no request signature. On the runner, the `alicloud` provider performs the exchange itself
from `ALIBABA_CLOUD_OIDC_TOKEN_FILE` (re-read on refresh). Revocation is instant by deleting the RAM role.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Permissions scope
records.text:
**Exactly what Alethia provisions in Alibaba Cloud:** ACK (managed cluster + node pool), VPC (vSwitches, NAT,
SNAT), EIP, RDS, Container Registry EE, KVStore (Redis), AliDNS, KMS, Message Service (MNS), OSS, Tablestore
(OTS), and WAF.

 
**Least-privilege by attached policy — never account admin.** The Alibaba RAM role attaches **one per-service
system policy for each service Alethia provisions** (`AliyunCS/VPC/ECS/SLB/EIP FullAccess` for the core ACK
path, plus RDS/ContainerRegistry/Kvstore/DNS/KMS/MNS/OSS/OTS/WAF for the optional modules) — never
`AliyunRAMFullAccess` or an admin policy. The service-linked roles ACK/NAT need are covered by a **narrow
custom policy granting only `ram:CreateServiceLinkedRole`** (service-linked roles are cloud-managed with
fixed permissions, so this can't escalate). See the [Alibaba connector guide](/console/connectors/alibaba).

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Key rotation
records.text:
The issuer's signing key is the trust root for all four clouds, so it rotates with **zero downtime** via an
overlap: the JWKS can publish two keys at once, and each assertion's `kid` header selects the right one.

1. `scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh` mints a new primary key and parks the outgoing key in a published-but-not-
 signing slot (`ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY_PREVIOUS`); redeploy. New assertions are signed by the new key;
 in-flight assertions signed by the old key still verify (both are published).
2. Wait ≥24 h — long enough for every cloud's cached JWKS to refresh and all ≤10-min old assertions to
 expire.
3. `scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh --finalize` drops the old key; redeploy.

See the [rotation runbook](/self-hosting/managed-cloud-connectors#rotating-the-issuer-signing-key).

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Execution isolation (managed untrusted BYO)
records.text:
Keyless auth controls *who* can touch a cloud; **execution isolation** controls what the *provisioning code
itself* can do. In **managed** mode Alethia runs the provisioning fleet, so the trust question is: whose code
runs on it?

- **Trusted templates** — Alethia's own project templates (EKS/GKE/AKS/ACK/Talos + supporting services) run
 in-process on the runner. They are reviewed platform code, so this is safe.
- **Untrusted bring-your-own** — customer OpenTofu / Helm (a provider, an `external` data source, a chart
 hook) is arbitrary code. It runs inside a **per-job container sandbox**: a fresh container per job with its
 own PID namespace, an **allowlisted environment** (no runner token, no storage key, no signing key — only
 the short-lived, job-scoped cloud credentials it needs), read-only credential mounts, and a **default-deny,
 domain-allowlisted network**. A fail-closed guard refuses to start the container if any platform secret
 would leak into it.

Three properties keep the blast radius of a single job to that one job:

- **No storage master key on the runner.** OpenTofu state goes through a Console **state proxy**
 (`backend "http"` → `/api/jobs/[id]/state`) authenticat

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: CLI Authentication (RFC 8628)
records.text:
The alethia CLI uses the **Device Authorization Grant** flow (RFC 8628), the same pattern used by GitHub CLI, Azure CLI, and AWS SSO:

 >CLI: generate device_code
 CLI->>B: open {console}/cli/login?device_code=...
 B->>B: user signs in, approves the device_code
 CLI->>API: poll POST /api/auth/cli/exchange (device_code)
 API-->>CLI: 404 while pending, then JWT pair once approved
 CLI->>CLI: store ~/.config/alethia/credentials.json
`} />

The user never types a password in the terminal. The refresh token is stored locally and used to obtain short-lived access tokens for API calls. Automatic refresh happens transparently via `POST /api/auth/cli/refresh`.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Web Authentication
records.text:
The web application uses **Better Auth** with OAuth providers (GitHub and Google native; GitLab and Bitbucket via genericOAuth) plus email sign-in via 6-digit OTP:

- GitHub
- GitLab
- Bitbucket
- Google

The user clicks a provider button, is redirected to the OAuth flow, and returns with a session JWT stored in an HTTP-only cookie. Sessions are managed entirely by Better Auth.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Row Level Security (RLS)
records.text:
Every org-scoped table in the database has a coarse Row Level Security policy keyed on `org_id`. The current org is set per request via `set_config('app.current_org', …)`, driven by a Policy Decision Point (PDP) in the app:

CREATE POLICY "Org members can only access own org data"
 ON projects FOR ALL
 USING (org_id = current_setting('app.current_org')::uuid);

This means even if there is a bug in application code that constructs a query without a `WHERE org_id = ?` clause, PostgreSQL will filter the results to only the current org's data. A raw `SELECT * FROM projects` returns only the current org's Projects.

RLS is the coarse, unbypassable backstop; fine-grained role/permission decisions are made by the PDP. See [Access Control](/access-control) for the full authorization model (roles, permissions, grants, and the org/tenancy model).

Cloud identities are additionally filtered by provider — an AWS identity will not appear in a GCP context.

records.title: Security Architecture
records.heading: Git Token Management
records.text:
OAuth tokens for Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) are stored on the Better Auth `account` table:

- Better Auth persists each linked provider's `accessToken` / `refreshToken` / expiry on `account` at login
- The `getValidProviderToken()` server action checks token expiry before use
- Expired tokens are automatically refreshed via the provider's refresh flow
- Access is scoped to the owning user (the `org_id` RLS backstop + the PDP)

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.text: OpenTofu State Management. How OpenTofu state is stored in S3-compatible object storage, organized by environment, and shared across runner executions.

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: OpenTofu State Management
records.text:
OpenTofu tracks the real-world resources it manages in a **state file**. Alethia never hands a runner an
object-storage key. Instead, **every** provisioning job reaches state through the Console **state proxy**
using OpenTofu's built-in `http` backend, and the Console persists the bytes to S3-compatible object
storage (SeaweedFS by default, swappable for Garage, MinIO, AWS S3, or R2). This lets runners share state
across executions and resume from failures while holding zero storage credentials.

 

 
 The state proxy is the **universal** path — trusted Alethia templates and managed **untrusted**
 bring-your-own code alike. The runner authenticates to `/api/jobs/{id}/state` with a per-job,
 key-scoped token minted at claim time (`stateBackend()` in `apps/runner/internal/agent/runner.go`),
 passed to `tofu` only via `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD` — never written to the workdir. See
 [Security → Execution isolation](/concepts/security#execution-isolation-managed-untrusted-byo).

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: State Backend
records.text:
The runner writes a `backend "http"` config (`WriteBackendHCL` in `packages/core/cloud/http_backend.go`)
and runs `tofu init -backend-config= `. The state, lock, and unlock endpoints all point at the
Console proxy for that job; the per-job token travels out-of-band in `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD` (App Router can't

hcl

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: backend.hcl — generated per job, consumed by tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
records.text:
address = "{console_url}/api/jobs/{job_id}/state"
lock_address = "{console_url}/api/jobs/{job_id}/state/lock"
unlock_address = "{console_url}/api/jobs/{job_id}/state/lock"

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: TF_HTTP_USERNAME=alethia, TF_HTTP_PASSWORD=<per-job state token>  (from the process env, not this file)
records.text:
Behind the proxy, the Console reads and writes each environment's state object in S3-compatible storage,
keyed as `{project}-{environment}-{region}/tofu.tfstate`. The S3 endpoint/bucket/credentials live
**server-side on the Console**, never on the runner.

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: Path Structure
records.text:
The state key is `{project}-{environment}-{region}/tofu.tfstate`, so **every environment** — not
just every project — gets its own state file:

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

This is why a project's `staging` and `production` are fully isolated: they are separate state files.
The key is derived from the environment's `name` (see [Domain Model](/concepts/domain-model#environments)).

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: During tofu plan
records.text:
1. The runner mints a per-job state token and writes `backend.hcl` pointing at the Console proxy
2. `tofu init` pulls the existing state (if any) from the proxy via the `http` backend
3. `tofu plan` compares the desired configuration against the current state
4. The plan output describes what will change

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: During tofu apply
records.text:
1. `tofu apply` executes the plan, taking a state lock via `POST` to the lock address
2. After each resource is created/updated/destroyed, OpenTofu writes the updated state back through the proxy
3. If the apply fails mid-way, the state reflects the partial progress

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: During tofu destroy
records.text:
1. `tofu destroy` removes all resources tracked in the state
2. The state file is left empty (or removed)

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: Why proxy the state?
records.text:
- **Zero storage credentials on the runner** — the runner never sees an S3 key; it holds only a per-job,
 key-scoped state token in `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`. This is what lets managed untrusted BYO code run safely.
- **Locked coordination** — the `http` backend takes a lock via the proxy's lock address (`POST`/`DELETE`),
 keeping concurrent runs on the same environment safe.
- **Consistent across runners** — every runner reads and writes the same state through the proxy, so
 executions share a single source of truth.
- **Portable storage** — behind the proxy, any S3-compatible backend works (SeaweedFS, Garage, MinIO, AWS
 S3, R2); the Console's endpoint is swappable, so you are never locked to one provider.
- **Cross-provider** — works regardless of whether the environment targets AWS, GCP, Azure, or Hetzner.

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: State and Failure Recovery
records.text:
Because state is persisted after every resource operation:

- If a runner dies mid-apply, the state reflects all resources created so far
- A retry (new job) runs `tofu init` → downloads the existing state → `tofu plan` only shows remaining resources
- No resources are double-created

This is why OpenTofu state is the foundation of the platform's reliability model.

records.title: OpenTofu State Management
records.heading: Plan Artifacts
records.text:
In addition to state, **plan artifacts** are stored in a separate S3-compatible bucket (`plan-artifacts`). These are the binary `plan.tfplan` files that capture exactly what OpenTofu will do.

When a user applies after reviewing a plan, the runner downloads the plan artifact and passes it to `tofu apply plan.tfplan`. This ensures the apply executes exactly what was planned, even if the configuration has changed since the plan was generated.

 
Plan artifacts are validated with a **config hash**. If the environment's configuration was modified after the plan was generated, the hash won't match and the apply will fail, forcing the user to re-plan. This prevents stale plans from being applied.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.text: Engineering Quality & Testing. How Alethia is tested — the Testing Trophy, automated coverage, and the CI gates that protect every release.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.heading: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.text:
Alethia provisions and manages production cloud infrastructure, so correctness is a feature.
This page describes how the platform is tested, how we measure it, and the gates that every
change passes before it ships — the same practices used by mature engineering organizations.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.heading: The Testing Trophy
records.text:
We test along four layers, weighted toward the ones that give the most confidence per test —
the **Testing Trophy** model (the modern successor to the test pyramid for full-stack apps
whose logic lives in server actions over a database):

 Layer What it protects Tooling 
 --- --- --- 
 **Static analysis** Type safety, lint rules, dead-code & boundary checks TypeScript (`tsc`), Biome / ESLint 
 **Unit** Pure business logic — billing math, entitlements, plan resolution, routing/slug rules, input validation Vitest 
 **Integration** (the bulk) Server actions and **real-database** queries, aggregations, and **row-level-security isolation** Vitest + Postgres 
 **End-to-end** Critical user journeys — sign-in, create an org/project, deploy an environment, usage, upgrade Playwright 

Integration is intentionally the widest band: a test that runs a real SQL aggregation or a
whole server action against a live database catches far more than a mocked unit test. The Go
components (CLI, Runner, shared `core` library) are covered by their own `go test` suites,
including HTTP-contract tests.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.heading: Coverage, measured in the open
records.text:
Every push runs the test suite with code-coverage instrumentation. CI merges the per-project
coverage and surfaces the number as a **badge on the repository** — coverage is a public signal,
not a private claim. The number is ratcheted upward over time as test suites land.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.heading: What CI enforces on every change
records.text:
Each pull request must pass, before it can merge:

- **Type-check + lint** across every app and package (fanned out automatically).
- **The unit, component, and mocked-action test suite**, with a coverage badge generated in CI.
- **Build smoke** of the console, marketing, and docs apps.
- **The Go build · vet · test · lint matrix** for the CLI, Runner, and `core`.
- **Guard checks** — open-core boundary, authorization-scope, database-migration linearity,
 and a **secret scan** (gitleaks).

A separate, database-backed **integration suite** verifies the SQL and row-level-security
isolation against a real Postgres instance.

records.title: Engineering Quality & Testing
records.heading: Why it matters for you
records.text:
- **Multi-tenant isolation is tested, not assumed** — row-level-security tests assert that one
 organization can never read another's data.
- **Billing and usage math is unit-tested** — runner-minutes, overage, AI credits, and plan
 entitlements are covered by deterministic tests.
- **Releases are gated** — nothing reaches `main` without passing the full suite above.

> Want the engineering detail? The contributor-facing `TESTING.md` in the repository documents
> the layers, how to run each suite, and how to write tests for each layer.

records.title: Alerts
records.text: Alerts. Route platform events to webhook, Slack, RocketChat, and email channels. Channels authenticate with a pasted incoming-webhook URL — no OAuth.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Alerts
records.text:
Alerting turns platform activity into notifications. A **policy** decides *what* fires, a **channel**
decides *where it goes*, and every routed event is recorded as a **delivery**. Find it under
**Dashboard → Alerts**, organised into **Policies · Channels · Activity**.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: How alerting works
records.text:
deploy fails · action denied · cloud-identity token breaks"] --> P["policies match event + conditions"]
 P --> C["route to channels"]
 C --> D["delivery attempted now, retried on failure, logged in Activity"]
`} />

- **Events** are emitted by the platform as things happen — you don't wire anything up.
- **Policies** watch a chosen set of events and route matches to channels.
- **Channels** are destinations (webhook, Slack, RocketChat, email).
- **Deliveries** are the audit trail: what fired, where it went, and whether it succeeded.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Policies
records.text:
A policy is a set of events plus the channels they route to. Under **Alerts → Policies**:

1. **Start from a template** — *Prod deploy failures*, *Security & policy denials*, *Access-changes audit*,
 *Cost guardrails*, *Worker health*, or **Blank** — then tune it. Templates pre-select sensible events.
2. **Pick events** from the catalog, grouped by category (see below). A policy can watch one event or many.
3. **Set conditions** — an optional **scope** filter, a **minimum severity**, and a **dedup/throttle
 window** (collapse repeats of the same event into one notification for N seconds).
4. **Route to channels** — toggle which channels this policy notifies; one policy can fan out to several,
 and one channel can serve many policies.
5. **Enable** it. Disabled policies keep their config but fire nothing.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: What you can alert on
records.text:
Events are grouped into categories. ~20 are **live** today; the rest are configurable now but inert until
their emitter ships (the UI tags these "soon").

 Category Example events Status 
 --- --- --- 
 **Deploy & drift** deploy started / succeeded / failed, destroy requested / completed Live · drift *soon* 
 **Policy (PDP)** action denied, sensitive action allowed, role permissions changed Live · policy-override *soon* 
 **Access & roles (RBAC)** grant added / revoked, role created / changed / deleted Live 
 **Members** member invited / joined / removed Live · suspended *soon* 
 **Cloud identities** identity connected / revoked, credential failed Live · rotation *soon* 
 **Cost** budget threshold, spend spike, overage started *Soon* 
 **Workers & runners** worker unhealthy (runner offline) Live · stalled / exhausted *soon* 
 **Authentication** SSO sign-in failed, new-device sign-in, sign-in blocked *Soon*

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Free vs Business+
records.text:
Alerting itself is in the open-source core. The split is by **event kind**:

- **Operational events are free** — deploys, destroys, members, cloud-identity connect/revoke, connector
 health, and runner health. These work in the community build with no license.
- **Security & governance events require Business+** (the `advancedAlerting` entitlement) — PDP **policy
 denials / sensitive actions**, and **access-control changes** (grant added/revoked, role
 created/changed/deleted). Without the entitlement these patterns are visible but won't deliver.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Severity
records.text:
Every event carries a severity — **info**, **warning**, or **critical** — surfaced in the channel payload
(e.g. a Slack colour/emoji, or a field your webhook consumer can route on). A policy's **minimum severity**
condition drops anything below the chosen level.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Channels
records.text:
There is no Alethia-hosted Slack/Teams app and no OAuth. A chat channel is just an **incoming-webhook
 URL** you create in your own Slack or RocketChat workspace and paste in. Alethia signs (where applicable) and POSTs to that
 URL — nothing more.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Prerequisite: the encryption key
records.text:
Webhook, Slack, and RocketChat channels store a **URL secret** — and that URL *is* the credential (anyone
holding it can post to your destination), so Alethia encrypts it at rest. That requires an encryption key:

bash

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: 32-byte base64 key — set once, keep it stable across restarts
records.text:
openssl rand -base64 32

Set it in your environment and restart:

ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY= 

Until it's set, secret-bearing channels are **disabled in the UI** (the channel dialog tells you so). The
same key protects `api_key` connector credentials, so a configured deployment already has it. **Email
channels need no key** — recipients aren't secret.

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Add a channel
records.text:
In the console, open **Alerts → Channels → New channel**, pick a type, and follow the matching steps. Use
**Test** afterwards to send a synthetic event before any policy routes real alerts to it.

 
 
 The generic escape hatch — delivers to any HTTPS endpoint you control. No third-party account, so it's
 also the easiest way to test alerting end to end (point it at your own service, or a throwaway
 request-bin / `ngrok` URL).

 

 ### Endpoint URL
 Paste the HTTPS URL that should receive the POST. The body is JSON: `{ channel, event, sent_at }`.

 ### Signing secret (optional)
 If you set one, Alethia signs each request:

 X-Alethia-Signature: sha256=HMAC-SHA256(secret, body)
 
 Recompute the HMAC over the raw body on your side and compare — the outbound mirror of how Alethia
 verifies inbound Stripe webhooks.

 ### Test
 Click **Test**. Your endpoint should receive one POST with a `Test alert from Alethia` payload.

 
 

 
 Slack delivery uses an **Incoming Webhook** — a per-channel URL Slack issues. No bot token, no OAuth,
 no Alethia Slack app to install.

 

 ### Create the incoming webhook
 In Slack, add the **Incoming Webhooks** app (or create a Slack app and enable the *Inco

records.title: Alerts
records.heading: Activity
records.text:
Every routed event becomes a row under **Alerts → Activity**: the event, the channel, status, attempts, and
any error. Status moves **pending → sent**, or **failed → dead** once it exhausts its retries. A background
sweeper retries failed deliveries with backoff, so a briefly-unreachable endpoint recovers on its own — no
cron to configure. (A channel's **Test** button writes no Activity row; it just probes the destination.)

records.title: Auth APIs
records.text: Auth APIs. Device code flow endpoints for CLI authentication — generate, exchange, refresh.

records.title: Auth APIs
records.heading: Auth APIs
records.text: These endpoints implement the **Device Authorization Grant** (RFC 8628) for CLI authentication. See [Authentication Flows](/concepts/authentication) for the complete flow.

records.title: Auth APIs
records.heading: POST /api/auth/cli/generate
records.text:
Called by the **browser** (authenticated user) to approve a CLI login attempt.

**Requires:** Active browser session (Better Auth session cookie).

**Body:**
{
 "device_code": "uuid-from-cli"
}

**Effect:**
- Creates a `cli_logins` record with the device code, user profile ID, and a 6-character verification code
- The verification code is displayed in the browser for the user to type into the CLI

records.title: Auth APIs
records.heading: POST /api/auth/cli/exchange
records.text:
Called by the **CLI** to exchange the device code + verification code for tokens.

**No authentication required** (the device code + verification code pair is the proof).

**Body:**
{
 "device_code": "uuid-from-cli"
}

**Validation:**
- Looks up the `cli_logins` record by device code
- Verifies that `profile_id` is set (meaning the browser approved the login)

**Response:**
{
 "access_token": "jwt...",
 "refresh_token": "jwt...",
 "provider_token": "git-oauth-token",
 "user_email": "user@example.com"
}

**Token Details:**
- **Access token**: 1-hour TTL, HS256 signed with `CLI_JWT_SECRET`
- **Refresh token**: 90-day TTL, same signing

JWT payload:
{
 "sub": "user-uuid",
 "email": "user@example.com",
 "type": "access",
 "iss": "urn:example:issuer",
 "aud": "urn:example:audience"
}

records.title: Auth APIs
records.heading: POST /api/auth/cli/refresh

records.title: Auth APIs
records.heading: GET /api/auth/[...all]
records.text:
Handles the OAuth redirect, handled by Better Auth at `/api/auth/[...all]` after provider authentication. Captures the authorization code and provider token, then redirects to the intended page.

The middleware intercepts OAuth callbacks to save `provider_token` before the redirect reaches the final destination.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.text: CLI APIs. API endpoints used by the alethia CLI for project, job, and runner management.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: CLI APIs
records.text: These endpoints are called by the alethia CLI. Authentication is via `Authorization: Bearer {jwt}` header with a CLI-issued JWT. See [Authentication Flows](/concepts/authentication).

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/configurations
records.text: List all project configurations for the authenticated user.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/configurations/{name}
records.text: Get a configuration by name.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/configurations/by-project-name/{project_name}
records.text: Look up a project by name.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/cli/configurations
records.text: Create a new project configuration from CLI input.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/jobs
records.text:
Create a new job. Accepts:

{
 "job_type": "PLAN",
 "configuration_id": "project-uuid",
 "assigned_runner_id": "runner-uuid",
 "plan_job_id": "plan-job-uuid"
}

Valid job types: PLAN, DEPLOY, DESTROY, DESTROY_RUNNER.

If `configuration_id` is provided, the server auto-resolves `cloud_identity_id`, computes config hash, and creates a config snapshot.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/jobs
records.text: List all jobs for the authenticated user. Supports a `?status={status}` filter.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/jobs/{id}
records.text: Get job status and metadata.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/jobs/{id}/logs
records.text: Get job logs (same format as the Runner API).

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/cli/jobs/{id}/cancel
records.text: Cancel a QUEUED or PROCESSING job.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/runners
records.text: List all registered Runners.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/cli/runners/register
records.text: Register a self-operated Runner. Always creates `operator=self`, `provisioning=registered` (no `mode` body field).

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/cli/runners/deploy
records.text: Deploy a new self-operated Runner into the customer's cloud (`operator=self`, `provisioning=deployed`).

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: DELETE /api/cli/runners/{id}
records.text: Remove a Runner's database record.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/repositories/github
records.text: List GitHub repositories for the authenticated user.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/repositories/gitlab
records.text: List GitLab repositories.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: GET /api/cli/repositories/bitbucket
records.text: List Bitbucket repositories.

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/releases/runner

records.title: CLI APIs
records.heading: POST /api/runners/register
records.text: Register a Runner (CI/CD only, requires `RELEASE_API_SECRET`). Used by OpenTofu during Runner deployment; always creates an `operator=managed` runner (no `mode` body field).

records.title: API Overview
records.text: API Overview. Alethia API endpoints — authentication methods, base URLs, and route categories.

records.title: API Overview
records.heading: API Reference
records.text: Alethia exposes three categories of API routes, each with its own authentication mechanism.

records.title: API Overview
records.heading: Authentication Methods
records.text:
Category Auth Header Token Type Used By 
 ---------- ----------- ------------ --------- 
 **Runner APIs** `X-Runner-ID` + `X-Runner-Token` SHA-256 hashed token Runner agents 
 **CLI APIs** `Authorization: Bearer {jwt}` HS256 JWT (1h access, 90d refresh) alethia CLI 
 **Release APIs** `Authorization: Bearer {secret}` Static secret (`RELEASE_API_SECRET`) CI/CD pipeline 

See [Authentication Flows](/concepts/authentication) for details on each mechanism.

records.title: API Overview
records.heading: API Categories
records.text:
Job claiming, heartbeat, status updates, log streaming, plan artifacts.
 
 
 Configurations, jobs, runners, repositories.
 
 
 Device code flow: generate, exchange, refresh.

records.title: API Overview
records.heading: Request Format
records.text:
All APIs accept and return JSON. Errors return a consistent format:

{
 "error": "Human-readable error message"
}

HTTP status codes:
- `200` — success
- `201` — created
- `400` — validation error
- `401` — authentication failure
- `404` — not found
- `409` — conflict (e.g., duplicate release version)
- `500` — internal error

records.title: Runner APIs
records.text: Runner APIs. API endpoints used by Runner agents for job claiming, heartbeat, and log streaming.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: Runner APIs
records.text: These endpoints are called by Runner agents. Authentication is via `X-Runner-ID` and `X-Runner-Token` headers. The token is hashed with SHA-256 and compared against the stored `runners.token_hash`.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/jobs/claim

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/runners/heartbeat

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: PUT /api/jobs/{id}/status

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/jobs/{id}/logs
records.text:
Receives log chunks from the Runner. See [Real-time Architecture](/concepts/realtime) for how these are delivered to the browser.

**Body:**
{
 "log_chunk": "Creating VPC...\nVPC created: vpc-12345\n",
 "stream_type": "STDOUT"
}

Stream types: `STDOUT`, `STDERR`, `SYSTEM`.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: GET /api/jobs/{id}/logs
records.text: Retrieves all logs for a job, ordered by ID. Supports `?after={id}` pagination.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/jobs/{id}/plan-artifact
records.text: Uploads the binary `tofu.plan.out` file to S3-compatible storage (the `plan-artifacts` bucket, max 50MB).

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: GET /api/jobs/{id}/plan-artifact
records.text: Downloads the plan artifact for apply execution.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/runners/register

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/runners/heartbeat
records.text: Also used for initial heartbeat after registration.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: GET /api/runners/{id}
records.text: Returns runner metadata including deploy configuration.

records.title: Runner APIs
records.heading: POST /api/runners/{id}/metadata
records.text: Updates runner metadata (used for tracking deployment state).

records.title: Classification
records.text: Classification. Organize every resource with a governed taxonomy — named dimensions and their allowed values, assigned across projects, clusters, cloud identities, runners, members and more.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: Classification
records.text:
Classification is **governed, structured labeling** for everything in your organization — the
opposite of free-form tags. Instead of anyone typing `env=prod`, `Environment=Production`, or
`PROD` and drifting, an admin defines a fixed set of **axes** and their **allowed values** once, and
every resource is labeled from that closed vocabulary. Find it under **Settings → Classification**.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: The model
records.text:
Three concepts, in a strict hierarchy:

 an axis — 'Environment'"] --> V["Value an allowed answer — dev · staging · prod"] --> A["Assignment pinned to a resource — this project's Environment = prod"]
`} />

- **Dimension** — a named axis: one question you can ask about every resource (*Environment*,
 *Team*, *Data classification*, *Cost center*). It has a label, an immutable `key` slug, an
 optional description, and a **single/multi** mode.
- **Value** — an allowed answer on that axis (`dev` / `staging` / `prod`). A closed set the admin
 curates; each value has a label, a slug, and an optional color accent.
- **Assignment** — a value pinned to one concrete resource. This is what you set on a project, a
 cluster, a cloud identity, and so on.

Two mental models that make it click:

- **Spreadsheet** — dimensions are the **columns**, values are a column's allowed **dropdown
 entries**, and assignments are the **filled-in cells**.
- **Governed cloud tags** — like AWS/GCP resource tags, except the keys *and* the permitted values
 are curated, so there's no typo or casing drift and every resource speaks the same vocabulary.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: Single vs multi
records.text:
Every dimension is either **single-valued** or **multi-valued**, and it's the most important choice
you make when creating one:

 Mode Meaning Example 
 --- --- --- 
 **Single** A resource holds **at most one** value on this axis. Assigning a new value **replaces** the current one. *Environment* — a thing is exactly one of dev / staging / prod. 
 **Multi** A resource may hold **several** values on this axis. *Team* — a service can belong to platform *and* payments.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: Where you can apply it
records.text:
Classification is assignable across the main resource types. On each, you'll see the value **chips**
and — if you can edit — a **picker** to change them:

 Resource Where in the console 
 --- --- 
 Projects Project → **Settings → General** 
 Environments A project's **Environments** 
 Clusters **Clusters** 
 Cloud identities (& pluggable connector credentials) **Connectors** → a cloud identity's detail 
 Runners **Runners** 
 Members **Settings → Members** 
 Roles **Settings → Roles** 
 Alert policies **Alerts → Policies** 
 Alert channels **Alerts → Channels** 
 Support cases A support case's detail 

 
 A few internal record types (alert *deliveries* and runner *usage sessions*) aren't
 classifiable — classification applies to the things you manage, not to the audit trail they
 generate.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: What it's for today
records.text:
Once resources carry consistent labels, classification gives you:

- **Organization** — a single, drift-free vocabulary across every resource type.
- **Filter & group** — read the chips to slice your fleet ("show the `prod` resources", "everything
 owned by the `payments` team").
- **Usage insight** — the **Settings → Classification** page surfaces how the taxonomy is actually
 used: an assignment count per value, a coverage figure per dimension, a per-resource-kind
 breakdown, and which values are **unused** (safe to delete or repurpose).

records.title: Classification
records.heading: What it is *not* — yet
records.text:
**Classification does not enforce anything.** No policy, permission check, provisioning step, or
 the verification gate reads these labels to allow, deny, route, or require. Today classification is
 **organizational metadata and a governance foundation** — it describes your resources, it does not
 govern them. Rules built *on top of* classification (for example, "a `prod` resource must carry a
 data-classification", or a policy scoped to a dimension) are a future step, not current behavior.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: Authoring the taxonomy
records.text:
Under **Settings → Classification** (requires the **Editor** role — `org:edit`; everyone else sees a
read-only view):

1. **Create a dimension.** Give it a label; the `key` slug auto-fills from it. Add a description and
 choose **single** or **multi**. The `key` is **immutable** after creation (it's wire-stable), so
 pick it deliberately — the label and everything else stay editable. Starting fresh? The empty
 state offers **templates** (Environment, Data classification, Team, Cost center) you can drop in
 and then adjust.
2. **Add values.** Type a label and press Enter for a quick add, or open the editor to set the slug
 and a **color accent** (a swatch, a custom color, or none).
3. **Reorder** dimensions and values by dragging — the order is what the pickers show everywhere.
4. **Drill into usage** — click a value's count to see which resource kinds carry it.

 
 Deleting a **dimension** removes its values *and every assignment of those values across all
 resources*; deleting a **value** clears it from every resource it was on. Both are irreversible.

records.title: Classification
records.heading: Assigning values to resources
records.text:
On a supported resource, the classification control shows the current values as chips. The **picker**
to change them appears only when you can edit **that** resource *and* you hold `org:edit` — so you'll
never see a control you'd be denied on. For a single-valued dimension, picking a new value swaps out
the old one; for a multi-valued dimension, you add and remove values freely.

records.title: Clusters
records.text: Clusters. Viewing provisioned Kubernetes clusters with ArgoCD access and kubeconfig.

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: Clusters
records.text: The Clusters page (`/dashboard/clusters`) shows all provisioned Kubernetes clusters from active projects.

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: Cluster Cards
records.text:
Each cluster displays:

 Field Description 
 ------- ------------- 
 **Project Name** The project's name + "Active" badge 
 **Provider** AWS/GCP/Azure icon with region and environment 
 **K8s Version** Kubernetes version (e.g., 1.32) 
 **Cluster Endpoint** API server URL (copy button) 
 **kubeconfig Command** Provider-specific command to configure kubectl (copy button) 
 **ArgoCD URL** External link to the ArgoCD dashboard 
 **Services** Databases and caches with their endpoints (copy buttons)

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: kubeconfig Commands

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: Pre-Installed Components
records.text:
Every cluster comes with these components installed via ArgoCD (see [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd)):

 Component Purpose 
 ----------- --------- 
 **ArgoCD** GitOps continuous delivery 
 **AWS Load Balancer Controller** Ingress via ALB/NLB (AWS) 
 **External DNS** Automatic DNS record sync 
 **External Secrets Operator** Cloud secrets → K8s Secrets 
 **Karpenter** Just-in-time node provisioning (AWS) 
 **Metrics Server** CPU/memory metrics for HPA 
 **Storage Class** GP3 EBS / Persistent Disk / Managed Disk

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: Real-time Updates
records.text: The Clusters page subscribes to `projects` table UPDATE events. When a project's status changes to ACTIVE (deploy completes), the clusters list refreshes automatically to show the new cluster.

records.title: Clusters
records.heading: Data Source
records.text: Cluster information comes from the DEPLOY job's `finalizeDeployment()` server action, which extracts OpenTofu outputs (cluster name, endpoint, ARN, ArgoCD URL, database/cache endpoints) and writes them to the project's component tables.

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.text: Alibaba Connector. Keyless + account-free access via a RAM OIDC provider and AssumeRoleWithOIDC.

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Alibaba Connector
records.text:
Connecting Alibaba registers a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) that is **keyless and account-free**. Alethia's control plane is its own OIDC issuer;
you register a **RAM OIDC provider** that trusts it and a **RAM role** that trusts that provider. Alethia
assumes the role by presenting a short-lived minted assertion (STS `AssumeRoleWithOIDC`) — Alethia holds
no Alibaba account, generates no AccessKey, and stores nothing but the role ARN. See
[Security Architecture](/concepts/security).

 
**Is this safe?** You create the trust entirely in **your own** Alibaba account — Alethia holds no Alibaba
account and generates no AccessKey. It signs in with a short-lived token (≤10 min) that Alibaba STS verifies
and swaps for a ~1-hour credential. The only thing stored is the **RAM role ARN** (a public identifier).
**Revoke anytime** by deleting the role.

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
The connect dialog gives you two equivalent options — a one-line `aliyun` command for the
**Alibaba Cloud Shell** (nothing to install), or the **OpenTofu module** for teams that manage
infrastructure as code. Both create everything in **your** Alibaba account:

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Create the RAM OIDC provider
records.text:
A RAM OIDC provider named `alethia` trusting Alethia's issuer (`https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc`), with
client id `sts.aliyuncs.com`. The provider name is fixed — Alethia derives its ARN from your role ARN.

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Create the RAM role
records.text:
A role whose trust policy allows `AssumeRoleWithOIDC` from that provider, scoped to Alethia's workload
identity (`oidc:sub = alethia-connector`, `oidc:aud = sts.aliyuncs.com`) — a wrong subject/audience is
rejected.

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Attach provisioning permissions
records.text:
The module attaches the RAM policies Alethia needs (ACK, VPC, ECS, SLB, EIP by default; tighten or
extend to match your Projects).

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Paste the role ARN into Alethia
records.text:
Run `tofu output` and paste `role_arn` — it looks like `acs:ram:: :role/ `. That's the
only value Alethia needs (the OIDC-provider ARN is derived from it).

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: Verification
records.text:
Alethia verifies the cloud identity **instantly, server-side** (no runner):

1. The console mints an OIDC assertion (audience `sts.aliyuncs.com`) and calls Alibaba STS
 `AssumeRoleWithOIDC` with your role + provider ARNs
2. Alibaba validates the assertion against the RAM OIDC provider and returns temporary credentials
3. A successful assume is the proof of access → `Connected` / `Disconnected`

records.title: Alibaba Connector
records.heading: What's Stored
records.text:
- The **RAM role ARN**
- The derived **RAM OIDC provider ARN** (`acs:ram:: :oidc-provider/alethia`)
- The **account id** (parsed from the role ARN)

**No credentials, ever.** Revoke access by deleting the RAM role (or the OIDC provider) in your account.

records.title: AWS Connector
records.text: AWS Connector. Keyless, issuer-direct IAM role — an IAM OIDC provider trusting the Alethia issuer, set up with CloudFormation or OpenTofu.

records.title: AWS Connector
records.heading: AWS Connector
records.text:
Connecting AWS registers a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) that is **keyless and issuer-direct**: your account registers an **IAM OIDC provider** that trusts
Alethia's OIDC issuer, plus an IAM role Alethia assumes via **`AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`** — no access keys, no
external id, and no trust in an Alethia AWS account. This is the same model GCP, Azure, and Alibaba use. See
[Security Architecture](/concepts/security) for the full model.

 
**Is this safe?** You create the trust entirely in **your own** AWS account — Alethia never sees a key.
It signs in with a short-lived token (≤10 min) that AWS verifies and swaps for a ~1-hour credential. The
only thing Alethia stores is the **role ARN** (a public identifier). **Revoke anytime** by deleting the
stack or the role.

records.title: AWS Connector
records.heading: Setup Methods
records.text:
### Launch CloudFormation Stack
 Click "Launch Stack in AWS" — this opens the AWS Console with a pre-filled CloudFormation template
 (the issuer URL is filled in for you) that creates:
 - An **IAM OIDC provider** for the Alethia issuer (`https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc`)
 - `AlethiaProvisionerRole` — an IAM role whose trust policy allows **only** `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`
 from that provider, pinned to the fixed audience (`sts.amazonaws.com`) and subject (`alethia-connector`)

 ### Acknowledge and Create
 In the AWS Console, acknowledge that CloudFormation will create IAM resources, then click "Create stack."

 ### Copy Role ARN
 After the stack completes (~1 minute), go to the Outputs tab and copy the Role ARN.

 ### Paste into Alethia
 Paste the Role ARN into the Alethia form and click "Connect."

 
 
 
 

 ### Open AWS CloudShell
 Open [AWS CloudShell](https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudshell/home) — the `aws` CLI is
 preinstalled and already authenticated. (Or run the script locally with a configured `aws` CLI.)

 ### Run the setup script
 Paste the one-liner from the connect sheet — `curl -sO …/alethia-aws-setup.sh && bash alethia-aws-setup.sh`.
 It creates the same IAM

records.title: AWS Connector
records.heading: Verification
records.text:
After submitting, Alethia verifies the cloud identity **instantly, server-side** (no runner):

1. The console mints a short-lived assertion and calls `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` on your role
2. Calls `sts:GetCallerIdentity` to confirm the assumed identity (proof of access)
3. Runs a small capability probe (representative reads) → the cloud identity is `Connected`, or `Degraded`
 when provisioning permissions are missing, or `Disconnected` when auth fails

If verification fails, the error message explains what went wrong (e.g., trust policy misconfiguration,
missing permissions). Existing resources (VPCs, subnets, …) are discovered separately by the
server-side [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory), not during this probe.

records.title: AWS Connector
records.heading: IAM Role Permissions
records.text:
What the role may *do* is independent of *who may assume it* (the OIDC trust above) — a separate axis.

**Exactly what Alethia provisions in your account** (feature-gated — a given Project uses a subset): EC2/VPC
(subnets, NAT, route tables, security groups, VPC endpoints), EKS (cluster, managed node groups, add-ons, the
IRSA OIDC provider + controller roles), RDS (Aurora), ElastiCache (Redis/Valkey), Route 53, ACM, Secrets
Manager, DynamoDB, SQS/SNS, ECR, S3, KMS envelope keys, CloudWatch Logs + Kinesis Firehose, EventBridge
(Karpenter), Application Auto Scaling, and the in-cluster controller IAM roles (external-dns, AWS Load
Balancer Controller, EBS CSI, Karpenter).

 
**Least-privilege by default — no account admin.** The setup attaches **four scoped policies** instead of
`AdministratorAccess`: `…-Compute`, `…-Data`, `…-Edge` (each limited to exactly the services above), and an
action-enumerated `…-IAM` policy. The IAM policy lets the templates create the EKS/IRSA roles they need but
**explicitly denies** attaching `AdministratorAccess`/`PowerUserAccess`/`IAMFullAccess` to anything, scopes
`PassRole` to the provisioned services, and denies all `organizations:*`/`account:*` — so

records.title: AWS Connector
records.heading: What's Stored
records.text:
Only the **Role ARN** (`arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AlethiaProvisionerRole`) is stored. No access keys,
secret keys, session tokens, or external id are ever persisted — every credential is a short-lived assertion
minted at call time.

records.title: Azure Connector
records.text: Azure Connector. Keyless Azure access — a managed identity in your own subscription, no client secrets.

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Azure Connector
records.text:
Connecting Azure registers a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) that is **keyless** and
**customer-side**. You create a **user-assigned managed identity** in your own subscription (a plain ARM
resource — no App Registration, no directory-admin rights) with a Federated Identity Credential that
trusts the Alethia OIDC issuer. Both the console (verification) and the runner (provisioning) authenticate
**as that identity** by presenting a short-lived OIDC assertion; Azure validates it and issues an ARM
access token. **You store no client secret**, and there is **no platform Entra app** — parity with the GCP
Workload Identity Federation model. See [Security Architecture](/concepts/security).

 
**Is this safe?** You create the identity in **your own** subscription and grant it a least-privilege role.
Alethia signs in with a short-lived token (≤10 min) that Entra ID verifies and swaps for a ~1-hour
credential. The only things stored are your **tenant, subscription, and managed-identity client id** (all
public). **Revoke anytime** by deleting the managed identity.

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
The connect dialog gives you a one-line `az` command (or an OpenTofu module). It runs entirely in **your**
subscription:

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Create the managed identity + federated credential
records.text:
`az identity create` provisions a user-assigned managed identity, and
`az identity federated-credential create` adds a credential trusting the Alethia issuer (subject
`alethia-connector`, audience `api://AzureADTokenExchange`). No App Registration, no secret.

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Assign the least-privilege role
records.text:
The script creates and assigns the **Alethia Provisioner** custom role — scoped to exactly the services
the project templates create, with an ABAC condition that limits role-assignment writes to the single
DNS Zone Contributor role (no path to self-escalation).

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Enter details in Alethia
records.text:
Paste back the three values the script prints:
- **Tenant ID** — your Entra directory ID
- **Client ID** — the managed identity's application id
- **Subscription ID** — the target Azure subscription

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: Verification
records.text:
Same as other providers — Alethia verifies access **instantly, server-side** (no runner):

1. The console mints an OIDC assertion for your managed identity and presents it to your tenant
2. Azure validates it against the identity's federated credential and returns an ARM access token
3. A small ARM read confirms provisioning access → `Connected` / `Degraded` / `Disconnected`

Existing VNets, subnets, and DNS zones are discovered separately by the server-side
[asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory).

records.title: Azure Connector
records.heading: What's Stored
records.text:
- **Tenant ID**
- **Client ID** (your managed identity's application id)
- **Subscription ID**

**No client secrets, ever.** Revoke access by deleting the managed identity (or its role assignment)
in your subscription.

records.title: GCP Connector
records.text: GCP Connector. Workload Identity Federation setup with OIDC token exchange.

records.title: GCP Connector
records.heading: GCP Connector
records.text:
Connecting GCP registers a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) that uses **Workload Identity Federation (WIF)** with **direct OIDC** — your project's WIF pool
trusts Alethia's issuer directly (no AWS hop), and Alethia federates in with a short-lived minted JWT. No
service account JSON keys are created or stored. See [Security Architecture](/concepts/security).

 
**Is this safe?** You create the trust entirely in **your own** GCP project — no service account key is
ever generated. Alethia signs in with a short-lived token (≤10 min) that GCP verifies and swaps for a
~1-hour access token. The only thing stored is the **WIF trust configuration** (no secret). **Revoke
anytime** by deleting the pool or unbinding the service account.
 

The connect sheet gives you two one-shot paths that create everything for you. When they finish, you enter
just **two values** — your **Project ID** and **Project Number** — and Alethia assembles the WIF credential
configuration for you (there is no JSON to copy). Both paths pin the same values the console mints: issuer
`https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc`, audience `alethia-gcp-wif`, subject `alethia-connector`, and the fixed
pool `alethia-pool` / provi

records.title: GCP Connector
records.heading: Verification
records.text:
Alethia verifies the cloud identity **instantly, server-side** (no runner):

1. The console presents an OIDC token to GCP's Security Token Service (Workload Identity Federation)
2. GCP validates the token against the registered issuer/subject and returns a short-lived access token
3. A small Compute read confirms provisioning access → `Connected` / `Degraded` / `Disconnected`

Existing resources (VPC networks, subnets, DNS zones) are discovered separately by the server-side
[asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory).

records.title: GCP Connector
records.heading: What's Stored
records.text:
- **GCP Project ID** and **Project Number**
- **WIF configuration** — assembled by the console from those two IDs: the pool + provider audience, the
 service account impersonation URL, and the token-file credential source. The provisioner service account
 is referenced by that impersonation URL; there's no separate key field.

No service account JSON keys. Access is revoked by deleting the WIF pool or unbinding the service account.

records.title: Git Providers
records.text: Git Providers. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket OAuth integration with automatic token refresh.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Git Providers
records.text: Git provider integrations enable repository access for GitOps workflows. Three providers are supported via OAuth.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Supported Providers
records.text:
Provider Auth Method Token Refresh 
 ---------- ----------- --------------- 
 GitHub OAuth 2.0 Automatic via refresh token 
 GitLab OAuth 2.0 Automatic via refresh token 
 Bitbucket OAuth 2.0 (Basic Auth for refresh) Automatic via refresh token

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Click Connect
records.text: Click "Connect" on the provider card in the Connectors page.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: OAuth Authorization
records.text: You're redirected to the provider's authorization page. Grant the application access to your repositories.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Callback
records.text: The provider redirects back to Alethia with an authorization code. Better Auth exchanges it for access and refresh tokens.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Token Storage
records.text: Better Auth stores the provider tokens in the `provider_tokens` table. All tokens are encrypted at rest.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Token Refresh
records.text:
Git tokens have limited lifetimes. The `getValidProviderToken()` server action handles refresh automatically:

1. Reads the token from `provider_tokens`
2. Checks `expires_at` — if in the past, initiates refresh
3. Calls the provider's token refresh endpoint:
 - GitHub: `POST github.com/login/oauth/access_token`
 - GitLab: `POST gitlab.com/oauth/token`
 - Bitbucket: `POST bitbucket.org/site/oauth2/access_token`
4. Updates the token in the database
5. Returns the valid access token

This happens transparently whenever a Git operation is needed.

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: What Tokens Are Used For
records.text:
- **Repository listing** — the Design a Project form shows repos from your connected provider
- **ArgoCD access** — after deploying, ArgoCD uses the token to watch repositories for changes
- **Template cloning** — infrastructure templates can be sourced from your repositories

records.title: Git Providers
records.heading: Disconnecting
records.text:
Disconnecting a Git provider:

1. Deletes the `provider_tokens` record
2. Revokes the OAuth grant via Better Auth / the provider
3. Existing ArgoCD installations that use this token will lose repository access

 
Disconnecting a Git provider affects all projects that use repositories from that provider. ArgoCD will show "sync failed" until a new token is provided.

records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.text: Connection Health & Inventory. How Alethia verifies cloud connections and keeps their asset inventory fresh — server-side, no runner.

records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.heading: Connection Health & Inventory
records.text:
Once a cloud account is connected, Alethia does two things **entirely server-side in the console** — no
[Runner](/runner) is involved. The runner is provisioning-only; it never tests connections or lists
resources.

 
 Every cloud identity is **platform-managed**: the control plane (the hosted service, or your own console when
 self-hosted) mints the short-lived credentials on demand — it federates off the Alethia OIDC issuer, so it
 holds no standing cloud credential (Alibaba is even account-free). There is no "bring the token to a
 self-hosted runner" path.

records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.heading: Connection health
records.text:
The moment you connect (and continuously afterward), Alethia runs an **instant health probe**:

1. **Auth** — assume the customer role / exchange the federated token / present the API token, using the
 platform credentials. Success proves access.
2. **Provisioning-capability probe** — a small set of representative reads to confirm the cloud identity can
 actually see what Alethia provisions into.

The result is one of three statuses, surfaced on the connector card:

 Status Meaning 
 -------- --------- 
 **Connected** Authenticated and the capability probe passed. Ready to provision. 
 **Degraded** Authenticated, but some provisioning permissions look missing — provisioning may fail until the role/policy is widened. The missing permissions are listed. 
 **Disconnected** Authentication failed (revoked trust, a trust policy that no longer pins the Alethia issuer, expired/invalid token). 

Health is re-checked on a schedule and can be re-run on demand with **Re-verify**. Because it's a direct
API call, it resolves in well under a second — there is no queue and no job to wait on.

records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.heading: Asset inventory
records.text:
Alethia keeps a normalized inventory of the resources you provision alongside (VPCs/networks, subnets,
NICs, Kubernetes clusters, databases, caches, queues, topics, NoSQL tables, container registries,
secrets, storage buckets, DNS zones, and regions). It powers the **"use existing VPC"** picker on the
canvas and gives the verification agent real-world context.

The inventory is maintained server-side:

- **On connect** — a first sync populates the baseline.
- **Reconciliation sweep** — an in-process scheduler (no external cron) re-lists per cloud identity via the
 cloud SDKs on a TTL cadence (health ~10 min, inventory ~1 h), upserts the typed `cloud_*` tables,
 soft-removes anything no longer present, and **backfills** cloud identities that were never synced. This is
 the reliable backstop and is **on by default**.
- **Event-driven updates (opt-in, near-real-time)** — a per-cloud forwarder can stream change events so
 the inventory reflects a new/deleted resource within seconds (**AWS** EventBridge → Lambda; **GCP**
 Cloud Asset Inventory → Pub/Sub → Cloud Function; **Azure** Event Grid → Function App). Each normalizes
 the change and `POST`s `{ kind, native_id, region, deleted }`

records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.heading: Data custody — how the inventory is stored
records.text:
The inventory is a **minimized, encrypted, expiring projection** — the cloud stays the source of truth:

- **Minimized** — only the identifiers features need (resource id, name, region, kind + low-sensitivity
 typed fields). We do **not** store resource tags (they routinely carry owner emails / cost centers /
 secrets); only the `Name` tag becomes the row name.
- **Encrypted at rest** — reconnaissance-sensitive attributes (CIDRs, IPs, endpoints, DNS domains) are
 sealed in a single AES-GCM `sensitive` blob per row, decrypted only in-app when a feature needs them.
- **Expiring + purged** — soft-removed rows are garbage-collected after a retention window
 (`ALETHIA_INVENTORY_RETENTION_DAYS`, default 7); **disconnecting a cloud identity purges its inventory
 entirely**.

Storing this metadata doesn't weaken the keyless model — a resource id or CIDR is not a
credential, and a breach grants no cloud access (that still requires the role trust, which Alethia holds
no key for).
