records.title: Connection Health & Inventory
records.heading: What the Runner does NOT do
records.text:
For clarity, the [Runner](/runner) is **provisioning-only**. It does **not**:

- test cloud connections (server-side, above),
- discover or list cloud resources (server-side asset inventory, above),
- cache resource lists.

It claims `PLAN` / `DEPLOY` / `DESTROY` (and the runner-lifecycle + drift) jobs, runs OpenTofu, streams
logs, and runs the [verify gate](/runner). See [Runner Jobs](/runner/job-execution).

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.text: Hetzner Connector. Scoped, project-scoped API token — encrypted at rest, decrypted only on the runner. Provisions a self-managed Talos Linux Kubernetes cluster on cheap Hetzner VMs.

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.heading: Hetzner Connector
records.text:
Connecting Hetzner Cloud registers a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) that is **token-based**. Unlike AWS, GCP, Azure, and Alibaba — which are
**keyless** (Alethia federates into a role you grant via short-lived, minted OIDC assertions) —
Hetzner Cloud offers **no OIDC or workload-identity federation**. Its only auth primitive is a
**project-scoped API token**. So Alethia connects with a token you create, **encrypted at rest
(AES-GCM)** and **decrypted only on the runner** at provision time — never written into a project
snapshot. This is the best-available model for a cloud without federation. See
[Security Architecture](/concepts/security) for the full model.

 
**Is this safe?** The token is stored **encrypted** (AES-256-GCM) and is only ever decrypted on the
runner during a provisioning job — it never appears in a job's config snapshot. A Hetzner token is
scoped to a **single Hetzner project** (the tightest boundary Hetzner offers). **Revoke anytime** by
deleting the token in the Hetzner Cloud Console — that instantly cuts Alethia's access.

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.heading: What Hetzner provisions
records.text:
Hetzner Cloud has no managed Kubernetes or managed data services. Instead, Alethia provisions a
**self-managed [Talos Linux](https://www.talos.dev/) Kubernetes cluster** on Hetzner's inexpensive
VMs (ARM `CAX` or x86 `CX`/`CPX`/`CCX`), with Cilium networking and the Hetzner CSI driver for block
storage. Data services (Postgres, cache, queue) that would be managed cloud resources on a
hyperscaler run **in-cluster via Helm** — CloudNativePG (Postgres), Valkey (cache), and RabbitMQ
(queue) — deployed through ArgoCD. See
[Health & Inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory) for how cloud identities are probed.

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.heading: Setup
records.text:
### Create a project-scoped API token
 In the [Hetzner Cloud Console](https://console.hetzner.cloud/), open your project, then
 **Security → API Tokens → Generate API token**. Give it **Read & Write** permission —
 provisioning creates servers, networks, and volumes. The token is scoped to just that one
 project.

 ### Paste into Alethia
 Paste the token into the Alethia connect sheet and click **Connect**. Alethia encrypts it at rest
 (AES-GCM) and verifies it immediately against the Hetzner Cloud API.

 
 
 
 

 ### Run a self-hosted runner
 If you operate your own runner, set **`HCLOUD_TOKEN`** in the runner's own environment. Alethia
 then stores **no token at all** — the runner supplies it directly at provision time. This is the
 zero-storage path for federation-less clouds.

 ### Connect
 Mark the cloud identity self-managed; provisioning jobs claimed by your runner use its environment's
 `HCLOUD_TOKEN` and Alethia never persists a credential.

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.heading: Verification
records.text:
After you submit, Alethia verifies the cloud identity **instantly, server-side** (no runner): it decrypts
the stored token and calls a cheap authenticated Hetzner Cloud API endpoint
(`GET https://api.hetzner.cloud/v1/datacenters`).

- `200` → the cloud identity is **Connected**
- `401` / `403` → the token was rejected (bad or revoked) → **Disconnected**

On success, Alethia also syncs the project's **regions** (used for placement) into the asset
[inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory). If verification fails, the error explains what
went wrong.

records.title: Hetzner Connector
records.heading: What's Stored
records.text:
- **Managed:** only your **encrypted API token** (AES-256-GCM), decrypted solely on the runner at
 provision time — never in a project snapshot.
- **Self-hosted runner:** **nothing** — the runner carries `HCLOUD_TOKEN` in its own environment.

Rotate or revoke the token in the Hetzner Cloud Console at any time to cut access.

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.text: Connectors Overview. Connect cloud providers and Git accounts to enable infrastructure provisioning.

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Connectors
records.text: The **connector** catalog is the registry of provider *types* you can connect. Each account you actually connect becomes a **cloud identity** (`cloud_identities`) — the credential anchor a project provisions against. The catalog spans the **federated clouds** (AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba) and the **token clouds** (Hetzner, DigitalOcean, Civo), plus Git providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) and **pluggable per-category providers** (DNS, secrets, registry, observability). At least one cloud identity and one Git provider must be connected before provisioning; pluggable providers are optional — they let a project use a tool you already run instead of the cloud-native default.

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: How It Works
records.text:
Connectors are displayed as cards organized by category:

 Category Providers Auth 
 ---------- ----------- ------ 
 **Cloud Providers** AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba keyless federation off the Alethia OIDC issuer (no stored keys) 
 **Token Clouds** Hetzner, DigitalOcean, Civo the customer's own API token (encrypted at rest) 
 **Git Providers** GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket OAuth 
 **DNS** Cloudflare API key 
 **Secrets** HashiCorp Vault API key 
 **Registry** Docker Hub API key 
 **Observability** Datadog, Grafana Cloud, Prometheus API key 

Each card shows:
- Provider icon and name
- Connection status — a grayscale status badge (dot + label, no color): **Connected**, **Needs Reconnection**, or **Not Connected**
- Connected account identifier (AWS Account ID, GCP Project, GitHub username)
- Action button (Connect / Disconnect / Reconnect)

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Connection Status
records.text:
Status Meaning 
 -------- --------- 
 **Connected** Credentials valid and verified 
 **Needs Reconnection** Git token expired, automatic refresh failed 
 **Not Connected** No credentials on file 
 **Coming Soon** Provider supported but not yet implemented

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Cloud Provider Connections
records.text:
The four **federated clouds** use the platform's [zero-credential model](/concepts/security) — the customer's
role/pool/provider trusts the Alethia OIDC issuer directly, and Alethia federates in with a short-lived minted
assertion. **No static keys are stored.**

 
 Keyless IAM role via an IAM OIDC provider 
 Workload Identity Federation — enter Project ID + Number, config assembled for you 
 Federated Identity via Entra ID 
 RAM AssumeRoleWithOIDC (account-free) 
 

The **token clouds** (Hetzner, DigitalOcean, Civo) instead use the customer's own API token — there is no
account to federate into. The token is encrypted at rest (`ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY`) and only decrypted
on the runner at provision time.

After connecting, Alethia verifies the credentials **instantly, server-side** (an auth + provisioning-permission probe — no runner, no job) and marks the cloud identity `Connected`, `Degraded`, or `Disconnected`. Existing resources (VPCs, subnets, DNS zones) are discovered + kept fresh by the server-side, event-driven asset inventory. See [Connection health & inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory).

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Git Provider Connections
records.text: Git connections use standard OAuth. See [Git Providers](/console/connectors/git-providers) for details.

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Pluggable Connectors
records.text:
DNS, secrets, registry, and observability providers authenticate with an **API key / token**. Secret fields are encrypted at rest (`ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY`) and only decrypted on the runner at provision time — never stored in a Project snapshot. After connecting, a verification ping confirms the credential works.

 
 Cloudflare · Vault · Docker Hub · Datadog · Grafana · Prometheus

records.title: Connectors Overview
records.heading: Disconnecting
records.text:
- **Cloud providers**: Removes the `cloud_identities` record. Existing projects referencing that identity can no longer run jobs.
- **Git providers**: Removes the Better Auth `account` record and revokes the OAuth grant.
- **Pluggable connectors**: Removes the `connector_credentials` record. Projects referencing that provider fall back to failing validation until reconnected.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.text: Pluggable Connectors. Bring your own DNS, secrets, registry, and observability tools via API-key connectors.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: Pluggable Connectors
records.text:
Beyond cloud and Git, Alethia supports **pluggable per-category connectors** that let a Project mix in the tools you already use instead of the cloud-native default — Cloudflare for DNS, HashiCorp Vault for secrets, Docker Hub for a registry, and Datadog / Grafana Cloud / Prometheus for observability.

These connectors authenticate with an **API key / token** (`auth_method = api_key`) rather than cloud federation or OAuth.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: Categories and providers
records.text:
Category Cloud-native default Pluggable provider Credential fields 
 ---------- ---------------------- -------------------- ------------------- 
 **DNS** Route 53 / Cloud DNS / Azure DNS **Cloudflare** API token 
 **Secrets** Secrets Manager / Secret Manager / Key Vault **HashiCorp Vault** Address, token 
 **Registry** ECR / Artifact Registry / ACR **Docker Hub** Username, access token 
 **Observability** — (none) **Datadog** API key, app key 
 **Observability** — **Grafana Cloud** Instance ID, API token 
 **Observability** — **Prometheus** (optional) remote-write user/password 

The catalog is data-driven — each provider is one row in the `connectors` table, seeded from a single manifest (`packages/core/categories/catalog.json`). See [Connectors Architecture](/concepts/connectors) for how this works end to end.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: Connecting a provider
records.text:
1. Open **Connectors**, pick the provider card, and choose **Connect**.
2. Fill in the API credential — the form fields are generated from the provider's credential specification.
3. Alethia runs a lightweight **verification ping** against the provider's API (e.g. Cloudflare's token-verify endpoint, Vault's token lookup, Docker Hub login, Datadog's key-validate endpoint). A verified credential turns the card green; an unverified one is still saved so you can retry.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: How credentials are stored
records.text:
Secret fields (tokens, passwords) are **encrypted at rest** with AES-256-GCM before being written to the `connector_credentials` table; non-secret fields (a Vault address, a Docker Hub username) are stored in clear. The encryption key comes from `ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY` (see [Configuration](/self-hosting/configuration)).

 
Secrets are **never** placed in a Project's `config_snapshot`. They are decrypted and attached to a job only at **claim time**, then injected into the runner's OpenTofu variables in memory — preserving the [zero-credential model](/concepts/security).

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: Using a connector in a Project
records.text:
Once connected, pick the provider per component in the [Project designer](/console/design-project):

- [DNS & WAF](/console/design-project/dns) — choose **Cloudflare** instead of cloud-native DNS.
- [Secrets](/console/design-project/secrets) — choose **Vault**.
- [Container Registry](/console/design-project/container-registry) — choose **Docker Hub**.
- [Observability](/console/design-project/observability) — choose **Datadog / Grafana / Prometheus**.

At provision time the runner composes the chosen provider's module into the plan and **skips** the cloud-native resource for that category.

records.title: Pluggable Connectors
records.heading: Adding a new provider
records.text:
Pluggable connectors are an open-ended catalog. Adding a seventh provider takes four steps and **no** changes to routes, the runner core, or the form engine:

1. Add a row to `packages/core/categories/catalog.json` (category, slug, auth_method, icon, credential + provider_config fields).
2. Add a `CategoryProvider` Go impl under `packages/core/categories/` (its `Tfvars`/`Validate` behavior).
3. Add the OpenTofu module under `infra/templates/categories/ / /`.
4. Run `pnpm -F console gen:connectors` to regenerate the registry + catalog seed.

See [Connectors Architecture](/concepts/connectors) for the contract details.

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.text: Dashboard Overview. The main dashboard page — stats, cloud identity status, and recent jobs.

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Dashboard Overview
records.text: The dashboard is the landing page after login. It provides a quick summary of your infrastructure state.

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Layout
records.text:
The dashboard shell wraps all authenticated pages with:

- **Header** — breadcrumbs, notifications bell (unread job count), user dropdown (profile, sign out)
- **Sidebar** — main navigation (Overview, Design a Project, Clusters, Jobs, Connectors, Runners) plus a dynamic project list
- **Real-time subscriptions** — established on layout mount for job status and project status updates (see [Real-time Architecture](/concepts/realtime))

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Overview Page
records.text:
The overview page shows four stat chips at the top:

 Stat Source 
 ------ -------- 
 Total Projects Count of all projects 
 Active Projects Projects with status `ACTIVE` 
 Online Runners Runners with heartbeat within 60 seconds 
 Total Jobs Count of all provision jobs 

Below the stats:

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Connectors Panel
records.text: Shows connected and disconnected [cloud identities](/console/connectors) as badges. Connected providers show a green indicator and account identifier (AWS Account ID, GCP Project, GitHub username). Disconnected providers link to the connectors page.

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Recent Jobs
records.text:
A data table showing the 5 most recent jobs with columns:
- **Type** — job type with icon (PLAN, DEPLOY, DESTROY, etc.)
- **Status** — color-coded badge (green=SUCCESS, red=FAILED, yellow=PROCESSING, gray=QUEUED)
- **Project** — target project name
- **Created** — relative timestamp

Clicking a job row navigates to the [job detail page](/console/jobs/job-detail).

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Notifications
records.text:
The notifications bell in the header shows a badge with the count of unread job state changes. Clicking it opens a popover with recent notifications. Clicking a notification navigates to the relevant job. "Mark all read" clears the count.

Notifications are driven by the real-time subscription to `jobs` — every status change (QUEUED → PROCESSING → SUCCESS/FAILED) creates a notification.

records.title: Dashboard Overview
records.heading: Sidebar Navigation
records.text:
The sidebar has two sections:

**Main Navigation:**
- Overview (`/dashboard`)
- Design a Project (`/dashboard/design`)
- Clusters (`/dashboard/clusters`)
- Jobs (`/dashboard/jobs`)
- Connectors (`/dashboard/connectors`)
- Runners (`/dashboard/runners`)

**Projects List:**
- Dynamically loaded from the projects store
- Projects show status dots (green=ACTIVE, yellow=PROVISIONING, red=FAILED) and provider icons
- Active project is highlighted based on current URL
- "Design a Project" link shown if no projects exist

records.title: Project Detail
records.text: Project Detail. The project detail page — services, infrastructure, plan, jobs, and lifecycle actions.

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Project Detail
records.text: Each project has a detail page at `/{org}/{project}` showing the complete infrastructure configuration and its deployment state.

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Header
records.text:
The header displays:
- **Project name** and provider icon (AWS/GCP/Azure)
- **Region** and **environment stage** (production/staging/development)
- **Status badge** — DRAFT, QUEUED, PROVISIONING, ACTIVE, FAILED, DESTROYING, DESTROYED
- **Estimated monthly cost**
- **Action buttons**: Plan, Apply, and a dropdown menu

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Action Dropdown
records.text:
Action Description 
 -------- ------------- 
 **Plan** Queue a PLAN job → Runner runs `tofu plan` 
 **Apply** Queue a DEPLOY job → Runner runs the [verify gate](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply#verify-gate) then `tofu apply` + installs ArgoCD 
 **Quick Duplicate** Create a copy with the same config 
 **Duplicate & Edit** Open the Design a Project form pre-filled with this project's config 
 **Delete** Delete the project record (does NOT destroy cloud resources) 

Plan and Apply actions open the **Runner Select Popover** where you choose which Runner executes the job. See [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Configuration Tab (Default)
records.text:
Shows quick stat chips:
- Service count (databases + caches + queues + topics + NoSQL + secrets)
- Estimated monthly cost
- Current status
- Provider and region

Then displays two sub-sections:

**Infrastructure** — read-only display of:
- Network: VPC/VNet (create new or existing), CIDR block, NAT gateway mode
- Cluster: K8s version, node sizing (min/desired/max), instance types, autoscaler
- DNS: domain, certificate, WAF settings (if enabled)
- Repositories: application repo (if configured)
- General: OpenTofu version (`iac_version`), region, environment

**Services** — grid of service buttons for each configured component:
- Databases (engine, capacity, endpoints if provisioned)
- Caches (engine, node type, endpoint if provisioned)
- Queues (FIFO/standard, visibility timeout)
- Topics (subscription count)
- NoSQL tables (key schema, billing mode)
- Secrets (length, generation settings)

Each service button opens a **Service Popover** with full details, including:
- Configuration parameters
- Status with timestamp
- Endpoint (if provisioned, with copy button)
- Monthly cost estimate

**Recent Jobs** — table showing the last 5 jobs for this project (type, status, created, duration

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Plan Tab
records.text:
Visible after running a Plan job. Shows:

- **Plan Summary Bar** — resource counts (add/change/destroy) and estimated cost
- **Resource Groups** — collapsible sections grouped by resource type, each showing individual resources with their action (create/update/delete) and cost
- **Verify Report** — the [verify gate](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply#verify-gate) result (`verify_result`): per-control pass / fail / `not_evaluable` over the plan JSON
- **Apply Button** — queues a DEPLOY job using this plan's artifact

See [Plan & Apply](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply) for the full workflow.

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Cluster Access
records.text:
After a successful DEPLOY, the project detail shows:
- **Cluster endpoint** (copy button)
- **kubeconfig command** (provider-specific, copy button)
- **ArgoCD URL** (external link to ArgoCD dashboard)
- **ArgoCD admin credentials**

records.title: Project Detail
records.heading: Project Duplication
records.text:
The **Duplicate & Edit** action opens the Design a Project form pre-filled with the project's current configuration. If you select a different cloud identity (e.g., switching from AWS to GCP), the configuration is converted automatically. See [Multi-Cloud Conversion](/concepts/multi-cloud-conversion) for how this works.

Conversion warnings are displayed in the form if any settings couldn't be mapped directly (e.g., instance types, WAF rules, DNS zones).

records.title: Caches
records.text: Caches. Redis and Valkey cache instances — node types, clustering, and multi-AZ.

records.title: Caches
records.heading: Caches
records.text: The caches section configures managed in-memory cache instances. Multiple caches can be added per project.

records.title: Caches
records.heading: Fields Per Cache
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Unique per project, lowercase 
 **Engine** Select `redis` or `valkey` 
 **Node Type** Select Instance size with pricing shown 
 **Nodes** Number 1–6 cache nodes 
 **Multi-AZ** Toggle Enable automatic failover across availability zones

records.title: Caches
records.heading: Engines
records.text:
Engine Description 
 -------- ------------- 
 **Redis** The standard in-memory data store. Supported on all three providers. 
 **Valkey** Open-source Redis fork (AWS only). Drop-in compatible with Redis, no licensing concerns.

records.title: Caches
records.heading: Node Types by Provider
records.text:
**ElastiCache** node types:

 Type Memory Cost (~) 
 ------ -------- --------- 
 cache.t3.micro 0.5 GiB ~$13/mo 
 cache.t3.small 1.4 GiB ~$25/mo 
 cache.t3.medium 3.1 GiB ~$50/mo 
 cache.r6g.large 13.1 GiB ~$130/mo 
 cache.r6g.xlarge 26.3 GiB ~$260/mo 

 Pricing shown next to each type is per node. Multiply by node count for total.
 
 
 **Memorystore** tiers:

 Tier Memory Notes 
 ------ -------- ------- 
 M1 1 GiB Basic 
 M2 5 GiB Standard 
 M3 16 GiB Large 
 M4 35 GiB Extra Large 
 
 
 **Azure Cache for Redis** tiers:

 Tier Memory Notes 
 ------ -------- ------- 
 C0 0.25 GiB Basic 
 C1 1 GiB Basic 
 C2 2.5 GiB Standard 
 C3 6 GiB Standard 
 P1 6 GiB Premium (clustering, persistence)

records.title: Caches
records.heading: Multi-AZ
records.text: When enabled, the cache replicates data across availability zones for automatic failover. If the primary node fails, a replica in another AZ is promoted automatically. This adds ~50% to the cost (replica nodes).

records.title: Caches
records.heading: After Provisioning
records.text:
The cache card in project detail shows:
- **Primary endpoint** — connection string for writes
- **Reader endpoint** — connection string for reads (if multi-AZ)
- **Port** — typically 6379
- **Status** — ACTIVE, CREATING, or FAILED

records.title: Cluster
records.text: Cluster. Kubernetes cluster configuration — version, instance types, node scaling, Karpenter, and cluster admins.

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Cluster
records.text: The cluster section configures the Kubernetes control plane and worker nodes.

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Fields
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **K8s Version** Select Provider-specific versions (e.g., 1.32 for EKS, 1.31 for GKE/AKS) 
 **Instance Types** Multi-select badges Up to 5 types, with hourly cost shown per type 
 **Min Nodes** Number Minimum node count (default: 2) 
 **Desired Nodes** Number Target node count (default: 3) 
 **Max Nodes** Number Maximum node count for autoscaling (default: 5) 
 **Autoscaler** Toggle Provider-specific autoscaler (Karpenter/Autopilot/Cluster Autoscaler) 
 **Cluster Admins** Array IAM users/emails with admin access

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Kubernetes Service
records.text:
- **Elastic Kubernetes Service** with managed node groups
 - Versions: 1.31, 1.32
 - Autoscaler: **Karpenter** — just-in-time node provisioning based on pod requirements
 - IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) configured automatically
 - OIDC provider created for workload identity
 
 
 - **Google Kubernetes Engine** with node pools
 - Versions: 1.30, 1.31
 - Autoscaler: **GKE Autopilot** — fully managed node provisioning
 - Workload Identity enabled by default
 
 
 - **Azure Kubernetes Service** with node pools
 - Versions: 1.30, 1.31
 - Autoscaler: **Cluster Autoscaler** — scales node count based on pending pods
 - Azure AD integration for RBAC

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Instance Types
records.text:
Instance types are selected as badges with pricing shown. Up to 5 types can be selected — when using Karpenter (AWS), multiple types allow the autoscaler to choose the best fit for each workload.

 Category AWS GCP Azure 
 ---------- ----- ----- ------- 
 General t3.medium, t3.large e2-medium, e2-standard-2 Standard_B2s, Standard_D2s_v5 
 Compute c5.large, c5.xlarge c2-standard-4 Standard_F2s_v2 
 Memory r5.large, r5.xlarge n2-highmem-2 Standard_E2s_v5 
 GPU g4dn.xlarge a2-highgpu-1g NC4as_T4_v3 

Pricing is fetched from the AWS Pricing API (or equivalent) and shown per hour next to each badge. See [Cost Estimation](/concepts/cost-estimation).

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Node Scaling
records.text:
Three numbers control the node group size:

- **Min**: Floor for the autoscaler (nodes will never scale below this)
- **Desired**: Initial node count at creation
- **Max**: Ceiling for the autoscaler (nodes will never scale above this)

Validation: min ≤ desired ≤ max.

records.title: Cluster
records.heading: Cluster Admins
records.text:
An array of identities with admin access to the cluster:

 
 
 IAM user ARNs or IAM user names. The form can look up IAM users from the cached resources (discovered via resource refresh). Admins are added as EKS access entries with `AmazonEKSClusterAdminPolicy`.
 
 
 Google email addresses granted `roles/container.admin`.
 
 
 Azure AD Object IDs granted cluster admin RBAC.

records.title: Container Registry
records.text: Container Registry. ECR, Artifact Registry, and ACR configuration — tag mutability and image scanning.

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: Container Registry
records.text: The container registry section creates managed Docker image registries for your application containers. Multiple registries can be added per project.

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: Fields Per Registry
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Repository name (e.g., `api`, `frontend`, `worker`) 
 **Provider** Select Cloud-native registry or a [pluggable connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) 
 **Tag Mutability** Select `MUTABLE` (tags can be overwritten) or `IMMUTABLE` (once pushed, a tag is permanent) 
 **Scan on Push** Toggle Automatically scan images for vulnerabilities on push

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: Registry Provider
records.text:
By default a cloud-native registry (ECR / Artifact Registry / ACR) is created. Select **Docker Hub** to use a Docker Hub repository instead:

- Requires a connected [Docker Hub connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) (username + access token).
- Optional **namespace / organization** (defaults to the username).
- Instead of creating cloud registry repos, the composed module wires a Kubernetes `imagePullSecret` so workloads can pull from Docker Hub; the cloud-native registry is skipped.

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: Services by Provider
records.text:
**ECR** (Elastic Container Registry)
 - Private repositories with IAM-based access
 - Lifecycle policies (auto-expire old images)
 - Integration with EKS for pull authentication
 
 
 **Artifact Registry**
 - Docker and OCI image support
 - IAM-based access control
 - Vulnerability scanning via Container Analysis
 
 
 **ACR** (Azure Container Registry)
 - SKU tiers: Basic, Standard, Premium
 - Geo-replication (Premium)
 - Integration with AKS for pull authentication

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: Tag Mutability
records.text:
Mode Behavior Use Case 
 ------ ---------- ---------- 
 **MUTABLE** Same tag (e.g., `latest`) can be pushed multiple times Development, CI/CD with `latest` tag 
 **IMMUTABLE** Once a tag is pushed, it cannot be overwritten Production (ensures deployed image is never changed) 

 
Immutable tags prevent accidental overwrites in production. A `v1.2.3` tag always points to the same image digest, making rollbacks deterministic.

records.title: Container Registry
records.heading: After Provisioning
records.text:
The registry card in project detail shows:
- **Repository URL** — full push/pull endpoint (e.g., `123456789012.dkr.ecr.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/api`)
- **Status** — ACTIVE or CREATING

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.text: Cost Sidebar. Real-time cost estimation sidebar with AWS Pricing API integration.

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.heading: Cost Sidebar
records.text:
The cost sidebar appears on the right side of the Design a Project form (visible on desktop screens). It shows the estimated monthly infrastructure cost, updating in real-time as you change configuration values.

For a deeper technical explanation, see [Cost Estimation](/concepts/cost-estimation).

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.heading: How It Works
records.text:
1. When a region is selected, pricing data is fetched from the AWS Pricing API via the `fetchPrices(region)` store action
2. Prices are cached in the Zustand store with a 24-hour TTL
3. Each form change triggers a recalculation
4. Fallback hardcoded rates are used if the API call fails

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.heading: Cost Breakdown
records.text:
The sidebar shows individual line items:

 Component Calculation Example 
 ----------- ------------ --------- 
 **EKS Control Plane** Fixed $0.10/hour × 730 $73/mo 
 **Compute Nodes** avg_instance_rate × desired_nodes × 730 $182/mo (3× t3.large) 
 **NAT Gateway** $0.048 × count × 730 $35/mo (single) 
 **Database (Aurora)** min_ACU × $0.14 × 730 per instance $51/mo (0.5 ACU) 
 **Cache** node_rate × count × 730 $50/mo (1× cache.t3.medium) 
 **WAF** $5 per enabled option $5–$10/mo 
 **Secrets** $0.40 per secret $1.60/mo (4 secrets) 
 **NoSQL/Queues/Topics** Usage-based $0 (not estimable) 

**Total** is the sum of all line items.

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.heading: Pricing Sources
records.text:
Resource Source Fallback 
 ---------- -------- ---------- 
 EC2 instances AWS Pricing API (on-demand, Linux, shared tenancy) Hardcoded rates per type 
 EKS control plane Hardcoded ($0.10/hr) — 
 NAT Gateway AWS Pricing API Hardcoded ($0.048/hr) 
 Aurora ACU AWS Pricing API Hardcoded ($0.14/ACU-hr) 
 ElastiCache AWS Pricing API Hardcoded rates per type 
 WAF Hardcoded ($5/mo) — 
 Secrets Hardcoded ($0.40/secret) —

records.title: Cost Sidebar
records.heading: Limitations
records.text:
- Pricing is currently **AWS-only** — GCP and Azure sections show approximate costs based on similar resource profiles
- Data transfer costs are not estimated (highly variable)
- NoSQL, messaging, and storage costs are usage-based and show $0
- Infracost provides more accurate post-plan costs — see [Cost Estimation](/concepts/cost-estimation)

records.title: Databases
records.text: Databases. Managed relational databases — Aurora, Cloud SQL, Azure Database with engine selection and capacity.

records.title: Databases
records.heading: Databases
records.text: The databases section configures managed relational database instances. Multiple databases can be added per project, each with its own engine, version, and capacity settings.

records.title: Databases
records.heading: Fields Per Database
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Unique per project, lowercase, used in resource naming 
 **Engine** Select Provider-specific engine (see below) 
 **Engine Version** Auto-set Determined by engine selection 
 **Min Capacity** Number Minimum capacity (ACUs for Aurora, vCPUs for Cloud SQL) 
 **Max Capacity** Number Maximum capacity for auto-scaling 
 **Port** Number Database port (default: 5432 for PostgreSQL, 3306 for MySQL) 
 **IAM Auth** Toggle Enable IAM-based database authentication (AWS only)

records.title: Databases
records.heading: Engines by Provider
records.text:
**Aurora Serverless v2** — auto-scaling managed database

 Engine Version Capacity Unit 
 -------- --------- --------------- 
 Aurora PostgreSQL 16.6 ACUs (0.5–128) 
 Aurora MySQL 8.0 ACUs (0.5–128) 

 Aurora Capacity Units (ACUs) scale automatically between min and max. Each ACU provides ~2 GiB of memory. At $0.14/ACU-hour, a 0.5–4 ACU database costs approximately $51–$408/month.
 
 
 **Cloud SQL** — managed database instances

 Engine Version Capacity Unit 
 -------- --------- --------------- 
 Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 15 vCPUs (1–96) 
 Cloud SQL MySQL 8.0 vCPUs (1–96) 
 
 
 **Azure Database Flexible Server** — managed database

 Engine Version Capacity Unit 
 -------- --------- --------------- 
 Azure PostgreSQL 16 vCores (1–64) 
 Azure MySQL 8.0 vCores (1–64)

records.title: Databases
records.heading: Adding Multiple Databases
records.text:
Click **"Add Database"** to add another instance. Each database gets its own card in the form. The first database defaults to:
- Name: `primary`
- Engine: Aurora PostgreSQL (or equivalent)
- Capacity: 0.5–4 ACUs

Common patterns:
- **Primary + Read Replica**: One write database + one read-optimized database
- **Multi-service**: Separate databases per microservice (e.g., `users-db`, `orders-db`)

records.title: Databases
records.heading: After Provisioning
records.text:
Once deployed, the database card in the [project detail](/console/dashboard/project-detail) Services tab shows:
- **Writer endpoint** — primary connection string
- **Reader endpoint** — read replica connection (Aurora only)
- **Master credentials** — stored in Secrets Manager (ARN shown, copy button)
- **Status** — ACTIVE, CREATING, or FAILED

 
IAM authentication (AWS) allows Kubernetes pods to authenticate to the database using their service account identity (IRSA) instead of username/password. This eliminates credential rotation. See [Security Architecture](/concepts/security).

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.text: DNS & WAF. Domain configuration, managed certificates, and Web Application Firewall options.

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: DNS & WAF
records.text: The DNS section is optional (controlled by a master toggle). When enabled, it configures domain management, SSL/TLS certificates, and web application firewall protection.

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: Fields
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Enabled** Toggle Master switch for DNS configuration 
 **DNS Provider** Select Cloud-native (Route 53 / Cloud DNS / Azure DNS) or a [pluggable connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) 
 **Zone ID** Select or text Existing DNS hosted zone 
 **Domain Name** Text input Auto-filled from zone, or manually entered 
 **Managed Certificate** Toggle Auto-provision SSL/TLS certificate (cloud-native only) 
 **WAF Options** Toggles Provider-specific WAF configuration (cloud-native only)

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: DNS Provider
records.text:
The **DNS Provider** selector decouples *what* the Project needs (a managed zone) from *who* provides it:

- **Cloud-native** (default) — uses the cluster cloud's DNS service; the managed-certificate and WAF toggles apply.
- **Cloudflare** — manages records on Cloudflare instead. Requires a connected [Cloudflare connector](/console/connectors/pluggable); set the **Zone ID** and the **Proxied** option (orange-cloud). The cloud-native DNS/cert resources are skipped at provision time.

When a pluggable provider is selected, its credential is pulled from `connector_credentials` on the runner at provision time — never stored in the Project.

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: DNS Zones
records.text:
The zone selector shows existing DNS zones from Alethia's server-side [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory):

 
 
 **Route 53** hosted zones (public only). Zone ID format: `Z1234567890ABC`.
 
 
 **Cloud DNS** managed zones. Zone name format: `my-zone`.
 
 
 **Azure DNS** zones. Zone name format: `example.com`.
 
 

 
DNS zones are provider-specific. When duplicating a project across providers, the zone ID and domain are cleared and must be re-configured. See [Multi-Cloud Conversion](/concepts/multi-cloud-conversion).

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: Managed Certificates
records.text:
When enabled, the platform provisions a TLS certificate automatically:

 Provider Service Validation 
 ---------- --------- ------------ 
 AWS ACM (AWS Certificate Manager) DNS validation via Route 53 
 GCP Google-managed certificate DNS validation via Cloud DNS 
 Azure App Service Certificate DNS validation via Azure DNS 

Certificates are renewed automatically before expiration.

records.title: DNS & WAF
records.heading: Web Application Firewall
records.text:
WAF protects web applications from common attacks (SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic).

 
 
 Two WAF options (can enable both):

 Option Protects Cost 
 -------- ---------- ------ 
 **CloudFront WAF** CDN-level protection ~$5/mo base 
 **Application WAF (ALB)** Load balancer level ~$5/mo base 

 AWS WAFv2 with managed rule groups (AWS Managed Rules, Bot Control).
 
 
 **Cloud Armor** — DDoS and application-layer protection at the load balancer.
 
 
 **Azure WAF** — protection via Application Gateway or Front Door.
 
 

WAF costs are reflected in the [cost sidebar](/console/design-project/cost-sidebar) immediately when toggled.

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.text: Design a Project Overview. The multi-section infrastructure configuration form with Provider Ribbon and Cloud Identity.

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Design a Project
records.text: The Design a Project form is the primary way to define infrastructure. It has 11 configurable sections, a Provider Ribbon for switching between AWS/GCP/Azure, a Cloud Identity selector, and a real-time cost sidebar.

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Form Structure
records.text: The form is built with `react-hook-form` + Zod validation, using `useFieldArray` for multi-instance components (databases, caches, etc.). Each section is an independent component that reads and writes to the shared form context.

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Provider Ribbon
records.text:
The Provider Ribbon sits at the top of the form and controls three things:

1. **Cloud Identity Selector** — dropdown showing connected cloud accounts. Each account displays its provider icon, name, and account identifier (AWS Account ID, GCP Project ID, Azure Subscription).

2. **Region Selector** — populated with the selected provider's available regions, grouped by geography (US, Europe, Asia Pacific, etc.).

3. **Existing resources** — VPCs, subnets, and other resources you can reuse come from Alethia's
 server-side [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory), which stays current
 automatically (event-driven + a reconciliation sweep). There's no manual refresh.

When the Cloud Identity changes (e.g., switching from an AWS account to a GCP project), the entire form adapts. See [Cloud Provider Abstraction](/concepts/cloud-abstraction) for how this works.

 
Switching providers resets all provider-specific defaults: instance types, K8s versions, database engines, cache node types, and DNS/WAF options. If you're duplicating a project across providers, the platform converts the config automatically with warnings. See [Multi-Cloud Conversion](/concepts/multi-cloud-con

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Sections
records.text:
Name, environment, OpenTofu version 
 VPC/VNet, CIDR, NAT gateway 
 K8s, instance types, node scaling, Karpenter 
 Aurora, Cloud SQL, Azure Database 
 Redis, Valkey, Memorystore 
 DynamoDB, Firestore, Cosmos DB 
 SQS, Pub/Sub, Service Bus 
 Route53, certificates, WAF 
 Password generation, secret stores 
 ECR, Artifact Registry, ACR 
 Git repos for GitOps 
 Real-time pricing estimates

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Review & Submit
records.text:
After filling in sections, the **Review Tab** shows a read-only summary of the entire configuration organized by category. On submit:

1. A `projects` row is created with status `DRAFT`
2. Component rows are created in the relevant tables (`project_network`, `project_cluster`, `project_databases`, etc.)
3. The project appears in the project listing
4. The user can now run Plan → Apply from the [project detail page](/console/dashboard/project-detail)

records.title: Design a Project Overview
records.heading: Form Validation
records.text:
Each section has its own Zod schema. Key validations:

- Project name: lowercase, alphanumeric + hyphens, max 25 characters
- CIDR blocks: regex validation + numeric range check
- Node sizing: min ≤ desired ≤ max
- Instance types: max 5 selected
- Database names: unique per project, lowercase
- Secret names: alphanumeric + hyphens only

On submit failure, the form auto-scrolls to the first error field.

records.title: Messaging
records.text: Messaging. Queues and topics — SQS, SNS, Pub/Sub, Service Bus configuration.

records.title: Messaging
records.heading: Messaging
records.text: The messaging section configures message queues and publish/subscribe topics. Queues and topics are configured independently.

records.title: Messaging
records.heading: Queues
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Queue name 
 **FIFO** Toggle First-In-First-Out ordering guarantee 
 **Visibility Timeout** Number 0–43,200 seconds (displayed as human-readable: s/m/h) 

 
 
 **SQS** (Simple Queue Service)
 - Standard queues: at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering
 - FIFO queues: exactly-once processing, strict ordering (name must end in `.fifo`)
 - Visibility timeout: how long a message is hidden after a consumer reads it
 - Dead letter queues configured automatically
 
 
 **Pub/Sub Subscriptions** (pull mode)
 - No direct FIFO support — use ordering keys for per-key ordering
 - At-least-once delivery by default
 
 When converting from AWS SQS with FIFO enabled, a warning is shown: "GCP Pub/Sub has no direct FIFO support. Consider using ordering keys."
 
 
 
 **Service Bus Queues**
 - FIFO support via Sessions
 - At-least-once or at-most-once delivery modes
 - Dead letter queues built-in

records.title: Messaging
records.heading: Topics
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Topic name 
 **Subscriptions** JSONB (backend) Configured per consumer after provisioning 

 
 
 **SNS** (Simple Notification Service) — fan-out messaging. Subscribers can be SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints, email, or SMS.
 
 
 **Pub/Sub Topics** — message publishing endpoint. Subscribers create pull or push subscriptions.
 
 
 **Service Bus Topics** — publish/subscribe messaging with subscription filters.

records.title: Messaging
records.heading: Cost
records.text: Messaging services are usage-based (per message/request), so they show $0 in the cost sidebar. Actual costs depend on message volume and size.

records.title: Network
records.text: Network. VPC/VNet configuration — create new or use existing, CIDR blocks, NAT gateways.

records.title: Network
records.heading: Network
records.text: The network section configures the virtual network that contains all provisioned resources.

records.title: Network
records.heading: Create New vs. Use Existing
records.text: Two modes controlled by a toggle:

records.title: Network
records.heading: Create New Network
records.text:
Provisions a new network from scratch. Fields:

 Field Description 
 ------- ------------- 
 **CIDR Block** IP address range (e.g., `10.0.0.0/16`) 
 **NAT Gateway** Single (cost-optimized) or HA (one per availability zone) 

 
 
 Creates a **VPC** with:
 - Public and private subnets across availability zones
 - Internet Gateway for public subnets
 - NAT Gateway(s) for private subnet internet access
 - Route tables for each subnet type
 - Default security group
 
 
 Creates a **VPC Network** with:
 - Custom-mode subnets in the selected region
 - Cloud NAT with Cloud Router
 - Firewall rules for internal communication
 
 
 Creates a **Virtual Network (VNet)** with:
 - Subnets for cluster nodes and services
 - NAT Gateway for outbound traffic
 - Network Security Groups (NSGs)

records.title: Network
records.heading: Use Existing Network
records.text:
Instead of creating a network, attach the project to one that **already exists** in the account — its id
comes from Alethia's server-side [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory) (network id,
name, CIDR, region).

**How it works — brownfield attach (AWS · GCP · Azure).** When you pick "existing", Alethia records the
network id and, at deploy time, the [Runner](/runner) does **not** create a network. Instead the OpenTofu
template *reads* your existing network and wires the cluster into it:

- **AWS** — the runner looks up the VPC's subnets and passes them in (public/private split).
- **GCP** — the template `data`-sources the VPC network and resolves its subnetwork in the project's
 region (including that subnet's pod/service secondary ranges) for GKE.
- **Azure** — the template `data`-sources the VNet and uses one of its subnets for AKS.

 
Existing networks must be in the same region as the project. The list comes from Alethia's server-side
[asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory), which stays fresh automatically (a
reconciliation sweep, plus optional event forwarders) — there's no manual refresh.

records.title: Network
records.heading: Reuse existing resources
records.text:
"Use existing network" is one case of a deliberately **narrow** idea: reuse the **shared substrate you
deploy _into_**, instead of creating a duplicate. The test for whether reuse is a provisioning concern is
simple — **does the rest of the infrastructure graph attach to it?**

- ✅ **Network (VPC/VNet + subnets)** — the cluster and nodes attach into its subnets. Available today on
 AWS, GCP, and Azure (a provision-new vs use-existing toggle; at deploy time the template `data`-sources
 the existing network rather than creating one). The list comes from the server-side
 [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory).
- 🚧 **Kubernetes cluster** — your workloads deploy _onto_ it (nobody wants a fresh cluster per app). Same
 toggle, on the roadmap.

That's the whole scope. **Databases, caches, queues, storage, etc. are _not_ "reuse" targets** — nothing
downstream attaches to them in the graph. If a database already exists, there's nothing to provision:
the app simply needs its **connection endpoint + secret**, which is a *connection/config* concern (a value
you supply), not a provisioned component. Trying to model that as "provision vs existing" just reintroduces
a credent

records.title: Network
records.heading: CIDR Block Validation
records.text:
The form validates CIDR notation in real-time and shows a breakdown. For `10.0.0.0/16`:

 Field Value 
 ------- ------- 
 Total addresses 65,536 
 Usable IPs 65,531 
 Range 10.0.0.0 – 10.0.255.255 
 Size Large 

Common ranges:
- `/16` — 65,536 addresses (recommended for production)
- `/20` — 4,096 addresses (suitable for small environments)
- `/24` — 256 addresses (minimal, development only)

records.title: Network
records.heading: NAT Gateway Cost Impact
records.text:
NAT gateways have significant cost implications:

 Mode AWS Cost Description 
 ------ --------- ------------- 
 Single ~$35/mo One NAT in a single AZ (cost-optimized, single point of failure) 
 HA ~$105/mo (3 AZ) One NAT per AZ (high availability, 3x cost) 

The [cost sidebar](/console/design-project/cost-sidebar) reflects the NAT gateway choice immediately.

records.title: Network
records.heading: Allowed CIDR Blocks
records.text: Optional field for restricting inbound access. Enter CIDR ranges that should be allowed to connect to resources within the network. Default: open to the VPC CIDR only.

records.title: NoSQL
records.text: NoSQL. DynamoDB, Firestore, and Cosmos DB table configuration with key schema and billing modes.

records.title: NoSQL
records.heading: NoSQL
records.text: The NoSQL section configures managed NoSQL database tables. Multiple tables can be added per project.

records.title: NoSQL
records.heading: Fields Per Table
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Table/collection name 
 **Hash Key** Text input Partition key name (default: `id`) 
 **Hash Key Type** Select `S` (String), `N` (Number), `B` (Binary) 
 **Range Key** Text input Optional sort key name 
 **Range Key Type** Select `S`, `N`, or `B` 
 **Billing Mode** Select `PAY_PER_REQUEST` or `PROVISIONED` 
 **Point-in-Time Recovery** Toggle Enable continuous backups 
 **Table Type** Select `standard` or `global` (multi-region replication)

records.title: NoSQL
records.heading: Services by Provider
records.text:
**DynamoDB** — fully managed key-value and document database

 - Hash key (partition key) + optional range key (sort key)
 - On-demand or provisioned capacity billing
 - Global tables for multi-region replication
 - KMS encryption at rest
 - Point-in-time recovery (continuous backups up to 35 days)
 
 
 **Firestore** — document-oriented NoSQL database

 - Document-based model (collections → documents → fields)
 - Serverless with automatic scaling
 - Multi-region replication built-in

 
 Firestore uses a document model rather than a key-value model. When converting from DynamoDB, hash/range keys map to document IDs. See [Multi-Cloud Conversion](/concepts/multi-cloud-conversion).
 
 
 
 **Cosmos DB** — globally distributed multi-model database

 - Partition key (maps to hash key)
 - Multiple APIs: SQL (document), Table (key-value), MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin
 - Multi-region writes for global distribution
 - Serverless or provisioned throughput (RU/s)

records.title: NoSQL
records.heading: Billing Modes
records.text:
Mode Description Best For 
 ------ ------------- ---------- 
 **PAY_PER_REQUEST** Pay per read/write operation, no capacity planning Unpredictable workloads, development 
 **PROVISIONED** Reserved read/write capacity units Predictable workloads, cost optimization 

 
NoSQL tables show $0 in the cost sidebar because pricing is usage-based (per operation), not estimable from configuration alone. Actual costs depend on read/write patterns.

records.title: Observability
records.text: Observability. Ship cluster metrics, logs, and traces to Datadog, Grafana Cloud, or Prometheus.

records.title: Observability
records.heading: Observability
records.text: The observability section installs a monitoring stack into the cluster. Unlike DNS or secrets, observability has **no cloud-native default** — it is a pluggable-only component, so enabling it means choosing a provider and connecting its credential.

records.title: Observability
records.heading: Fields
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Enabled** Toggle Master switch for observability 
 **Provider** Select Datadog, Grafana Cloud, or Prometheus 
 **Provider options** Varies Provider-specific configuration (see below)

records.title: Observability
records.heading: Providers
records.text:
Installs the **Datadog agent** via Helm. Requires a connected [Datadog connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) (API key + application key). Choose the **site** (e.g. `datadoghq.com`, `datadoghq.eu`).
 
 
 Installs the **Grafana Agent** remote-writing metrics to Grafana Cloud. Requires a connected [Grafana Cloud connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) (instance ID + API token) and the **remote-write URL**.
 
 
 Installs **kube-prometheus-stack** in-cluster, optionally remote-writing to an external Prometheus-compatible store. The remote-write URL and basic-auth credentials are optional — a local-only install is valid. Set the local **retention** window (days).

records.title: Observability
records.heading: Credentials
records.text:
Provider credentials are connected on the [Connectors](/console/connectors/pluggable) page and encrypted at rest. They are decrypted and injected into the runner's OpenTofu variables at provision time — never stored in the Project snapshot.

 
Observability is a **target-state** component — the connector backends and modules exist; the Project-designer selector is rolling out. Track the [Connectors Architecture](/concepts/connectors) page for the current contract.

records.title: Project Basics
records.text: Project Basics. Project name, environment stage, and OpenTofu (iac_version) version.

records.title: Project Basics
records.heading: Project Basics
records.text: The first section of the Design a Project form establishes the project identity and its default target environment. A project owns **N environments** (`project_environments`); this form seeds the default one.

records.title: Project Basics
records.heading: Fields
records.text:
Field Type Validation Description 
 ------- ------ ----------- ------------- 
 **Project Name** Text input Required, lowercase, a-z0-9 + hyphens, max 25 chars Used in resource naming and OpenTofu state paths 
 **Environment Stage** Select Required `development`, `staging`, or `production` — the environment's `stage` 
 **OpenTofu Version** Select Required The `iac_version` field — default: 1.11.4. Also available: 1.10.5, 1.9.8

records.title: Project Basics
records.heading: Project Name
records.text:
The project name is used extensively:
- Resource naming in OpenTofu (e.g., `{project}-{env}-eks-cluster`)
- State file path (see [OpenTofu State](/concepts/terraform-state))
- Display in the UI (project cards, job listings)

 
Names are enforced lowercase with only letters, numbers, and hyphens. This ensures compatibility with cloud resource naming constraints across all three providers.

records.title: Project Basics
records.heading: Environment Stage
records.text:
`stage` is a property of the **environment**, not the project. A project can own several environments
(dev / staging / prod), each independently provisionable with its own `stage`. The form's three options:

 Stage Purpose Naming Convention 
 ------- --------- ------------------ 
 `development` Dev/testing `{project}-dev-*` 
 `staging` Pre-production `{project}-staging-*` 
 `production` Production workloads `{project}-prod-*` 

The environment stage affects OpenTofu state isolation — each stage gets its own state file, even within the same project and region.

records.title: Repositories
records.text: Repositories. Git repository selection for application deployment via ArgoCD.

records.title: Repositories
records.heading: Repositories
records.text: The repositories section configures the Git repository that ArgoCD watches for application manifests. This is optional — if not configured, ArgoCD is installed but doesn't manage any applications automatically.

records.title: Repositories
records.heading: Fields
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Application Repo** Repository selector Git repo containing Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts

records.title: Repositories
records.heading: Repository Selector
records.text: The selector shows repositories from your connected Git provider (GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket). It fetches the repository list using the stored OAuth token (see [Authentication](/concepts/authentication)).

records.title: Repositories
records.heading: How It Works with ArgoCD
records.text:
When a repository is configured:

1. During DEPLOY, the Runner clones the repo and sets it up as an ArgoCD Application source
2. ArgoCD watches the repo for changes (polling or webhook)
3. Any push to the configured branch triggers ArgoCD to sync the manifests to the cluster
4. The ArgoCD UI shows deployment status, sync state, and health

See [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd) for the full ArgoCD architecture.

 
The repository is accessed using the OAuth token from your Git provider integration. ArgoCD stores a copy of the token as a Kubernetes Secret in the cluster. Token refresh is handled automatically.

records.title: Secrets
records.text: Secrets. Managed secrets with auto-generation, presets, and cloud-native secret stores.

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Secrets
records.text: The secrets section creates entries in your cloud provider's secret management service. Secrets can be auto-generated with configurable parameters or set to custom values after provisioning.

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Fields Per Secret
records.text:
Field Type Description 
 ------- ------ ------------- 
 **Name** Text input Secret name (alphanumeric + hyphens only) 
 **Provider** Select Cloud-native store or a [pluggable connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) 
 **Generate** Toggle Auto-generate a random value 
 **Length** Number 8–128 characters 
 **Special Characters** Toggle Include `!@#$%^&*()` in generated value

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Secrets Provider
records.text:
By default secrets are created in the cluster cloud's native secret store. Select **HashiCorp Vault** to manage them in a Vault instance instead:

- Requires a connected [Vault connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) (Vault address + token).
- Per-Project knobs: the KV **mount path** and **KV version** (1 or 2).
- The cloud-native secret resources are skipped at provision time; Vault entries are managed by the composed module.

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Presets
records.text:
Quick-add buttons create secrets with recommended settings:

 Preset Length Special Chars Use Case 
 -------- -------- -------------- ---------- 
 PostgreSQL Password 32 Yes Database master password 
 API Token 48 No Application API key 
 JWT Secret 64 No Token signing key 
 Redis Password 32 No Cache authentication 
 Custom Secret 32 No User-defined

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Secret Store by Provider
records.text:
**AWS Secrets Manager** — $0.40/secret/month + $0.05 per 10,000 API calls.
 Supports automatic rotation, cross-account access, and integration with RDS for database credential management.
 
 
 **Secret Manager** — $0.06 per 10,000 access operations.
 Supports automatic replication, IAM-based access control, and versioning.
 
 
 **Key Vault** — $0.03 per 10,000 operations.
 Supports secrets, keys, and certificates. Integrates with Azure AD for access control.

records.title: Secrets
records.heading: Integration with Kubernetes
records.text: After provisioning, secrets are synced to Kubernetes via the **External Secrets Operator** (installed by ArgoCD — see [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd)). This creates Kubernetes `Secret` resources from cloud secret store entries, keeping them in sync automatically.

records.title: Getting Started
records.text: Getting Started. Quick start guide — sign up, connect a cloud provider, design a Project, and deploy.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Getting Started
records.text: This guide walks through the complete flow from sign-up to a running Kubernetes cluster with ArgoCD.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- An AWS, GCP, or Azure account with admin-level access
- A GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket account

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Sign Up
records.text:
Open the Console and sign in with a supported social provider (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or Google) or with a 6-digit email OTP. Your account is created automatically on first login.

After sign-in, you'll land on the dashboard — empty, with no projects or jobs.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Connect a Cloud Provider
records.text:
Go to **Connectors** in the sidebar. Connect your cloud account:

 
 
 1. Click **Connect** on the AWS card
 2. Deploy the provided **CloudFormation template** in your AWS account (it creates an IAM OIDC provider trusting the Alethia issuer + a role — no external id)
 3. Paste the resulting **Role ARN** back into Alethia
 4. Alethia verifies the new cloud identity by assuming the role via `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`
 
 
 1. Click **Connect** on the GCP card
 2. Run the provided setup script (or OpenTofu module) — it creates the **Workload Identity Pool** + OIDC provider + provisioner service account in your project
 3. Enter the two values it prints — **Project ID** and **Project Number** (Alethia assembles the WIF config; there is no JSON to copy)
 4. Alethia verifies the new cloud identity via Workload Identity Federation
 
 
 1. Click **Connect** on the Azure card
 2. Run the one-line `az ad sp create --id ` — you consent to Alethia's multi-tenant Entra app in **your** tenant (you register no app, store no secret)
 3. Assign it RBAC roles (Contributor + User Access Administrator) on your subscription
 4. Enter **Tenant ID** and **Subscription ID** (the Client ID is Alethia's own

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Connect a Git Provider
records.text: On the same Connectors page, click **Connect** on GitHub (or GitLab/Bitbucket). This uses standard OAuth — authorize the app, and you're connected.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Design a Project
records.text:
Go to **Design a Project** in the sidebar. Fill out the configuration form:

1. **Project Basics** — name your project, select environment (production/staging/dev)
2. **Network** — create a new VPC or select an existing one
3. **Cluster** — choose K8s version, instance types, node scaling
4. **Database** — add Aurora/Cloud SQL/Azure Database instances
5. **Cache** — add Redis/Valkey instances
6. Additional sections for NoSQL, messaging, DNS, secrets, container registries, and repositories

The **cost sidebar** shows estimated monthly costs as you configure. Click **Create Project** when ready.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Plan
records.text:
From the project detail page, click **Plan**. Select a Runner (or "Any available") to execute. The Runner runs `tofu plan` and streams the output in real-time.

Review the plan: how many resources will be created, what they'll cost.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Apply
records.text: Click **Apply** to provision the infrastructure. The Runner runs the [verify gate](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply#verify-gate) then `tofu apply`, provisions all resources, installs ArgoCD, and reports back with cluster credentials.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: Access Your Cluster
records.text:
Go to **Clusters** in the sidebar. Your new cluster shows:
- Cluster endpoint
- ArgoCD URL (with admin credentials)
- kubeconfig command

 

 
Full cluster provisioning takes ~20 minutes (EKS) due to AWS control plane creation time. The Alethia UI shows real-time progress via log streaming.

records.title: Getting Started
records.heading: What's Next?
records.text:
- **[Dashboard](/console/dashboard)** — manage projects
- **[Design a Project](/console/design-project)** — deep dive into each form section
- **[Jobs](/console/jobs)** — understand job types and monitoring
- **[Concepts](/concepts)** — technical deep dives on security, job queues, real-time, etc.

records.title: Console
records.text: Console. Alethia's web control plane for multi-cloud infrastructure orchestration.

records.title: Console
records.heading: Console
records.text: The Console (`apps/console`) is Alethia's web control plane. It provides a visual interface for configuring infrastructure, managing [cloud identities](/console/connectors), monitoring [jobs](/console/jobs), and viewing provisioned clusters — all from the browser. Alethia is the platform; the Console is the web app you drive it from.

records.title: Console
records.heading: Tech Stack
records.text:
Layer Technology 
 ------- ----------- 
 Framework Next.js 15 (App Router, Server Actions) 
 Database PostgreSQL + Drizzle ORM with org-scoped Row Level Security 
 Auth Better Auth (social OAuth: GitHub, Google native; GitLab, Bitbucket genericOAuth; email 6-digit OTP) 
 Realtime Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE ([how it works](/concepts/realtime)) 
 Storage S3-compatible storage (default SeaweedFS) for OpenTofu state and plan artifacts 
 UI Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Zustand state management

records.title: Console
records.heading: Key Pages
records.text:
Overview stats, project management, project detail with services and infrastructure.
 
 
 11-section form for configuring multi-cloud infrastructure with real-time cost estimation.
 
 
 Connect AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, and Hetzner cloud accounts (each a cloud identity) plus GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket Git providers.
 
 
 Monitor infrastructure operations with real-time log streaming.
 
 
 Register, monitor, and manage Runner execution agents.
 
 
 View provisioned Kubernetes clusters with ArgoCD access.
 
 
 Runner, CLI, and Auth API endpoints.

records.title: Console
records.heading: Architecture
records.text:
A[Next.js App Router]
 A --> SA[Server Actions Drizzle · org-scoped RLS]
 A --> API[API Routes Runner + CLI token auth]
 A --> SSE[LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE]
 SSE --> ST[Live updates → browser stores]
`} />

All database access goes through Drizzle with org-scoped RLS policies enforcing per-org data isolation through a Policy Decision Point (PDP). Server Actions set the current org context (`app.current_org` via `set_config`) automatically.

records.title: Console
records.heading: State Management
records.text:
The Console uses five Zustand stores for client-side state, each with a 30-second stale threshold and real-time event handlers:

 Store Purpose 
 ------- --------- 
 `useProjectStore` Form pricing data during project creation 
 `useJobsStore` Job list with filters, pagination, real-time upserts 
 `useRunnersStore` Runner list with deploy/update/destroy actions 
 `useClustersStore` Active clusters from provisioned projects 
 `useCloudProviderStore` Selected provider, identity, cached resources 

Real-time subscriptions are established on dashboard layout mount and feed updates into these stores. See [Real-time Architecture](/concepts/realtime) for details.

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.text: Jobs Overview. Job list page with status and type filtering, search, and real-time updates.

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Jobs
records.text: Every infrastructure operation is modeled as a **job**. The Jobs page shows all jobs with filtering, search, and real-time status updates. For the technical deep dive on how jobs are queued and claimed, see [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Job List Page
records.text: Located at `/dashboard/jobs`, the page shows:

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Filters
records.text:
**Status filters** (button group):
- All, QUEUED, PROCESSING, SUCCESS, FAILED, CANCELLED

**Type filters** (button group):
- All Types, DEPLOY, PLAN, DESTROY, DEPLOY_RUNNER, UPDATE_RUNNER, DESTROY_RUNNER, DETECT_DRIFT

**Search** — filter by job ID, Runner name, or project name.

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Data Table
records.text:
Column Description 
 -------- ------------- 
 Type Job type with icon 
 Status Color-coded badge 
 Project Target project name 
 Runner Assigned Runner name 
 Started Timestamp 
 Completed Timestamp 
 Duration Elapsed time 

Click any row to navigate to the [job detail page](/console/jobs/job-detail).

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Real-time Updates
records.text: The jobs store receives real-time events from the dashboard layout's LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE subscription. New jobs appear automatically, and status changes update badges in-place without page refresh. See [Real-time Architecture](/concepts/realtime).

records.title: Jobs Overview
records.heading: Runner Select Popover
records.text:
When triggering Plan, Apply, or Destroy from a project, the **Runner Select Popover** appears:

- Lists all registered Runners with status indicators (green = ONLINE, yellow = DRAINING, red = OFFLINE)
- "Any available" option for automatic assignment
- Only ONLINE Runners can accept new jobs

The selected Runner's ID is stored as `assigned_runner_id` on the job.

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.text: Job Detail & Logs. Real-time log streaming, status polling, and job actions.

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Job Detail & Logs
records.text: The job detail page (`/dashboard/jobs/{id}`) shows the full execution state of a job with real-time log streaming. For the underlying streaming architecture, see [Real-time Architecture](/concepts/realtime).

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Header
records.text:
- Job type icon and label
- Status badge (animated for PROCESSING)
- Duration (elapsed time, updates live)
- Job ID (first 8 chars, copyable)
- Assigned Runner name
- Created timestamp
- Action buttons

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Log Viewer
records.text:
A full-height, monospace, auto-scrolling log area showing the runner's `tofu` (OpenTofu) output in real-time:

- **STDOUT** — normal output (white/light gray)
- **STDERR** — errors and warnings (red)
- **SYSTEM** — platform messages like "Assuming IAM role..." (blue)

Logs are streamed via a Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE subscription on `provision_job_logs` INSERT events, filtered by `job_id`. New chunks appear within ~2–3 seconds of the Runner writing them.

Auto-scroll pins to the bottom as new logs arrive. Scrolling up manually disables auto-scroll until the user scrolls back to the bottom.

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Collapsible Details
records.text:
Three expandable sections below the logs:

- **Job Details** — full metadata (ID, type, status, runner, all timestamps)
- **Config Snapshot** — the project configuration at time of job creation (JSON)
- **Execution Metadata** — OpenTofu outputs, cost data, cluster info, and the **`verify_result`** report (JSON)

For PLAN and DEPLOY jobs, `execution_metadata["verify_result"]` carries the [verify gate](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply#verify-gate) report — the fail-closed policy evaluation over the plan JSON. The Console renders it as the **Verify Report** in the project's Plan tab; see [engine internals](/elench/engine-internals).

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Actions
records.text:
Button When Visible Effect 
 -------- ------------- -------- 
 **Cancel** QUEUED or PROCESSING Sets status to CANCELLED, Runner terminates gracefully 
 **Apply** PLAN job with SUCCESS status Opens Runner Select Popover → creates DEPLOY job 
 **Re-run** Any terminal status (SUCCESS, FAILED, CANCELLED) Creates a new job with the same configuration

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Status Polling
records.text: The page polls `getJobStatus(jobId)` every 3 seconds until the job reaches a terminal state. This is a lightweight query that returns only `status` and `error_message` — the full log streaming happens via the Realtime subscription.

records.title: Job Detail & Logs
records.heading: Status Banner
records.text:
When the job reaches a terminal state, a banner appears:

- **SUCCESS** — green banner with checkmark
- **FAILED** — red banner with error message
- **CANCELLED** — gray banner with "Cancelled by user"

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.text: Plan & Apply. The Plan-Review-Apply workflow for infrastructure provisioning.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Plan & Apply
records.text: Infrastructure provisioning follows a strict **Plan-Review-Apply** workflow. Nothing is provisioned without explicit user confirmation.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Generate Plan
records.text:
From the [project detail page](/console/dashboard/project-detail), click **Plan**. Select a Runner to execute. A `PLAN` job is queued.

The Runner:
1. Mints short-lived cloud credentials via keyless OIDC ([how](/concepts/authentication))
2. Generates OpenTofu variable files (`tofu.OverrideTfvarsFromMap`) from the project's config snapshot
3. Runs `tofu init` — state goes through the Console **state proxy** (`backend "http"` → `/api/jobs/{id}/state`), so the runner holds no object-storage credentials
4. Runs `tofu plan -out=plan.tfplan`
5. Runs `tofu show -json` for structured output
6. Evaluates the [verify gate](#verify-gate) over the plan JSON and attaches the `verify_result` report
7. Optionally runs Infracost for cost breakdown ([cost estimation](/concepts/cost-estimation))
8. Uploads the plan artifact and reports resource change counts and costs

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Review Plan
records.text:
The Plan tab in project detail shows:

**Plan Summary Bar:**
- Resources to **create** (green count)
- Resources to **update** (yellow count)
- Resources to **destroy** (red count)
- Estimated monthly cost

**Resource Groups:**
Collapsible sections grouped by resource type (e.g., "AWS EKS", "AWS VPC", "AWS RDS"). Each resource shows:
- Resource address (e.g., `module.eks.aws_eks_cluster.main`)
- Action: create / update / delete
- Per-resource monthly cost (from Infracost)

**Verify Report:**
The Plan tab renders the [verify gate](#verify-gate) result (`execution_metadata["verify_result"]`) — per-control pass / fail / `not_evaluable` outcomes over the plan JSON. See [engine internals](/elench/engine-internals).

**Cost Breakdown:**
Total estimated monthly cost with per-resource itemization.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
Click **Apply** to provision. Select a Runner. A `DEPLOY` job is queued. `DEPLOY` runs through `provisioner.RunDeployV2`.

The Runner:
1. Validates the config hash against the referenced PLAN job — if the project was modified after planning, the apply fails with a hash mismatch error
2. Runs `tofu plan -out` and `tofu show -json` from the config snapshot
3. **Verify gate** — evaluates `packages/core/verify` over the plan JSON; this is **fail-closed** (see [below](#verify-gate))
4. Runs `tofu apply` (only if the gate passes or an authorized override waives it)
5. Installs ArgoCD via Helm / scaffolds its manifests ([GitOps](/concepts/gitops-argocd))
6. Reports cluster metadata back to the Console

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Finalization
records.text:
On DEPLOY success, Alethia's `finalizeDeployment()` server action:
- Updates `project_cluster` with cluster name, endpoint, ARN, ArgoCD URL and password
- Updates `project_databases` with writer/reader endpoints, credentials ARNs
- Updates `project_caches` with primary/reader endpoints
- Sets the environment's provisioning `status` (on `project_environments`) to `ACTIVE`

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Verify gate
records.text:
Between `tofu plan` and `tofu apply`, the runner runs the [Elench](/elench) verification gate
(`packages/core/verify`, invoked from `provisioner.RunDeployV2`) over the OpenTofu **plan JSON**. It
evaluates the plan against a catalog of security controls (keyless auth, least-privilege, OIDC-subject
pinning) and produces a structured `verify.Report` — per-control `pass`, `fail`, or an honest
`not_evaluable` for anything the plan JSON can't prove.

The gate is **fail-closed**: a hard control failure **blocks before `tofu apply`** unless an authorized
`verify.Override` waives the specific failing controls (audited, expiry-bounded). PLAN jobs evaluate and
attach the report too, so you see the outcome before ever applying.

The report rides back on `execution_metadata["verify_result"]` and the Console renders it in the **Plan
tab**. For the control catalog, the `not_evaluable` honesty rule, and the signed evidence receipt, see
[Engine internals](/elench/engine-internals).

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Config Hash Validation
records.text:
The DEPLOY job references a specific PLAN job. Before applying, the Runner compares:
- The project's current configuration hash
- The plan's configuration hash (captured at plan time)

If they differ (user modified the project after planning), the job fails with:

> "Configuration has changed since the plan was generated. Please re-plan."

This prevents applying a stale plan that doesn't match the current desired state.

records.title: Plan & Apply
records.heading: Destroy Workflow
records.text:
Destroy follows a simpler flow — no plan step required:

1. Click **Destroy** from project detail
2. Select a Runner
3. A `DESTROY` job is queued
4. The Runner runs `tofu destroy -auto-approve`
5. Environment status → DESTROYING → DESTROYED

 
Destroy permanently removes all cloud resources (VPC, cluster, databases, etc.). OpenTofu state is preserved so accidental destroys can be recovered by re-applying, but data in databases will be lost.

records.title: Runners
records.text: Runners. Registering, monitoring, and managing Runner execution agents.

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Runners
records.text: Runners are the execution agents that run OpenTofu (the `tofu` binary) on behalf of users. The Runners page manages their lifecycle. Every runner sits on a two-axis taxonomy — **operator** (`managed` `self`) × **provisioning** (`deployed` `registered`, for `self` only). For how Runners work internally, see the [Runner Agent](/runner) documentation.

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Runners Page
records.text: Located at `/dashboard/runners`, showing:

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Status Filters
records.text: - All, ONLINE, OFFLINE, DRAINING

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Search
records.text: Filter by Runner name.

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Data Table
records.text:
Column Description 
 -------- ------------- 
 Name Runner identifier 
 Operator `managed` (Alethia-operated fleet) or `self` — self runners also show provisioning: `self · deployed` or `self · registered` 
 Region Deployment region/location (managed & self·deployed runners) 
 Status Badge: ONLINE (green), DRAINING (yellow), OFFLINE (red) 
 Version Runner release version 
 Created Registration timestamp 
 Actions Deploy, update, remove buttons

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Active Jobs Section
records.text: A separate table showing jobs currently assigned to Runners (QUEUED, CLAIMED, or PROCESSING).

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Update Alert
records.text: If outdated Runners are detected (version < latest release), an alert appears with an **"Update All"** button that queues `UPDATE_RUNNER` jobs for each outdated Runner.

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Adding a Runner
records.text:
Click **"Add Runner"** to open the registration sheet with two tabs:

 
 
 Provisions a Runner into your cloud account automatically (an existing runner runs OpenTofu to stand it up — the AWS path uses ECS Fargate via `infra/templates/runner/aws`). This is a `self`-operated, `deployed` runner:

 1. Select a cloud identity and region
 2. Click Deploy → queues a `DEPLOY_RUNNER` job
 3. The job provisions: ECS task definition, IAM roles, CloudWatch log group, security groups
 4. After ~2 minutes, the Runner appears as ONLINE

 Deployed Runners support [scale-to-zero](/runner/scale-to-zero) — they shut down when no jobs are queued and restart automatically when needed.
