 For bringing your own Runner — your own IaC (OpenTofu/Terraform), or just `alethia runner start` — on infrastructure you control. This creates a `self`-operated, `registered` runner:

 1. Enter a name for the Runner
 2. Click Register → generates a `runner_id` and `runner_token`
 3. Copy the credentials (shown once, never stored in plaintext)
 4. Set the environment variables on your machine and run the Runner binary

 See [Self-Operated Runners](/runner/self-hosted) for full setup instructions.
 
 

 
**Managed** r

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Status Indicators
records.text:
Status is determined by heartbeat recency:

 Status Meaning Heartbeat 
 -------- --------- ----------- 
 **ONLINE** Ready to accept jobs Within last 60 seconds 
 **DRAINING** Finishing current job, not accepting new ones Sent with DRAINING flag 
 **OFFLINE** Missed heartbeat, jobs will be requeued > 60 seconds ago

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Real-time Updates
records.text: The Runners page subscribes to `runners` table changes and `jobs` changes via [Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE](/concepts/realtime). Status changes, new registrations, and job assignments appear instantly.

records.title: Runners
records.heading: Version Tracking
records.text:
Each Runner reports its version on heartbeat. The page shows:
- Current version per Runner
- Latest available version (from `runner_releases` table)
- **Release Notes** dialog showing the changelog for each version

When a new Runner release is published via the CI pipeline, existing runners are rolled onto the new image via `UPDATE_RUNNER` jobs — ECS force-new-deployment for self·deployed AWS runners; VM replacement for the managed Hetzner fleet.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.text: Enterprise setup. The end-to-end runbook for turning on the licensed Enterprise edition — packages, migrations, OpenFGA, and the license.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Enterprise setup
records.text:
The ordered checklist to take a deployment from Community to
 Enterprise . None of this is needed for the open-source edition — see
[Licensing](/editions/licensing) for the mechanism this turns on.

 
 Everything below is **additive and reversible.** If the license is removed, the deployment degrades back to
 Community behavior without data loss.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A commercial **license key** for Alethia Enterprise.
- The Enterprise package `@alethia/ee` (ships with the Enterprise distribution; absent from the open-source build).

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Install the Enterprise dependencies
records.text:
`@alethia/ee` and its peers add the organization/SSO/OpenFGA machinery:

pnpm install

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: brings in @alethia/ee, @openfga/sdk, @better-auth/sso (+ samlify, jose)
records.text: The core never imports these — they're loaded only through the `lib/enterprise.ts` seam at runtime.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Run the database migrations
records.text:
The org/team/SSO/authz tables ship as ordinary migrations (inert in Community — never written without the
plugins). Apply them:

pnpm -F console db:migrate # applies migrations 0001–0018 + programmables (RLS, triggers)

Then reconcile the Better Auth org/team/SSO tables against the plugin's expected shape:

npx @better-auth/cli generate

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: (Optional) Bring up OpenFGA
records.text:
Enterprise runs fine on the Postgres engine. To use the [OpenFGA fine-grained engine](/access-control/openfga),
start the `enterprise` compose profile (OpenFGA + its own datastore):

docker compose --profile enterprise up -d openfga

Create a store, then point the app at it:

OPENFGA_API_URL=http://openfga:8080
OPENFGA_STORE_ID= 

On boot the app writes the authorization model (generated from the registry) and backfills tuples from your
existing grants and hierarchy. Leaving `OPENFGA_*` unset keeps `PostgresRbacPDP` as the engine.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Activate the license
records.text:
Provide the license so the entitlements (`organizations`, `sso`, `customRoles`, `activityExport`) resolve to
`true`:

ALETHIA_LICENSE_ACTIVE=true # see your license materials for the exact variable/key

Restart the console. The org switcher, Members, Teams, Roles (custom), Access, SSO, and activity export now light
up.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: (Optional) Register an SSO provider
records.text:
In **Settings → Single Sign-On**, register your IdP over [OIDC or SAML](/access-control/sso). Members signing
in with a matching email domain are routed to it and provisioned into the org.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: Verify
records.text:
- Create an organization, invite a second member, and confirm the [invite lifecycle](/access-control/members-and-invitations).
- In **Settings → Access**, grant a role to a member or team, then add a **deny** exception
 ([grants & scopes](/access-control/grants-and-scopes)) and confirm it takes effect.
- If OpenFGA is on, run the parity check — the two engines must agree across roles, scopes, and actions.

records.title: Enterprise setup
records.heading: What you get
records.text:
Once the license is active, the full Enterprise surface is available — see the
[feature matrix](/editions/feature-matrix) and the [Access Control](/access-control) reference.

records.title: Feature matrix
records.text: Feature matrix. The authoritative, capability-by-capability comparison of the open-source Community edition and the licensed Enterprise edition.

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Feature matrix
records.text:
The single source of truth for what each edition includes. Community is free and
open-source (AGPL-3.0); Enterprise is licensed.

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Authorization core
records.text:
Everything here is **open-source** — the authorization model is the same in both editions.

 Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Policy Decision Point (`can` / `enforce` / `bulkCheck` / `listAccessible`) ✅ ✅ 
 Built-in roles — owner · admin · operator · viewer ✅ ✅ 
 Per-resource grants + Org → Project inheritance ✅ ✅ 
 Explicit **deny** with hierarchy + exceptions ✅ ✅ 
 Single-permission grants (not just roles) ✅ ✅ 
 Row-Level Security tenant isolation (the blast wall) ✅ ✅ 
 Full **Activity log** (every action + denials) ✅ ✅ 
 Registry-as-code (resources × actions, exhaustively typed) ✅ ✅

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Organizations & teams
records.text:
Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Personal organization (single owner — you) ✅ ✅ 
 **Multi-member organizations** — ✅ 
 **Member invitations** (email → accept → join) — ✅ 
 **Active-organization switcher** — ✅ 
 **Teams** (group-based grants) — ✅

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Roles & access
records.text:
Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Built-in role catalog (read-only) ✅ ✅ 
 **Custom roles** — the permission-matrix editor — ✅ 
 **Access portal** — AWS-IAM-style grant assignment — ✅ 
 Grant to a **user or a team**, allow/deny, any scope — ✅

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Authentication
records.text:
Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Email OTP + social sign-in (GitHub · Google · GitLab · Bitbucket) ✅ ✅ 
 **SSO — OIDC** (Okta · Entra ID · Google · AWS IAM Identity Center · …) — ✅ 
 **SSO — SAML** — ✅

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Activity
records.text:
Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 Browse the Activity log in the console ✅ ✅ 
 **Export** the Activity log (CSV) — ✅

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: Engine
records.text:
Capability Community Enterprise 
 --- :--: :--: 
 `PostgresRbacPDP` — the Postgres-backed engine ✅ ✅ 
 **OpenFGA fine-grained engine** (ReBAC at scale) — ✅ (optional) 
 Dual-write tuple sync (Postgres → OpenFGA) — ✅ 

 
 The OpenFGA engine is **optional even with a license** — Enterprise runs perfectly on `PostgresRbacPDP`.
 Turn OpenFGA on when you want a dedicated relationship-graph engine. See the
 [OpenFGA operator guide](/access-control/openfga).

records.title: Feature matrix
records.heading: How the line is drawn
records.text:
Community is a **complete single-tenant product**: roles, grants, hierarchy, deny, the PDP, RLS, and the Activity
log all ship open-source. Enterprise is about **organizations of people** — inviting and grouping members,
authenticating them with SSO, authoring fine-grained custom roles, assigning access through the portal, and
(optionally) deciding it with OpenFGA.

Next: [how licensing works](/editions/licensing).

records.title: Editions
records.text: Editions. Alethia is open-core — a free, open-source Community edition and a licensed Enterprise edition. What each includes, and why the split is where it is.

records.title: Editions
records.heading: Editions
records.text:
Alethia is **open-core**. There are two editions, built from the *same codebase*:

- Community — the free, **open-source** edition, licensed **AGPL-3.0**. The complete
 single-tenant product. You can run it for yourself or your team forever, at no cost, with no license key.
- Enterprise — the **licensed** edition (commercial). Everything in Community **plus**
 multi-member organizations, teams, SSO, custom roles, the fine-grained access portal, the OpenFGA policy
 engine, and activity export.

 
 You can **self-host either edition.** "Self-hosting" is about *where Alethia runs* (your own
 infrastructure); "edition" is about *which features are licensed*. Community needs nothing extra; Enterprise
 needs a license key and a couple of extra services (see [Licensing](/editions/licensing)).

records.title: Editions
records.heading: The dividing line
records.text:
The simplest way to think about it:

> **Everything you need to run Alethia for yourself, or for a team that shares one account, is free and
> open-source. The Enterprise edition is about *many people, many identities* — organizations with multiple
> members, the teams and roles to organize them, the SSO to let them in, and the engine to authorize them at
> scale.**

Concretely:

 
 
 The Policy Decision Point, built-in roles, per-resource grants with Org → Project inheritance,
 Row-Level Security tenant isolation, and the authorization Activity log — all open-source. Your account is a
 single-owner "personal organization".
 
 
 Multi-member organizations, teams, member invitations, SSO (OIDC + SAML), configurable custom roles, the
 AWS-IAM-style access portal (allow/deny, per-resource), the OpenFGA fine-grained engine, and activity export.

records.title: Editions
records.heading: Why open-core?
records.text:
The authorization *model* — how a decision is made — is the same in both editions, and it's open. What
Enterprise adds is the machinery for **organizations of people**: ways to invite and group them (members,
teams, invitations), authenticate them (SSO), describe what they can do (custom roles, allow/deny), and decide
it quickly at scale (OpenFGA). None of that is needed to run Alethia for a single tenant — so it isn't in the
free core.

The boundary is enforced in code: **the open-source core never depends on the Enterprise package, and runs
completely without it present.** See the [open-core architecture](/access-control/open-core) for how that's
guaranteed (a one-way dependency + CI guards), and [Licensing](/editions/licensing) for how a license turns
the Enterprise features on.

records.title: Editions
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- The complete [**feature matrix**](/editions/feature-matrix) — every capability, Community vs Enterprise.
- How [**licensing**](/editions/licensing) works (entitlements, activation, graceful degradation).
- The [**Enterprise setup runbook**](/editions/enterprise-setup) — turning Enterprise on, end to end.
- The [**Access Control**](/access-control) section — the full authorization reference.

records.title: Licensing
records.text: Licensing. How the Enterprise license works — entitlements, activation, and graceful degradation back to the open-source defaults.

records.title: Licensing
records.heading: Licensing
records.text:
The Enterprise features are gated by a license. This page explains the mechanism;
the [Enterprise setup runbook](/editions/enterprise-setup) walks through turning it all on.

records.title: Licensing
records.heading: Entitlements, not scattered checks
records.text:
Alethia never sprinkles `if (licensed)` through the code. Instead, the licensed layer answers a small set of
**entitlements**, and the product reads those:

 Entitlement Unlocks 
 --- --- 
 `organizations` Multi-member orgs, members, invitations, the active-org switcher, teams 
 `sso` SSO sign-in (OIDC + SAML) 
 `customRoles` The custom-role editor + the access portal 
 `activityExport` CSV export of the Activity log 

Without a license, **every entitlement is `false`** and the console shows the open-source experience: a single
personal organization, the four built-in roles (read-only), and a browsable (non-exportable) Activity log. The
features aren't hidden behind a paywalled binary — the open-source build simply has nothing to switch them on.

records.title: Licensing
records.heading: Where entitlements come from
records.text:
Entitlements are produced by the Enterprise package (`@alethia/ee`), which the core loads through **one
tolerant seam** (`lib/enterprise.ts`). The rule that makes this open-core:

> **The open-source core never imports the Enterprise package, and runs completely if it's absent.** The seam
> tries to load `@alethia/ee`; if it isn't installed, the core falls back to the Community defaults — nothing
> breaks.

So there are really two ways a deployment is "Community":

1. **The open-source build** — `@alethia/ee` isn't installed at all.
2. **An Enterprise build without an active license** — `@alethia/ee` is present, but the entitlements resolve
 to all-`false`.

Both behave identically: the Community product. See the
[open-core architecture](/access-control/open-core) for the boundary guarantees and CI guards.

records.title: Licensing
records.heading: Graceful degradation
records.text:
Because the seam is tolerant, an Enterprise deployment **never hard-fails** if the license is missing or
expires — it degrades to Community behavior. Members already in an organization keep their data; the org
simply stops accepting new enterprise-only operations until the license is restored.

records.title: Licensing
records.heading: Activating Enterprise
records.text:
At a high level, enabling Enterprise is: install `@alethia/ee`, provide a license, run the extra migrations,
and (optionally) bring up OpenFGA. The full, ordered checklist is in the
[**Enterprise setup runbook**](/editions/enterprise-setup).

 
 Self-hosting an Enterprise deployment requires a commercial license. Running the **open-source Community
 edition** never does. If you only need a single-tenant Alethia, you never touch any of this.

records.title: Agent harness
records.text: Agent harness. One tool registry exposed read-only to external agents, per-agent identities with isolated memory, a stateless executor, and a supervisor that delegates to a colony.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: Agent harness
records.text:
The harness exposes the platform's tools **once** and reuses them everywhere — the in-app agent, and the
customer's own agents (Claude Code, Codex, Gemini) — without ever forking the tool definitions. It then
layers scoped agent identities, isolated memory, and a supervisor on top.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: Tool registry — the single source of truth
records.text:
Every agent tool is an AI-SDK `tool()` definition in `apps/console/lib/ai/tools/`, classified by
**audience** in `registry.ts`:

 Audience Who sees it 
 --- --- 
 `in-app` Dashboard agent only — HITL proposals, canvas-context tools, and job-queuing writes. 
 `external` / `both` Read-only, PDP-gated, stateless — safe to expose to a customer's agent. 

`buildExternalAgentTools()` is the **read-only projection** (`externalToolsOnly`, audience
`external both`). An anti-drift test (`assertAudienceCoverage`) fails CI if a new tool ships without an
explicit audience, so the external surface can never silently widen.

 
 Authorization is **not** re-implemented in the registry. Each tool's `execute` still resolves the
 actor and enforces its PDP verb at call time; the audience map only decides *visibility*.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: The MCP surface — read-only at launch
records.text:
The remote MCP server (`app/api/mcp/route.ts`, OAuth 2.1 via Better Auth + actor binding) consumes
`buildExternalAgentTools()` — so HITL proposals, canvas tools, and job-queuing writes (`scan_repo`) never
reach an external agent. A single endpoint serves Claude Code, ChatGPT, Codex, and Gemini/Zed-via-ACP.

 
 Writes stay **out** of the external surface until an approval-over-MCP design exists: a multi-turn
 HITL approval (propose → human click → resume) does not project onto a single stateless MCP
 `tools/call`. The endpoint is a connector, not the moat — the moat is the governed, provable execution
 behind it.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: The proof surface over MCP
records.text:
The evidence half of elench is readable by an external agent, not just the dashboard:

 Tool Audience Returns 
 --- --- --- 
 `get_plan_result` `both` A job's status + the elench `verify_verdict` / `verify_result` (the signed gate report). 
 `get_drift_posture` `both` The latest day-2 [drift `Posture`](/elench/engine-internals) for a project environment. 
 `audit_infrastructure` `in-app` Queues an `AUDIT` job over a bring-your-own plan / manifests (a job-queuing write → stays in-app). 

So a customer's own agent can ask "is this cluster still in sync, and did the last deploy pass the gate?"
over the read-only MCP surface — the "keep proving it" evidence, not just the build.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: Agents are DATA, not processes
records.text:
A "colony of per-project agents" is realized as **durable per-tenant data on stateless compute**, not
standing daemons (a long-lived in-context process accrues context rot). An agent is a row
(`agent_identities`, migration `0045`): persona, mission, tool-scope, and a memory namespace. A stateless
executor reconstructs context per turn.

Memory lives in `agent_memory`, keyed by `(namespace, path)`. The namespace is the per-tenant prefix
(`org/{orgId}/project/{projectId}`); the path is validated by the tenant-isolation guard
`lib/agent/memory-path.ts`, which **rejects every traversal escape** (`..`, absolute paths outside the
`/memories` mount, backslashes, null bytes) so one tenant can never read another's memory.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: The stateless executor
records.text:
`lib/agent/executor.ts` is the deterministic core of running a turn *as* an agent:

- `buildAgentSystemPrompt(identity, memorySummary?)` composes the persona + mission (+ just-in-time
 memory) into a system prompt.
- `scopeToolsToAgent(tools, tool_scope)` narrows the tool set to the agent's allowlist (least privilege
 per agent — deployer / auditor / cost-optimizer roles).

The agent-scoped chat route (`/api/agent/[agentId]`) loads the identity, applies these, and runs the turn
via the AI SDK — mirroring the main agent route, tools still PDP-gated at execute time.

records.title: Agent harness
records.heading: The supervisor / colony
records.text:
`lib/agent/supervisor.ts` is a deterministic, replayable orchestrator. It delegates tasks to sub-agents
and drives them via a **Magentic-style Task / Progress ledger** (facts + plan + per-task status) with a
**stall counter that triggers a bounded re-plan** (`stall > 2 → re-plan`). The actual sub-agent execution
is **injected** (`SubAgentRunner`) — exactly the "AI executes, deterministic code decides" pattern, so the
control flow is testable without a model and always terminates.

 
 **Use it where it pays.** A standing colony costs roughly 15× the tokens of a single agent and loses on
 work over one convergent state (e.g. IaC plan/apply). The supervisor is for breadth-first **read**
 fan-out (drift / cost / security surveys across accounts) and tenant-isolated tasks; write/converge
 work stays on a single-threaded path.

records.title: Architecture
records.text: Architecture. The full system — repos to a verified GitOps Kubernetes cluster on your cloud. The "A" (infer-and-generate) and "B" (bring-your-own + audit) flows, stage by stage, and where the elench proof layer sits.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Architecture
records.text:
Alethia is **one product**: connect your repos and get a **verified GitOps Kubernetes cluster on your own
cloud**. Two operators — **managed** (Alethia's runner fleet assumes a role into your account) and
**self** (a runner you run) — and two entry flows that share the same design surface, provisioner, and
proof layer:

- **"A" — infer & generate.** Scan repos → infer the stack → propose a project → provision the cluster +
 backing services + ArgoCD → generate app manifests → GitOps deploy → **verify**.
- **"B" — bring your own + audit.** Point at existing OpenTofu/Terraform or Kubernetes manifests →
 **audit** them with the same engine → get a signed report.

 
 **Core vs Enterprise.** The generate/provision **"A" flow is core** (open-source, self-hostable). The
 **elench proof layer — verify · audit · drift · agent · MCP — is Enterprise.** This page is the map;
 the proof-layer pages carry the badge.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The "A" pipeline
records.text:
Each stage names its source-of-truth code so the docs stay honest to the implementation.

 connect 1:N] --> scan[scan Go scanner · per-svc]
 scan --> infer[infer + merge lib/scanner → to-project]
 infer --> propose[propose canvas design]
 propose --> provision[provision RunDeployV2 · tofu]
 provision --> generate[generate manifests .FromServices]
 generate --> gitops[GitOps ArgoCD apps · path .]
 gitops --> verify[VERIFY elench gate + receipt]
 verify --> drift[day-2 drift DETECT_DRIFT → Posture loop]
`} />

1. **Connect repos (1:N, monorepo-aware).** `project_source_repos` (migration `0050`) holds every scanned
 repo + its detected services.
2. **Scan.** `packages/core/scanner` clones + statically reads a repo (never executes it) into a
 `RepoDigest`, and detects per-directory **services** (Dockerfile / workspace manifests) — so a monorepo
 yields several deployable services, not one blob.
3. **Infer + merge.** `apps/console/lib/scanner/infer.ts` turns the digest into an `InferredStack` (LLM,
 structured output); `to-project.ts` `mergeScansToFormData` folds **N repos / services** into one
 guaranteed-valid `ProjectFormData` (union backing needs, de-duped names, one `source_repos`

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The "B" pipeline — bring your own + audit
records.text:
The verification engine is **plan-agnostic**, so auditing infrastructure Alethia *didn't* generate needs
no new controls:

 B[AUDIT job]
 B --> C[verify same controls]
 C --> D[signed report]
 D --> E[VerifyBlock]
`} />

- **OpenTofu / Terraform plan:** `verify.ParseCustomerPlan(planJSON)` → `Evaluate` — the same AWS/GCP/Azure controls.
- **Kubernetes:** `verify.EvaluateManifests(yaml)` → the k8s [control set](/elench/control-catalog).
- The `AUDIT` job (migration `0052`) runs on the runner (`executeAudit`) and posts the report to
 `jobs.execution_metadata.verify_result`, surfaced by the existing `get_plan_result` tool + the
 `VerifyBlock`. Queue it from the assistant (`audit_infrastructure` tool) or `queueAudit`.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The data model
records.text:
Table / field What it holds 
 --- --- 
 `project_source_repos` (0050) Scanned source repos (1:N) + detected services (JSONB). 
 `environment_drift` (0051) Latest day-2 `Posture` per environment. 
 `jobs.job_type` `ANALYZE_REPO` · `PLAN` · `DEPLOY` · `DESTROY` · `DETECT_DRIFT` · `AUDIT` · `*_RUNNER`. 
 `jobs.execution_metadata` `repo_digest` · `verify_result` (the signed report) · `drift_posture`.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
How AWS / GCP / Azure all deploy apps: cloud-agnostic InfraFacts, per-cloud workload identity, and
 manifest generation.
 
 
 The Evaluate seam, the bring-your-own + manifest audit entry points, signed receipts, and the drift
 loop.
 
 
 The core form → snapshot → ProjectConfig → OpenTofu stages the "A" flow rides on.

records.title: Control catalog
records.text: Control catalog. The authored controls per cloud, what each detects, the engine's blind spots, framework mapping, and catalog versioning.

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Control catalog
records.text:
The current catalog is **`elench-controls-0.4.0`**. `Evaluate` detects the plan's cloud and runs that
provider's control set (a mixed or unknown plan runs all of them, so nothing is silently skipped). Every
control records its `provider`, so a uniform posture UI can never imply coverage a control does not have.

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: AWS
records.text:
ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `KEYLESS-001` No static IAM access keys creating an `aws_iam_access_key` (use OIDC federation instead) 
 `OIDC-001` Federated trust bound to a specific subject a federated (`sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`) role whose trust **lacks a `:sub` condition**, or binds `sub` only with a **`StringLike` wildcard** (the "any repo can assume" footgun) 
 `LEASTPRIV-001` No over-broad IAM grants (named patterns) `Action:"*"` + `Resource:"*"`; `Allow` + `NotAction`; attaching `AdministratorAccess` / `PowerUserAccess` / `IAMFullAccess`. **Warns** on sensitive service wildcards (`iam:*`, `kms:*`, …) with `Resource:"*"`. **Not-evaluable** when the policy body is computed or referenced only by ARN. 
 `ACCESS-ANALYZER-001` No sensitive actions grantable (opt-in) a policy AWS IAM Access Analyzer proves *could* grant any action in a sensitive denylist.

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: GCP
records.text:
ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `GCP-KEYLESS-001` No static service-account keys creating a `google_service_account_key` (use Workload Identity) 
 `GCP-WIF-001` WIF providers are attribute-conditioned a `google_iam_workload_identity_pool_provider` with an empty `attribute_condition` 
 `GCP-LEASTPRIV-001` No project-wide primitive roles binding `roles/owner` (fail) — **warns** on `roles/editor` — via `google_project_iam_member`/`_binding`

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Azure
records.text:
ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `AZURE-KEYLESS-001` No static app/SP secrets creating an `azuread_application_password` / `azuread_service_principal_password` (use a federated identity credential) 
 `AZURE-FED-001` Federated credentials bind a subject an `azuread_application_federated_identity_credential` with an empty or wildcard `subject` 
 `AZURE-LEASTPRIV-001` No Owner/Contributor assignments an `azurerm_role_assignment` of `Owner` (fail) — **warns** on `Contributor` — scope annotated

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Alibaba Cloud
records.text:
Alibaba has a real RAM/OIDC authority surface — **RRSA** (RAM Roles for Service Accounts) is its
workload-identity mechanism, the analogue of AWS IRSA / GCP WIF — so its control set mirrors the AWS
one. RAM trust and policy documents share AWS IAM's statement shape; the one Alibaba-specific
difference is the trust action (`sts:AssumeRole`, not `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`).

 ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `ALI-KEYLESS-001` No static RAM access keys creating an `alicloud_ram_access_key` (use RRSA / AssumeRoleWithOIDC instead) 
 `ALI-OIDC-001` RRSA trust bound to a specific subject an `alicloud_ram_role` whose Federated (RRSA) trust **lacks an `oidc:sub` condition**, or binds it only with a **`StringLike` wildcard** (any pod from the issuer can assume). Service-principal roles are out of scope; a computed trust doc → **not_evaluable**. 
 `ALI-LEASTPRIV-001` No over-broad RAM grants (named patterns) an `alicloud_ram_policy` granting `Action:"*"` on `Resource:"*"` or using `Allow` + `NotAction`; or a **role/user/group** policy attachment of an admin-family System policy (`AdministratorAccess`, `AliyunRAMFullAccess` — control of RAM itself is admin one hop awa

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Hetzner
records.text:
Hetzner is **token-auth** — the API token is the ceiling, with no OIDC/federation/IAM surface to bind —
so its controls assert **network/firewall posture**, the attack surface a Hetzner plan can actually
misconfigure. They are tuned so the shipped Talos template evaluates to **warns only** (never a
hard block of the nightly apply).

 ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `HCLOUD-FW-001` Every server is behind a firewall an `hcloud_server` with **no firewall anywhere** — no known `firewall_ids`, no configuration reference to an `hcloud_firewall`, and no `hcloud_firewall_attachment` covering it (a bare public node). **Not-evaluable** (never a false brick) for unresolvable expressions, `label_selectors` / `apply_to` selection, or a plan without a configuration section. 
 `HCLOUD-NET-001` No world-open SSH; management ports flagged a firewall rule opening **tcp/22** to the whole internet — including split-CIDR spellings that union to full coverage (Talos runs no SSH daemon; world-open 22 is always wrong). **Warns** on world-open Kubernetes API `6443` / Talos apid `50000`/`50001` (open by design today), any other world-open inbound rule, and very broad partial sources on

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Kubernetes
records.text:
A separate input path — `EvaluateManifests(yaml)` — decodes (multi-doc) manifests and runs the k8s set
(`provider` = `k8s`). It backs the [bring-your-own audit](/elench/engine-internals) and grades the
manifests Alethia *generates* (which pass by construction).

 ID Title Hard-fail on 
 ---- ------- -------------- 
 `CONTAINERSECURITY-001` Containers run non-root, unprivileged, on pinned images a `privileged` container, `runAsUser: 0`, or a mutable `:latest` / untagged image. **Warns** when `runAsNonRoot` is unset (may run as root). 
 `RBAC-001` No wildcard RBAC or anonymous bindings a `ClusterRole`/`Role` rule granting `"*"` verbs on `"*"` resources in `"*"` apiGroups; a binding to `system:anonymous` / `system:unauthenticated` 
 `HOSTACCESS-001` Pods don't reach the host namespace or filesystem `hostNetwork` / `hostPID` / `hostIPC: true`, or a `hostPath` volume 
 `RESOURCES-001` Containers declare CPU + memory limits a container missing CPU and/or memory `limits` (**warn** — reliability, not a breach) 

Coverage is honest the same way the cloud controls are: a control with no in-scope resources is a vacuous
pass, and workload kinds without a decodable pod spec are simply not count

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Blind spots
records.text:
The gate inspects what the plan JSON can actually show. It is honest about the rest rather than
 guessing:

 - **Computed bodies** (`after_unknown`) — interpolated IAM/trust/bucket policies → `not_evaluable`.
 - **Managed-policy bodies** — AWS-managed (non-admin) policies attached by ARN, and Alibaba's
 non-admin System policies, have no body in the plan → coverage gap, not a pass.
 - **Cross-state / cross-module** — resources created in another state or module, permission
 boundaries, and SCPs are out of view.
 - **AWS is deepest; Alibaba mirrors it.** GCP/Azure cover the static-credential, federation-binding,
 and primitive-role cases; Hetzner is network **posture** (token-auth has no authority surface to
 audit); the per-cloud `provider` on each control + the report makes coverage explicit.

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Framework mapping
records.text:
Each control carries a small, conservative set of framework references (CIS / SOC 2 criteria), surfaced
in the posture and the receipt. These map to controls, not certification — see
[security & compliance readiness](/standards/security-and-compliance) for how the evidence supports an
audit rather than replacing one.

 
 A wrong control-to-framework mapping (e.g. claiming a control satisfies "SOC 2 CC6.1" when it does
 not) is **worse than no mapping** — it manufactures false assurance. Mappings are kept deliberately
 small, owned, and reviewed, never auto-generated.

records.title: Control catalog
records.heading: Catalog versioning
records.text:
`CatalogVersion` is part of the evidence. **Bump it whenever a control's logic changes in a way that
could alter a verdict** — an old receipt is only meaningful against the catalog (and provider schema)
that produced it. The version is recorded on every report and sealed into the receipt.

records.title: Verification engine
records.text: Verification engine. How the deterministic policy gate works — the Evaluate seam, statuses, fail-closed gating, overrides, Access Analyzer corroboration, signed receipts, and drift.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Verification engine
records.text:
The engine (`packages/core/verify`) is a deterministic policy gate over the OpenTofu **plan JSON**. It
runs **between `tofu plan` and `tofu apply`** inside `provisioner.RunDeployV2`, and it is **fail-closed**:
a hard control failure blocks the apply before any infrastructure changes.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: The Evaluate seam
records.text:
The whole engine sits behind one entrypoint:

func Evaluate(ctx context.Context, plan *tfjson.Plan) (*Report, error)

It is deterministic and side-effect free — the same plan always yields the same verdict, which is what
the evidence receipt relies on. The seam is **engine-agnostic**: Phase 0 ships a pure-Go evaluator
(builds and tests offline, no external policy runtime); an OPA/Rego backend for customer-authorable
control bundles can be swapped in behind the identical `Report` contract without touching any caller.

`EvaluateWithOptions(ctx, plan, opts)` is the same path with optional AWS Access Analyzer corroboration
(below).

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Three ways in — same Report
records.text:
The engine is plan-agnostic, so the same `Report` (and receipt) shape serves three inputs:

 Entrypoint Input Use 
 --- --- --- 
 `Evaluate(plan)` An Alethia-generated OpenTofu plan The apply-time gate. 
 `ParseCustomerPlan(json)` → `Evaluate` A **bring-your-own** `tofu show -json` plan Audit infrastructure Alethia didn't generate — **zero control changes**. 
 `EvaluateManifests(yaml)` Kubernetes manifests (multi-doc) The k8s [control set](/elench/control-catalog); `Provider` = `k8s`. 

The **"B" flow** (bring-your-own + audit) rides the latter two: an `AUDIT` job (migration `0052`) runs on
the runner (`executeAudit`) — parsing the customer's plan or manifests, evaluating them, and posting the
report to `jobs.execution_metadata.verify_result`. It provisions nothing; the console surfaces it through
the same `get_plan_result` tool + `VerifyBlock` a PLAN job's gate uses. Queue it with the
`audit_infrastructure` agent tool or the `queueAudit` action, or run it locally with
[`elench-verify -manifests`](/elench/extending).

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Statuses and the verdict
records.text:
Every control reports one of four statuses, and the report's overall verdict follows a strict
precedence:

 Status Meaning 
 --- --- 
 `pass` The control was evaluated and the plan satisfies it. 
 `fail` Evaluated and violated. On a real apply this **blocks** (unless overridden). 
 `warn` A recorded concern that does not block. 
 `not_evaluable` The plan JSON lacked the information to judge it. **Never** treated as a pass. 

> **Verdict precedence:** `fail` › `warn` › `not_evaluable` › `pass`.

The `not_evaluable` rung is the heart of the engine's honesty.

 
 **Never a silent pass.** A policy body that is computed until apply (`after_unknown`), or an
 AWS-managed policy referenced only by ARN (whose body is not in the plan), is reported `not_evaluable`
 with a plain-language coverage note — never a quiet `pass`. A silent pass on an un-inspectable
 resource is exactly the false result the verification claim must never make.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Fail-closed, with a real exception path
records.text:
A fail-closed gate with no escape hatch gets disabled within a week. Elench ships the exception path
**with** the gate:

- A `verify.Override` is an **authorized, time-boxed** waiver of *specific* control ids — never a way to
 disable the gate wholesale.
- It is persisted on the deploy job (`jobs.verify_override`, migration `0043`), stamped server-side with
 the authorizing actor and an expiry by the `recordVerifyOverride` action.
- The runner reconstructs it (`buildVerifyOverride`) and passes it to the gate; `Report.Unwaived(ov)`
 returns any failing control the override does **not** cover. A non-empty result keeps the apply blocked.
- The waiver is recorded in the evidence receipt as an exception (who, why, expiry) — the verdict itself
 is **not** rewritten to pass.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: AWS IAM Access Analyzer corroboration
records.text:
Beyond the pattern checks, the gate can corroborate IAM policies with **AWS IAM Access Analyzer's
automated reasoning** — a *provable* statement about what a policy could grant, run pre-apply without
deploying anything.

- `verify.PolicyChecker` is the seam (the pure `verify` package carries no cloud SDK); the AWS-backed
 implementation lives in `packages/core/accessanalyzer` and calls `CheckAccessNotGranted` per action.
- Control `ACCESS-ANALYZER-001` fails when a planned policy could grant any action in a sensitive-action
 denylist; a checker error is `not_evaluable`, never a pass.
- It is **off by default** (no AWS calls). The runner enables it for AWS jobs when
 `ALETHIA_VERIFY_ACCESS_ANALYZER=1`, building the checker from the assumed-role config.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Signed evidence receipt
records.text:
After the gate decides (and any override is applied), the runner seals a **`Receipt`** for the apply and
signs it (ed25519 detached signature → tamper-evident; offline `Verify`).

 
 

 Field Why 
 --- --- 
 `plan_sha256` Binds the receipt to the exact plan evaluated. 
 `tofu_version`, `catalog_version` A verdict is only meaningful against the schema + controls that produced it. 
 `report` The full per-control result, including `not_evaluable` and coverage. 
 `exception` Any recorded override (controls, reason, by, expiry). 
 `runner`, `evaluated_at` Provenance. 

 
 

A receipt signed by Alethia's own runner is **tamper-evidence on self-attestation** — it proves "Alethia's
runner asserted these controls passed on plan `sha256` and the file wasn't edited since", not independent
compliance proof. To make it meaningful to a customer's auditor, anchor the root of trust outside Alethia:
sign with a **customer-controlled key** and/or log the signature to a **transparency log (Rekor)**. The
key is loaded from `ALETHIA_RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY`; with none configured the receipt is attached
**unsigned** rather than blocking the apply — signing is additive evidence, not a precondition.

records.title: Verification engine
records.heading: Continuous drift — "keep proving it"
records.text:
`packages/core/drift` turns a `tofu plan -refresh-only -json` into a per-environment **`Posture`**
(`in_sync`, `drifted` count, per-resource `kind` ∈ modified/deleted/other) via `Analyze(plan)`. The full
loop is closed end-to-end:

 schedule] --> B[DETECT_DRIFT job migration 0044]
 B --> C[runner refresh-only → Analyze]
 C --> D[persist job-status route]
 D --> E[environment_drift 0051 · latest per env]
 E --> F[read get_drift_posture + MCP]
`} />

1. A tiered scheduler (`lib/drift/schedule` + `dispatch`) enqueues checks per environment on a cadence
 (production most often) through the cron route `/api/internal/drift/sweep` (`sweepDriftSchedule`).
2. `DETECT_DRIFT` (migration `0044`) runs on the runner (`executeDriftDetection`) and posts the `Posture`.
3. The job-status route persists it to **`environment_drift`** (migration `0051`, latest per environment)
 via `recordDriftPosture`.
4. `getLatestDriftPosture` reads it back for the UI, the assistant, and — via the
 [`get_drift_posture`](/elench/agent-runtime) tool (audience `both`) — external agents over MCP.

 
 **Coverage is stated, not hidden.** A refresh-only plan only sees resources in state, so it detects
 *modified* and *dele

records.title: Extending & running
records.text: Extending & running. Adding a control, the injection seams that keep AI advisory, the elench-verify CLI for CI, and the gated live exercises.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: Write the control
records.text:
Add a `control…` function in `packages/core/verify` returning a `ControlResult`. Use `resolveStatus` so
the pass / fail / warn / not_evaluable / vacuous-pass precedence stays consistent, and reuse the policy
helpers (`parseIAMPolicy`, `attrUnknown`, `toStringSlice`) — they already handle string-vs-array IAM
fields and unknown values.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: Add a fixture and a test
records.text:
Drop a plan-JSON fixture under `testdata/` and a case in the test suite. **Always include a
`not_evaluable` case** for any control that reads a body that can be computed — that path is the
engine's honesty guarantee.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: Bump the catalog version
records.text:
Change `CatalogVersion` whenever the new (or changed) control could alter a verdict. Old receipts are
only meaningful against the catalog that produced them.
 

 

A **Kubernetes** control is the same shape in `packages/core/verify/k8s.go`: add a `control…` function over
the decoded `k8sResource` list, wire it into `EvaluateManifests`, and add a fixture (an insecure manifest
→ fail, a clean one → pass). Reuse the `podSpec` / `containers` helpers.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: Adding a cloud
records.text:
Extending the provisioner + GitOps seam to a new cloud is a separate, documented checklist — the
`CloudProvider` impl, the OpenTofu template + keyless workload-identity outputs, runner credential
activation, and the per-cloud ArgoCD facts. See **[Multi-cloud](/elench/multi-cloud)** and
`packages/core/cloud/README.md`.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: The injection seams — "AI proposes, the gate disposes"
records.text:
Elench keeps AI strictly advisory through a set of injected interfaces, so the deterministic engine
always has the final say:

 Seam Role 
 --- --- 
 `PolicyChecker` AWS Access Analyzer corroboration (real implementation in `packages/core/accessanalyzer`). 
 `Remediator` Proposes a fix + re-plans; the loop only accepts a gate-confirmed result. 
 `SubAgentRunner` Runs one delegated colony task; the supervisor decides what to do with the outcome. 
 injected model call The LLM in `explainFindings` / the agent runner — swappable, so logic is tested with a fake. 

The safety spine is `ReVerify(original, candidatePlan)`: it re-runs the gate on a proposed fix and reports
which original failures it **resolved**, which **still fail**, and which it made **newly fail**. A fix is
`Accepted` only when every original failure is resolved with no regression. `RunRemediationLoop` wraps it
in a bounded harness so "AI proposes, the gate disposes" can never loop forever or apply an unverified fix.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: The elench-verify CLI
records.text:
`cmd/elench-verify` runs the gate over any plan JSON outside the runner — for local checks, CI gating, or
producing the go/no-go corpus measurement:

go build -o elench-verify ./packages/core/verify/cmd/elench-verify
tofu show -json tfplan elench-verify # human report; exit 2 if the verdict blocks
elench-verify -json plan.json # machine-readable report on stdout
elench-verify -manifests deploy.yaml # audit Kubernetes manifests instead of a plan

The default mode accepts a **bring-your-own** plan (via `ParseCustomerPlan`), not just an Alethia-generated
one — the same controls run either way. `-manifests` runs the k8s control set. Exit codes: `0`
pass/warn/not_evaluable · `2` blocking (fail) · `1` usage/parse error — so it drops straight into a CI step.

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: Gated live exercises
records.text:
The deterministic logic is unit-tested with injected fakes; the live integrations are proven by **gated**
tests (skipped in normal CI, run explicitly) that exercise the real services end-to-end:

 
 

ELENCH_LIVE=1 ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-… \
 pnpm -F console exec vitest run tests/lib/agent/llm-live.test.ts

Drives the supervisor → LLM sub-agent runner → **real Claude model** → parse → completion.

 
 

ELENCH_LIVE_AWS=1 AWS_REGION=us-east-1 \
 go test ./packages/core/accessanalyzer -run TestLive -v

Calls the **real `CheckAccessNotGranted` API**: an admin policy is reported as granting the denied
actions; a scoped policy grants none.

 
 

bash

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: verify a real OpenTofu plan
records.text: tofu show -json plan.out elench-verify # blocks an admin policy (exit 2)

records.title: Extending & running
records.heading: remediation re-verify across two real plans
records.text:
ELENCH_PLAN_BAD=bad.json ELENCH_PLAN_FIXED=fixed.json \
 go test ./packages/core/verify -run TestLiveReVerifyRealPlans -v

`ReVerify(bad, fixed)` over genuine `tofu show -json` output resolves the failing control with no
regression (`accepted=true`).

 
 

 
 These tests stay **gated** so CI remains deterministic and never spends credits or hits a cloud; the
 env flags above run them on demand against real services.

records.title: Elench
records.text: Elench. The verification layer and agent harness — Alethia generates infrastructure and proves it, then keeps proving it.

records.title: Elench
records.heading: Elench
records.text:
**Elench** (Greek _elenchus_ — proof by cross-examination) is Alethia's verification layer and agent
harness. The thesis is simple: don't just generate infrastructure — **scrutinise it and produce
evidence**. Every change, human- or agent-initiated, runs through a deterministic gate between
`tofu plan` and `tofu apply`, and leaves a signed evidence receipt; drift re-checks keep proving it
after apply.

 
 **Honest framing.** Elench produces *evidence that named, versioned controls passed on a specific
 plan* — not a claim of "provably compliant" or "guaranteed least-privilege". A verdict is
 reproducible **given the same plan**; compliance is an auditor's judgment, not a property of one
 apply. The docs here are deliberately precise about what the engine can and cannot see.

records.title: Elench
records.heading: The load-bearing principle
records.text:
> The **deterministic engine is the trusted verdict. The LLM only proposes** — and every proposal is
> re-validated by that engine before it is trusted.

This division of labor runs through the whole system. The policy gate over the OpenTofu plan JSON is
pure, reproducible code. An LLM may explain a failing control or suggest a fix, but a suggested fix is
offered only if re-running the gate confirms it resolves the failure with no regression. The model is
never the gate and has no write path.

records.title: Elench
records.heading: Layered architecture
records.text:
native AI SDK MCP
 EXT --> remote MCP MCP
 MCP[Exposure — one adapter: remote MCP server read-only at launch]
 MCP --> CORE
 CORE[Elench core Tool Registry SSOT · agent-as-data runtime · supervisor]
 CORE --> calls ACT[Platform actions PDP-gated]
 CORE --> calls VER[Verification engine plan-JSON gate · override · signed receipt · drift]
 VER --> new verify stage RUN[Go runner · OpenTofu]
`} />

Two halves, joined at the runner:

- **The verification engine** (`packages/core/verify`, `packages/core/drift`,
 `packages/core/accessanalyzer`) — the deterministic gate, the signed receipt, and continuous drift.
 This is the headline.
- **The agent harness** (`apps/console/lib/ai/tools`, `lib/agent/*`, `app/api/mcp`) — one tool registry
 exposed read-only to external agents, scoped per-agent identities with isolated memory, and a
 supervisor that delegates to a colony of sub-agents.

records.title: Elench
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
Start with the **[Architecture](/elench/architecture)** page for the full repos → verified-GitOps-cluster
system (the "A" infer-and-generate flow and the "B" bring-your-own + audit flow); the pages below go deep
on each layer.

 
 
 The whole A→B system — scan → provision → generate → GitOps → verify → drift — and where the elench
 proof layer sits. Core; the proof layers are Enterprise.
 
 
 How AWS / GCP / Azure all deploy apps: cloud-agnostic InfraFacts, per-cloud workload identity, and
 manifest generation.
 
 
 The Evaluate seam, statuses and fail-closed gating, the override workflow, Access Analyzer
 corroboration, signed receipts, and drift posture.
 
 
 The AWS / GCP / Azure controls, what each detects, the blind spots, framework mapping, and catalog
 versioning.
 
 
 The tool-registry SSOT, the read-only MCP surface, agents-as-data + memory isolation, the stateless
 executor, and the supervisor/colony.
 
 
 Adding a control, the injection seams ("AI proposes, the gate disposes"), the `elench-verify` CLI,
 and the gated live exercises.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.text: Multi-cloud. How AWS, GCP and Azure all deploy apps — the cloud-agnostic GitOps seam, per-cloud workload identity, manifest generation, and the checklist for adding a cloud.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Multi-cloud GitOps seam
records.text:
The provisioner was already ~95% cloud-agnostic — the `CloudProvider` interface, `ProviderTfvars`, and
`ConfigureKubeconfig` all work for AWS, GCP and Azure. The **one** thing keeping GCP/Azure from actually
*deploying apps* was the GitOps seam: `argocd.BuildFromOutputs` + the ArgoCD templates were
AWS/IRSA/EKS-hardcoded. That seam is now `Provider`-discriminated, so all three clouds render a working
ArgoCD layer.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Cloud-agnostic InfraFacts
records.text:
`packages/core/argocd/infra_facts.go` carries **common facts** (cluster name/endpoint, region, DNS) plus a
**per-cloud workload-identity block**, and `BuildFromOutputs` extracts the right outputs per provider:

 Provider Cluster output Workload identity external-dns SA annotation 
 --- --- --- --- 
 AWS `eks_cluster_name` IRSA role ARN `eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn` 
 GCP `gke_cluster_name` Workload Identity SA email (`external_dns_service_account`) `iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account` 
 Azure `aks_cluster_name` Federated Identity client id (`external_dns_client_id`) `azure.workload.identity/client-id` 

`InfraFacts.DNSProvider()` maps the cloud to the external-dns `provider` value (`aws` / `google` /
`azure`). The ArgoCD templates (`infra/templates/argocd/*.yaml`) switch on `.Provider`; AWS-only apps
(`aws-load-balancer-controller`, `karpenter`, `storage-class-gp3`) are guarded so they **render empty →
are skipped** on other clouds. `render_test.go` pins AWS-unchanged and asserts the GCP/Azure output.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Per-cloud workload identity, keyless
records.text:
Each cloud binds the in-cluster `external-dns` service account to a cloud identity with **no static key**:

- **GCP** — `infra/templates/project/gcp/workload-identity.tf`: a Google service account with `roles/dns.admin`,
 bound to `external-dns/external-dns-sa` via GKE Workload Identity; exported as `external_dns_service_account`.
- **Azure** — `azure/workload-identity.tf`: a user-assigned managed identity + a **federated credential** on
 the AKS OIDC issuer (`oidc_issuer_url` output added to the AKS module), `DNS Zone Contributor`; exported as
 `external_dns_client_id`.

Both templates are `tofu validate`-clean.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Manifest generation (the "generate" apps path)
records.text:
`packages/core/manifests` renders a hardened Deployment/Service/Ingress per detected service
(`FromServices` → `WriteManifests`): non-root, `drop: [ALL]`, read-only rootfs, resource limits, no
privilege escalation — so the generated manifests **pass the k8s [audit](/elench/control-catalog) by
construction**. At deploy, `RunDeployV2` commits them to the GitOps repo **only when the repo has no
manifests yet** (`hasManifests` guard):

 
 **Never clobbers bring-your-own.** Generation is safe-by-construction: it writes to an *empty* apps repo
 or does nothing, and is **non-fatal** — a git edge case warns, it never fails an otherwise-healthy
 cluster. "Bring your own manifests" simply means ArgoCD points at a repo that already has them.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Adding a cloud
records.text:
The deploy flow itself never changes — a new cloud is five edits (mirrors
`packages/core/cloud/README.md`):

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: CloudProvider impl + factory case
records.text: Add ` _provider.go` implementing the four-method interface and a `case` in `NewCloudProvider`.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: OpenTofu template + outputs
records.text:
Provision a cluster + backing services + a **keyless** workload-identity binding for the external-dns KSA,
exporting the identity. Validate with `tofu validate`.

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: Runner credential activation
records.text: Teach the runner how to activate the cloud's credentials before `tofu` runs (AssumeRole / WIF / federated).

records.title: Multi-cloud
records.heading: ArgoCD facts + templates
records.text:
Add a `BuildFromOutputs` case + the per-cloud block, and the branch in `external-dns.yaml` (provider value
+ SA annotation). Empty render = "not applicable".
 
 

 
 **Honest status.** The seam is green — `BuildFromOutputs` per-cloud unit tests, template render tests,
 and `tofu validate` for the GCP/Azure workload-identity OpenTofu. The remaining proof is a real
 GKE/AKS end-to-end run + a live apps-repo git push; only a live cluster/repo can validate those.

records.title: Introduction
records.text: Introduction. Comprehensive documentation for the Alethia Console web platform, alethia CLI, and Runner execution agents.

records.title: Introduction
records.heading: Introduction
records.text:
Documentation for **Alethia** — the multi-cloud Kubernetes control plane by Alethia Labs. Configure
infrastructure visually in the Console, deploy from the terminal with the `alethia` CLI, and let Runners
execute OpenTofu and reconcile GitOps. Choose a section to get started.

 
 **Two editions, one codebase.** Alethia is open-core: a free, open-source Community 
 edition (the complete single-tenant product) and a licensed Enterprise edition
 (multi-member organizations, teams, SSO, custom roles, and the fine-grained access portal). See
 [**Editions**](/editions) for the full comparison.
 

 
 
 Visual web interface for configuring infrastructure, managing integrations, monitoring jobs, and viewing clusters.
 
 
 Command-line tool for managing infrastructure from the terminal — plan, deploy, destroy, and monitor.
 
 
 Autonomous execution agent that runs Terraform, installs ArgoCD, and streams logs in real-time.
 
 
 How managed bring-your-own Terraform/Helm runs sealed off — per-job sandbox, no shared secrets, no cloud metadata, allowlisted egress.
 
 
 Cross-cutting topics — domain model, security architecture, database schema, and release system.
 
 
 Open-source Community vs li

records.title: Architecture
records.text: Architecture. How isolated execution works — the per-job container, the environment allowlist, PID and network isolation, the state-proxy source-drop, per-job and per-VM tokens, and default-deny domain-allowlist egress.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Architecture
records.text:
Isolated execution is a **seam** in the runner (`packages/core/sandbox`): the runner calls `Sandbox.Run`
instead of invoking the provisioner directly, and a backend decides how much isolation the work gets. Trusted
templates use the in-process `Passthrough` backend; untrusted bring-your-own on the managed fleet uses the
`Container` backend described here.

 
 This page is the customer/operator view. The full engineering reference — types, the re-exec contract, the
 allowlist tables, and the shipped dependency chain — lives in the
 [`packages/core/sandbox` README](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/blob/main/packages/core/sandbox/README.md).

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The per-job container
records.text:
A container can't run a Go closure, so the runner **serializes** the work and **re-execs itself** inside a
fresh per-job container. The child reconstructs the exact work from a serialized stage
(`deploy` / `plan` / `destroy` / `chart_scan`), runs it, and writes its result back into the per-job working
directory for the parent to read. One job, one container, torn down after — the fleet VM itself is disposable
(one job per VM).

Hardening on the container: dropped Linux capabilities, no-new-privileges, its own PID namespace (so it
cannot read the parent runner's `/proc`), a read-only root with a writable per-job workdir, and pid/memory
limits.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: The environment allowlist (and a fail-closed guard)
records.text:
The child gets **only** an allowlisted set of environment variables:

- the cloud-auth vars the credential activators set — file paths and non-secret ids (`AWS_CONFIG_FILE` /
 `AWS_PROFILE`, `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`, `ARM_*` / `AZURE_*`, `ALIBABA_CLOUD_*`),
- the token-cloud tokens the provider needs (e.g. `HCLOUD_TOKEN`),
- the state-proxy auth and the egress-proxy vars,
- a minimal toolchain (`PATH`, locale).

Everything else — the runner token, the storage master key, the receipt-signing key, static cloud keys — is
**dropped**. A **fail-closed guard** runs on the final child environment and refuses to start the container
if any denylisted secret survived, so a mistake that widens the allowlist is caught *before* untrusted code
runs. Credentials that are files are mounted **read-only** at their original paths (as directories, so the
runner's ~5-minute token refresh stays visible to a long `apply`).

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Removing secrets at the source
records.text:
The container can only contain what it isn't handed. Three source-drops keep the allowlist short and the
fleet's boot configuration secret-free:

- **OpenTofu state via a console proxy, not storage keys.** Project templates use an `http` backend pointed
 at the console; auth is a **per-job, key-scoped** token, and the state object key is derived server-side
 from the job. The runner never holds an object-storage credential, and one job's token can only reach its
 own state.
- **Per-job cloud tokens.** Cloud credentials are minted per job and bound to it — a stolen runner token
 can't mint another cloud's credentials.
- **Per-VM bootstrap tokens.** Each fleet VM self-registers with its own short-TTL, instance-bound token
 rather than a shared fleet secret.

Together these mean a managed VM's boot configuration carries no long-lived secret — so the network control
below closes the *last* read rather than being the only line of defense.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Network: default-deny + domain allowlist
records.text:
The untrusted child has **no network route of its own**. On the managed fleet:

- the VM runs an internal-only network (no gateway → no route off the box) plus a **forward proxy** that
 allowlists egress **by domain** (registries, Git hosts, the target cloud's API);
- the runner — and the child, which inherits the runner's network namespace — reach the internet *only*
 through that proxy;
- so the cloud metadata service (`169.254.169.254`) and arbitrary exfiltration endpoints are **unreachable by
 construction**, not merely blocked by a rule.

A `chart_scan` stage needs no network at all and runs fully **deny-all** (`--network none`) with zero secrets.

The backend is **fail-closed on managed**: it refuses to run untrusted work unless egress enforcement is
confirmed on the fleet — it never silently falls back to running unsandboxed.

records.title: Architecture
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
The trust model, the guarantees, and availability.
 
 
 Where the sandbox seam sits in the runner's poll loop and job dispatch.
 
 
 Enabling the container sandbox on the fleet (the 3b turn-on sequence + the real-VM canary).

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.text: Isolated Execution. When Alethia runs your bring-your-own OpenTofu/Terraform or Helm on the managed fleet, each job runs in a per-job sealed sandbox — no shared secrets, no cloud metadata, default-deny domain-allowlisted egress.

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: Isolated Execution
records.text:
Zero-credential connections control **who** can touch a cloud. Isolated execution controls what the
**provisioning code itself** can do once it runs. When Alethia runs *your* code — bring-your-own OpenTofu/Terraform
providers, `external` data sources, Helm charts and their hooks — on the **managed fleet**, that code is
arbitrary. Isolated execution is the boundary that runs it **sealed off** from the platform.

 
 **What this is — and isn't.** This is the isolation boundary that makes *managed bring-your-own execution*
 safe: a per-job sandbox with an allowlisted environment and a locked-down network. It is **not** a
 general-purpose "spin up a sandbox and run anything" service, and there is no customer-invokable sandbox API
 today — a customer-facing sandbox/preview surface is on the roadmap. What ships now is the **guarantee**
 underneath managed BYO: your code can't reach the platform's secrets, the cloud metadata service, or the
 open internet.

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: The trust model
records.text:
Alethia runs two kinds of code, and treats them differently:

- **Trusted templates** — Alethia's own project templates (EKS / GKE / AKS / ACK / Talos + supporting
 services). Reviewed platform code, so it runs in-process on the runner. This is every managed provision
 today.
- **Untrusted bring-your-own** — your OpenTofu/Terraform or Helm. Arbitrary code, so on the managed fleet it runs in a
 **per-job container sandbox**: a fresh container per job, its own PID namespace, an allowlisted environment
 (no runner token, no storage key, no signing key — only the short-lived, job-scoped cloud credentials the
 job actually needs), read-only credential mounts, and a **default-deny, domain-allowlisted** network.

The container is what actually contains a hostile provider. Scrubbing environment variables at the last
moment does **not** — a child process sharing the worker's PID namespace can just read the parent's
`/proc/1/environ`. Only a real uid / pid / net namespace boundary contains it.

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: The guarantees
records.text:
For an untrusted job on the managed fleet:

 Guarantee How 
 --- --- 
 **No platform secrets** The child gets an allowlist-only environment; a fail-closed guard refuses to start the container if any platform secret (runner token, storage key, signing key) would leak in. 
 **No cross-job state** OpenTofu state goes through a console **state proxy** with a per-job, key-scoped token — one job's token can only touch its own state. The runner holds no object-storage credential. 
 **No cloud metadata** The child has no network route of its own; it reaches only a domain-allowlist forward proxy, so `169.254.169.254` (which serves the VM's boot config) is unreachable by construction. 
 **No open egress** Only the registries, Git hosts, and the target cloud's API are reachable — everything else is default-denied. 
 **No host reach** Its own PID namespace, dropped capabilities, no-new-privileges, read-only root, resource limits.

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: Why it matters
records.text:
Alethia sits in the gap between IaC orchestrators (which run *your IaC* on shared workers) and BYOC PaaSes
(which run *your apps*). Running **bring-your-own infrastructure code in a per-job sealed sandbox** is the
execution-trust complement to the [zero-credential](/concepts/security) connection model — and the safety
floor under the "proof, not just generation" story ([Elench](/elench) verifies *what* the change does; this
contains *how* it runs).

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: Availability
records.text:
Isolated execution applies to the **managed fleet**. Bring-your-own Helm/IaC is gated by
`ALETHIA_BYO_HELM_ENABLED` and, until the container backend is enabled and verified on the fleet, stays
trusted-templates-only. Operators turn it on per the
[managed provisioning runbook](/self-hosting/managed-provisioning#untrusted-byo-container-sandbox-3b).

records.title: Isolated Execution
records.heading: Where to go next
records.text:
The mechanism: the per-job container, the environment allowlist + fail-closed guard, PID/network
 isolation, read-only credential mounts, the state-proxy source-drop, per-job / per-VM tokens, and the
 default-deny domain-allowlist egress.
 
 
 Where isolated execution sits in the overall zero-credential / OIDC security model.
 
 
 The operator guide to the managed fleet + the 3b turn-on sequence for the container sandbox.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.text: Internal Architecture. Main loop, heartbeat mechanism, graceful shutdown, and the core shared package.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Internal Architecture
records.text: Runner is a single Go binary with a straightforward architecture: a main poll loop, a heartbeat goroutine, and shared libraries from the `core` package.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Main Loop
records.text:
Reg[Register with Alethia API]
 Reg --> HB[Start heartbeat goroutine · every 30s]
 Reg --> Poll{Poll loop · every 10s POST /api/jobs/claim}
 Poll --> no job Sleep[sleep 10s]
 Sleep --> Poll
 Poll --> job claimed Mint[Mint per-job cloud creds · keyless OIDC]
 Mint --> Exec[Execute via sandbox seam · tofu, ArgoCD]
 Exec --> Logs[Stream logs]
 Logs --> Report[Report result]
 Report --> Poll
`} />

The poll loop runs on the main goroutine; the heartbeat runs as a background goroutine. Both share
the runner configuration.

Provisioning work runs through the **sandbox seam** (`packages/core/sandbox`), not by invoking the provisioner
directly. For trusted Alethia templates the default `Passthrough` backend runs it in-process; on the managed
fleet, untrusted bring-your-own IaC/Helm runs in a per-job **container** with an allowlisted environment and a
locked-down network. See the [`sandbox` package README](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/blob/main/packages/core/sandbox/README.md)
and [Security Architecture → Execution isolation](/concepts/security#execution-isolation-managed-untrusted-byo).

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Job types
records.text:
The dispatch switch (`internal/agent/runner.go`) routes each claimed job to its handler:

 Job type Handler Does 
 --- --- --- 
 `PLAN` / `DEPLOY` / `DESTROY` `executePlan` / `executeDeploy` / … OpenTofu plan/apply/destroy (+ the verify gate, ArgoCD, and manifest scaffold on deploy). Routed through the **sandbox seam**. 
 `CHART_SCAN` `executeChartScan` Render a bring-your-own Helm chart to manifests + scan it (zero secrets, deny-all egress) — always sandboxed. 
 `ANALYZE_REPO` `executeAnalyzeRepo` Clone + statically scan a repo into a `RepoDigest`. 
 `DETECT_DRIFT` `executeDriftDetection` `tofu plan -refresh-only` → `drift.Analyze` → post a `Posture`. 
 `AUDIT` `executeAudit` Run the elench verify engine over a **bring-your-own** plan or k8s manifests → post `verify_result`. Provisions nothing. 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` / `UPDATE_RUNNER` / `DESTROY_RUNNER` `executeDeployRunner` / … Provision, update, or tear down a self-operated runner.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Configuration
records.text:
Runner receives its configuration via environment variables:

 Variable Description 
 ---------- ------------- 
 `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` Base URL of the Alethia instance 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_ID` Unique Runner identifier (assigned at registration) 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_TOKEN` Authentication token for API calls 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_OPERATOR` `managed` or `self` (the legacy `ALETHIA_RUNNER_MODE` with `cloud-hosted`/`self-hosted` still works as a back-compat fallback) 
 `INFRACOST_API_KEY` Optional, for cost estimation 

The runner holds **no object-storage credentials**. tofu state goes through the console **state proxy**
(`backend "http"` → `/api/jobs/{id}/state`) authenticated by a per-job token in `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`, and cloud
auth is minted per job via keyless OIDC — so a managed fleet VM's cloud-init carries no long-lived secret.
Managed fleet VMs additionally self-register with a per-VM, short-TTL bootstrap token rather than any static
credential.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Heartbeat Mechanism
records.text:
The heartbeat goroutine sends `POST /api/runners/heartbeat` every 30 seconds with:

- Runner ID
- Current status (ONLINE or DRAINING)
- Currently executing job ID (if any)

Alethia uses the heartbeat to:
- Mark Runners as ONLINE (heartbeat within 60s) or OFFLINE (heartbeat missed)
- Detect dead Runners and requeue their jobs
- Track which Runner is running which job

If a Runner crashes mid-job:
1. Heartbeat stops arriving
2. After 60 seconds, Alethia marks the Runner OFFLINE
3. If no log updates for 5+ minutes, the job is marked FAILED
4. User can retry the job — tofu state is preserved in the console state proxy (`project-tofu-state`, with fenced locks so a reclaimed lock can't clobber a slow writer)

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Graceful Shutdown
records.text:
When Runner receives SIGINT or SIGTERM:

1. Sets status to **DRAINING** — stops accepting new jobs
2. Sends a DRAINING heartbeat to Alethia
3. If a job is in progress, waits for it to complete (up to 10-minute grace period)
4. Sends final heartbeat with OFFLINE status
5. Exits

In the managed fleet, a warm-pool VM claims jobs until the scaler decides it is surplus. Scale-down
is a two-step **drain then destroy**: the controller marks the runner `DRAINING` (it stops claiming
but finishes its in-flight job), and the next tick destroys it once idle — so the SIGTERM the VM
sends always lands on a runner that already drained gracefully. See
[Warm-pool scaling](/runner/scale-to-zero). (Self-operated runners get the same on any orchestrator's
stop signal.)

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: API Client
records.text:
The `RunnerAPIClient` handles all communication with Alethia:

 Method Endpoint Purpose 
 -------- ---------- --------- 
 `ClaimJob()` `POST /api/jobs/claim` Atomic job claiming 
 `Heartbeat()` `POST /api/runners/heartbeat` Keepalive 
 `UpdateJobStatus()` `PUT /api/jobs/{id}/status` Status transitions 
 `SendLog()` `POST /api/jobs/{id}/logs` Log batch delivery 
 `UploadPlanArtifact()` `POST /api/jobs/{id}/plan-artifact` Plan JSON upload 
 `DownloadPlanArtifact()` `GET /api/jobs/{id}/plan-artifact` Plan JSON download 

All requests include `X-Runner-ID` and `X-Runner-Token` headers for authentication.

records.title: Internal Architecture
records.heading: Shared core Package
records.text:
Runner and Alethia CLI both import from `packages/core/`:

 Package Purpose 
 --------- --------- 
 `provisioner/` Bootstrap, deploy, destroy orchestration 
 `tofu/` OpenTofu CLI wrapper (init, plan, apply, destroy) — drives the `tofu` binary via `terraform-exec` 
 `cloud/aws/` AWS SDK for provisioning (keyless `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`, EKS kubeconfig) 
 `cloud/` (`http_backend.go`) The console HTTP state backend (per-job token via `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`) — no storage credential on the runner 
 `sandbox/` The per-job isolation seam (Passthrough / Container backends) 
 `cloud/gcp/` GCP Workload Identity Federation + kubeconfig 
 `cloud/azure/` Azure federated auth + kubeconfig 
 `argocd/` ArgoCD installation and configuration 
 `helm/` Helm chart operations 
 `git/` Git clone, branch, commit, push 
 `k8s/` kubectl operations 
 `infracost/` Infracost CLI wrapper and output parsing 

This shared package ensures both the CLI and Runner use the same provisioning logic.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.text: Cloud Credential Management. How Runner assumes temporary credentials per job — AWS, Alibaba, GCP, and Azure, plus token clouds (DigitalOcean / Hetzner / Civo) with an optional self-managed path where the token never leaves your infrastructure.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Cloud Credential Management
records.text: Runner assumes temporary cloud credentials at the start of every job and clears them when the job completes. No credentials persist between jobs.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Per-job credential lifecycle
records.text:
B[Read cloud_identity from the claim]
 B --> C[Provider-specific keyless minting]
 C --> D[Set env vars · OIDC token file]
 D --> E[Execute tofu · reads env vars]
 E --> F[defer: clear credentials + remove token file]
`} />

The `defer` ensures credentials and any on-disk assertion are always cleared, even if the job fails
or panics.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: AWS: keyless AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity
records.text:
**Input from cloud_identity:**
- `role_arn` — ARN of the IAM role in the user's AWS account (its trust policy trusts the Alethia issuer)

**Execution (managed runner — keyless, no ambient AWS identity):** the runner mints a web-identity assertion
(audience `sts.amazonaws.com`) from the console and writes a single AWS profile that federates **straight into
the customer role** — no platform account, no ExternalId:

[profile alethia-customer]
web_identity_token_file = /tmp/alethia-aws-XXXX/web-identity-token
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AlethiaProvisionerRole
role_session_name = alethia-runner

It points OpenTofu's aws provider at that profile (`AWS_CONFIG_FILE` + `AWS_PROFILE=alethia-customer`). A
background refresher re-mints the assertion into the token **file** every few minutes; the SDK re-reads it
and re-assumes on session expiry, so a `tofu apply` longer than the ~1 hour session keeps a live assertion
(parity with GCP/Azure/Alibaba). No access key ever reaches the runner (see
[Security Architecture → AWS](/concepts/security#aws-keyless-issuer-direct-iam-role)).

**Self-operated runners** (`operator=self`) run in the customer's own cloud with their own ambient
crede

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Alibaba Cloud: RAM Role via OIDC (keyless, account-free)
records.text:
Alibaba is **keyless** — there is no platform AccessKey (the old `ALETHIA_ALIBABA_ACCESS_KEY_*` was
retired). The runner authenticates the same way the console does: an anonymous **`AssumeRoleWithOIDC`**
using a short-lived assertion minted by the Alethia issuer. Alethia holds no Alibaba account.

**Input from cloud_identity:**
- `role_arn` — ARN of the RAM role in the user's account (`acs:ram:: :role/ `)
- `oidc_provider_arn` — ARN of the customer's RAM OIDC provider (fixed name `alethia`)

**Execution:**

func ActivateAlibabaOIDC(ctx, fetcher, roleArn, oidcProviderArn string) (cleanup func(), err error) {
 token, err := fetcher.FetchAlibabaToken() // mint an assertion from the console (/api/runners/alibaba-token)
 tokenFile := writeToTempFile(token)

 // The alicloud provider runs AssumeRoleWithOIDC itself from these — no AccessKey on the runner.
 os.Setenv("ALIBABA_CLOUD_ROLE_ARN", roleArn)
 os.Setenv("ALIBABA_CLOUD_OIDC_PROVIDER_ARN", oidcProviderArn)
 os.Setenv("ALIBABA_CLOUD_OIDC_TOKEN_FILE", tokenFile)
 // …a background refresher re-mints into tokenFile every ~5 min; cleanup stops it + removes the file
 return cleanup, nil
}

The `alicloud` provider reads `ALIBABA_CLOUD_OIDC

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: GCP: Workload Identity Federation

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Azure: Federated Identity
records.text:
Azure is **keyless** — no client secret reaches the runner. The runner authenticates as Alethia's
platform Entra app by fetching a short-lived OIDC assertion from the console (the console holds the
issuer signing key) and handing it to OpenTofu.

**Input from cloud_identity:**
- `tenant_id` — the customer's Azure Entra tenant
- `client_id` — Alethia's platform app id (the same for every connection)
- `subscription_id` — Target Azure subscription

**Execution:**

func ActivateAzureFederated(fetcher azureTokenFetcher, tenantId, clientId, subscriptionId string) (cleanup func(), err error) {
 // Mint an assertion from the console (POST /api/runners/azure-token, runner-authed).
 token, err := fetcher.FetchAzureToken()
 if err != nil {
 return nil, err
 }
 tokenFile := writeToTempFile(token)

 // azurerm/azuread (OpenTofu) read ARM_*; the Azure SDK + kubelogin read AZURE_*.
 os.Setenv("ARM_USE_OIDC", "true")
 os.Setenv("ARM_CLIENT_ID", clientId)
 os.Setenv("ARM_TENANT_ID", tenantId)
 os.Setenv("ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID", subscriptionId)
 os.Setenv("ARM_OIDC_TOKEN_FILE_PATH", tokenFile) // the FILE, not a frozen literal token
 os.Setenv("AZURE_FEDERATED_TOKEN_FILE", tokenFile)
 // …a backgroun

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Token Clouds: DigitalOcean / Hetzner / Civo
records.text:
These providers have no cross-account role federation — they authenticate with a single API token.
Alethia supports two models.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Stored scoped token (encrypted)
records.text:
The customer pastes a scoped API token; Alethia stores it **encrypted at rest** and decrypts it only at
claim time, passing it to the runner. The runner exports the provider's env var
(`DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN` / `HCLOUD_TOKEN` / `CIVO_TOKEN`) for the job and clears it after.

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Self-managed — token stays in your infrastructure (true zero-trust)
records.text:
For clouds without federation, the honest zero-trust path: the token lives **only** in your self-hosted
runner's environment, never in Alethia's database.

- The connection is recorded with `self_managed: true` and **no token**.
- Its jobs are flagged `requires_self_runner`, and `claim_next_job` **excludes managed runners** from
 them — so only *your* runner, which holds `HCLOUD_TOKEN` / `CIVO_TOKEN` / `DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN`
 in its own env, can claim and run them.
- The runner reads the token from its environment (the `selfManaged` path) and never receives it from
 Alethia.

Pick the model in the connect sheet: **"Store a scoped token"** (encrypted at rest) vs **"Self-hosted
runner — token stays in your infra."** See [Self-operated runners](/runner/self-hosted).

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: Security Properties
records.text:
Property Guarantee 
 ---------- ----------- 
 No persistent credentials Env vars are cleared after every job via `defer` 
 No credential sharing Each job gets its own assumed session
