 Time-bounded AWS sessions expire after 1 hour 
 Scoped Credentials only have permissions defined in the IAM role / service account / app registration 
 Revocable User deletes the IAM role / WIF config / App Registration → instant revocation 
 No file leaks Temporary credential files are deleted in `defer` cleanup 
 Optional zero-storage Self-managed token clouds keep the secret in your runner's env — it never enters Alethia's database

records.title: Cloud Credential Management
records.heading: CloudIdentity Structure

records.title: core Library
records.text: core Library. Shared Go packages used by both the alethia CLI and the runner agent.

records.title: core Library
records.heading: core Library
records.text:
`packages/core/` is a shared Go module imported by both the alethia CLI and the runner. It holds the
provisioning orchestration, the cloud-provider abstraction, the OpenTofu wrapper, and the Elench
verify/drift engines — so both callers use identical logic.

records.title: core Library
records.heading: Package map
records.text:
Package Purpose 
 --------- --------- 
 `api/` HTTP client for the Alethia control plane (runner + Bearer auth) 
 `provisioner/` Orchestrates deploy, destroy, and bootstrap workflows (`RunDeployV2`, `RunDestroy`, `RunBootstrap`) 
 `tofu/` Wrapper around the `tofu` binary via `hashicorp/terraform-exec` 
 `cloud/` `CloudProvider` interface + per-cloud implementations, the HTTP state backend, and kubeconfig writing 
 `verify/` The deterministic, fail-closed Elench policy gate over the plan JSON (+ ed25519 evidence receipt) 
 `drift/` Turns `tofu plan -refresh-only -json` into a per-environment drift `Posture` 
 `sandbox/` The per-job isolation seam (Passthrough / Container backends) 
 `argocd/` ArgoCD install, manifest rendering, infra facts 
 `helm/` Helm chart operations with retry logic 
 `k8s/` kubeconfig management 
 `git/` Git clone, checkout, commit, push 
 `infracost/` Infracost CLI wrapper and JSON output parser 
 `accessanalyzer/` AWS IAM Access Analyzer corroboration for the verify gate (opt-in) 
 `scanner/` / `iacsafety/` Static scanning of bring-your-own charts + IaC 
 `catalog/` / `categories/` / `manifests/` Add-on catalog, component categories, rendered manifests 
 `ty

records.title: core Library
records.heading: provisioner/
records.text:
The main orchestration layer. Entry point: `RunDeployV2()`.

 B[Write backend HCL · console HTTP proxy]
 B --> C[Generate tfvars · OverrideTfvarsFromMap]
 C --> D[tofu init]
 D --> E[tofu plan]
 E --> F[verify gate · verify.Evaluate]
 F --> hard fail unwaived X[refuse before apply]
 F --> pass / warn / waived G[attach signed receipt]
 G --> H[tofu apply]
 H --> I[write keyless kubeconfig · runner kube-token]
 I --> J[install ArgoCD · reachability gate]
 J --> K[PlanResult + execution_metadata]
`} />

`RunDeployV2` is the fail-closed spine: it evaluates the plan JSON with `verify.Evaluate`, refuses
the apply if a hard control is failing and unwaived (`gateRequiresReport` + `Report.Unwaived`), and
seals a `verify.SignedReceipt`. Also exposes `RunDestroy()` and `RunBootstrap()`. Brownfield attach
is handled here: a component marked "existing" injects the resource id (`vpc_id` / `network_id` /
`vnet_id`) as a tfvar instead of creating one.

records.title: core Library
records.heading: tofu/
records.text:
Drives the `tofu` binary through `hashicorp/terraform-exec/tfexec` (state- and CLI-compatible with
the Terraform 1.6 HCL line, hence the legacy library name):

- `NewTofuCLI()` — construct against a workdir with stage-aware graceful cancellation (SIGINT to the
 `tofu` child on ctx-done, then a grace period)
- `Init()` / `InitWithBackendFile()` — init with backend config (the console HTTP backend file)
- `Plan()` / `PlanRefreshOnly()` — plan, or refresh-only for drift detection
- `Apply()` / `Destroy()` — apply a plan file / destroy
- `ShowPlanJSON()` — the structured plan JSON the UI diff and verify gate consume
- `OverrideTfvarsFromMap()` — materialize a tfvars file from `ProviderTfvars`

The `tofu` binary is **baked into the runner image** (SHA-verified), so no binary is downloaded at
provision time.

records.title: core Library
records.heading: cloud/
records.text:
Defines the `CloudProvider` interface:

type CloudProvider interface {
 Name() string
 RequiredCLIs() []string
 ProviderTfvars(config ProjectConfig) map[string]interface{}
 ConfigureKubeconfig(clusterName, region string) error
}

Implementations are provisioning-only — the runner no longer lists cloud resources; the Console
maintains the [asset inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory) server-side:

- `aws_provider.go` — AWS SDK: keyless `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`, EKS kubeconfig, Access Analyzer,
 EC2 subnet lookup for reusing an existing VPC
- `gcp_provider.go` — GCP Workload Identity Federation + kubeconfig
- `azure_provider.go` — Azure federated identity + kubeconfig (`azidentity`)
- `alibaba_provider.go` — Alibaba RAM `AssumeRoleWithOIDC`
- `hetzner_provider.go` — Hetzner token auth + Talos kubeconfig (`RequiredCLIs` empty — CLI-free)
- `http_backend.go` — the **Console HTTP state backend** (`backend "http"`, per-job token via
 `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`) — there is no S3 storage credential on the runner
- `kubeconfig.go` — writes the exec-credential kubeconfig that invokes `runner kube-token`

records.title: core Library
records.heading: verify/ & drift/
records.text:
`verify` is the deterministic, engine-agnostic policy gate (keyless / least-privilege / OIDC-subject
controls, honest `not_evaluable`, ed25519 receipt). `drift` turns a refresh-only plan into a per-env
`Posture`. See [Engine internals](/elench/engine-internals) and `packages/core/verify/README.md`.

records.title: core Library
records.heading: infracost/
records.text:
- `CheckToken()` — validates the API key
- `ensureBinary()` — downloads infracost if missing
- `Run()` — executes `infracost breakdown` on the plan JSON
- `parse.go` / `types.go` — parses into `CostBreakdown` / `ResourceCost`

records.title: core Library
records.heading: argocd/ & helm/
records.text:
- `argocd/install.go` — deploys ArgoCD via Helm, waits for Ready
- `argocd/render.go` — renders Application manifests from templates with infra facts
- `argocd/infra_facts.go` — extracts cluster info, DB/cache endpoints from tofu outputs
- `helm/` — `UpgradeInstall()` (`helm upgrade --install`, server-side dry-run), 3× retry with backoff

records.title: core Library
records.heading: assets/

records.title: Runner Agent
records.text: Runner Agent. Autonomous execution agent that runs OpenTofu, installs ArgoCD, and streams logs.

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Runner Agent
records.text:
Runner is the execution engine — a single Go binary shipped as a container image. Runners claim
provisioning jobs from the queue, mint per-job cloud credentials keylessly, execute the `tofu`
binary (OpenTofu), install ArgoCD, and stream logs back in real time. The binary is **CLI-free**:
it bundles no `aws`/`gcloud`/`az`, and mints cluster tokens in-process via `runner kube-token` (see
[Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials)).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Key facts
records.text:
Property Value 
 ---------- ------- 
 Language Go (single binary) 
 IaC engine OpenTofu (`tofu`) `v1.9.0`, baked into the image 
 Base image Alpine 3.21, non-root `runner` user 
 Registries GitHub Container Registry (`ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner*`) 
 Versioning release-please (`runner-v*` tags). See [Release System](/concepts/release-system). 
 Poll interval 10 seconds (`POST /api/jobs/claim`) 
 Heartbeat interval 30 seconds (`POST /api/runners/heartbeat`) 
 tofu state Console HTTP state proxy — no object-storage credential on the runner 
 Cloud auth Keyless OIDC per job (no static keys)

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Where a runner runs — the managed fleet vs. self-deployed
records.text:
The word "runtime" splits cleanly into two paths. Do not conflate them.

 Managed fleet Self-deployed (AWS) 
 --- --- --- 
 Operator `managed` `self` / `deployed` 
 Substrate **Hetzner VM warm pool** AWS ECS Fargate (ARM64) 
 Provisioned by the in-app scaler (`apps/console/lib/fleet/`) a `DEPLOY_RUNNER` job running `infra/templates/runner/aws/` 
 Image source GHCR per-cloud image, self-registers via `ALETHIA_RUNNER_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` GHCR image, registration token injected as an ECS env var 
 Scaling 60s warm-pool loop, sized by queue depth ([Warm-pool scaling](/runner/scale-to-zero)) ECS service `desired_count`, managed by the customer 
 Billing provisioned hours via `runner_usage_sessions` the customer's own AWS bill 

The **managed fleet** is Alethia running runners in the platform account and assuming into the
customer cloud — cheap Hetzner VMs from a GHCR image, sized by an in-app scaler. **ECS Fargate is
not the managed substrate anymore** (the retired AWS ECS + Lambda fleet lived under `infra/fleet-aws`
and is gone). ECS Fargate is real only for a **self-deployed** runner, which a `DEPLOY_RUNNER` job
provisions into the customer's own AWS account from `infra/templates/runner/a

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Operator & provisioning
records.text:
A runner is described by two orthogonal axes: **operator** (`managed` `self`) and, for self
runners, **provisioning** (`deployed` `registered`). This yields three effective categories:

 Category Operator Provisioning Description 
 ---------- ---------- -------------- ------------- 
 **Managed** `managed` — Alethia runs the runner in the platform account (Hetzner VM warm pool) and bills it by provisioned hours. Automatic scaling, no user setup. 
 **Self · Deployed** `self` `deployed` Provisioned into the customer's cloud by an existing runner running `tofu` (Console "Deploy" tab / `alethia runner deploy`). AWS lands on ECS Fargate. 
 **Self · Registered** `self` `registered` Customer brings their own runner — their own IaC or just `alethia runner start` — and registers it for a token. For on-prem or air-gapped environments. 

The legacy `ALETHIA_RUNNER_MODE` (`cloud-hosted`/`self-hosted`) still works as a back-compat
fallback (`cloud-hosted` → `managed`, `self-hosted` → `self`), but `ALETHIA_RUNNER_OPERATOR` is
canonical.

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Polls for jobs
records.text: Every 10 seconds, calls `POST /api/jobs/claim`. See [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Claims atomically
records.text: PostgreSQL `claim_next_job()` with `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` prevents double-execution.

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Mints credentials
records.text:
Keyless OIDC per job — AWS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`, GCP Workload Identity Federation, Azure
federated identity, Alibaba `AssumeRoleWithOIDC` — no stored cloud keys. Token clouds
(DigitalOcean / Hetzner / Civo) use an API token, either stored encrypted or kept entirely in a
self-operated runner. See [Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Executes OpenTofu
records.text:
`tofu init` → `tofu plan` → the [verify gate](/runner/job-execution/plan-jobs) → `tofu apply` or
`tofu destroy`. Provisions **greenfield** infrastructure, or **attaches to existing** resources when
the config marks a component "existing" (brownfield — e.g. an existing VPC/VNet). See
[Job Execution](/runner/job-execution).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Installs ArgoCD
records.text: Helm chart deployment with the App-of-Apps pattern. See [GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Streams logs
records.text:
Batched POST every 2 seconds → Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY` → SSE → browser. See
[Log Streaming](/runner/log-streaming).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Reports results
records.text:
Updates job status with execution metadata (timing, tofu outputs, and the verify report on
`execution_metadata["verify_result"]`).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Sends heartbeat
records.text: Every 30 seconds to confirm it is alive.

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Container contents
records.text:
The image builds `FROM` a shared `runner-base` (Alpine 3.21, non-root `runner` user). The base
carries everything cloud-agnostic; each per-cloud image adds only that cloud's project template.

 Tool Purpose 
 ------ --------- 
 Go binary (`runner`) The runner executable — also the `kube-token` exec-credential plugin 
 OpenTofu (`tofu`) `v1.9.0` Infrastructure provisioning (SHA256-verified download, baked in) 
 kubectl Kubernetes cluster access 
 Helm ArgoCD and component installation 
 Infracost `v0.10.39` Cost estimation 
 Infrastructure templates Per-cloud project templates + runner + ArgoCD manifests 
 podman / crun / fuse-overlayfs Nested rootless sandbox runtime — **inert** until `ALETHIA_SANDBOX_BACKEND=container` 

 
 **No cloud CLIs.** The image bundles **no** `aws-cli`, `gcloud`, or `azure-cli` — the
 `Dockerfile`s say so explicitly. EKS/GKE/AKS cluster tokens are minted **in-process** by
 `runner kube-token --provider `, invoked by the exec-credential kubeconfig the runner
 writes. See [Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials).

records.title: Runner Agent
records.heading: Documentation
records.text:
Main loop, heartbeat, sandbox seam, graceful shutdown 
 Per-job-type execution details 
 Keyless, per-job credential minting 
 Real-time log delivery 
 In-app scaler + Hetzner VM fleet 
 Running a runner you operate yourself 
 OpenTofu modules for all providers 
 Shared Go packages

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.text: ArgoCD Components. Pre-installed Kubernetes components managed by ArgoCD after cluster provisioning.

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.heading: ArgoCD Components
records.text: After cluster provisioning, Runner installs ArgoCD and deploys a set of supporting components via the [App of Apps pattern](/concepts/gitops-argocd). These templates live in `infra/templates/argocd/`.

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.heading: Component Manifests
records.text:
Template Component Purpose 
 ---------- ----------- --------- 
 `aws-load-balancer-controller.yaml` AWS Load Balancer Controller Provisions ALB/NLB for Kubernetes Ingress and Service resources 
 `external-dns.yaml` External DNS Syncs DNS records from Kubernetes Ingress annotations. Backends: Route53/Cloud DNS/Azure DNS via workload identity, **Hetzner Cloud DNS** via the official webhook sidecar (the connector's Cloud API token; the domain's zone must already exist in Hetzner Cloud DNS), and **Cloudflare** whenever the Cloudflare DNS connector is attached (any cloud). Renders only when DNS is enabled with a domain and the selected backend's identity/token is actually present — otherwise it is skipped rather than deployed broken 
 `external-secrets-operator.yaml` External Secrets Operator Pulls secrets from Secrets Manager/Secret Manager/Key Vault into Kubernetes Secrets. The AWS SecretsManager `ClusterSecretStore` renders on AWS only; the operator itself ships on every cloud 
 `karpenter.yaml` Karpenter Just-in-time node provisioning based on pod resource requests (AWS) 
 `metrics-server.yaml` Metrics Server Provides CPU/memory metrics for Horizontal Pod Autoscaler 
 `storage-class

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.heading: How Templates Are Rendered
records.text:
Each template is a Kubernetes manifest with Helm-style placeholders. During deployment, the Runner:

1. Extracts **infrastructure facts** from tofu outputs (cluster name, VPC ID, database endpoints, etc.)
2. Renders the facts into a Helm values file
3. Applies each component manifest as an ArgoCD Application

The ArgoCD Application resources reference Helm charts from public repositories with the infrastructure facts injected as values.

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.heading: Infrastructure Facts
records.text:
Example facts passed to component Helm charts:

cluster:
 name: api-backend-production
 endpoint: https://ABC123.gr7.eu-west-1.eks.amazonaws.com
 oidcProviderArn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/...

vpc:
 id: vpc-0123456789abcdef0

database:
 endpoint: api-backend-production.cluster-abc123.eu-west-1.rds.amazonaws.com
 port: 5432

cache:
 endpoint: api-backend-production.abc123.euw1.cache.amazonaws.com
 port: 6379

dns:
 domain: api.example.com
 zoneId: Z1234567890

Components use these values for provider-specific configuration (e.g., External DNS needs the hosted zone ID, External Secrets Operator needs the cluster OIDC provider ARN for IRSA).

records.title: ArgoCD Components
records.heading: Adding Custom Applications
records.text: After the initial deployment, users can add their own applications by pushing YAML files to the `manifests/applications/` directory in the infrastructure Git repository. ArgoCD automatically deploys any new Application manifest it discovers.

records.title: AWS Resources
records.text: AWS Resources. OpenTofu modules for AWS — EKS, Aurora, ElastiCache, DynamoDB, and networking.

records.title: AWS Resources
records.heading: AWS Resources
records.text: The AWS template set (`infra/templates/project/aws/`) is the most extensive, with ~15 top-level `.tf` files and 10 reusable modules.

records.title: AWS Resources
records.heading: Top-Level Resources
records.text:
File Resources Created 
 ------ ------------------ 
 `networking.tf` VPC, public/private subnets, NAT gateway, internet gateway, route tables, security groups 
 `eks.tf` EKS cluster, managed node group, OIDC provider for IRSA 
 `karpenter.tf` Karpenter provisioner, node pools, instance types 
 `rds.tf` Aurora PostgreSQL/MySQL cluster, instances, subnet group 
 `elasticache.tf` ElastiCache Redis replication group, subnet group 
 `valkey.tf` Valkey (open-source Redis fork) node 
 `dynamodb.tf` DynamoDB tables with KMS encryption 
 `ecr.tf` ECR repositories with lifecycle policies, scan-on-push 
 `s3.tf` S3 buckets with encryption, versioning, lifecycle rules 
 `sqs.tf` SQS queues (standard + FIFO) + SNS topics with subscriptions 
 `waf.tf` WAFv2 web ACL with managed rule groups 
 `acm-certificate.tf` ACM SSL/TLS certificates with DNS validation 
 `irsa.tf` IAM Roles for Service Accounts (pod-level AWS access) 
 `custom_secrets.tf` Secrets Manager secrets with auto-generated passwords

records.title: AWS Resources
records.heading: Modules
records.text:
Module Purpose 
 -------- --------- 
 `modules/eks/` EKS cluster, access entries, OIDC provider 
 `modules/rds/` Aurora cluster, instances, IAM auth, S3 export 
 `modules/redis/` ElastiCache Redis with auth token, encryption 
 `modules/valkey/` Valkey node with security group 
 `modules/dynamodb/` DynamoDB table with KMS, local encryption module 
 `modules/ecr/` ECR repository with lifecycle policy 
 `modules/s3/` S3 bucket with encryption, versioning 
 `modules/sqs-sns/` SQS queue + SNS topic with IAM policies 
 `modules/wafv2/` WAF web ACL with CloudWatch logging 
 `modules/acm/` ACM certificate with DNS validation 
 `modules/awssm-passgen/` Secrets Manager password generation

records.title: AWS Resources
records.heading: Key Features
records.text:
**IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)** — Kubernetes pods can assume IAM roles via their service account, eliminating the need for static credentials in containers. The EKS module creates an OIDC provider and the IRSA module creates per-service-account IAM roles.

**Karpenter** — Just-in-time node provisioning. Instead of pre-scaling node groups, Karpenter watches for pending pods and launches appropriately-sized EC2 instances within seconds. Supports spot instances and multiple instance families.

**Aurora Serverless v2** — Auto-scaling database with ACU-based capacity (0.5–128 ACUs). Scales to zero-ish (0.5 ACU minimum) during idle periods.

records.title: Azure Resources
records.text: Azure Resources. OpenTofu modules for Azure — AKS, Azure Database, Cosmos DB, and more.

records.title: Azure Resources
records.heading: Azure Resources
records.text: The Azure template set (`infra/templates/project/azure/`) provides Azure-native equivalents for all supported resource types.

records.title: Azure Resources
records.heading: Resources
records.text:
File Resources Created

 `aks.tf` AKS cluster, default node pool, Azure AD integration 
 `vnet.tf` Virtual Network, subnets, NSG, NAT Gateway 
 `azure-db.tf` Azure Database for PostgreSQL/MySQL (Flexible Server) 
 `azure-cache-redis.tf` Azure Cache for Redis 
 `cosmos-db.tf` Cosmos DB account, database, container 
 `acr.tf` Azure Container Registry 
 `storage-account.tf` Storage account, blob containers 
 `service-bus.tf` Service Bus namespace, queues, topics 
 `azure-dns.tf` Azure DNS zone, record sets 
 `key-vault.tf` Key Vault, access policies

records.title: Azure Resources
records.heading: Modules
records.text:
Module Purpose 
 -------- --------- 
 `modules/aks/` AKS cluster with AAD integration 
 `modules/azure-db/` Flexible Server PostgreSQL/MySQL 
 `modules/azure-cache-redis/` Azure Cache for Redis 
 `modules/cosmos-db/` Cosmos DB (multi-model NoSQL) 
 `modules/acr/` Azure Container Registry 
 `modules/storage-account/` Azure Blob Storage 
 `modules/service-bus/` Service Bus queues and topics

records.title: Azure Resources
records.heading: Key Features
records.text:
**Azure AD Integration** — AKS integrates with Azure AD for RBAC. Cluster admins are Azure AD users or groups, not separate IAM identities.

**Flexible Server** — Azure's latest managed database tier with zone-redundant HA, automatic backups, and vCore-based scaling (1–64 vCores).

**Cosmos DB** — Globally distributed multi-model database supporting SQL (document), Table (key-value), MongoDB, Cassandra, and Gremlin APIs. Multi-region writes for global distribution.

records.title: GCP Resources
records.text: GCP Resources. OpenTofu modules for GCP — GKE, Cloud SQL, Memorystore, Firestore, and more.

records.title: GCP Resources
records.heading: GCP Resources
records.text: The GCP template set (`infra/templates/project/gcp/`) provides cloud-native equivalents for all supported resource types.

records.title: GCP Resources
records.heading: Resources
records.text:
File Resources Created 
 ------ ------------------

records.title: GCP Resources
records.heading: Modules
records.text:
Module Purpose 
 -------- --------- 
 `modules/gke/` GKE cluster with Workload Identity, Autopilot option

 `modules/memorystore/` Memorystore Redis 
 `modules/firestore/` Firestore database

 `modules/pubsub/` Pub/Sub topics 
 `modules/secret-manager/` Secrets

records.title: GCP Resources
records.heading: Key Features
records.text:
**Workload Identity** — GKE pods authenticate to GCP services via their Kubernetes service account, mapped to a GCP service account. No key files needed.

**GKE Autopilot** — Fully managed node provisioning. Google handles node scaling, patching, and resource optimization. Enabled via the `enable_autopilot` provider config toggle.

**Cloud NAT** — Managed NAT service for outbound internet access from private subnets. Uses Cloud Router for BGP routing.

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.text: Infrastructure Templates Overview. OpenTofu modules the runner uses to provision infrastructure across AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, and Hetzner.

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.heading: Infrastructure Templates
records.text:
The runner provisions infrastructure using pre-built OpenTofu modules (HCL). These templates are
baked into the runner image at build time — each per-cloud image pre-runs `tofu init` on its
template so no provider/module download happens at job time.

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.heading: Template Categories
records.text:
Category Path Purpose 
 ---------- ------ --------- 
 AWS project `infra/templates/project/aws/` User infrastructure on AWS 
 GCP project `infra/templates/project/gcp/` User infrastructure on GCP 
 Azure project `infra/templates/project/azure/` User infrastructure on Azure 
 Alibaba project `infra/templates/project/alibaba/` User infrastructure on Alibaba Cloud 
 Hetzner project `infra/templates/project/hetzner/` Self-managed Talos K8s on Hetzner VMs 
 Runner `infra/templates/runner/aws/` Self-deployed runner infrastructure (ECS Fargate) 
 ArgoCD `infra/templates/argocd/` ArgoCD applications and components 

 
 EKS, Aurora, ElastiCache, DynamoDB, and more 
 GKE, Cloud SQL, Memorystore, Firestore, and more 
 AKS, Azure Database, Azure Cache, Cosmos DB, and more 
 Pre-installed cluster components

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.heading: Template staging (image build)
records.text:
During the [runner release pipeline](/concepts/release-system), templates are `COPY`d into the image
and pre-initialized. The full `runner` image carries all five project templates; each per-cloud image
(`runner-aws`, `runner-hetzner`, …) carries only its own:

infra/templates/project/ → /home/runner/project-templates/ (+ tofu init)
infra/templates/runner/ → /home/runner/runner-templates/
infra/templates/argocd/ → /home/runner/argocd-templates/

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.heading: Variable generation
records.text:
When executing a job, the runner materializes a tfvars file from the environment's config snapshot
(`tofu.OverrideTfvarsFromMap`). The provisioner in `packages/core/provisioner/` maps each component
to specific tofu variables (`ProviderTfvars`). See
[Cloud Provider Abstraction](/concepts/cloud-abstraction) for the mapping layer.

records.title: Infrastructure Templates Overview
records.heading: tofu state
records.text:
The runner holds **no object-storage credentials**. State for every template is written through the
**Console HTTP state proxy** — the templates declare `backend "http" {}`, and the runner fetches a
per-job token it exposes as `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`, so state I/O flows through
`/api/jobs/{id}/state`. See [tofu state](/concepts/terraform-state).

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.text: Deploy Jobs. How the runner executes DEPLOY jobs — the verify gate, tofu apply, keyless kubeconfig, and ArgoCD.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Deploy Jobs
records.text:
`DEPLOY` jobs apply the OpenTofu plan and install ArgoCD on the provisioned cluster. Between the
plan and the apply sits the **fail-closed verify gate** — a real apply will not run while a hard
control is failing.

 
 **Brownfield attach.** A DEPLOY doesn't only create new infrastructure — it can attach to what you
 **already have**. When the environment config marks a component "existing" (e.g. an existing
 VPC/VNet — see [Reuse existing resources](/console/design-project/network#reuse-existing-resources)),
 the runner passes the resource's id into the tofu template instead of creating it, and the template
 `data`-sources the existing resource (and, for networks, resolves its subnets) so the rest of the
 graph wires into it. The provision-vs-existing seam is a `count = var.provision_X ? 1 : 0` guard on
 the create path plus a data source on the existing path. Today this covers networks on AWS/GCP/Azure.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Mint cloud credentials
records.text: Same keyless per-job minting as PLAN jobs. See [Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials).

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Download plan artifact
records.text:
Fetches the binary `plan.tfplan` from the PLAN job (`GET /api/jobs/{planJobId}/plan-artifact`) into
the job workdir. If it can't be fetched, the runner re-plans.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Validate config hash
records.text:
Compares the environment's current `configuration_hash` against the plan job's. If they differ (the
project was edited after planning), the job fails:

> "configuration changed since plan was generated"

This prevents applying a stale plan. See [Plan & Apply](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply).

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: tofu init
records.text:
Initializes against the Console HTTP state proxy (`backend "http"`, per-job `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`) —
same as PLAN. No cloud storage credential is involved.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Verify gate (fail-closed)
records.text:
`provisioner.RunDeployV2` re-derives the plan JSON and runs `verify.Evaluate`
(`packages/core/verify`). It attaches a `verify.Report` and, on this **non-dry-run** apply, calls
`gateRequiresReport` + `Report.Unwaived(override)` **before `tofu apply`**:

- A hard control failure with no waiver → the job **fails here**, before any resource is touched.
- An authorized `verify.Override` (time-boxed, carrying `Reason`/`By`) waives specific control IDs
 so the apply can proceed deliberately.
- An ed25519 evidence **receipt** is sealed for the apply (unsigned `algorithm: "none"` when no
 `ALETHIA_RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY` is set).

The report and receipt ride back on `execution_metadata["verify_result"]` /
`["verify_receipt"]` and render in the Console **Plan tab**
(`components/agent/artifact-panel.tsx`). See [Engine internals](/elench/engine-internals).

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: tofu apply
records.text:
tofu apply plan.tfplan
State is written back through the state proxy as each resource settles. (`RunDeployV2` writes
`apply` to a phase file just before this, so an interrupted apply can be flagged orphan-risk.)

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Extract tofu outputs
records.text: Reads cluster name / endpoint / ARN, database endpoints, cache endpoints, VPC id, etc.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Generate kubeconfig (CLI-free)
records.text:
No cloud CLI is invoked. The runner writes an **exec-credential kubeconfig** whose `user` runs the
runner itself as the credential plugin:

users:
 - name: alethia
 user:
 exec:
 apiVersion: client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1
 command: runner
 args: ["kube-token", "--provider", "aws", "--cluster", " ", "--region", " "]

`runner kube-token` mints a short-lived cluster token in-process from the already-active keyless
cloud credentials — a presigned STS `GetCallerIdentity` for EKS, a cloud-platform OAuth token for
GKE, an AAD workload-identity token for AKS. kubectl/Helm re-invoke it on expiry, so tokens
auto-refresh with no `aws`/`gcloud`/`az` present.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Install ArgoCD
records.text:
Deploys ArgoCD via Helm and waits for pods Ready. The admin password stays in the cluster's
`argocd-initial-admin-secret` (never extracted, never persisted to `execution_metadata`). See
[GitOps & ArgoCD](/concepts/gitops-argocd).

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Configure App of Apps
records.text:
Renders ArgoCD Application manifests with infrastructure facts (cluster name, DB endpoints, …) as
Helm values and applies them.

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Report results
records.text:
Job status → SUCCESS with `execution_metadata`:
- Cluster name, endpoint, `cluster_ready` (a real reachability gate — not just "tofu apply exited 0")
- ArgoCD URL
- tofu outputs, credential-bearing keys scrubbed (kubeconfigs, passwords, tokens, generated secrets
 never leave the runner — `scrubSensitiveOutputs` + a whole-blob `scrubMetadataTree` backstop)
- `verify_result` / `verify_receipt`
- Per-phase execution timing

records.title: Deploy Jobs
records.heading: Finalization
records.text:
On the Alethia side, when the job reaches SUCCESS the `finalizeDeployment()` server action:
- Updates `project_cluster` with cluster metadata and ArgoCD credentials
- Updates `project_databases` with writer/reader endpoints and credential ARNs
- Updates `project_caches` with primary/reader endpoints
- Sets the environment's `status` to `ACTIVE`

This data appears in the [Clusters page](/console/clusters) and the environment detail.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.text: Destroy Jobs. How the runner executes DESTROY jobs — tofu destroy with state cleanup.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: Destroy Jobs
records.text: `DESTROY` jobs tear down all infrastructure provisioned for an environment.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: Mint cloud credentials
records.text: Same keyless per-job minting as other job types. See [Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials).

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: tofu init
records.text:
Initializes against the Console HTTP state proxy (`backend "http"`, per-job `TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`) to
read the existing state — no cloud storage credential on the runner.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: tofu destroy
records.text:
tofu destroy -auto-approve
Removes all resources tracked in the tofu state. No confirmation prompt — the user already confirmed
via the Console or CLI.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: State cleanup
records.text: The state managed by the proxy is left in place (empty or minimal) for audit purposes.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: Report results
records.text: Job status → SUCCESS. The environment's `status` is set to `DESTROYED`.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: Partial destroy recovery
records.text:
If the destroy fails mid-way (resource dependency, timeout):

1. tofu state reflects which resources were already destroyed.
2. The job is marked FAILED with the error message.
3. The user retries — the next destroy only attempts the remaining resources.
4. This continues until all resources are removed.

records.title: Destroy Jobs
records.heading: What gets destroyed
records.text:
Everything provisioned for the environment:
- VPC/VNet and all networking resources (subnets, NAT gateways, security groups)
- Kubernetes cluster (EKS/GKE/AKS) and node groups
- Databases (Aurora/Cloud SQL/Azure Database instances)
- Caches (ElastiCache/Memorystore/Azure Cache)
- NoSQL tables, queues, topics
- Container registries
- Secrets
- DNS records and certificates
- WAF rules
- IAM roles (IRSA)

 
Destroy is permanent for stateful resources. Database data, cache contents, and secret values are **lost**. tofu state is preserved so re-apply is possible, but the data is gone.

records.title: Job Execution Overview
records.text: Job Execution Overview. How the runner claims and executes each job type.

records.title: Job Execution Overview
records.heading: Job Execution
records.text:
All jobs share the same claiming mechanism and keyless credential handling, but differ in what they
execute. For the queue mechanics, see [Job Queue Pattern](/concepts/job-queue).

records.title: Job Execution Overview
records.heading: Claiming
records.text:
Every 10 seconds the runner calls `POST /api/jobs/claim`. The server runs `claim_next_job()` — a
PostgreSQL function using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` for atomic, exactly-once claiming. Job status
walks `QUEUED → CLAIMED → PROCESSING → SUCCESS FAILED CANCELLED`.

When a job is returned, the runner:
1. Sets status to `PROCESSING`.
2. Reads the environment config snapshot and cloud identity from the claim.
3. Mints cloud credentials keylessly ([details](/runner/cloud-credentials)).
4. Dispatches on `types.JobType` (the switch in `internal/agent/runner.go`) to the handler.
5. Sets status to `SUCCESS` or `FAILED`.

records.title: Job Execution Overview
records.heading: Job types
records.text:
The dispatch switch routes each of these `provisionJobType` values:

 Job type Handler Does 
 ---------- --------- ------ 
 `PLAN` `executePlan` `tofu plan` + verify gate + Infracost → plan artifact. [Details](/runner/job-execution/plan-jobs) 
 `DEPLOY` `executeDeploy` verify gate (fail-closed) → `tofu apply` → keyless kubeconfig → ArgoCD. [Details](/runner/job-execution/deploy-jobs) 
 `DESTROY` `executeDestroy` `tofu destroy`. [Details](/runner/job-execution/destroy-jobs) 
 `DEPLOY_RUNNER` / `UPDATE_RUNNER` / `DESTROY_RUNNER` `executeDeployRunner` / … Provision, update, or tear down a self-deployed runner. [Details](/runner/job-execution/runner-jobs) 
 `ANALYZE_REPO` `executeAnalyzeRepo` Clone + statically scan a repo into a `RepoDigest`. 
 `DETECT_DRIFT` `executeDriftDetection` `tofu plan -refresh-only` → `drift.Analyze` → post a `Posture`. 
 `AUDIT` `executeAudit` Run the Elench verify engine over a bring-your-own plan or k8s manifests → post `verify_result`. Provisions nothing. 
 `CHART_SCAN` `executeChartScan` Render a BYO Helm chart to manifests + scan it (zero secrets, deny-all egress) — always sandboxed. 
 `IAC_SCAN` `executeIacScan` Static scan of bring-your-own IaC. 
 `ST

records.title: Job Execution Overview
records.heading: Error handling
records.text:
When any step fails:
1. The error is logged to the STDERR stream.
2. Job status is set to `FAILED` with the error message.
3. tofu state is preserved through the [Console state proxy](/concepts/terraform-state) (partial
 apply state, with fenced locks so a reclaimed lock can't clobber a slow writer).
4. Cloud credentials are cleared (the deferred cleanup always runs).
5. The runner returns to the poll loop for the next job.

Users can retry failed jobs — since state is preserved, retries continue from the last successful
resource rather than starting over.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.text: Plan Jobs. How the runner executes PLAN jobs — tofu plan, the verify gate, structured output, and Infracost.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Plan Jobs
records.text:
`PLAN` jobs run `tofu plan` to preview infrastructure changes without applying them, and run the
plan JSON through the [Elench verify gate](/elench/engine-internals) so the Plan tab shows both the
diff and the policy verdict.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Mint cloud credentials
records.text:
Keyless per job — AWS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`, GCP WIF, Azure federated identity, Alibaba
`AssumeRoleWithOIDC`. No stored keys. See [Cloud Credentials](/runner/cloud-credentials).

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Generate tofu variables
records.text:
The project's config snapshot is transformed into a tfvars map by the provisioner
(`tofu.OverrideTfvarsFromMap`). Each environment component maps to specific tofu variables.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: tofu init
records.text:
Initializes against the **Console HTTP state proxy**, not a cloud bucket the runner can reach:
backend "http" {} # /api/jobs/{id}/state, auth via TF_HTTP_PASSWORD (per-job token)
The runner holds no object-storage credentials. See [tofu state](/concepts/terraform-state).

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: tofu plan
records.text:
tofu plan -out=plan.tfplan
Generates a binary plan file describing what will change.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: tofu show (plan JSON)
records.text:
tofu show -json plan.tfplan
Converts the binary plan to structured JSON — the input both to the UI diff and to the verify gate.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Verify gate (fail-closed)
records.text:
`provisioner.RunDeployV2` calls `verify.Evaluate` on the plan JSON right after it is produced. The
gate runs the deterministic Elench control set (keyless / least-privilege / OIDC-subject, with
honest `not_evaluable` for what the plan can't show) and attaches a `verify.Report` to the result.
On a PLAN this is **advisory** — the report is surfaced but nothing is applied — but the same code
path is **fail-closed on apply**: a `DEPLOY` will refuse before `tofu apply` if a hard control is
failing and unwaived. See [Deploy Jobs](/runner/job-execution/deploy-jobs) and
[Engine internals](/elench/engine-internals).

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Infracost analysis (optional)
records.text:
If `INFRACOST_API_KEY` is configured:
infracost breakdown --path plan.json --format json
Parses per-resource cost estimates. See [Cost Estimation](/concepts/cost-estimation).

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Upload plan artifact
records.text:
The binary `plan.tfplan` is uploaded to the Console (`POST /api/jobs/{id}/plan-artifact`) for a
later `DEPLOY` job to apply verbatim.

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: Report results
records.text:
Job status → SUCCESS with `execution_metadata`:
- Resource change counts (add / change / destroy)
- `verify_result` — the `verify.Report` (rendered in the Plan tab)
- Cost breakdown (if Infracost ran)
- Plan artifact reference

records.title: Plan Jobs
records.heading: What the user sees
records.text:
After the plan completes, the [Plan tab](/console/jobs/plan-and-apply) in the environment detail
shows:
- Resource groups with create / update / delete actions
- The verify report — per-control `pass` / `fail` / `warn` / `not_evaluable`
- Per-resource costs and total estimated monthly cost
- An "Apply" button (blocked, or requiring an override, if a hard control failed)

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.text: Runner Lifecycle Jobs. DEPLOY_RUNNER, UPDATE_RUNNER, and DESTROY_RUNNER jobs.

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.text:
These job types manage Runner infrastructure.

 
 Connection verification and resource discovery are **not** runner jobs. Cloud connections are
 verified server-side by the console the moment you connect (an instant auth + provisioning-permission
 probe), and the asset inventory is maintained server-side + event-driven — see
 [Connection health & inventory](/console/connectors/health-and-inventory). The runner is
 provisioning-only.
 

These are the **self-deployed** runner path — `operator=self`, `provisioning=deployed`. They are
distinct from the [managed fleet](/runner/scale-to-zero), which the in-app scaler sizes over Hetzner
VMs (no job runs to create a managed runner — it self-registers with a bootstrap token).

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: DEPLOY_RUNNER
records.text:
Provisions a new self-deployed runner into the customer's cloud account. On AWS this is ECS Fargate,
from [`infra/templates/runner/aws/`](/runner/infrastructure-templates/aws).

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: Use platform credentials
records.text: Runner lifecycle jobs use the platform's own cloud credentials (not a customer cloud identity).

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: Generate tofu config
records.text:
Writes the tfvars for the AWS runner template — an ECS cluster + Fargate service (ARM64), execution
+ task IAM roles, a CloudWatch log group, and security groups. State goes through the Console proxy
(`backend "http"`).

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: tofu apply
records.text: Provisions the infrastructure in the specified region.

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: Register with Alethia
records.text:
Calls `POST /api/runners/register` to create a runner record. The runner ID and token are injected
as environment variables into the ECS task.

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: Report results
records.text:
Job status → SUCCESS. The new runner boots, sends its first heartbeat, and appears as ONLINE on the
[Runners page](/console/runners).

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: UPDATE_RUNNER
records.text:
Updates an existing self-deployed runner to the latest release.

1. Updates the ECS task definition with the latest image tag.
2. Forces a new ECS Fargate deployment (`--force-new-deployment`).
3. The old task drains gracefully; the new task starts on the updated image.

records.title: Runner Lifecycle Jobs
records.heading: DESTROY_RUNNER
records.text:
Tears down a self-deployed runner's infrastructure.

1. Runs `tofu destroy` on the runner's ECS resources.
2. Removes the runner registration from Alethia.
3. Any jobs assigned to that runner are requeued to other available runners.

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.text: Real-Time Log Streaming. How the runner streams OpenTofu output to the browser in real-time.

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.text:
The runner streams all `tofu` output back to Alethia in real time. Users see `tofu plan` and
`tofu apply` output in the browser as it happens.

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: Architecture
records.text:
Out[JobLogger STDOUT]
 Tofu --> Err[JobLogger STDERR]
 Out --> Buf[Buffer]
 Err --> Buf
 Buf --> POST /api/jobs/id/logs Tbl[(provision_job_logs)]
 Tbl --> NOTIFY on INSERT Listen[Console LISTEN]
 Listen --> SSE stream Browser[Browser EventSource]
`} />

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: JobLogger
records.text:
The `JobLogger` is a Go struct that wraps an `io.Writer`. It buffers output and flushes to the
Alethia API periodically.

**Two loggers per job:**
- **STDOUT logger** — captures normal `tofu` output (plan details, resource progress)
- **STDERR logger** — captures errors and warnings

**Buffer flush triggers:**
- Every **2 seconds** (ticker-based flush)
- When the buffer exceeds **10 KB** (size-based flush)
- On job completion (final flush)

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: API Delivery

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: Log Display
records.text:
The job detail page renders logs in a terminal-like viewer:

- **STDOUT** lines in default color (white/light gray)
- **STDERR** lines in red
- **SYSTEM** lines in blue (platform messages like "Minting keyless credentials..." or "Starting tofu plan...")
- Auto-scroll to bottom as new lines arrive
- Manual scroll lock when the user scrolls up

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: Local Echo
records.text:
All log output is also written to the container's stdout — journald/`docker logs` on a managed
Hetzner fleet VM, CloudWatch Logs for a self-deployed runner on ECS Fargate, or the local terminal
for a registered runner. This provides a secondary log source for debugging.

records.title: Real-Time Log Streaming
records.heading: Timing
records.text:
Event Latency 
 ------- --------- 
 tofu writes to stdout 0ms (piped to JobLogger) 
 JobLogger buffer flush ≤ 2 seconds (ticker interval) 
 API POST to Alethia ~50ms (network) 
 Database INSERT ~5ms 
 NOTIFY → SSE delivery ~50ms (server-sent event) 
 **Total: tofu output → browser** **~2-3 seconds**

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.text: Warm-pool scaling. How the managed fleet sizes itself — the in-app scaler's 60s warm-pool loop over cheap Hetzner VMs.

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: Warm-pool scaling
records.text:
The managed fleet has no standing capacity to pay for when idle. Instead of a fixed set of
always-on runners, an **in-app scaler** sizes a per-provider warm pool by queue depth: it creates
cheap VMs when jobs back up and destroys them when the backlog clears. There is no separate
scaling service — the loop runs inside the Console process (`apps/console/lib/fleet/`).

 
 **The old mechanism is retired.** The managed fleet used to be AWS ECS Fargate services scaled by
 a Python Lambda on an EventBridge schedule (`infra/fleet-aws`). That is gone. There is no Lambda,
 no EventBridge rule, and no `ecs:UpdateService` desired-count toggle in the managed path. ECS
 Fargate survives only in the **self-deployed** runner template (`infra/templates/runner/aws/`) —
 see [Self-operated runners](/runner/self-hosted). For the conceptual overview see
 [Runner scaling](/concepts/runner-scaling); for the managed-fleet operator's guide see
 [Managed provisioning](/self-hosting/managed-provisioning).

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: The loop
records.text:
`startFleetScaler()` (`apps/console/lib/fleet/scaler.ts`) arms a `setInterval` on a **60-second**
tick (`TICK_INTERVAL_MS = 60_000`) whenever `ALETHIA_DATABASE_URL` is set. Pools live in the
database and are read fresh each tick, so a tick with zero enabled pools is a cheap no-op and a
newly-created pool converges without a restart. On top of the interval, every job enqueue calls
`wakeFleetScaler()` (≈15 call sites), so a fresh `QUEUED` job doesn't wait up to a minute for the
next tick.

 >S: request a pass
 S->>C: run exactly one pass (coalesce concurrent wakes)
 C->>C: observe reality (runners, backlog, peak)
 C->>P: plan(target, observed)
 P-->>C: create / drain / destroy actions
 C->>H: apply actions
 C->>C: append fleet_actions ledger row
`} />

`scheduleTick()` guarantees **at most one pass is ever in flight** per process: a wake that arrives
mid-pass coalesces into a single follow-up rather than launching a second concurrent read → plan →
act (two overlapping passes would each read the same stale VM list and both create for the same
backlog, over-provisioning past the pool max). This serializer is per-process; true multi-replica
safety needs a Postgres advisory lock, and tod

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: Sizing: targetCount
records.text:
The desired healthy-instance count is a pure function (`plan.ts`, `targetCount`):

demand = max(recentPeak, ceil(backlog / slotsPerRunner))
target = clamp(max(warmMin, demand + buffer), 0, max)

 Input Source Meaning 
 ------- -------- --------- 
 `backlog` `deps.backlog(provider)` `QUEUED` jobs targeting this provider 
 `recentPeak` `deps.recentPeak(provider)` in-flight (`CLAIMED`/`PROCESSING`) jobs — auto-grows the floor 
 `slotsPerRunner` pool config concurrent jobs per runner (`ALETHIA_RUNNER_SLOTS`) 
 `warmMin` pool config warm floor — never plan the online count below this 
 `buffer` pool config N+1 headroom above demand 
 `max` / `surge` pool config hard ceiling; `surge` is transient headroom for rollouts 

Scale-down is not a desired-count decrement. A surplus online VM is **drained** (marked `DRAINING`
so it stops claiming but finishes its in-flight job); once it goes idle the next tick **destroys**
it, and `deps.retire()` closes its `runner_usage_sessions` billing row. Dead instances (no
registration past the boot grace) are reaped directly. Placement is location-aware: below-min
locations fill first, then least-loaded (`minPerLocation`).

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: Provider seam
records.text:
`getFleetProvider()` (`provider.ts`) selects the `FleetProvider` implementation. Default is
`manual` (a no-op — nothing is provisioned); **`FLEET_PROVIDER=hcloud`** selects the Hetzner
provider (`hcloud.ts`). The provider is a thin `list` / `create` / `destroy` over the Hetzner REST
API (`https://api.hetzner.cloud/v1`, plain `fetch`, no SDK) — all diff logic lives in the pure
planner, so the whole reconcile is unit-testable against a fake world (`fake-provider.ts`).

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: VM shape and self-registration
records.text:
`create` boots a Hetzner VM (default `HCLOUD_SERVER_TYPE=cax21`, an ARM instance;
`HCLOUD_IMAGE=ubuntu-24.04`) whose **cloud-init** does a single `docker run` of the per-cloud
runner image from GHCR:

ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner-${provider}:${tag}

The VM carries **no long-lived credential**. It self-registers with a per-VM, short-TTL
**`ALETHIA_RUNNER_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN`** (minted per `create`, swept by `sweepBootstrapTokens`), and
once registered the runner mints cloud auth per job via keyless OIDC and reaches tofu state through
the Console state proxy. There is no static cloud key baked into the image or the cloud-init. (An
`INSTANCE_ID` override lets several runners on one host register distinctly.)

 
 The E0 container-sandbox knobs on the cloud-init (`ALETHIA_SANDBOX_BACKEND=container`,
 egress-enforced proxy) are **inert** until enabled — trusted provisioning boots a byte-identical
 cloud-init. See [Architecture → sandbox seam](/runner/architecture).

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: The fleet_actions ledger
records.text:
Every applied action appends a durable row via `deps.recordAction()` — the audit trail behind each
VM lifecycle event. Each `FleetActionRecord` (`controller.ts`) carries:

 Field Meaning 
 ------- --------- 
 `action` `create` \ `drain` \ `destroy` 
 `reason` `scale-up-demand`, `min-per-location`, `teardown`, `reap-dead`, `reap-drained`, version-rollout, … 
 `runnerId` correlated runner (drain/destroy); `null` for a create (the runner self-registers later) 
 `queueDepth` `QUEUED` backlog for the provider at decision time 
 `poolSize` observed online (claimable) pool size at decision time 

A ledger write is best-effort and must never break a reconcile. Actual `create`/`drain`/`destroy`
application is additionally guarded so **only one replica** applies a given provider's actions per
tick (a `withScaleGuard` around the per-pool span), bounding cross-replica over-provisioning until
a real DB advisory lock lands.

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: Cold start
records.text:
When the pool scales `0 → 1` for a fresh backlog:

1. **Hetzner VM boot + image pull** — cloud-init pulls `runner- ` from GHCR
2. **Runner boot + self-register** — a few seconds (`POST /api/runners/register` with the bootstrap token)
3. **First heartbeat** — `POST /api/runners/heartbeat` (30s cadence)
4. **First job claim** — next poll cycle (`POST /api/jobs/claim`, ≤ 10s)

During this window the job stays `QUEUED`. The `wakeFleetScaler()` on enqueue is what starts the
VM immediately rather than waiting for the next 60s tick.

records.title: Warm-pool scaling
records.heading: Cost model
records.text:
You pay for Hetzner VM-hours only while the pool is above its warm floor, plus whatever `warmMin`
you choose to keep hot for latency. Managed runner cost is billed by provisioned hours through the
`runner_usage_sessions` ledger — a session opens when a managed runner comes online and closes when
`retire()` reaps it. There is no Lambda invocation cost and no idle Fargate task.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.text: Self-operated runners. Running a runner you operate yourself — registered or deployed — for on-premises or air-gapped environments.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Self-operated runners
records.text:
A runner is described by two axes: **operator** and **provisioning**. Self-operation is the
**operator = `self`** case — you run the runner rather than letting Alethia operate it in the
managed fleet. Within `self`, the **provisioning** axis says who placed it:

- **`deployed`** — provisioned into your cloud by an existing runner running `tofu` (Console
 "Deploy" tab / `alethia runner deploy`). On AWS this lands on ECS Fargate from
 [`infra/templates/runner/aws/`](/runner/infrastructure-templates/aws).
- **`registered`** — you bring your own runner (your own IaC, or just `alethia runner start`) and
 register it for a token. For on-premises, air-gapped, or development hosts.

 
 "Self-hosted" is the legacy term for this whole family; the canonical field is
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_OPERATOR=self`. `ALETHIA_RUNNER_MODE=self-hosted` still works as a back-compat
 fallback. This page covers running the binary directly — a **registered** runner.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
The runner is a single Go binary and is **CLI-free** — it does **not** need `aws`, `gcloud`, or
`azure-cli`. Cloud auth is minted in-process (keyless OIDC), and cluster tokens come from
`runner kube-token`, so the only host tools it shells out to are:

 Tool Purpose Minimum version 
 ------ --------- ----------------- 
 OpenTofu (`tofu`) Infrastructure provisioning 1.9+ 
 kubectl Kubernetes cluster access 1.28+ 
 Helm Chart installation (ArgoCD, components) 3.12+ 

The official runner image bundles all three (plus the `tofu` provider cache). When running the
binary directly you install them yourself; the container path needs nothing extra.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Registration
records.text:
Before running, the runner must be registered with Alethia:

1. Register the runner via the Console or API (or, in the managed fleet, self-register with a
 bootstrap token).
2. Receive a `runner_id` and `runner_token` (shown once).
3. Pass these as environment variables.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Running
records.text:
Set the required environment variables and start the binary. Note there are **no S3 credentials** —
tofu state flows through the Console state proxy, authenticated by a per-job token the runner
fetches itself:

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Optional
records.text:
./runner

The runner will:
1. Register / confirm with the Console.
2. Start the heartbeat goroutine (30s).
3. Begin polling for jobs (10s) and execute them keylessly.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Environment variables
records.text:
Variable Required Description 
 ---------- ---------- ------------- 
 `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` Yes Alethia control-plane base URL 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_ID` Yes Assigned runner ID 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_TOKEN` Yes Authentication token 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_OPERATOR` Yes Must be `self` (legacy `ALETHIA_RUNNER_MODE=self-hosted` still works) 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_PROVIDERS` No Comma-separated clouds this runner serves (e.g. `aws`); unset = any 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_SLOTS` No Concurrent jobs per runner (default `1`). Above `1`, the runner supervises N isolated worker subprocesses — run the container with an init (`docker --init` / compose `init: true`) so orphaned tools are reaped 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_INSTANCE_ID` No Distinct self-registration id when several runners share a host 
 `INFRACOST_API_KEY` No Enables cost estimation 
 `HCLOUD_TOKEN` No Hetzner API token — set it to run **self-managed** Hetzner jobs (token stays here, never in Alethia) 
 `CIVO_TOKEN` No Civo API token — same self-managed token-cloud path 
 `DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN` No DigitalOcean API token — same self-managed token-cloud path 

 
 There are no `ALETHIA_STORAGE_*` variables. The runner holds **no object-storage credentials**:
 tofu st

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: On-premises infrastructure
records.text:
For organizations that need infrastructure provisioned from within their network (private cloud,
bare metal):
- Run the runner on a bastion host or CI/CD runner.
- It polls Alethia over HTTPS (outbound only — no inbound ports required).
- `tofu` provisions resources reachable from the internal network.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Self-managed token clouds (DigitalOcean / Hetzner / Civo)
records.text:
These clouds have no role federation — they authenticate with a single API token. For true
zero-trust, keep that token in your own runner instead of storing it in Alethia:

- In the connect sheet, pick **"Self-hosted runner — token stays in your infra"** (the cloud
 identity is recorded with no token).
- Set the matching env var on this runner — `HCLOUD_TOKEN`, `CIVO_TOKEN`, or
 `DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN`.
- Those jobs are flagged `requires_self_runner`, so `claim_next_job` excludes managed runners from
 them — only this runner, which holds the token, runs them. Alethia stores nothing.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Air-gapped environments
records.text:
For high-security environments without internet access:
- Pre-download OpenTofu providers and modules.
- Configure a local provider mirror.
- The runner communicates with Alethia over an approved network path.

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Development and testing
records.text:
For local development:
- Run the runner alongside the Alethia dev console.
- Point `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` at `http://localhost:3000`.
- Exercise job execution without any cloud deploy. (`pnpm dev:runner` stands one up natively.)

records.title: Self-operated runners
records.heading: Differences from managed / deployed
records.text:
Aspect Managed (Hetzner fleet) Self · Deployed (AWS ECS) Self · Registered (this page) 
 -------- ------------------------- --------------------------- ------------------------------- 
 Substrate Hetzner VM warm pool ECS Fargate Any host you manage 
 Deployment In-app scaler, automatic `DEPLOY_RUNNER` job (`tofu`) Manual 
 Scaling 60s warm-pool loop, by queue depth ECS `desired_count` (you) You start/stop it 
 Tool management Baked into the GHCR image Baked into the GHCR image You install `tofu`/kubectl/Helm 
 Updates New image + drain-replace `UPDATE_RUNNER` (force-redeploy) Manual binary update 
 Monitoring Console fleet cockpit + logs CloudWatch + Console Local stdout/stderr + Console 
 Network Platform account Customer VPC Your network 
 Billing Provisioned hours (`runner_usage_sessions`) Your AWS bill Not billed by Alethia

records.title: Configuration
records.text: Configuration. The .env keys that configure a self-hosted Alethia bundle.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Configuration
records.text:
All configuration is environment variables in `.env` (copy from `.env.example`).
The installer generates the secret values; the rest you set. Internal service
URLs (Postgres, SeaweedFS) are wired by compose — you don't set those.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Ingress
records.text:
Key Notes 
 --- --- 
 `ALETHIA_DOMAIN` Your domain; Caddy gets TLS for it. Empty → plain HTTP on `:80`. 
 `ALETHIA_ACME_EMAIL` Let's Encrypt contact email. 
 `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` Public origin, e.g. `https://alethia.example.com`.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Secrets (generated by the installer)
records.text:
Key Generate with 
 --- --- 
 `CLI_JWT_SECRET` `openssl rand -hex 32` — signs CLI/runner tokens (shared with the runner). 
 `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` `openssl rand -hex 32` — signs sessions/cookies. 
 `ALETHIA_DB_PASSWORD` / `ALETHIA_APP_DB_PASSWORD` strong random — bundled Postgres roles. 
 `ALETHIA_CRED_ENCRYPTION_KEY` `openssl rand -base64 32` — AES-256-GCM key encrypting api-key [connector](/console/connectors/pluggable) secrets (Cloudflare/Vault/Docker Hub/…). Required to connect pluggable connectors.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Database & storage
records.text:
Defaults target the bundled `postgres` + `seaweedfs`. To use a managed Postgres or
S3/R2 instead, point `ALETHIA_DATABASE_URL` / `ALETHIA_STORAGE_*` at them and set
`ALETHIA_STORAGE_AUTO_CREATE_BUCKETS=false`.

 Key Notes 
 --- --- 
 `ALETHIA_DB_USER` / `ALETHIA_DB_NAME` bundled Postgres user/db 
 `ALETHIA_STORAGE_ENDPOINT` S3 endpoint (bundled SeaweedFS by default) 
 `ALETHIA_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY_ID` / `_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` must match `deploy/seaweedfs/s3.json` for the bundle

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Email (AWS SES)
records.text:
Set `ALETHIA_SES_REGION` to enable sending; with none, sign-in/account emails are
logged to the console instead of sent. From-addresses must be verified SES identities.

 Key Notes 
 --- --- 
 `ALETHIA_SES_REGION` SES region, e.g. `eu-central-1`. Enables sending. 
 `ALETHIA_SES_ACCESS_KEY_ID` / `_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` Optional; omit to use the AWS default credential chain (IAM role / `AWS_*`). 
 `AUTH_EMAIL_FROM` Auth/security from address, e.g. `Alethia ` (quote it). 
 `EMAIL_FROM` Product/general from address; falls back to `AUTH_EMAIL_FROM`.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: OAuth providers (optional)
records.text:
Only the providers you configure appear on the sign-in screen.

 Key 
 --- 
 `GITHUB_CLIENT_ID` / `GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET` 
 `GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID` / `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET` 
 `GITLAB_APPLICATION_ID` / `GITLAB_SECRET` 
 `BITBUCKET_KEY` / `BITBUCKET_SECRET` 

Set each provider's callback to `https:// /api/auth/callback/ `.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Cloud connectors (optional)
records.text:
Key Default Notes 
 --- --- --- 
 `NEXT_PUBLIC_CONNECTOR_ASSETS_ORIGIN` hosted bucket Origin serving the connector setup artifacts the AWS/GCP/Azure connect flows hand out (`alethia-bootstrap.yaml`, `alethia-{gcp,azure}-setup.sh`). Point it at your own console origin — the files ship in `apps/console/public/`, e.g. `https://alethia.example.com`. The AWS CloudFormation quick-create flow needs an S3-hosted template, so AWS uses the bucket default or the OpenTofu method.

records.title: Configuration
records.heading: Managed runner fleet (optional)
records.text:
By default, runners are static — you register them yourself (see [Runners](/console/runners)).
Set `FLEET_PROVIDER=hcloud` to let the in-app scaler provision warm runner VMs on Hetzner Cloud and
scale them by queue depth. Unset / `manual` → no managed fleet (the scaler is a no-op).

 Key Default Notes 
 --- --- --- 
 `FLEET_PROVIDER` `manual` `hcloud` provisions real Hetzner VMs; `manual` = no-op. 
 `HCLOUD_TOKEN` — Hetzner Cloud API token. Required when `FLEET_PROVIDER=hcloud`. 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` — Low-privilege shared token fleet VMs present to self-register. 
 `ALETHIA_WEB_ORIGIN` — Console URL the VMs call back to (must be reachable from them). 
 `HCLOUD_SERVER_TYPE` `cax21` Hetzner server type (ARM CAX recommended for runner workloads). 
 `HCLOUD_IMAGE` `ubuntu-24.04` Base OS image for fleet VMs. 
 `HCLOUD_SSH_KEYS` — Comma-separated Hetzner SSH key IDs. 
 `FLEET_RUNNER_IMAGE_TAG` `latest` Runner image tag pulled from GHCR. 
 `FLEET_RUNNER_SLOTS` `1` Concurrent job slots per fleet runner. 
 `FLEET_BOOT_GRACE_SECONDS` `180` Seconds a freshly-booted VM is exempt from scale-down (cloud-init grace). 

Warm pools are managed in the console **Fleet** page (DB-backed)

records.title: Email
records.text: Email. Set up transactional email on AWS SES with reputation-isolated sending subdomains (auth, product, marketing).

records.title: Email
records.heading: Email (AWS SES)
records.text:
Alethia sends transactional email through **AWS SES** — there is no third-party
email SaaS. Until you configure SES, sign-in codes and other emails are **logged
to the server console** (run `docker compose logs app`), so a fresh self-host
works with zero email setup. This page is what you do to send real email.

records.title: Email
records.heading: Why subdomains
records.text:
Mail is split across **dedicated sending subdomains**, one per *stream*, so a
reputation hit on one stream can never poison another — which matters most for
auth mail (a sign-in code in spam = a locked-out user). The root domain is
reserved for your human/corporate mail and never sends app email.

 Stream Subdomain From-address (env) Used for 
 --- --- --- --- 
 Auth / security `auth.alethialabs.io` `AUTH_EMAIL_FROM` sign-in codes, email verification, password reset 
 Product / general `mail.alethialabs.io` `EMAIL_FROM` welcome, org invites, job notifications 
 Marketing *(future)* `marketing.alethialabs.io` — bulk/marketing — give it its **own** SES identity and ideally a separate IP pool / configuration set, so a campaign flagged as spam never touches auth or product deliverability 
 Human / corporate `alethialabs.io` (root) — your mailboxes only; locked down with DMARC, the app never sends from it 

Swap `alethialabs.io` for your own domain throughout.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 1. Verify the sending domains in SES
records.text:
In the SES console (in your chosen AWS region), create a **domain identity** for
each sending subdomain you'll use now — `auth.alethialabs.io` and
`mail.alethialabs.io`. (Add `marketing.alethialabs.io` later when you need it.)

records.title: Email
records.heading: 2. Enable DKIM
records.text:
For each identity, turn on **Easy DKIM**. SES gives you **3 CNAME records** per
domain — add them to your DNS. DKIM-signs every message and is required for DMARC
alignment.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 3. Set a custom MAIL FROM
records.text:
For each identity, set a **custom MAIL FROM** subdomain (e.g.
`bounce.auth.alethialabs.io`, `bounce.mail.alethialabs.io`) and add the records
SES shows:

- an **MX** record pointing at the SES regional mail endpoint, and
- an **SPF** `TXT` record: `"v=spf1 include:amazonses.com ~all"`.

This aligns SPF (and bounces) with your domain so DMARC passes.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 4. Add a DMARC policy
records.text:
Add a `TXT` record at `_dmarc.alethialabs.io`. Start in monitor mode and tighten
once reports look clean:

v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc@alethialabs.io

Progression: `p=none` → `p=quarantine` → `p=reject`.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 5. Leave the SES sandbox
records.text:
New SES accounts are **sandboxed** — they can only mail verified addresses.
Request **production access** in the SES console to send to anyone.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 6. Grant send permission
records.text:
SES authenticates with AWS credentials. Either:

- attach an **IAM role** with `ses:SendEmail` / `ses:SendRawEmail` to the host
 running the console (cleanest — no secrets in env), **or**
- create an **IAM user** with that permission and set `ALETHIA_SES_ACCESS_KEY_ID`
 / `ALETHIA_SES_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`.

records.title: Email
records.heading: 7. Configure the console
records.text:
Set these in `.env` (then `docker compose up -d app`):

ALETHIA_SES_REGION=eu-west-1 # the region you verified the domains in

records.title: Email
records.heading: Only if NOT using an IAM role:
records.text:
ALETHIA_SES_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
ALETHIA_SES_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...

AUTH_EMAIL_FROM="Alethia "
EMAIL_FROM="Alethia "

records.title: Email
records.heading: Optional — per-stream configuration sets (see "Bounce & complaint handling").
records.text:
ALETHIA_SES_AUTH_CONFIG_SET=alethia-auth
ALETHIA_SES_GENERAL_CONFIG_SET=alethia-general

`ALETHIA_SES_REGION` is the switch — set it and SES is used; leave it empty and
emails are logged. `EMAIL_FROM` falls back to `AUTH_EMAIL_FROM` if unset.

records.title: Email
records.heading: DNS records, at a glance
records.text:
Per sending subdomain (`auth.`, `mail.`, …):

 Record Host Type Value 
 --- --- --- --- 
 DKIM ×3 ` ._domainkey. ` CNAME ` .dkim.amazonses.com` (from SES) 
 MAIL FROM MX `bounce. ` MX `feedback-smtp. .amazonses.com` (priority 10) 
 MAIL FROM SPF `bounce. ` TXT `v=spf1 include:amazonses.com ~all` 

Once per root domain:

 Record Host Type Value 
 --- --- --- --- 
 DMARC `_dmarc.alethialabs.io` TXT `v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc@alethialabs.io`

records.title: Email
records.heading: Bounce & complaint handling (production)
records.text:
Optional, but recommended for a hosted deployment: track bounces and complaints
so the app stops mailing dead or complaining addresses (which protects your
sending reputation).

How it fits together:

- Each stream sends through an SES **configuration set**
 (`ALETHIA_SES_AUTH_CONFIG_SET` / `ALETHIA_SES_GENERAL_CONFIG_SET`).
- The configuration set publishes `Bounce` / `Complaint` events to an **SNS
 topic**, which posts them to the console at **`/api/webhooks/ses`**.
- That handler verifies the SNS signature and, on a **permanent** bounce or a
 **complaint**, adds the recipient to the **suppression list**. Every send then
 skips suppressed addresses.

For the hosted platform this whole stack — identities, DKIM, MAIL FROM, the
configuration sets, the SNS topic + subscription, account VDM, and reputation
alarms — is codified in OpenTofu at **`infra/email-ses/`** (see its `README.md`).
Self-hosters can either run that stack against their own domain or wire the same
pieces by hand; leaving the config-set vars unset keeps the simpler send-only
behavior.

records.title: Email
records.heading: Verify
records.text:
1. Open the console and request an email sign-in code.
2. It should arrive from `no-reply@auth.alethialabs.io` (check spam on the first
 send). If nothing arrives, `docker compose logs app` shows either the SES
 send error or — if SES isn't configured — the code itself.

records.title: Helm chart
records.text: Helm chart. Run the Alethia bundle on any managed Kubernetes cluster with the Helm chart.

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Helm chart (deploy/helm/alethia)
records.text:
For teams already on Kubernetes, the chart deploys the same bundle as the
[compose stack](./index) — console, docs, runner, and (optionally) bundled Postgres + object
store — wired behind a single Ingress (console at `/`, docs at `/docs`).

> **x86 only.** The published app-bundle images are built `linux/amd64`, so every pod carries a
> `nodeSelector` of `kubernetes.io/arch=amd64`. Use an x86 node pool, or clear it with
> `--set nodeSelector=null` once you publish multi-arch images.

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: What it deploys
records.text:
- **console** Deployment + Service. Two init containers run first: `wait-for-postgres`, then
 `migrate` (idempotent — applies schema/programmables and sets the app-role password).
- **docs** Deployment + Service.
- **runner** Deployment (self-registers with the console over the in-cluster Service URL).
- **postgres** StatefulSet + headless Service + PVC — *optional* (`postgres.enabled`).
- **seaweedfs** StatefulSet + headless Service + PVC + ConfigMap (S3 identity) — *optional*
 (`objectStore.enabled`).
- **Ingress** (`/docs*` → docs, `/` → console) with optional cert-manager TLS.
- **Secret** holding the connection strings + app secrets (or bring your own via
 `secrets.existingSecret`).

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A Kubernetes cluster with an **x86 node pool**, an **Ingress controller** (e.g. ingress-nginx),
 and a **default StorageClass** (for the bundled Postgres/object-store PVCs).
- **cert-manager** with a `ClusterIssuer` if you want automatic TLS.

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Install
records.text:
helm install alethia deploy/helm/alethia \
 --namespace alethia --create-namespace \
 --set domain=alethialabs.io \
 --set secrets.cliJwtSecret="$(openssl rand -hex 32)" \
 --set secrets.betterAuthSecret="$(openssl rand -hex 32)" \
 --set postgres.password="$(openssl rand -hex 16)" \
 --set postgres.appPassword="$(openssl rand -hex 16)" \
 --set objectStore.secretKey="$(openssl rand -hex 16)" \
 --set ingress.tls.clusterIssuer=letsencrypt-prod

Then point DNS for your domain at the ingress controller's external IP.

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Bring your own Postgres / object store
records.text:
Disable the bundled ones and point at managed services:
helm install alethia deploy/helm/alethia \
 --set postgres.enabled=false \
 --set postgres.host=db.internal --set postgres.port=5432 \
 --set postgres.user=alethia --set postgres.password=... \
 --set postgres.database=alethia --set postgres.appPassword=... \
 --set objectStore.enabled=false \
 --set objectStore.endpoint=https://s3.amazonaws.com \
 ...

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: App secrets (OAuth, email, …)
records.text:
Provider client IDs/secrets, `ALETHIA_SES_*` (email), and other app env go under `secrets.extraEnv` as a
map — they're merged into the chart-managed Secret and exposed to the console/runner via `envFrom`:
secrets:
 extraEnv:
 ALETHIA_SES_REGION: eu-central-1
 AUTH_EMAIL_FROM: Alethia 
 GITHUB_CLIENT_ID: ...
 GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET: ...
Or manage everything yourself in one Secret and set `secrets.existingSecret=my-secret` (it must hold
`ALETHIA_DATABASE_URL`, `ALETHIA_APP_DATABASE_URL`, `ALETHIA_APP_DB_PASSWORD`,
`ALETHIA_STORAGE_ENDPOINT`, `CLI_JWT_SECRET`, `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET`).

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Upgrade
records.text:
helm upgrade alethia deploy/helm/alethia --reuse-values --set image.tag=v1.2.3
The migrate init container re-runs on each rollout, so schema changes apply automatically.

records.title: Helm chart
records.heading: Key values
records.text:
Value Default Notes 
 --- --- --- 
 `image.registry` / `image.tag` `ghcr.io/alethialabs-io` / `latest` Pinned across components 
 `nodeSelector` `kubernetes.io/arch=amd64` `null` to disable 
 `domain` `alethialabs.io` Ingress host 
 `postgres.enabled` `true` `false` → set `postgres.host` 
 `objectStore.enabled` `true` `false` → set `objectStore.endpoint` 
 `ingress.className` `nginx` Ingress controller class 
 `ingress.tls.clusterIssuer` `letsencrypt-prod` cert-manager issuer

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.text: Self-Hosting. Run the whole Alethia platform on your own infrastructure with one docker-compose bundle.

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: Self-Hosting Alethia
records.text:
Alethia ships as a single **docker-compose bundle** — the control plane (console),
docs, Postgres, S3-compatible object storage (SeaweedFS), and a runner — behind a
Caddy reverse proxy with automatic HTTPS. It runs on **any cloud's VM** (AWS, GCP,
Azure, Alibaba, Hetzner) or your own hardware. No managed database or object store
required; nothing is welded to a single SaaS.

The whole stack is permissively licensed (Better Auth · Drizzle · SeaweedFS ·
Postgres · Caddy) — only Alethia's own code is AGPL.

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: Quickstart (one command)
records.text:
On a fresh Linux VM with a domain pointed at it:

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/main/deploy/install.sh \
 DOMAIN=alethia.example.com ACME_EMAIL=you@example.com sh

The installer ensures Docker, fetches the repo to `/opt/alethia`, generates secrets
into `.env`, and starts the bundle from prebuilt public images. Caddy obtains TLS
for your domain automatically. Omit `DOMAIN` to serve plain HTTP on `:80` for a
quick IP-only test.

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: Quickstart (manual)
records.text:
git clone https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs.git && cd alethialabs
cp .env.example .env # then edit: secrets, OAuth, ALETHIA_SES_* (email)
docker compose up -d # builds locally; or use the prod overlay (below) for prebuilt images

Production (prebuilt images + TLS), after setting `ALETHIA_DOMAIN`/`ALETHIA_ACME_EMAIL` in `.env`:

docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f deploy/prod/docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: Requirements
records.text:
- A Linux VM: **2+ vCPU / 4–8 GB RAM** (8 GB comfortable), ~25 GB disk.
- **Docker** + the Compose v2 plugin (the installer adds them).
- A **domain** (for automatic HTTPS). DNS `A`/`AAAA` → the VM's IP.
- Outbound network for image pulls and to reach the clouds you provision into.

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: What's in the bundle
records.text:
Service Role 
 --- --- 
 `app` Console (Next.js control plane) + the marketing site 
 `docs` This documentation, served at `/docs` 
 `postgres` Application database (Drizzle) 
 `seaweedfs` S3-compatible object storage (OpenTofu state + plan artifacts) 
 `runner` Executes provisioning jobs (OpenTofu) — runs as a self-operated runner 
 `caddy` Single ingress, automatic TLS 
 `pgweb` Optional DB browser (`--profile tools` → `:8081`)

records.title: Self-Hosting
records.heading: Next
records.text:
- **[Configuration](./configuration)** — every `.env` key (DB, storage, auth, OAuth, email).
- **[Terraform](./terraform)** — provision a hardened control-plane VM on a specific cloud (Hetzner, AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba), closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel (no open web ports).
- **[Helm chart](./helm)** — run the same bundle on a Kubernetes cluster instead of a single VM.
- **[One-click deploy](./one-click)** — Launch Stack (AWS) / Deploy to Azure buttons, no tooling.
- **[Upgrading & backups](./upgrading)** — updates, Postgres dumps, volume snapshots.

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.text: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup). Provision the platform cloud identity so your instance can verify and provision into customer AWS/GCP/Azure accounts. Every cloud federates keylessly off Alethia's own OIDC issuer.

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: Managed cloud connectors — operator setup
records.text:
Your customers connect their cloud accounts from **Connectors** in the console. For that to work, your
instance is its **own OIDC issuer** that customers' clouds trust directly. This page is the operator side;
the per-cloud *customer* flows live under [Connectors](/console/connectors).
