There is almost nothing to stand up: the issuer's signing key is auto-generated, so **AWS, GCP, Azure, and
Alibaba all work out of the box** — no platform cloud account, no platform Entra app, no per-cloud operator
step. Token clouds (Hetzner / DigitalOcean / Civo) use the customer's own API token and need nothing here.

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: The model: every cloud federates off Alethia's OIDC issuer
records.text:
There is a single root of trust — **Alethia's own OIDC issuer** (`/api/oidc`, signing key
`ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY`, auto-generated by `bootstrap-secrets.sh`). Every managed cloud trusts that
issuer directly and exchanges a short-lived, audience-scoped assertion for cloud credentials — **no platform
cloud account, no static key anywhere:**

 Cloud How it federates off the issuer 
 --- --- 
 **AWS** the customer's IAM role trusts the issuer as an **IAM OIDC provider** directly; the console/runner mint an assertion (audience `sts.amazonaws.com`) and call `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` straight into it. **No platform AWS account, no ExternalId.** 
 **GCP** the customer Workload-Identity pool trusts the issuer as an **OIDC provider** directly; the console/runner mint a fresh assertion (audience `alethia-gcp-wif`) and google-auth exchanges it for a GCP token. **No separate GCP secret.** 
 **Azure** the customer's [user-assigned managed identity](#azure-keyless-via-the-alethia-oidc-issuer) has a federated-identity credential trusting the issuer; the console/runner authenticate as it (`ClientAssertionCredential`) — no client secret, no platform Entra app. 
 **Alibaba** anonymous [`AssumeRol

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: AWS / GCP / Alibaba — nothing to configure
records.text:
These three are keyless **and** account-free on the operator side: the customer's IAM role (AWS) / WIF pool
(GCP) / RAM OIDC provider (Alibaba) trusts your issuer directly, and the console mints an assertion per call.
As long as `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY` is set (it is, by default), their **Connectors** tiles show **Connect**
immediately. Verify by connecting a test account (customer side: [AWS](/console/connectors/aws) ·
[GCP](/console/connectors/gcp) · [Alibaba](/console/connectors/alibaba)) — each verifies to **Connected** via
a server-side probe (no runner).

If a tile is unexpectedly greyed out, confirm the issuer's JWKS is reachable (the customer cloud validates
assertions against it):

curl -fsS https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc/jwks >/dev/null && echo OK

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: Azure — keyless via the Alethia OIDC issuer
records.text:
Azure is fully **keyless** — **no client secret** anywhere, and **no platform Entra app**. Alethia's
control plane is its own workload-identity OIDC issuer (`/api/oidc`, key `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY`,
auto-generated by `bootstrap-secrets.sh`). **There is no one-time operator step for Azure** — like
AWS/GCP/Alibaba, availability tracks the issuer key alone.

The customer runs the connect script (`infra/connector/azure`), which — entirely in **their own**
subscription — creates a **user-assigned managed identity** (a plain ARM resource, no App Registration),
adds a **federated-identity credential** trusting the issuer, and grants it the least-privilege
`Alethia Provisioner` role:

bash

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: subject "alethia-connector"; session/azure.ts: audience "api://AzureADTokenExchange").
records.text:
az identity create -g alethia-connector -n alethia-provisioner
az identity federated-credential create -g alethia-connector --identity-name alethia-provisioner \
 --name alethia-connector --issuer https://alethialabs.io/api/oidc \
 --subject alethia-connector --audiences api://AzureADTokenExchange

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: → prints tenant_id + client_id + subscription_id to paste into the connect sheet.
records.text:
The issuer must be **publicly reachable** — Azure fetches `${APP_URL}/api/oidc/jwks` to validate the
assertion, and that route 404s until `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY` exists (auto-generated by
`bootstrap-secrets.sh`, so run that before you expect Azure to verify).

Both halves then authenticate **as the customer's managed identity** with a minted assertion:
- **Console verification / inventory** mint an assertion and present it to the customer's stored tenant,
 as the identity's `client_id` (stored per connection).
- **Runner provisioning** fetches an assertion from the console (`/api/runners/azure-token`, runner-authed)
 and hands it to OpenTofu's azurerm provider via `ARM_OIDC_TOKEN` (exchanged for a ~1h ARM token at init).

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: Alibaba — keyless + account-free
records.text:
Alibaba is **keyless and account-free**: the console assumes the customer RAM role via STS
`AssumeRoleWithOIDC` with an assertion minted by the **same OIDC issuer** as Azure — Alethia holds **no
Alibaba account** and no AccessKey. There is **nothing to set here** (no `ALETHIA_ALIBABA_*`); availability
tracks the OIDC issuer key alone (`ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY`, auto-generated).

**Customer side** (documented at [Connectors → Alibaba](/console/connectors/alibaba)): the customer applies
an OpenTofu module that creates a **RAM OIDC provider** (fixed name `alethia`) trusting the Alethia issuer
+ a **RAM role**, then pastes the role ARN — Alethia derives the provider ARN from it. No operator action
beyond having the issuer key.

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: Rotating the issuer signing key
records.text:
The issuer's signing key (`ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY`) is the trust root for **all four** managed clouds, so
treat it like a CA key. It rotates with **zero downtime** via a two-key overlap — the JWKS publishes the old
and new key at once, and each assertion's `kid` header selects the right one, so in-flight assertions keep
verifying while new ones are signed by the new key. `scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh` drives it against the vault:

bash

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: 1. Mint a new primary; park the outgoing key in ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY_PREVIOUS (published, not signing).
records.text:
./scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh
gh workflow run deploy-console.yml # publish both keys; start signing with the new one

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: 3. Retire the old key.
records.text:
./scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh --finalize
gh workflow run deploy-console.yml # redeploy without the previous key

`./scripts/rotate-oidc-key.sh --status` shows what's currently in the vault (key fingerprints, never private
material). The script refuses to start a second rotation while one is unfinalized. No cloud-side change is
needed — Azure/Alibaba/AWS/GCP all re-fetch the JWKS and validate against whichever key signed the assertion.

records.title: Managed Cloud Connectors (operator setup)
records.heading: Reference
records.text:
- Vault key catalog: `deploy/prod/.env.production.example`; operator inputs:
 `deploy/prod/secrets.local.env.example`.
- The emit list in `.github/workflows/deploy-console.yml` is the gate — a key only reaches the app if
 it's emitted there.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.text: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook). Turn on the managed fleet so your instance provisions Kubernetes into customer AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, and Hetzner accounts — connectors, runner images, fleet pools, per-cloud verification, and the optional untrusted-BYO container sandbox.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Managed provisioning — operator runbook
records.text:
With the **managed** operator, your instance runs the provisioning runners itself: a cheap Hetzner VM fleet claims each
customer's plan/deploy job, assumes into the customer's cloud (keyless OIDC, or a token for Hetzner), runs
OpenTofu against Alethia's own project templates, and streams state + logs back through the console. The
customer never runs a runner.

All five clouds — **AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, Hetzner** — provision end-to-end. This page is the operator
checklist to enable it and verify each cloud. The per-cloud *customer* connect flow and the OIDC-issuer
platform setup live in [Managed cloud connectors](/self-hosting/managed-cloud-connectors); this page assumes
you've done that and covers the **fleet** side.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Architecture (what happens on a deploy)
records.text:
1. A customer connects a cloud (keyless OIDC for AWS/GCP/Azure/Alibaba; an API token for Hetzner) and
 creates a project, then queues a **plan** or **deploy**.
2. The in-app **scaler** (`lib/fleet`, 60s tick) sizes a per-provider warm pool and creates a Hetzner VM.
 Its cloud-init runs the per-cloud runner image `ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner- ` and the VM
 **self-registers** with a per-VM, short-TTL, instance-bound bootstrap token.
3. The runner claims the job, mints a **per-job cloud token** (OIDC) and a **per-job tofu-state token**, and
 runs `tofu init/plan/apply` against the baked project template. Tofu **state** goes to the console HTTP
 state proxy (`/api/jobs/[id]/state`) — no storage credential ever touches the runner.
4. The kubeconfig comes from the cloud (EKS/GKE/AKS API) or a tofu output (ACK/Talos), post-apply Helm/ArgoCD
 runs, and the job completes. The VM is disposable (SLOTS=1).

The fleet holds **no** storage master key and **no** shared bootstrap token — both were drained from the
cloud-init (E0 2b/0b). Untrusted **BYO** execution additionally requires the container sandbox — see
[Untrusted BYO](#untrusted-byo-container-sandbox-3b) at the end.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A working self-hosted deploy (console + migrate + storage reachable). See [Configuration](/self-hosting/configuration).
- The **OIDC issuer** signing key `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY` — auto-generated by `scripts/bootstrap-secrets.sh`.
 Its discovery + JWKS routes (`/api/oidc/...`) must be **publicly reachable** (Azure/Alibaba validate the
 assertion). This is set up in [Managed cloud connectors](/self-hosting/managed-cloud-connectors).
- Object storage configured (`ALETHIA_STORAGE_*` on the **console** — NOT the fleet).

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: 1. Per-cloud connector setup (one-time)
records.text:
Do this once per cloud you want to offer. Full detail in
[Managed cloud connectors](/self-hosting/managed-cloud-connectors); summary:

 Cloud Platform operator step Customer step 
 --- --- --- 
 **AWS** none (issuer key only) `tofu apply infra/connector/aws` → paste **role ARN** 
 **GCP** none (issuer key only) `tofu apply infra/connector/gcp` → paste **credential config** 
 **Alibaba** none (issuer key only) `tofu apply infra/connector/alibaba` → paste **role ARN** 
 **Azure** none (issuer key only) `tofu apply infra/connector/azure` → paste **tenant + client + subscription ids** 
 **Hetzner** none paste a Hetzner **API token** (token auth — no OIDC) 

The connect UI for each provider only lights up once its platform prerequisite is satisfied
(`computePlatformConfigured`): AWS/GCP/Azure/Alibaba all track the issuer signing key alone, Hetzner is
always available.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: 2. Runner images
records.text:
Managed provisioning of a cloud requires its **per-cloud runner image** to be published to GHCR. The deploy
pipeline (`deploy-console.yml`) builds and pushes all five on a push to `main`:

ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner-aws runner-gcp runner-azure
ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner-alibaba runner-hetzner

`runner-alibaba` and `runner-hetzner` carry no cloud CLI (Alibaba is keyless OIDC with a static kubeconfig
from the ACK output; Hetzner is Talos with a static kubeconfig from the talos output). Confirm the tags exist
for the tag your fleet pins (`FLEET_RUNNER_IMAGE_TAG`, default `latest`):

for c in aws gcp azure alibaba hetzner; do
 docker manifest inspect ghcr.io/alethialabs-io/runner-$c:latest >/dev/null && echo "runner-$c OK"
done

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: 3. Fleet enablement
records.text:
The fleet is off until you select the Hetzner provider and give it a token. Set on the **console** env:

 Var Required Notes 
 --- --- --- 
 `FLEET_PROVIDER=hcloud` yes selects the Hetzner backend (default `manual` = no-op) 
 `HCLOUD_TOKEN` yes Hetzner API token for the **fleet** project (distinct from a customer connector token) 
 `HCLOUD_SSH_KEYS` recommended comma-separated Hetzner SSH key IDs (break-glass access) 
 `ALETHIA_RUNNER_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN` auto seeded by the runner script on first run; the scaler mints per-VM tokens from it 
 `HCLOUD_SERVER_TYPE` no default `cax21` (arm64) 
 `FLEET_RUNNER_IMAGE_TAG` no default `latest` 
 `FLEET_RUNNER_SLOTS` no default `1` (one job per VM) 

Then create **one fleet pool per cloud** you want to provision. Pools are DB rows (`fleet_pools`), created
from the console fleet UI (self-managed deployments) — the `createFleetPool` action, gated on the `fleet`
resource. Each pool sets `provider`, `warm_min`, `max`, `locations`, etc.; the scaler re-reads enabled pools
every tick (no restart). A paused (`enabled=false`) pool is skipped.

> The fleet UI + pool reads/writes are only available on a **self-managed** deployment
> (`isFleetOperatorDeploy

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: 4. Per-cloud verification
records.text:
For each cloud, end to end:

1. **Connect** the cloud in the console (paste the role ARN / credential config / token). The cloud identity
 verifies server-side (no runner needed).
2. **Create a project** targeting that cloud and a small cluster.
3. **Plan** — a VM spins up in that cloud's pool, claims the job, and returns a plan + a `verify.Report`
 (elench) in the console Plan tab. Confirm:
 - a `fleet- -…` server appears in Hetzner and self-registers as a runner,
 - the plan streams logs and completes,
 - no `ALETHIA_STORAGE_*` / bootstrap secret is in the job env (they're not injected).
4. **Deploy** — apply provisions the cluster; the kubeconfig is captured (EKS/GKE/AKS API call, or the
 ACK/Talos tofu output) and post-apply Helm/ArgoCD runs.
5. **Destroy** — tears the cluster down and purges the tofu state (`DELETE …/state`).

Repeat for AWS, GCP, Azure, Alibaba, Hetzner. Alibaba provisions a real managed **ACK** cluster; Hetzner
provisions a self-managed **Talos** cluster.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Troubleshooting
records.text:
- **VM boots but never registers** — check the per-VM bootstrap token TTL (`FLEET_BOOTSTRAP_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS`,
 default 3600) vs. a slow multi-GB image pull, and the boot grace (`FLEET_BOOT_GRACE_SECONDS`).
- **`runner- ` image pull fails** — the tag isn't published for that cloud; re-check step 2.
- **Cloud auth fails on plan** — the customer's connector `tofu apply` is missing (no managed identity /
 role), or the issuer isn't publicly reachable (`${APP_URL}/api/oidc/jwks` must resolve).

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Untrusted BYO (container sandbox, 3b)
records.text:
Everything above provisions Alethia's **own** (trusted) templates — safe to run in-process (Passthrough).
Running **untrusted** customer code (BYO Helm/IaC) on the managed fleet additionally requires the per-job
**container sandbox**: a nested rootless-podman container per job with an allowlisted env, its own PID
namespace, and a default-deny domain-allowlist egress net (so it cannot reach the `169.254.169.254` metadata
service, which serves the VM's cloud-init).

The Go backend and the fleet plumbing ship **inert**. Turn-on is **config-only** on the fleet — but the
isolation must be **proven on a real VM first**, because nested podman + `/dev/fuse` + IMDS egress cannot be
tested in CI.

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Turn-on sequence
records.text:
1. **Enable the sandbox on a canary pool** — set on the fleet cloud-init env (`renderCloudInit`, gated by
 `FLEET_SANDBOX_CONTAINER=1`). This renders the default-deny egress net + squid forward proxy and starts
 the runner with the container backend + proxy env. **Do NOT set `FLEET_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ENFORCED` yet** — with
 it unset the container backend **fail-closes on managed** (jobs refuse), which proves the runtime works
 before you trust egress.

 FLEET_SANDBOX_CONTAINER=1
 FLEET_EGRESS_EXTRA_DOMAINS= # optional
 FLEET_EGRESS_PROXY_IMAGE=ubuntu/squid@sha256: # pin a digest in prod
 
2. **Run the real-VM canary** on a live Hetzner VM. All must pass:
 - `podman run --rm true` (as `runner`) succeeds — proves subuid/subgid + fuse-overlayfs +
 crun + the `--device /dev/fuse` + seccomp/apparmor/systempaths relaxations.
 - From inside the nested child: it **cannot** read `/proc/1/environ`, `$ALETHIA_RUNNER_TOKEN`, or the
 storage/bootstrap secrets, and sees only its own PID tree.
 - `curl --max-time 3 http://169.254.169.254/...` from the child **times out** — with the proxy env set
 AND unset (the `--internal` net + iptables belt + non-allowlisted domain all deny it).
 - Through the pro

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: Fleet env — 3b knobs
records.text:
Var Meaning 
 --- --- 
 `FLEET_SANDBOX_CONTAINER` render the egress net + proxy + container-backend runner (master switch) 
 `FLEET_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ENFORCED` set `ALETHIA_SANDBOX_EGRESS_ENFORCED=1` — **only after** the canary passes 
 `FLEET_SANDBOX_ENFORCE_MANAGED` fleet-wide kill-switch: a managed pool lacking the container backend refuses 
 `FLEET_EGRESS_EXTRA_DOMAINS` extra allowlist domains (comma-separated) 
 `FLEET_EGRESS_PROXY_IMAGE` the forward-proxy image (pin a digest)

records.title: Managed Provisioning (5-cloud runbook)
records.heading: If nested podman is fragile
records.text:
Nested rootless podman + fuse-overlayfs on the arm64 Hetzner kernel is the fragile layer. Fallbacks, in order:
switch the containers `storage.conf` driver to `vfs` (slower but robust) → add `--cap-add SETUID --cap-add
SETGID` to the outer run for `newuidmap` → escalate to a microVM SKU (sysbox/Kata) — a larger fleet change.

Until the canary passes, managed **untrusted** BYO stays flag-gated trusted-only; the trusted 5-cloud
provisioning above is unaffected.

records.title: One-click deploy
records.text: One-click deploy. Launch the Alethia bundle on AWS or Azure with a single button — no OpenTofu, no shell.

records.title: One-click deploy
records.heading: One-click deploy
records.text:
The fastest way to stand up a control plane on a public cloud: a button that opens the cloud's own
console with a prefilled template. Each template provisions **one x86 VM**, opens 22/80/443, and runs
the [installer](./index) via cloud-init — the same bundle as everywhere else.

> **x86 only.** The published app-bundle images are `linux/amd64`, so both templates default to x86
> instances (AWS `t3`, Azure `Dsv5`).

> **Domain afterwards.** Leave the domain blank to come up on HTTP at the public IP first, then point
> DNS at it and redeploy with the domain set for automatic HTTPS. Or pre-create the DNS record so the
> first boot can obtain TLS.

records.title: One-click deploy
records.heading: AWS — Launch Stack (CloudFormation)
records.text:
[![Launch Stack](https://s3.amazonaws.com/cloudformation-examples/cloudformation-launch-stack.png)](https://console.aws.amazon.com/cloudformation/home#/stacks/create/review?templateURL=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/main/deploy/oneclick/aws-cloudformation.yaml&stackName=alethia)

Template: [`deploy/oneclick/aws-cloudformation.yaml`](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/blob/main/deploy/oneclick/aws-cloudformation.yaml)

Creates an EC2 `t3.medium` (Ubuntu 24.04 amd64, gp3 root), a security group (22/80/443), and an
Elastic IP. Parameters: `Domain`, `AcmeEmail`, `InstanceType`, `KeyName` (an existing key pair),
`AllowedSshCidr`, `VolumeSize`. The AMI resolves from Canonical's public SSM parameter, so it's
current in every region. Read `PublicIp` from the stack **Outputs**.

You can also launch it from the CLI:
aws cloudformation deploy --stack-name alethia \
 --template-file deploy/oneclick/aws-cloudformation.yaml \
 --parameter-overrides Domain=alethia.example.com AcmeEmail=you@example.com KeyName=my-key \
 --capabilities CAPABILITY_IAM

records.title: One-click deploy
records.heading: Azure — Deploy to Azure (ARM template)
records.text:
[![Deploy to Azure](https://aka.ms/deploytoazurebutton)](https://portal.azure.com/#create/Microsoft.Template/uri/https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Falethialabs-io%2Falethialabs%2Fmain%2Fdeploy%2Foneclick%2Fazure-arm.json)

Template: [`deploy/oneclick/azure-arm.json`](https://github.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/blob/main/deploy/oneclick/azure-arm.json)

Creates a `Standard_D2s_v5` (x86, Ubuntu 24.04 amd64) VM with a static public IP, VNet,
and an NSG (22/80/443). Parameters: `domain`, `acmeEmail`, `adminUsername`, `sshPublicKey`,
`vmSize`, `location`. Read `publicIp` from the deployment **Outputs**.

From the CLI:
az deployment group create --resource-group alethia-cp \
 --template-file deploy/oneclick/azure-arm.json \
 --parameters domain=alethia.example.com acmeEmail=you@example.com \
 sshPublicKey="$(cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub)"

records.title: One-click deploy
records.heading: When to use what
records.text:
One-click [Terraform](./terraform) [Helm](./helm) 
 --- --- --- --- 
 Best for a quick managed VM, no tooling repeatable IaC + CI (plan/apply) existing Kubernetes 
 State the cloud's stack/deployment your S3 state bucket the cluster 
 DNS manual / pre-created Cloudflare, automated your ingress + DNS 

All three land on the same bundle — switch later without reinstalling.

records.title: Alibaba
records.text: Alibaba. Provision the Alethia control plane on an x86 ECS instance, closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel, admin via Session Manager.

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: Alibaba (infra/cp-alibaba)
records.text:
A single x86 **`ecs.g7.large`** (2 vCPU / 8 GB) ECS instance fronted by a Cloudflare Tunnel.
**≈ $75–95/mo pay-as-you-go** (Alibaba list price, region-dependent; markedly cheaper on a monthly
subscription — not priced by Infracost).

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: What it creates
records.text:
- `alicloud_instance` (`ecs.g7.large`, Ubuntu 24.04 x86_64) with a 40 GB **encrypted** `cloud_essd`
 system disk
- `alicloud_vpc` + `alicloud_vswitch` (AZ auto-picked to one that has the x86 type)
- `alicloud_security_group` with **no ingress rules** — web is the tunnel, admin is Session Manager
- `cloudflare_zero_trust_tunnel_cloudflared` (+ config) and **proxied** CNAMEs (apex + `www`)
- cloud-init via `user_data` (installs Docker, clones the repo)

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: Access — Session Manager / Cloud Assistant (no open port)
records.text:
The Cloud Assistant agent (preinstalled on the Ubuntu image) dials out:
- **Interactive:** ECS Session Manager (Alibaba console → ECS → the instance → *Connect*).
- **Automation:** `aliyun ecs RunCommand`.

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- An Alibaba Cloud account with a **RAM access key** (ECS + VPC permissions).
- A Cloudflare **API token** (DNS edit + Zero Trust), the **zone id**, and the **account id**.

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
cd infra/cp-alibaba
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # region, cloudflare_* (token/zone/account)
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: CI secrets (infra-cp-alibaba.yml)
records.text:
`ALICLOUD_ACCESS_KEY`, `ALICLOUD_SECRET_KEY` (RAM key), `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID`,
`CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`, and the `TF_STATE_S3_*` backend secrets. `apply` is gated behind
`vars.INFRA_ALIBABA_APPLY`.

records.title: Alibaba
records.heading: Tuning
records.text:
`instance_type` (`ecs.g7.large` default — **must be x86**), `system_disk_size`, `region`, `zone_id`
(leave empty to auto-pick). An auto-assigned public IP handles egress.

records.title: AWS
records.text: AWS. Provision the Alethia control plane on an x86 EC2 instance, closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel, SSH-free via SSM.

records.title: AWS
records.heading: AWS (infra/cp-aws)
records.text:
A single x86 **EC2 `t3.large`** (2 vCPU / 8 GB) fronted by a Cloudflare Tunnel — **no bastion, no
open port**. **≈ $72/mo** (t3.large + 60 GB gp3; Infracost, on-demand, `eu-west-1`; `t3.medium` ≈ half if 4 GB fits).

records.title: AWS
records.heading: What it creates
records.text:
- `aws_instance` (`t3.large`, Ubuntu 24.04 amd64) with an encrypted 60 GB gp3 root; **IMDSv2 required**
- `aws_security_group` with **no ingress** (egress only) — web is the tunnel, SSH is SSM
- IAM role + instance profile with **`AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore`** only (Session Manager)
- `cloudflare_zero_trust_tunnel_cloudflared` (+ config) and **proxied** CNAMEs (apex + `www`)
- cloud-init: installs Docker, clones the repo (Docker data on the durable EBS root)

records.title: AWS
records.heading: Access — SSM Session Manager
records.text:
No SSH port is open. The SSM agent (preinstalled on the AMI) dials out; connect with:
aws ssm start-session --target

records.title: AWS
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- AWS credentials for the target account (`AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` / `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`).
- A Cloudflare **API token** with **DNS edit + Zero Trust (Tunnel)** perms, the **zone id**, and the
 **account id**.

records.title: AWS
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
cd infra/cp-aws
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # cloudflare_* (token/zone/account), domain, aws_region
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

records.title: AWS
records.heading: CI secrets (infra-cp-aws.yml)
records.text:
`AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` (provider), `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID`,
`CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`, and the `TF_STATE_S3_*` backend secrets. `apply` is gated behind
`vars.INFRA_AWS_APPLY`.

records.title: AWS
records.heading: Tuning
records.text:
`instance_type` (`t3.large` default — **must be x86**), `root_volume_size`, `aws_region`. Runs in the
account's default VPC with an auto-assigned public IP (egress only).

records.title: Azure
records.text: Azure. Provision the Alethia control plane on an x86 Gen2 Linux VM, closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel, admin via run-command.

records.title: Azure
records.heading: Azure (infra/cp-azure)
records.text:
A single x86 Gen2 **`Standard_D2s_v5`** (2 vCPU / 8 GB) Linux VM fronted by a Cloudflare Tunnel.
**≈ $90/mo** (D2s_v5 + OS disk + public IP; Infracost, on-demand, `westeurope`).

records.title: Azure
records.heading: What it creates
records.text:
- `azurerm_linux_virtual_machine` (`Standard_D2s_v5`, Ubuntu 24.04 amd64) with a 30 GB StandardSSD OS
 disk; **Trusted Launch** (secure boot + vTPM); a system-assigned **managed identity**; boot
 diagnostics (for Serial Console)
- `azurerm_resource_group`, `azurerm_virtual_network` + `azurerm_subnet`
- `azurerm_network_security_group` with **no inbound rules** + NIC association
- `azurerm_public_ip` (Standard) — egress only
- `cloudflare_zero_trust_tunnel_cloudflared` (+ config) and **proxied** CNAMEs (apex + `www`)
- cloud-init via `custom_data` (installs Docker, clones the repo)

records.title: Azure
records.heading: Access — no open port
records.text:
No Bastion, no open SSH. Automation via **`az vm run-command`** (control-plane, no port); interactive
break-glass via the **Serial Console**:
az vm run-command invoke -g -n alethia-cp --command-id RunShellScript --scripts '…'
az serial-console connect -g -n alethia-cp

records.title: Azure
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- An Azure **subscription** and a **service principal** with Contributor on it.
- A Cloudflare **API token** (DNS edit + Zero Trust), the **zone id**, and the **account id**.

records.title: Azure
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
cd infra/cp-azure
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # location, cloudflare_* (token/zone/account)
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

records.title: Azure
records.heading: CI secrets (infra-cp-azure.yml)
records.text:
`ARM_CLIENT_ID`, `ARM_CLIENT_SECRET`, `ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID`, `ARM_TENANT_ID` (service principal),
`CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID`, `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`, and the `TF_STATE_S3_*` backend
secrets. `apply` is gated behind `vars.INFRA_AZURE_APPLY`.

records.title: Azure
records.heading: Tuning
records.text:
`vm_size` (`Standard_D2s_v5` default — **must be x86 Gen2** for Trusted Launch), `os_disk_size`,
`location`.

records.title: GCP
records.text: GCP. Provision the Alethia control plane on an x86 Compute Engine VM, closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel, SSH via IAP.

records.title: GCP
records.heading: GCP (infra/cp-gcp)
records.text:
A single x86 **`e2-standard-2`** (2 vCPU / 8 GB) Compute Engine VM fronted by a Cloudflare Tunnel.
**≈ $57/mo** (e2-standard-2 + 30 GB pd-balanced; Infracost, on-demand, `europe-west4`).

records.title: GCP
records.heading: What it creates
records.text:
- `google_compute_instance` (`e2-standard-2`, Ubuntu 24.04 amd64) with a 30 GB `pd-balanced` boot
 disk; **Shielded VM** (secure boot + vTPM); a dedicated least-priv service account
- `google_compute_firewall` — **SSH (`22`) from the IAP range `35.235.240.0/20` only**; no 80/443
- an ephemeral external IP (egress only)
- `cloudflare_zero_trust_tunnel_cloudflared` (+ config) and **proxied** CNAMEs (apex + `www`)
- cloud-init via the `user-data` metadata key (installs Docker, clones the repo)

records.title: GCP
records.heading: Access — IAP (no public SSH)
records.text: gcloud compute ssh alethia-cp --zone --tunnel-through-iap

records.title: GCP
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A GCP **project** with the Compute Engine + IAP APIs enabled.
- A **service-account JSON key** (provider auth → `GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS`).
- A Cloudflare **API token** (DNS edit + Zero Trust), the **zone id**, and the **account id**.

records.title: GCP
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
cd infra/cp-gcp
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # gcp_project, gcp_region/zone, cloudflare_* (token/zone/account)
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

records.title: GCP
records.heading: CI secrets (infra-cp-gcp.yml)
records.text:
`GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS` (SA JSON), `GCP_PROJECT`, `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID`,
`CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`, and the `TF_STATE_S3_*` backend secrets. `apply` is gated behind
`vars.INFRA_GCP_APPLY`.

records.title: GCP
records.heading: Tuning
records.text:
`machine_type` (`e2-standard-2` default — **must be x86**), `boot_disk_size`, `gcp_region`/`gcp_zone`.

> The egress-only public IP is a single reviewed Trivy exception (`AVD-GCP-0031`) — inbound is closed
> (web via tunnel, SSH via IAP), so a Cloud NAT (~10× the cost for one box) isn't worth it.

records.title: Hetzner
records.text: Hetzner. Provision the Alethia control plane on a Hetzner Cloud x86 VM, closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel.

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: Hetzner (infra/cp-hetzner)
records.text:
The cheapest target, and the one the hosted alethialabs.io runs on: a Hetzner Cloud **CX33** (Intel
x86, 4 vCPU / 8 GB) with an attached data volume, fronted by a Cloudflare Tunnel. **≈ €14/mo**
(Hetzner list price: `cx33` €13.10 + 25 GB volume €1.19; not priced by Infracost).

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: What it creates
records.text:
- `hcloud_server` (`cx33`) + `hcloud_volume` (25 GB, holds Postgres + object storage) + attachment
- `hcloud_firewall` — **SSH only** (`22`); no 80/443 (ingress is the tunnel)
- `cloudflare_zero_trust_tunnel_cloudflared` (+ config) and **proxied** `cloudflare_record` CNAMEs
 (apex + `www`) onto ` .cfargotunnel.com`
- cloud-init: installs Docker, moves Docker's data-root onto the volume, clones the repo

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A Hetzner Cloud **project + API token** (`HCLOUD_TOKEN`).
- A Cloudflare **API token** with **DNS edit + Zero Trust (Tunnel)** perms, the **zone id**, and the
 **account id**.
- An SSH **public key** (SSH is the admin channel on Hetzner).

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: Apply
records.text:
cd infra/cp-hetzner
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl # fill S3 endpoint + creds
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # hcloud_token, cloudflare_* (token/zone/account), ssh_public_key, domain
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: CI secrets (infra-cp-hetzner.yml)
records.text:
`HCLOUD_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN`, `CLOUDFLARE_ZONE_ID`, `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID`,
`DEPLOY_SSH_PUBLIC_KEY`, `TF_STATE_S3_ENDPOINT REGION ACCESS_KEY_ID SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`.

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: Tuning
records.text:
`server_type` (`cx33` default; `cx43` if busy), `data_volume_size`, `location` (any EU region),
`ssh_allowed_cidrs` (SSH source restriction).

records.title: Hetzner
records.heading: Also here — inbound email
records.text:
Hetzner owns the zone-global Cloudflare **Email Routing** for the apex addresses (support@, sales@,
…) — see [Email](../email). It's gated off until you import the existing routing.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.text: Terraform (per-cloud). Provision a hardened control-plane VM on any of five clouds — closed-origin behind a Cloudflare Tunnel — with OpenTofu.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: Terraform modules
records.text:
The repeatable IaC path. Each cloud has a self-contained OpenTofu stack under `infra/cp- /`
that provisions **one** control-plane VM and fronts it with a **Cloudflare Tunnel**, so the box has
**no public web ports**. It lands on the same [bundle](../index) as the [one-click](../one-click) VM
and the [Helm](../helm) chart — pick this when you want version-controlled infrastructure with a
plan/apply CI loop.

> **Pick one.** The stacks all serve the **same domain/zone**, so they are alternatives — apply
> **one instead of another**, never two at once (two would fight over the apex DNS + tunnel). The
> hosted alethialabs.io runs on Hetzner.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: Status
records.text:
Cloud Module Box (x86) Cost/mo Admin access (no open web port) Page 
 --- --- --- --- --- --- 
 Hetzner `infra/cp-hetzner` `cx33` ≈ €14 SSH, key-only (firewall SSH-only) [Hetzner](./hetzner) 
 AWS `infra/cp-aws` `t3.large` ≈ $72 SSM Session Manager [AWS](./aws) 
 GCP `infra/cp-gcp` `e2-standard-2` ≈ $57 IAP tunnel [GCP](./gcp) 
 Azure `infra/cp-azure` `Standard_D2s_v5` ≈ $90 `az vm run-command` + Serial Console [Azure](./azure) 
 Alibaba `infra/cp-alibaba` `ecs.g7.large` ≈ $75–95 ECS Session Manager [Alibaba](./alibaba) 

> **x86 boxes.** The published app-bundle images are `linux/amd64`, so every module provisions an x86
> host (the per-cloud *runner* images are arm64, but they run on the managed fleet, not this box).

> **Costs** are on-demand estimates at each module's default region. AWS/GCP/Azure are from
> **Infracost**; Hetzner and Alibaba (which Infracost doesn't price) are **list prices**. See each
> cloud's page for the breakdown; a monthly/subscription commitment is cheaper on most clouds.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: The model — closed origin, zero standing inbound
records.text:
Every stack follows the same shape:

- **Ingress is a Cloudflare Tunnel.** `cloudflared` (a service in the bundle) dials **out**; nothing
 dials in. The firewall / security group exposes **no 80/443**. Apex and `www` are **proxied**
 `CNAME`s onto ` .cfargotunnel.com`, and TLS terminates at Cloudflare's edge.
- **The connector token is an output.** The stack exports `tunnel_token` (sensitive); set it as
 `TUNNEL_TOKEN` in the box's `.env` and the compose `cloudflared` connects with nothing else
 (`config_src = "cloudflare"`, remotely-managed).
- **The public IP is egress-only** (image pulls, `git`, dialing the tunnel out); inbound is closed.
- **Hardened + asserted.** Each stack ships a `checks.tf` that fails the plan if the posture regresses
 (no web ingress, disk encryption / verified boot, tunnel remotely-managed).

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: Shared workflow
records.text:
Every module is the same three steps:

cd infra/cp- 
cp backend.hcl.example backend.hcl # S3-compatible state backend (endpoint + creds)
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars # cloud creds, domain, cloudflare_* ids
tofu init -backend-config=backend.hcl
tofu plan && tofu apply

Then wire the tunnel token into the bundle `.env`:


State lives in an S3-compatible bucket (`TF_STATE_S3_*`), one key per stack
(` -cp/terraform.tfstate`). DNS records are **proxied** CNAMEs onto the tunnel — there is no
open origin. See each cloud's page for the exact GitHub secrets.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: Prerequisites
records.text:
- A **Cloudflare account + zone** and an API token with **DNS edit** and **Zero Trust (Cloudflare
 Tunnel)** permissions — you pass `cloudflare_account_id` and `cloudflare_zone_id`.
- An **S3-compatible state backend** (`backend.hcl`).
- The chosen cloud's own credentials.

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: CI
records.text:
Each stack has a workflow (`infra-cp-hetzner.yml`, `infra-cp-aws.yml`, `infra-cp-gcp.yml`,
`infra-cp-azure.yml`, `infra-cp-alibaba.yml`): the PR **`plan`** job runs `tofu validate` + fmt/tflint/
Trivy; the **`apply`** job is **gated off** behind `vars.INFRA_ _APPLY` (prod runs on Hetzner).

records.title: Terraform (per-cloud)
records.heading: Not managed here
records.text:
Cloudflare **Email Routing** is zone-global and lives only in `cp-hetzner` (see [Email](../email)),
so the stacks don't collide on the shared zone.

records.title: Upgrading & Backups
records.text: Upgrading & Backups. Update a self-hosted Alethia bundle and back up its data.

records.title: Upgrading & Backups
records.heading: Upgrading
records.text:
Re-run the installer (it's idempotent — it pulls the latest images and restarts):

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/alethialabs-io/alethialabs/main/deploy/install.sh sh

Or manually from `/opt/alethia`:

git pull
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f deploy/prod/docker-compose.prod.yml pull
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f deploy/prod/docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --remove-orphans

Database migrations run automatically on boot (the one-shot `migrate` service runs
before `app` starts). Your `.env` is never overwritten.

records.title: Upgrading & Backups
records.heading: Backups
records.text:
Two things hold state: **Postgres** and the **object store** (OpenTofu state +
plan artifacts). OpenTofu state is the only un-regenerable data — back it up.

**Postgres** (schedule via cron):

docker compose exec -T postgres pg_dump -U alethia alethia gzip > backup-$(date +%F).sql.gz

**Object storage** — snapshot the `seaweedfs-data` volume, or `aws s3 sync` it to
an off-box bucket. If you point `ALETHIA_STORAGE_*` at managed S3/R2, use that
provider's backups instead.

On managed hosts, also enable the provider's **volume/server snapshots** for an
off-box copy (e.g. Hetzner backups, EBS snapshots).

records.title: Upgrading & Backups
records.heading: Inspecting the database
records.text: docker compose --profile tools up -d pgweb # → http://localhost:8081

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.text: Device Authorization Grant. How `alethia login` / `alethia init` authenticate the CLI over a browser-approval flow modeled on OAuth 2.0's Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) — device code, exchange, CLI JWT, and refresh.

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
The **OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant** ([RFC 8628](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8628))
authenticates a client that cannot easily host a browser or receive a redirect — a CLI, a TV, an IoT
device. The device asks the authorization server for a **device code** plus a human-readable
**user code**, tells the user to open a **verification URI** and enter the code, then **polls** a
token endpoint until the user approves (`authorization_pending` / `slow_down`) or the code expires.
The user authenticates in a full browser on another device; the CLI never handles their password.

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
`alethia login` (and the guided `alethia init`) run a browser-approval flow in this spirit, with a
couple of deliberate deviations from the literal RFC — documented below so the behaviour is
auditable. The CLI code lives in `apps/cli/cmd/login.go`; the control-plane routes are under
`apps/console/app/api/auth/cli/`.

 >CLI: device_code = new UUID
 CLI->>BR: open /cli/login?device_code=UUID
 CLI->>EX: poll {device_code} every 2s
 BR->>GEN: approve (requires Better Auth session)
 GEN->>DB: upsert device_code -> profile_id (+ git token)
 EX->>DB: lookup approved record
 EX-->>CLI: 404 while pending
 EX->>DB: found -> delete record (one-time)
 EX-->>CLI: 200 { access_token, refresh_token }
 CLI->>CLI: write ~/.config/alethia/credentials.json
`} />

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Start
records.text:
`alethia login` generates a `device_code` (a client-side **UUID**, `uuid.New()`), opens
`${web-origin}/cli/login?device_code= ` in the browser, and starts polling.

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Approve in the browser
records.text:
The `/cli/login` page (`apps/console/app/(private)/cli/login/page.tsx`) requires an authenticated
Better Auth session and `POST`s to `/api/auth/cli/generate`. That route upserts a `cli_logins` row
binding the `device_code` to `session.user.id`, and best-effort stashes the user's first linked git
provider token (held transiently in `verification_code`).

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Poll to exchange
records.text:
The CLI polls `POST /api/auth/cli/exchange` with `{ device_code }` every 2 seconds. A **404** means
"pending" (`pollForToken` keeps waiting); any other non-2xx is fatal.

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Mint and store
records.text:
Once the row shows a `profile_id`, `exchange` **deletes the record** (single use) and mints two JWTs
with the `jose` library, signed HS256 with `CLI_JWT_SECRET`:

- `access_token` — `type: "access"`, **1 hour** TTL.
- `refresh_token` — `type: "refresh"`, **90 day** TTL.

Both are written to `~/.config/alethia/credentials.json` (`os.UserConfigDir()/alethia/credentials.json`,
mode `0755` dir). The git provider token rides back as `provider_token`.

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Refresh
records.text:
Before each authed call, `getAuthTokenInternal` (`apps/cli/cmd/auth_utils.go`) parses the access
token's `exp`; if it is within 1 minute of expiry it calls `POST /api/auth/cli/refresh` with the
refresh token, which verifies `type === "refresh"` and issues a fresh 1-hour access token. When the
refresh token is gone or invalid, the CLI re-runs the browser flow.

 

 
 **Deviations from RFC 8628 (be precise).** Alethia's flow is *modeled on* the device grant but is
 not a literal implementation:
 • There is **no separate short `user_code` the human types** — the `device_code` is a
 client-generated UUID passed directly in the browser URL, and approval is a click, not a code entry.
 • Pending status is signalled with **HTTP 404**, not the RFC's `authorization_pending` /
 `slow_down` token-endpoint errors; the poll interval is a fixed 2s.
 • The CLI tokens are **HS256 JWTs** with placeholder `iss`/`aud` (`urn:example:issuer` /
 `urn:example:audience`), distinct from the Better Auth web session. See [JWT / JWS](/standards/jose).

records.title: Device Authorization Grant
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text: - [RFC 8628](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8628) — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant.

records.title: Standards & Protocols
records.text: Standards & Protocols. The open standards Alethia builds on — OAuth 2.0, OIDC, the Device Authorization Grant, cloud workload identity, JOSE, SAML/SCIM — with the exact code that implements each.

records.title: Standards & Protocols
records.heading: Standards & Protocols
records.text:
Alethia authenticates people, machines, and cloud accounts entirely on published standards — no
bespoke crypto, no proprietary handshake. Every credential is short-lived, scoped, and verifiable
against a spec you can read. This section names each standard, shows the exact file/route/function
that implements it, and links the authoritative spec so you can audit the claim yourself.

Two design rules run through everything here:

- **No long-lived secrets cross a trust boundary.** Console sessions, CLI tokens, and cloud
 federation are all time-boxed. The only durable private keys (`ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY`,
 `CLI_JWT_SECRET`, `ALETHIA_RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY`) never leave the control plane.
- **Claims are verifiable.** Cloud assertions verify against a published JWKS; verify-gate evidence
 is ed25519-signed; the standards below define the wire format so a third party can check them.

records.title: Standards & Protocols
records.heading: Standards matrix
records.text:
Protocol / Standard Spec Where it is used in Alethia Status 
 --- --- --- --- 
 [OAuth 2.0](/standards/oauth2) [RFC 6749](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749) Console social sign-in (GitHub/Google native, GitLab/Bitbucket generic) via Better Auth Live 
 [OAuth 2.0 PKCE](/standards/oauth2) [RFC 7636](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7636) Better Auth authorization-code exchange for social sign-in Live 
 [OpenID Connect](/standards/oidc) [OIDC Core 1.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html) Enterprise SSO (Okta/Entra/Google/…) as Service Provider; MCP OAuth authorization server Live 
 [Device Authorization Grant](/standards/device-code) [RFC 8628](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8628) `alethia login` / `alethia init` browser-approval flow → CLI JWT Live (variant) 
 [Workload Identity / OIDC federation](/standards/workload-identity) [OIDC Core 1.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html) Keyless cloud connectors: AWS STS, GCP WIF, Azure federated identity Live 
 [JWT / JWS](/standards/jose) [RFC 7519](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7519) · [RFC 7515](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7515) CLI access/refresh tokens (HS256), workload assertions

records.title: Standards & Protocols
records.heading: How to read these pages
records.text:
Each page has the same three parts:

1. **What the standard is** — one or two paragraphs, no marketing.
2. **How Alethia implements it** — the real files, routes, functions, env vars, and tables.
3. **The authoritative spec** — the IETF RFC, OASIS document, or `openid.net` reference.

For the security model in prose, see [concepts/security](/concepts/security); for the authorization
layer (roles, grants, the PDP), see [access control](/access-control).

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.text: JWT, JWS & JOSE. Where Alethia uses JSON Web Tokens and signatures — Better Auth sessions, HS256 CLI tokens, RS256 workload assertions, and the ed25519-signed Elench verify evidence receipts.

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**JOSE** (JSON Object Signing and Encryption) is the family of IETF standards for representing keys,
signatures, and encryption over JSON. The two that matter here:

- **JWS** ([RFC 7515](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7515)) — a JSON Web Signature: a payload plus
 a detached or compact signature, with a header naming the algorithm (`HS256`, `RS256`, `EdDSA`, …).
- **JWT** ([RFC 7519](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7519)) — a JSON Web Token: a set of claims
 (`sub`, `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, …) carried as a JWS. A signed JWT is verifiable and tamper-evident:
 anyone with the key (symmetric) or public key (asymmetric) can confirm the claims were not altered.

Alethia uses signed tokens in several distinct places, each with the algorithm that fits its trust
boundary. It never encrypts tokens (no JWE) — every token here is *signed*, not secret-bearing.

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
Surface Format Alg Key Lifetime Code 
 --- --- --- --- --- --- 
 Console web session Better Auth session cookie (HTTP-only), backed by the `session` table — signed with `BETTER_AUTH_SECRET` rolling `lib/auth/index.ts` 
 CLI access token JWT `HS256` `CLI_JWT_SECRET` 1 hour `api/auth/cli/exchange` 
 CLI refresh token JWT `HS256` `CLI_JWT_SECRET` 90 days `api/auth/cli/exchange` 
 Cloud workload assertion JWT `RS256` `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY` ≤ 600s `lib/oidc/issuer.ts` 
 MCP access token OAuth 2.1 token (OIDC provider) — Better Auth `mcp()` plugin short `lib/auth/index.ts` 
 Verify evidence receipt detached ed25519 signature over canonical JSON `EdDSA` / ed25519 `ALETHIA_RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY` per-apply `packages/core/verify` 

 
 The **web session is not a JWT** in this build — there is no `jwt()` plugin. It is a Better Auth
 signed, HTTP-only session cookie referencing a `session` row (validated by Next.js middleware). The
 genuine JWT/JWS surfaces are the CLI tokens, the workload assertions, and the MCP tokens. The verify
 receipt is a JOSE-adjacent EdDSA signature, described below.

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: CLI tokens (HS256)
records.text:
`apps/console/app/api/auth/cli/exchange/route.ts` mints access/refresh JWTs with the `jose` library
(`new jose.SignJWT(...).setProtectedHeader({ alg: "HS256" })`). Claims: `sub` = profile id, `email`,
`type` ∈ {`access`, `refresh`}, `iss`/`aud` set to placeholder `urn:example:*` values, and `exp` of
1h / 90d respectively. `/api/auth/cli/refresh` verifies the refresh token
(`jose.jwtVerify`, matching `iss`/`aud`), rejects any token whose `type !== "refresh"`, and issues a
new 1-hour access token. See [device code](/standards/device-code) for the full flow.

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: Workload assertions (RS256)
records.text:
The [workload-identity](/standards/workload-identity) issuer signs its federation assertions RS256 —
the widest-compatible alg for AWS/GCP/Azure token exchange — and publishes the public key at
`/api/oidc/jwks` so clouds verify independently. Key rotation is supported by publishing a second
(previous) key while the primary rolls over.

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: Elench verify receipts (ed25519)
records.text:
The [Elench](/elench) verify gate emits a per-apply **evidence receipt** — a precise, honest record of
what was checked, against which plan, under which control set (`packages/core/verify/receipt.go`). The
receipt binds to the exact plan via `plan_sha256` and pins `tofu_version` / `provider_versions` /
`catalog_version`. When `ALETHIA_RECEIPT_SIGNING_KEY` is set (a base64 64-byte ed25519 private key,
`packages/core/verify/signing.go`), `Sign()` produces a `SignedReceipt`: a detached **ed25519**
([RFC 8032](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8032)) signature over the receipt's canonical JSON bytes,
plus `algorithm: "ed25519"` and a short `key_id` (`KeyID` = first 8 bytes of SHA-256 of the public
key). `Verify()` recomputes the canonical bytes and checks the signature — any tampering fails.

 
 **Root-of-trust honesty (from the code's own comments).** Signing is *additive evidence*, not a
 precondition — an unset key attaches an **unsigned** receipt rather than blocking the apply. And a
 signature from Alethia's runner only proves "Alethia asserted this". For a receipt to mean something
 to a customer's auditor, sign with a customer-controlled key and/or anchor the signature in a
 tra

records.title: JWT, JWS & JOSE
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text:
- [RFC 7519](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7519) — JSON Web Token (JWT).
- [RFC 7515](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7515) — JSON Web Signature (JWS).
- [RFC 8032](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8032) — EdDSA (Ed25519).

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.text: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE. Console social sign-in over OAuth 2.0 (RFC 6749) with PKCE (RFC 7636), implemented by Better Auth — GitHub/Google natively, GitLab/Bitbucket via the generic OAuth plugin.

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**OAuth 2.0** ([RFC 6749](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749)) is the authorization framework
that lets a user grant an application access to a resource without sharing a password. The web flow
Alethia uses is the **authorization-code grant**: the app redirects the user to the provider's
authorization endpoint, the provider redirects back with a short-lived `code`, and the app exchanges
that code at the token endpoint for an access token.

**PKCE** ([RFC 7636](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7636), "Proof Key for Code Exchange") hardens
that exchange against code-interception. The client generates a random `code_verifier`, sends its
SHA-256 hash (`code_challenge`) on the authorization request, and presents the raw verifier at token
exchange — so a stolen `code` is useless without the verifier.

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
The [Console](/concepts/glossary) delegates web sign-in to **Better Auth** (in-process, Drizzle
adapter). The auth instance is built in `apps/console/lib/auth/index.ts`; the single catch-all route
`apps/console/app/api/auth/[...all]/route.ts` serves every Better Auth endpoint (authorization start,
callback, token exchange). Better Auth drives the authorization-code + PKCE handshake — the console
never hand-rolls the flow.

Four social providers are registered, in two ways:

 Provider Registration Scopes requested 
 --- --- --- 
 GitHub native `socialProviders.github` `repo` (+ Better Auth's `read:user`/`user:email` defaults) 
 Google native `socialProviders.google` provider defaults 
 GitLab `genericOAuth` plugin (self-hosted base URL) `read_api read_user read_repository read_registry openid profile email` 
 Bitbucket `genericOAuth` plugin `account repository` 

Native GitHub/Google use Better Auth's built-in provider configs. GitLab and Bitbucket go through the
`genericOAuth` plugin (`import { genericOAuth } from "better-auth/plugins"`), configured with explicit
`authorizationUrl` / `tokenUrl` / `userInfoUrl` in `genericOAuthConfigs`. Each provider is registered
**only when its cr

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.heading: Where the token lands
records.text:
On callback, Better Auth exchanges the code (with PKCE) and creates a session — an HTTP-only cookie
backed by the `session` table. Next.js middleware validates it on every request; the
[PDP](/access-control/architecture) then resolves the active org and sets `app.current_org` for the
coarse Postgres [RLS](/concepts/security) tenant guard. See
[JWT / JWS](/standards/jose) for the token formats and [OIDC](/standards/oidc) for enterprise SSO.

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.heading: The MCP authorization server
records.text:
Alethia also *acts as* an OAuth 2.1 authorization server for its Model Context Protocol endpoint. The
`mcp()` plugin (also in `lib/auth/index.ts`) lets remote MCP clients register dynamically and obtain
an access token that `/api/mcp` resolves into a PDP-scoped actor — no new authority beyond the token
owner's normal scope.

records.title: OAuth 2.0 & PKCE
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text:
- [oauth.net/2](https://oauth.net/2/) — the OAuth 2.0 landing page and flow overview.
- [RFC 6749](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749) — The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework.
- [RFC 7636](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7636) — Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE).

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.text: OpenID Connect. Enterprise SSO with Alethia as the OIDC Service Provider — Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace, AWS IAM Identity Center and any OIDC IdP, via @better-auth/sso.

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**OpenID Connect (OIDC)** is a thin identity layer on top of [OAuth 2.0](/standards/oauth2). Where
OAuth 2.0 answers *"is this app allowed to act?"*, OIDC answers *"who is this user?"* — the IdP
returns a signed **ID token** (a JWT) with verified identity claims (`sub`, `email`, group/role
claims), and the Relying Party verifies it against the IdP's published JWKS. It is the protocol behind
"sign in with your company account".

Alethia plays two OIDC roles, kept strictly separate:

- **Relying Party / Service Provider** for enterprise SSO — it *consumes* a customer's IdP so members
 sign in with their corporate identity (this page).
- **Identity Provider** for keyless cloud federation — it *is* an OIDC issuer that clouds trust. That
 is a different subsystem; see [workload identity](/standards/workload-identity).

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
Enterprise SSO is an [Enterprise](/editions)-gated capability contributed by the `@alethia/ee` module
(`ee/src/index.ts`) through the `getAuthPlugins()` seam in `apps/console/lib/auth/plugins.ts`. It wraps
the **`@better-auth/sso`** plugin. Alethia is always the **Service Provider** — it never runs an IdP
of its own for user login (no Hydra, no Alethia-as-IdP).

Each [organization](/concepts/glossary) registers its own provider (rows in the `ssoProvider` table,
scoped by `organizationId`), so different orgs can federate different directories. Supported IdPs
include Okta, Microsoft Entra ID, Google Workspace, AWS IAM Identity Center, OneLogin, and Ping — any
standards-compliant OIDC provider. The server actions in `apps/console/app/server/actions/sso.ts`
manage provider CRUD and report each row's protocol (`oidc` `saml` `unknown`) from which config
block is present.

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: Domain verification and JIT provisioning
records.text: Two safeguards gate first sign-in:

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: Domain proof
records.text:
Registration is bound to domain ownership. `@better-auth/sso` is configured with
`domainVerification: { enabled: true, tokenPrefix: "alethia-sso" }` — the customer publishes a DNS
`TXT` record at `_alethia-sso- `. Until the domain is verified, sign-in through that
provider is rejected and no email-domain account-linking is trusted.

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: Entitlement gate
records.text:
A custom `alethia-sso-entitlement-guard` middleware blocks `POST /sso/register` unless the target org
holds the `sso` entitlement — so a non-Enterprise admin cannot register a provider by calling the API
directly. The UI shows the upsell instead.

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: Just-in-time member provisioning
records.text:
On first successful SSO sign-in, the user is provisioned into their org with
`organizationProvisioning.defaultRole: "member"` (least-privileged). The
[PDP](/access-control/architecture) then authorizes them exactly like any other
[member](/access-control/members-and-invitations) — mapping better-auth's `member` to Alethia's
viewer-scoped access.

 

 
 **Honest current state.** SSO users are always provisioned as least-privileged members — automatic
 **IdP group-claim → Alethia role** mapping (owner/admin/operator/viewer) is a documented follow-up,
 not yet wired. Directory-driven provisioning/deprovisioning (SCIM) is **not implemented**; see
 [SAML & SCIM](/standards/scim-saml).
 

For the operator-facing setup (fields per protocol, the register flow), see
[access control → SSO](/access-control/sso).

records.title: OpenID Connect
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text:
- [openid.net — How OpenID Connect works](https://openid.net/developers/how-connect-works/).
- [OpenID Connect Core 1.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html) — the specification.

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.text: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0. Enterprise directory federation — SAML 2.0 SSO is implemented (Enterprise, Service Provider) with documented hardening follow-ups; SCIM 2.0 provisioning is NOT implemented (just-in-time provisioning only).

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.text:
Two enterprise directory standards, at two different maturities. Read the status on each honestly —
one is live, one is roadmap.

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**SAML 2.0** (Security Assertion Markup Language, OASIS) is the long-standing XML protocol for
enterprise SSO. The Identity Provider (IdP) issues a digitally-signed XML **assertion** about the
user; the Service Provider (SP) validates the signature against the IdP's X.509 certificate and logs
the user in. It predates OIDC and is still the default in many enterprise directories.

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
Alethia is the **Service Provider** — same posture as [OIDC](/standards/oidc). SAML providers are
registered per [organization](/concepts/glossary) via the `@better-auth/sso` plugin (`ee/src/index.ts`,
[Enterprise](/editions)-gated). SAML config is stored in the `ssoProvider.samlConfig` column; the
server actions in `apps/console/app/server/actions/sso.ts` parse it to surface the IdP entry point and
a **SHA-256 fingerprint of the signing certificate**, and classify each row's protocol as
`oidc` `saml` `unknown` (a row with neither config is reported `unknown` rather than mislabelled).
Registration collects the IdP entity ID, SSO entry-point URL, and X.509 signing certificate (see
[access control → SSO](/access-control/sso)).

Domain verification (DNS `TXT` at `_alethia-sso- `) and the same
`alethia-sso-entitlement-guard` gate apply to SAML exactly as to OIDC, and users are provisioned
just-in-time as least-privileged members.

 
 **Documented hardening follow-ups (from `ee/src/index.ts`).** The SAML integration ships with two
 standup items still open: enabling `algorithms.onDeprecated: "reject"` (reject deprecated signature
 algorithms) and turning on `InResponseTo` validation. Al

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**SCIM 2.0** (System for Cross-domain Identity Management, [RFC 7644](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7644)
protocol + [RFC 7643](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7643) schema) is the REST standard for
**automatic** user/group provisioning and *de*-provisioning. A directory (Okta, Entra) pushes
create/update/delete against the SP's `/scim/v2/Users` and `/scim/v2/Groups` endpoints, so access is
granted and — critically — **revoked** the moment HR changes the directory, with no sign-in required.

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: Reality in Alethia
records.text:
SCIM is **not built**. There is no `/scim/v2` endpoint. Membership is populated only by **just-in-time
provisioning** — a user becomes a member the first time they successfully sign in through SSO, and is
never automatically deprovisioned by the directory.

This is stated honestly in the product itself: the console SSO settings
(`apps/console/components/settings/sso/sso-manager.tsx`) render a plain note that *"automatic user
provisioning/deprovisioning from your IdP isn't available yet — SSO users are provisioned
just-in-time on first sign-in"*, with a "talk to us" link. The code comments record that an earlier
UI advertised a `/scim/v2/ ` URL that never existed; that fake card was removed. The plan
catalog lists SCIM as *"coming soon"*.

 
 Do not represent SCIM as available. If a deployment's compliance posture depends on **automatic
 deprovisioning** (offboarding removing access without an admin action), that requirement is **not
 met today** — plan around JIT provisioning and manual member removal
 ([members & invitations](/access-control/members-and-invitations)).

records.title: SAML 2.0 & SCIM 2.0
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text:
- [OASIS — SAML 2.0 Core](https://docs.oasis-open.org/security/saml/v2.0/saml-core-2.0-os.pdf).
- [RFC 7644](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7644) — SCIM 2.0 Protocol · [RFC 7643](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7643) — SCIM 2.0 Core Schema.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.text: Security & Compliance. The real security controls behind Alethia — tenant isolation via Postgres RLS, keyless cloud credentials, the fail-closed verify gate, least-privilege connector roles, audit logging, and the state proxy — mapped to the standards, with an honest SOC 2 posture.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Security & Compliance
records.text:
This page maps Alethia's **real, shipped** controls to the standards in this section. Every control
below has code evidence. The compliance framing is deliberately conservative — see the SOC 2 note at
the end.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Tenant isolation — coarse Postgres RLS
records.text:
Every tenant-owned table is scoped by `org_id`, and Postgres **Row-Level Security** enforces the
boundary at the database. After authentication, the [PDP](/access-control/architecture) resolves the
active [organization](/concepts/glossary) and sets `app.current_org` via `set_config`; RLS policies
key on that setting, so a query can only ever see its own org's rows even if application code forgets a
`WHERE`. This is *coarse* isolation (org-level), complemented by the fine-grained PDP for
role/resource authorization. See [concepts/security](/concepts/security) and
[access control](/access-control).

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Keyless cloud credentials — zero stored secrets
records.text:
Alethia stores **no** static cloud credentials. All four managed clouds federate keylessly through the
Alethia OIDC issuer ([workload identity](/standards/workload-identity)); the only durable private key
is the issuer's own signing key, held on the control plane and never shared. Revocation is the
customer deleting the trust in their own account — there is nothing to leak from Alethia's side.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Fail-closed verify gate — Elench
records.text:
On a real apply the [runner](/runner) runs the [Elench](/elench) `verify` gate between `tofu plan` and
`tofu apply` (`provisioner.RunDeployV2` over `packages/core/verify`). It is **fail-closed**: a hard
control failure (keyless / least-privilege / OIDC-subject checks) blocks the apply unless an
authorized, time-boxed `verify.Override` waives it — and the waiver is recorded in the evidence
receipt. The report is forwarded on `execution_metadata["verify_result"]` and rendered in the Console
Plan tab. Evidence receipts are ed25519-signable ([JOSE](/standards/jose)).

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Least-privilege connector roles
records.text:
The cloud roles Alethia assumes are scoped down from cloud-admin to the minimum needed to provision
and inspect — not blanket administrative access — narrowing blast radius even within a federated
session.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Runner holds no storage credentials — the state proxy
records.text:
The runner never receives object-storage keys. `tofu` state is read and written through the Console
**state proxy** (`backend "http"` → `/api/jobs/{id}/state`, authenticated by a per-job token in
`TF_HTTP_PASSWORD`). A compromised runner cannot reach the state bucket directly. The runner images
also ship **CLI-free** (no bundled `aws`/`gcloud`/`az`); cluster tokens are minted in-process.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Audit / activity log
records.text:
Membership and authorization-relevant actions (invites, removals, role changes, policy decisions) are
written to the org **activity log** (`recordActivity` / `enforceDecision`, surfaced at
[access control → activity log](/access-control/activity-log)). It gives a per-org trail of who did
what.

 
 **Known honest limitation.** The activity/audit log today has no dedicated RLS policy or
 write-once (WORM) retention guarantee — it is an application-level trail, not yet a tamper-evident
 store. Note this when scoping evidence requirements; it is a tracked hardening item, not a shipped
 guarantee.

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Control-to-standard map
records.text:
Control Backing standard Evidence 
 --- --- --- 
 Web sign-in [OAuth 2.0 + PKCE](/standards/oauth2) `lib/auth/index.ts`, Better Auth 
 Enterprise SSO [OIDC](/standards/oidc) · [SAML 2.0](/standards/scim-saml) `ee/src/index.ts`, `@better-auth/sso` 
 CLI auth [Device grant](/standards/device-code) · [JWT](/standards/jose) `api/auth/cli/*` 
 Keyless cloud access [Workload identity](/standards/workload-identity) `lib/oidc/issuer.ts`, `lib/cloud-providers/session/*` 
 Tamper-evident apply evidence [ed25519 / EdDSA](/standards/jose) `packages/core/verify` 
 Tenant isolation Postgres RLS (`org_id`) `app.current_org` + RLS policies

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: SOC 2 posture — read this carefully
records.text:
Alethia is **built to support SOC 2** — the controls above map to the Security and Confidentiality
Trust Services Criteria (least-privilege access, no stored credentials, tenant isolation, change
verification, audit trail). This is **SOC 2 readiness / control mapping**, and nothing more.

 
 Alethia is **not SOC 2 certified**, **not SOC 2 compliant**, and no SOC 2 report has been issued.
 SOC 2 is an attestation performed by an independent auditor over an observation period; **no such
 formal audit has been performed and no report exists yet.** Anyone stating otherwise is wrong. The
 accurate phrasing is "designed for SOC 2 readiness" / "controls mapped to SOC 2 criteria".
 

For the security model in prose, see [concepts/security](/concepts/security).

records.title: Security & Compliance
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text: - [AICPA — SOC 2](https://www.aicpa-cima.com/topic/audit-assurance/audit-and-assurance-greater-than-soc-2) — what a SOC 2 report is (and requires).

records.title: Cloud Workload Identity
records.text: Cloud Workload Identity. Keyless cloud connectors — one Alethia OIDC issuer federates into AWS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity), GCP (Workload Identity Federation), and Azure (federated identity). No static cloud secrets are ever stored.

records.title: Cloud Workload Identity
records.heading: What the standard is
records.text:
**Workload identity federation** lets a workload prove *what it is* to a cloud without holding a
long-lived cloud key. The workload obtains a short-lived, signed **OIDC token** from an issuer the
cloud trusts, presents it to the cloud's token exchange, and receives temporary cloud credentials in
return. Trust is established once (the cloud is configured to trust the issuer's URL, its published
JWKS, and a specific `sub`/`aud`); after that every access is a fresh, expiring assertion. The static
attack surface — access keys, service-account JSON, client secrets — disappears.

Each major cloud has its own exchange built on the same OIDC primitive:

- **AWS** — STS `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` against an IAM OIDC identity provider.
- **GCP** — Workload Identity Federation (STS token exchange into a workload identity pool).
- **Azure** — a federated identity credential on an Entra ID app (client-assertion grant).

records.title: Cloud Workload Identity
records.heading: How Alethia implements it
records.text:
The control plane **is its own OIDC issuer**, purpose-built for machine federation and nothing else
(`apps/console/lib/oidc/issuer.ts`). It mints short-lived **RS256** JWTs and publishes the matching
public material so any cloud can verify them:

 Property Value 
 --- --- 
 Issuer `${APP_URL}/api/oidc` 
 Discovery `/api/oidc/.well-known/openid-configuration` 
 JWKS `/api/oidc/jwks` 
 Signing alg `RS256` (widest cloud-federation compatibility) 
 Subject (`sub`) `alethia-connector` (single, stable, non-secret) 
 Max token TTL 600 seconds 
 Signing key `ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY` (PKCS8, vault-held) `+ ALETHIA_OIDC_SIGNING_KEY_PREVIOUS` for zero-downtime rotation 

`mintWorkloadToken()` produces a per-cloud-scoped assertion (audience set per provider); the same
issuer serves both the [Console](/concepts/glossary) (server-side inventory/health) and the
[runner](/runner) (provisioning). **No platform cloud account and no stored customer secret exists
anywhere on the Alethia side** — revocation is the customer deleting the trust in their own cloud.

 api/oidc RS256] --> mint sub=alethia-connector TOK[Short-lived JWT]
 TOK --> AWS[AWS STS AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity]
 TOK --> GCP[GCP STS Wor

records.title: Cloud Workload Identity
records.heading: Per-cloud exchange
records.text:
`apps/console/lib/cloud-providers/session/aws.ts`. The customer's IAM role trusts the Alethia issuer
**directly** as an IAM OIDC provider, so the console/runner federate straight in via
`AssumeRoleWithWebIdentityCommand` (`@aws-sdk/client-sts`) presenting a freshly minted assertion.
`AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` is an unauthenticated STS action — **no platform AWS account, no
`ExternalId`, no stored key**. Session ≈ 1 hour, auto-refreshed.

 
 

`apps/console/lib/cloud-providers/session/gcp.ts` + `gcp-wif.ts`. A direct-OIDC Workload Identity
Federation config exchanges the Alethia-minted JWT for a GCP access token. The audience encodes the
pool/provider:
`//iam.googleapis.com/projects/ /locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/ /providers/ `.
Connection validation asserts the audience contains `workloadIdentityPools` (`connections.ts`).

 
 

`apps/console/lib/cloud-providers/session/azure.ts`. Alethia registers **one multi-tenant Entra app**
(`ALETHIA_AZURE_CLIENT_ID`) whose **federated-identity credential** trusts the Alethia issuer. The
console authenticates as that app via a client assertion (the minted JWT) — keyless, no client
secret. The federated-credential `subject` must equal

records.title: Cloud Workload Identity
records.heading: Authoritative reference
records.text:
- [OpenID Connect Core 1.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html) — the token format clouds verify.
- [AWS — `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity.html) and [creating an IAM OIDC provider](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_providers_create_oidc.html).
- [GCP — Workload Identity Federation](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workload-identity-federation).
- [Azure — Workload identity federation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/workload-id/workload-identity-federation).
